22.meaning: Where great dark rain-clouds settle in masses and thunder, and mighty serpents, mistaking the sound for an elephant, stir and move about like the very mountain — there is our Lord of Pridhi. This garland of resounding verse of Kaliyan (Tirumangai Alwar), lord of Tirumangai of cool groves where striped bees hum, for the good devotees who sing its rare sweet music, no cruel karma shall ever cling.

23.translit:
mutram mooththu kOl thuNaiyaa mun adi nOkki vaLaindhu
iRRa kaal pOl thaLLi meLLa irundhu angu iLaiyaa mun,
peRRa thaay pOl vandha pEychchi peru mulai oodu uyirai
vaRRa vaangi uNda vaayaan vadari vaNanguthumE.
23.meaning: Before we grow utterly old, leaning on a staff for support, peering at our own feet, bent over, tottering on legs that seem broken, and there waste away in feebleness — let us worship Him of Badari (Vadari), whose mouth sucked dry the very life through the great breast of the demoness Putana, who came disguised like the mother who bore Him.

24.translit:
mudhugu paRRi kai thalaththaal mun oru kOl oondri,
vidhir vidhirththu kaN suzhandru mEl kiLai koNdu irumi,
idhu en appar mooththavaaru endru iLaiyavar Esaamun,
madhu uN vaNdu paNgaL paadum vadari vaNanguthumE.
24.meaning: Before, gripping our back with our hand and planting a staff before us, shivering and shaking, eyes rolling, coughing with a rasping in the chest, and the young ones jeer, ‘What is this, how aged our father has become!’ — let us worship Him of Badari, where honey-drinking bees sing their melodies.

25.translit:
uRigaL pOl mei narambu ezhundhu oon thaLarndhu uLLam eLgi,
neRiyai nOkki kaN suzhandru nindru nadungaamun,
aRidhi aagil nenjam anbaay aayira naamam solli,
veRi koL vaNdu paNgaL paadum vadari vaNanguthumE.
25.meaning: Before the sinews stand out on the body like the cords of a hanging sling, the flesh slackens, the mind weakens, the eyes roll while we stare at the road ahead and stand trembling — O heart, if you have any wisdom, lovingly speak His thousand names; let us worship Him of Badari, where fragrant bees sing their melodies.

26.translit:
peeLai sora kaN nidungi pith ezha mooththu irumi
thaaLgaL nOva thammil mutti thaLLi nadavaamun,
kaaLai aagi kandru mEyththu kundru eduththu andru nindraan,
vaaLai paayum thaN thadam soozh vadari vaNanguthumE.
26.meaning: Before, with rheum oozing, eyes shrinking, bile rising, aged and coughing, knees aching and knocking against one another, we stagger and totter as we walk — let us worship Him of Badari, ringed by cool tanks where the vaalai fish leap, He who as a youth herded the calves and once stood lifting up the mountain (Govardhana).

27.translit:
paNdu kaamar aanavaarum paavaiyar vaay amudham
uNdavaarum, vaazhndha vaarum okka uraiththu irumi,
thaNdu kaalaa oondri oondri thaLLi nadavaamun,
vaNdu paadum thaN thuzhaayaan vadari vaNanguthumE.
27.meaning: Before, recounting over and over with coughing how desirable we once were, how we tasted the nectar of young women’s lips, and how grandly we lived, and leaning again and again on a staff for a leg, we stagger and totter as we walk — let us worship Him of Badari, who wears the cool tulasi over which bees hum.

28.translit:
eyththa sollOdu eeLai Engi irumi iLaiththu udalam
piththar pOla siththam vERaay pEsi ayaraamun,
aththan endhai aadhi moorththi aazh kadalai kadaindha
maiththa sOdhi em perumaan vadari vaNanguthumE.
28.meaning: Before, with faltering speech and phlegm we gasp and cough and grow weak in body, and like men deranged our mind wanders and we babble and faint — let us worship Him of Badari, our Father, our Sire, the Primal Form, the dark radiant Lord who churned the deep ocean.

29.translit:
pappa appar mooththa aaRu paazhppadhu seeththiraLai
oppa aikkaL pOdha undha un thamarkku aaNmin endru,
seppu nEr men kongai nallaar thaam siriyaadha munnam,
vaippum nangaL vaazhvum aanaan vadari vaNanguthumE.
29.meaning: Before the women with soft breasts shapely as caskets laugh and say, ‘Alas, see how the father has aged into ruin! The phlegm collects, and as the breath is forced out he pants — attend to your own folk!’ — let us worship Him of Badari, who is our treasure and our very life.

30.translit:
eesi pOmin neengirEnmin irumi iLaiththeer uLLam
koosiyitteer endru pEsum kuvaLai am kaNNiyar paal,
naasam aana paasam vittu nal neRi nOkkal uRil,
vaasam malgu thaN thuzhaayaan vadari vaNanguthumE.
30.meaning: ‘Shoo, be off, do not come near — you cough, you are worn out, you have shrunk in spirit!’ say the women with eyes like the blue lily; if we would cast off the ruinous bondage to them and look to the good path — let us worship Him of Badari, who wears the cool fragrance-laden tulasi.

31.translit:
pulangaL naiya meyyil mooththu pOndhu irundhu uLLam eLgi,
kalanga aikkaL pOdha undhi kaNda pidhaRRaamun,
alangal aaya thaN thuzhaay koNdu aayira naamam solli,
valam koL thoNdar paadi aadum vadari vaNanguthumE.
31.meaning: Before the senses fail and the body ages, and we sit slumped, the mind weakening and troubled, the phlegm welling up and forced out, and we babble whatever we see — taking the cool tulasi garland and speaking His thousand names — let us worship Him of Badari, where mighty devotees sing and dance.

32.translit:
vaNdu thaN thEn uNdu vaazhum vadari nedumaalai,
kaNdal vEli mangai vEndhan kaliyan oli maalai,
koNdu thoNdar paadi aada koodidil neeL visumbil,
aNdam allaal maRRavarkku Or aatchi aRiyOmE.
32.meaning: This resounding garland of Kaliyan (Tirumangai Alwar), king of Tirumangai hedged with screw-pine, on the great Lord of Badari where bees live drinking the cool honey — if devotees take it up to sing and dance, then for them, in the vast high heavens, we know of no dominion other than the supreme realm itself.

33.translit:
Enam un aagi iru nilam idandhu andru iNai adi imaiyavar vaNanga,
thaanavan aagam tharaNiyil puraLa thadam silai kuniththa em thalaivan,
thEn amar sOlai kaRpagam payandha deyva nal naRu malar koNarndhu,
vaanavar vaNangum gangaiyin karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
33.meaning: Long ago becoming the Boar, He raised up the wide earth, and the celestials worshipped His twin feet; and as our Lord He bent His great bow so that the demon’s body rolled upon the ground (Rama slaying Ravana). He dwells in Badari-Ashram on the bank of the Ganga, where the gods bring the divine fragrant blossoms borne by the kalpaka tree of the honey-filled groves and worship Him.

34.translit:
kaan idai uruvai sudu saram thurandhu kaNdum un kodun thozhil uravOn,
oon udai agalaththu adu kaNai kuLippa uyir kavarndhu ugandha em oruvan,
thEn udai kamalaththu ayanodu thEvar sendru sendru iRainjida perugu
vaan idai mudhu neer gangai ing karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
34.meaning: In the forest He sped the searing arrow at the form (of Maricha the deer), and the mighty one of fierce deeds — even seeing it — our peerless One rejoiced as His deadly shaft sank into the broad chest (of the foe) and seized away the life. He dwells in Badari-Ashram here on the bank of the Ganga of ancient swelling waters that pours from the heavens, where Brahma seated on the honeyed lotus and the gods come again and again to bow before Him.

35.translit:
ilangaiyum kadalum adal arun thuppin iru nidhikku iraivanum, arakkar
kulangaLum keda mun kodun thozhil purindha kotravan kozhum sudar suzhandra,
vilangalil urinji mEl nindra visumbil veN thugil kodi ena virindhu,
valam tharu maNi neer gangaiyin karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
35.meaning: He, the victor, who of old wrought fierce deeds so that Lanka and the sea fell, and the lord of the two treasures (Kubera, or the strong-armed Ravana) and the clans of demons were destroyed — He dwells in Badari-Ashram on the bank of the bright-watered, boon-granting Ganga, which, rubbing against the mountains where the radiant sun wheels and spreading like a white-cloth banner across the high sky, descends.

36.translit:
thuNivu ini unakku solluvan manamE, thozhudhu ezhu thoNdargaL thamakku,
piNi ozhiththu amarar peru visumbu aruLum pEr aruLaaLan em perumaan,
aNi malar kuzhalaar arambaiyar thugilum aaramum vaari vandhu,
aNi neer maNi kozhiththu izhindha gangaiyin karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
36.meaning: My resolve I now declare to you, O heart: worship and rise! For the devotees who bow to Him, He removes their afflictions and grants them the great heaven of the immortals — our Lord of boundless grace. He dwells in Badari-Ashram on the bank of the Ganga, whose lovely waters come sweeping along the garments and garlands of the celestial nymphs (apsaras) with flower-decked tresses and wash down gems as they descend.

37.translit:
pEy idai kirundhu vandha matravaL than peru mulai suvaiththida petra
thaay idai kiruththal anjuvan endru thaLarndhida vaLarndha em thalaivan,
sEy mugattu sui aNdamum sumandha sem pon sei vilangalil ilangu,
vaay mugattu izhindha gangaiyin karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
37.meaning: When He had sucked the great breast of that demoness (Putana) who came in the form of a foster-nurse, He grew up making her despair, as if to say, ‘I am afraid to be set in the lap of the very mother who bore me!’ — our Lord. He dwells in Badari-Ashram on the bank of the Ganga that descends from the mouth-like summit shining on the reddish-gold Meru mountain that bears the lofty crown of the universe.

38.translit:
thEr aNangu algul sezhum kayal kaNNi thiRaththu oru maRa thozhil purindhu,
paar aNangu imil ERu Ezhum un adarththa pani mugil vaNNan em perumaan,
kaaraNam thannaal kadum punal kayaththa karu varai piLavu ezha kuththi,
vaaraNam koNarndha gangaiyin karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
38.meaning: For the sake of her of the chariot-shapely waist and lush carp-like eyes (Nappinnai), He wrought a deed of valour and once subdued the seven humped bulls that were a torment to the world — our Lord, dark as the cool rain-cloud. He dwells in Badari-Ashram on the bank of the Ganga, which for good cause pierced the dark mountain so that it split, bringing its fierce deep waters — the river that bore an elephant along.

39.translit:
vendiRal kaLiRum vElai vaay amudhum viNNodu viNNavarkku arasum,
indhiraRku aruLi emakkum eendhu aruLum endhai em adigaL em perumaan,
andharaththu amarar adi iNai vaNanga aayira mugaththinaal aruLi,
mandharaththu izhindha gangaiyin karai mEl vadari aachchiraamaththu uLLaanE.
39.meaning: He granted to Indra the fierce mighty elephant (Airavata), the nectar from the sea, the heavens, and the sovereignty over the celestials, and He grants His grace to us too — our Father, our Master, our Lord. He dwells in Badari-Ashram on the bank of the Ganga, which, while the immortals of the firmament bow at His twin feet, He graciously sent forth through a thousand mouths to descend upon Mount Mandara.

200.translit:
paareeRu perumbaaran theerap paNDu paarathaththuth thoothiyangki,
paarththan selvath theereeRu saarathiyaay ethirndhaar seenai serukkaLaththuth thiRalazhiyas seRRaanRannai,
pooreeR onRudaiyaanum aLakaik koonum purandharanum naanmukanum porundhum oorppool,
seereeRu maRaiyaaLar niRaindha selvath thirukkovaloor athanuL kaNDeen naanee.
200.meaning: Long ago, to relieve the earth of its great burden, He went as an envoy in the Mahabharata war and, becoming the charioteer driving the splendid chariot of Arjuna, He destroyed in the battlefield the might of the opposing armies that confronted Him. Him I beheld in the wealthy Tirukkovalur, filled with glorious Vedic brahmins, dwelling there as in a city where Shiva who bears the bull-banner, Kubera lord of Alaka, Indra, and the four-faced Brahma abide together. Him I myself beheld there.

201.translit:
thoovaDivin paarmakaL poomangkaiyoDu suDaraazhi sangku irubaal polindhu thoonRa,
kaavaDivin kaRpakamee pola ninRu kalandhavarkaT karuLpuriyung karuththinaanai,
seevaDi kai thiruvaay kaN sivandha aaDai sembonsey thiruvuruvam aanaan Rannai,
theevaDivin sivanayanee polvaar mannu thirukkovaloor athanuL kaNDeen naanee.
201.meaning: With the pure-formed Earth-goddess and Lakshmi the lotus-lady, and the radiant discus and conch shining on either side, He stands like a wish-granting Kalpaka tree of the grove, His mind set on granting grace to those who unite with Him. With ruddy holy feet, hands, sacred mouth and eyes, clad in a reddened garment, His sacred form fashioned of pure gold. Him I beheld in Tirukkovalur, where dwell forever those resembling Shiva of fiery form and Brahma. Him I myself beheld there.

202.translit:
vaaraNangkoL iDarkkaDindha maalai neela maradhakaththai mazhaimukilee polvaanRannai,
seeraNangku maRaiyaaLar niRaindha selvath thirukkovaloor athanuL kaNDeen, enRu
vaaraNangku mulaimaDavaar mangkai veendhan vaaTkaliyan oliyaindhum aindhum vallaar,
kaaraNangk alaaluham kalandhangk eeththak karandhengkum parandhaanaik kaaNbar thaamee.
202.meaning: Tirumal who removed the distress of the elephant Gajendra, dark like the blue emerald, resembling a rain-cloud, I beheld in the wealthy Tirukkovalur filled with glorious Vedic brahmins. Those who master all ten verses of the garland by Kaliyan, king of the city of women whose breasts are bound by bodices, lord of the sword. They themselves shall behold Him who, hidden, pervades everywhere, when, for no other cause than His own grace, they mingle and praise Him as the world joins in.

203.translit:
irundhaN maanilam eenamadhaay vaLai maruppinil akaththoDukki,
karundhaN maakaDal kaNDuyinRavan iDam kamala nan malarththeeRal arundhi,
innisai muranRezhum aLikulam pothuLiyam pozhiloodee,
serundhi naaNmalar senRaNaindhu uzhitharu thiruvayindhirapuramee.
203.meaning: He who became the boar and tucked the cool wide earth into His curved tusk, and who reclines in sleep upon the dark cool great ocean. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram, where through the thick groves swarms of bees, drinking the nectar of the fine lotus blossoms, rise humming sweet music and wander, approaching the fresh-blooming serundhi flowers.

204.translit:
minnu maazhiyangkaiyavan seyyavaL uRaitharu thirumaarban,
pannu naanmaRaip palaporuL aakiya paranidam varaichchaaral,
pinnu maadhavip pandhalil peDaivarap piNiyavizh kamalaththu,
thenna venRuvaN Din innisai muraltharu thiruvayindhirapuramee.
204.meaning: He of the beautiful hand bearing the flashing discus, on whose sacred chest dwells the red-hued Lakshmi, the Supreme One who is the manifold meaning of the four recited Vedas. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram, on the mountain slope, where the female bees come to the intertwined Madhavi-creeper bowers, and upon the loosed-bud lotuses the rich bees hum sweet music crying 'thenna'.

205.translit:
vaiya meezhum uNDaalilai vaikiya maayavan,
aDiyavarkku meyyanaakiya theyvanaayakan iDam meythaku varaichchaaral,
moykoL maadhavi saNbakam muyangkiya mullaiyam koDiyaaDa,
seyyathaamaraich chezhumpaNai thikazhtharu thiruvayindhirapuramee.
205.meaning: The wondrous one who swallowed all seven worlds and lay upon the banyan leaf, the divine Lord who is true to His devotees. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram, on the true beautiful mountain slope, where the lovely jasmine creepers sway as they entwine the swarming Madhavi and Champaka, and the rich pools of red lotuses gleam.

206.translit:
maaRu koNDuDanRu ethirndha vallavuNanRan maarpaham irupiLavaa,
kooRu koNDavan kulamakaRk innaruL koDuththavan iDam,
miDaindhu saaRu koNDamen karumbiLang kazhaithakai visumbuRa maNineezhal,
seeRu koNDathaN pazhanam athezhilthikazh thiruvayindhirapuramee.
206.meaning: He who split into two the chest of the mighty demon Hiranya, who in enmity stood and confronted Him, and who bestowed sweet grace upon that demon's noble son Prahlada. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram, where the cool fields gleam with beauty, where the tender dark sugarcanes, dense and full of juice, with their young stalks rise toward the sky giving a jewel-like shade, holding rich mire.

207.translit:
aangku maavali veeLviyil irandhusenRu akaliDam aLandhu,
aayar poongkoDikkina viDaiporudha avanidam ponmalar thikazh,
veengkai koongku seNbakak kombinil kuthikoDu kurakkinam iraiththooDi,
theenkalandhaN palangkani nukarttharu thiruvayindhirapuramee.
207.meaning: There at Mahabali's sacrifice He went and begged, and measured the vast earth; and He fought and slew the bulls for the sake of the flower-creeper-like cowherd maiden Nappinnai. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram, where troops of monkeys leap upon the branches of the golden-blossomed Vengai, Kongu and Champaka trees, and rush about clamouring, feeding on the cool ripe fruits mingled with honey.

208.translit:
koonulaaviya maDandhaithan koDunjsolin thiRaththiLang koDiyoDum,
kaanulaaviya karumukil thiruniRath thavanidam kavinaarum,
vaanulaaviya mathithavazh maalvarai maamathiL puDaisoozha,
theenulaaviya sezhumpozhil thazhuviya thiruvayindhirapuramee.
208.meaning: He who, on account of the cruel words of the hunchbacked Manthara, went to the forest together with the tender creeper-like Sita, He of the sacred hue of the dark cloud that roams the forest. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram of surpassing beauty, surrounded by great ramparts like high mountains over which the moon roaming the sky glides, embraced by rich groves where honey flows.

209.translit:
minnin nuNNiDai maDakkoDi kaaraNam vilangkalin misaiyilangkai mannan,
neeNmuDi poDiseydha maindhan athiDam maNi varaineezhal,
anna maamala aravindhaththu amaLiyil peDaiyoDum inidhamara,
sennel aarkkavarik kulai veesuthaN thiruvayindhirapuramee.
209.meaning: The mighty one who, for the sake of the slender-waisted creeper-like Sita, ground to dust the long crowns of the king of Lanka set upon the mountain. His place is the cool Tiruvayindirapuram, in the shade of the jewel-mountain, where the swans rest sweetly with their mates upon a couch of great lotus blossoms, while the ripe red paddy waves its sheaves like fanning chowries.

210.translit:
viraikamazhndha men karungkuzhal kaaraNam villiRuththu aDalmazhaikku,
niraikalangkiDa varaikuDai yeDuththavan nilaviya iDam thaDamaar,
varaivaLandhikazh mathakari maruppoDu malaivaLar akilundhi,
thiraikoNarndhaNai sezhunadhi vayalpuku thiruvayindhirapuramee.
210.meaning: He who broke the bow for the sake of Sita of soft dark fragrant tresses, and who, when the fierce rain came and the herds were thrown into confusion, lifted up the mountain as an umbrella. His abiding place is Tiruvayindirapuram, full of broad expanses, where the rich river, its waves carrying along the tusks of rut-elephants from the bountiful mountain and the mountain-grown eagle-wood, flows into the fields.

211.translit:
veelkoL kaiththalath tharasarvem poorinil visayanukkaay,
maNiththeerk koolkoL kaiththalath thendhaipem maanidam kulavuthaN varaichchaaral,
kaalkoL kaNkoDik kaiyezhak kamukiLam paaLaikaL kamazhsaaral,
seelkaL paaytharu sezhunadhi vayalpuku thiruvayindhirapuramee.
211.meaning: In the fierce battle of the spear-wielding kings, for the sake of Arjuna, my Father and Lord took the rein-staff in His hand upon the jewelled chariot. His place is Tiruvayindirapuram, on the cool delightful mountain slope, where the slope is fragrant with the young spathes of the areca palms whose stems stand like raised flag-poles, and where the rich river into which sel-fish leap flows into the fields.

212.translit:
moovaraakiya oruvanai moovula kuNDumizhndh aLandhaanai,
theevar thaanavar senRusenR iRainjsaththaN thiruvayindhirapuraththu,
meevu soothiyai veelvalavan kalikanRi viriththuraiththa,
paavu thaNDamizh paththivai paaDiDap paavangkaL payilaavee.
212.meaning: The One who is the three (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva), who swallowed, spat forth and measured the three worlds. The radiant Light who dwells in cool Tiruvayindirapuram, whom gods and demons come again and again to worship. These ten verses of flowing cool Tamil composed by Kaliyan, master of the spear: for those who sing them, sins will not multiply.

213.translit:
oonvaaDa uNNaadhu uyirkkaaval iTTu uDalil piriyaap pulanaindhum nondhu,
thaamvaaDa vaaDath thavamseyya veeNDaa thamadhaa imaiyoor ulakaaLak iRpeer
kaanaaDa manjnjaik kaNamaaDa maaDee kayalaaDu kaaneerp pazhanam puDaipooy,
theenaaDa maaDak koDiyaaDu thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
213.meaning: You need not perform austerities, starving until the flesh withers, holding the life-breath in restraint, tormenting the five senses that do not leave the body, until you waste away utterly. If you wish to rule as your own the world of the celestials who never blink, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in Chillai, where the groves dance, flocks of peacocks dance, nearby the carp-fish leap in the forest-waters of the fields, and where over the mansions honey-flow abounds and the banners flutter.

214.translit:
kaayoDu neeDu kaniyuNDu veesu kaDungkaal nukarndhu neDungkaalam,
aindhu theeyoDu ninRu thavanjseyya veeNDaa thirumaarbanais sindhai yuLvaiththu menbeer,
vaayoodhu veedham malkinRa tholseer maRaiyaaLar naaLum muRaiyaal vaLarththa,
theeyoongka voongkap pukazhoongku thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
214.meaning: You need not perform austerities for long ages, eating unripe and ripe fruits and consuming only the rushing wind, standing amid the five fires. If you wish to keep the sacred-chested Lord within your thoughts, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in Chillai, where the ancient glory thrives, where the Veda-reciting brahmins day by day nurture the sacrificial fires in due order, so that as the fires rise high its fame too rises high.

215.translit:
vembum sinaththup punakkeezhal onRaay virineer muthuveLLam uTpuk kazhundha,
vambuN pozhilsoo zhulakanRu eDuththaan aDippoo thaNaivaan viruppoDu iruppeer,
paimponnu muththum maNiyum koNarndhu paDaimannavan pallavarkkoon paNindha,
sembon maNimaaDangkaL soozhndha thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
215.meaning: He became a single wild boar of burning wrath and, plunging into the deep ancient flood of the spreading waters, long ago lifted up the world girt by fragrant groves. If you abide with longing to reach the flower of His feet, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in Chillai, surrounded by mansions of pure gold and gems, which the king of armies, the lord of the Pallavas, worshipped, bringing fresh gold, pearls and jewels.

216.translit:
arumaa nilam anR aLappaan kuRaLaay avuNan peruveeL viyilsenR irandha,
perumaan thirunaamam pithaRRi nundham piRavith thuyarneengku thumenna kiRpeer!
karumaa kaDaluL kiDandhaan uvandhu kavainaa aravin aNaippaLLi yinmeel,
thirumaal thirumang kaiyoDaaDu thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
216.meaning: As a dwarf, long ago, to measure the rare great earth, He went and begged at the great sacrifice of the demon Mahabali. If you say you will rid yourselves of the sorrow of birth by babbling the sacred names of that Lord, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in Chillai, where Tirumal, gladly reclining upon the couch-bed of the fork-tongued serpent as He lay in the dark great ocean, sports with Lakshmi the sacred lady.

217.translit:
koomangka vangkak kaDalvaiya muyyak kulamanna rangkam mazhuvil thuNiya,
thaamang kamaruL paDaithoTTa venRith thavamaa muniyaith thamakkaakk kiRpeer,
poomangkai thangkip pulamangkai mannip pukazhmangkai yengkum thikazhppukazhseer
seemangkoL paimpoom pozhilsoozhndha thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
217.meaning: That the world girt by the ship-bearing seas might be saved and kings perish, He took up the axe in battle and cut down the bodies of the royal lineage. If you would make your own that victorious great ascetic-sage Parashurama, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in Chillai, full of glory and protection, surrounded by fresh flowering groves, where Lakshmi the flower-lady abides, the goddess of the land endures, and the goddess of fame shines everywhere.

218.translit:
neyvaay azhalam puthurandhu munneer thuNiyap paNikoND aNiyaarndhu, ilangku
maiyaar vaNivaN Nanaiyo eNNi nundham manaththee iruththum paDivaazha valleer,
avvaayiLamang kaiyarppeesa vundhaan arumaa maRaiyandhaNar sindhai puka,
sevvaayk kiLinaan maRaipaaDu thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
218.meaning: He shot ghee-tipped fiery arrows so that the ocean parted and yielded, took service of the sea, and is adorned and radiant, dark like collyrium. If you are able to live keeping that beautiful-hued Lord in mind and seating Him within your hearts, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in Chillai, where, even as the young maidens there chatter, the red-mouthed parrots sing the four Vedas, entering the thoughts of the rare great Veda-knowing brahmins.

219.translit:
mauval kuzhalaaychchi mendhooL nayandhu makaram suzhalas suzhalneer payandha,
theyvath thirumaa malarmangkai thangku thirumaar panaichchindhai yuLvaiththu menbeer,
kauvaik kaLiRRin maruppum poruppil kamazhsandhu mundhi nivaaval angkoL,
theyvap punalsoozhndh azhakaaya thillaith thiruchchithra kooDam senRuseer minkaLee.
219.meaning: He cherished the soft shoulders of the cowherd-maiden Nappinnai of jasmine tresses; on His sacred chest dwells the divine great lotus-lady Lakshmi, born of the swirling waters where the makara fish turn about. If you would keep that sacred-chested Lord within your thoughts, go and reach Tiruchitrakudam in beautiful Chillai, encircled by sacred waters, where the Nivaa river, pushing forward the tusks of clamorous elephants and fragrant sandalwood from the hills, flows surrounding it.

220.translit:
maavaayi nangam matiyaadu keeri mazhaimaa muthukun reduthu,aayar thangaL
kovaay niraimeyth thulaguNda maayan kuraimaa kazhalkoo dumkuRip pudaiyeer,
moovaa yiranaan maRaiyaaLar naaLum muRaiyaal vaNanga aNangaaya sodhi,
devaadhi devan thigazhkinRa thillaith thiruchchithra koodam senRusEr minkaLE.
220.meaning: He who, heedless of its strength, split open the body of the horse-demon through its mouth, who lifted up the great mountain as a shield against the rain, who as cowherd-chief grazed the cattle-herds and swallowed all the worlds — O you who seek to attain the resounding anklets of that great Maayan's feet! Where the three-thousand reciters of the four Vedas worship daily in due order, the radiant Light, the God of gods, shines: go and reach Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

221.translit:
seruneela vERkaN madavaar thiRaththuch chinaththodu ninRu manaththaal vaLarkkum,
aruneela paava magalap pugazhsEr amararkku meythaadha aNdaththi ruppeer,
peruneer nivaavunthi muththang koNarnthu engum viththum vayaluL kayalpaayn thugaLa,
thiruneela ninRu thigazhkinRa thillaith thiruchchithra koodam senRusEr minkaLE.
221.meaning: That the grievous dark sin nurtured in the mind through standing in wrath over women whose eyes are like dark battling spears may depart — O you who would dwell in the heavenly realm of glory unreachable even by the immortals! Where the great waters of the Nivaa river surge, bringing pearls and scattering them everywhere, and carp leap in the fields raising spray, the holy dark-blue Lord stands shining: go and reach Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

222.translit:
seeraar pozhilsoozhn thazhagaaya thillaith thiruchithra koodath thuRaisengaN maalukku,
aaraadha vuLLath thavarkkEt tuvappa alainee rulaguk karuLE puriyum,
kaaraar puyaRkaik kalikanRi kunRaa olimaalai yoRonpa thodonRum vallaar,
paaraa rulaga maLanthaa nadikkeezhp palakaalam niRkum padivaazhvar thaamE.
222.meaning: For the red-eyed Tirumal who abides in beautiful Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai, surrounded by splendid groves — those who, with hearts that never tire, hear and rejoice in this — Kalikanri of the cloud-like generous hand, who graces the wave-girt world, composed this unfailing garland of resounding verses, ten in all. Those able in these shall live long, abiding forever beneath the feet of Him who measured the wide world.

223.translit:
vaada maruthidai pogi mallaraik konRokka littittu,
aadalnal maavudaith thaayar aaniraik kanRidar theerppaan,
koodiya maamazhai kaaththa kooththa nenevaru kinRaan,
sEduyar poompozhil thillaich chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
223.meaning: Crawling between the maruda trees so they fell, slaying the wrestlers and heaping them up, He who is the dancer that warded off the gathered great rain to relieve the distress of the cowherds, their cattle and calves — He comes, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai with its lofty flowering groves.

224.translit:
peymagaL kongainanj suNda piLLai parisidhu venRaal,
maanila maamagaL maadhar kELva nivanenRum, vaNduN
poomagaL naayaga nenRum pulangezhu koviyar paadi,
thEmalar thoova varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
224.meaning: When they say 'such is the nature of the child who sucked the poison from the ogress's breast' — that He is the lord of the great Earth-lady, that He is the consort of the Lotus-lady where bees feast — the wise cowherd-women sing thus, and as they scatter honeyed blossoms He comes, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

225.translit:
paNdivan veNNeyuN daanen Raaychchiyar koodi yizhippa
eNdisai yorumva Nanga iNaimaru thoodu nadanthittu,
aNdarum vaanath thavaru maayira naamanga Lodu,
thiNdiRal paada varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
225.meaning: While the cowherd-women gather and chide saying 'this one stole and ate the butter of old,' as those of the eight directions bow down, He walked between the twin maruda trees; and as the celestials and the dwellers of heaven sing of His firm valour with a thousand names, He comes, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

226.translit:
vaLaikkai nedungaN madavaa raaychchiya ranji yazhaippa,
thaLaiththavizh thaamaraip poygaith thaNthadam pukkaNdar kaaNa,
muLaiththa eyiRRazhal naagath thuchchiyil ninRadhu vaada,
thilaiththamar seydhu varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
226.meaning: While the bangled, long-eyed cowherd-women called out in fear, He entered the cool, broad pond of blossoming lotuses where their fetters loosen; and as the celestials watched, He stood upon the crest of the fiery serpent with sprouted fangs, making it wilt — He fought sportingly and comes, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

227.translit:
paruvak karumugi loththu muttudai maakada loththu,
aruvith thiraLthigazh kinRa vaayiram ponmalai yoththu,
uruvak karunguzha laaychchi thiRaththina maalvidai seRRu,
theruvil thiLaiththu varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
227.meaning: Like the dark cloud of the rainy season, like the surging mighty ocean, like a thousand golden mountains gleaming with cascading torrents of waterfalls — for the sake of the cowherd-girl with beautiful dark tresses He slew the rutting bulls, and revelling He comes through the street, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

228.translit:
eyyach chithainda thilangai malanga varumazhai kaappaan,
uyyap paruvarai thaangi aanirai kaaththaanen REththi,
vaiyath thevarum vaNanga aNangezhu maamalai polE,
theyvappuL LERi varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
228.meaning: As He shot and Lanka was shattered and thrown into confusion; lauded as the one who, to save them, held up the great mountain and guarded the cattle-herds against the oncoming rain — while everyone on earth worships, like a great mountain rising in divine power, He comes riding the sacred bird, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

229.translit:
aava rivaisey thaRivaar anjana maamalai polE,
mEvu sinaththadal vEzham veezha muninthu,azha gaaya
kaavi malarnedung kaNNaar kaidozha veedhi varuvaan,
dEvar vaNangudhaN thillaich chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
229.meaning: Who else has done such deeds and known them? Like a great mountain of dark collyrium, He raged so that the fierce, wrathful elephant fell; and as women with long beautiful eyes like dark water-lily blossoms fold their hands in worship, He comes through the street — He of cool Thillai's Tiru-Chitrakootam, whom the celestials worship.

230.translit:
pongi yamari lorukaal ponpeya ronai veruva,
anga vanaaga maLainthit taayiran thoLezhun thaada,
painga NiraNderi kaanRa neeNda eyiRRodu pEzhvaay,
singa vuruvil varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
230.meaning: Surging in battle, to terrify Hiranya (the golden-named one), He tore into that one's body; and with His thousand shoulders rising and dancing, with two green eyes spewing fire, with long fangs and a gaping mouth, in the form of the lion (Narasimha) He comes, He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

231.translit:
karumugil polvadhor mEni kaiyana vaazhiyum sangum,
peruviRal vaanavar soozha Ezhula gumthozhu thEththa,
orumaga Laayar madanthai yoruththi nilamagaL, maRRaith
thirumaga Lodum varuvaan chiththira koodaththuL LaanE.
231.meaning: With a form like the dark cloud, bearing the discus and the conch in His hands, surrounded by the immortals of great might, while the seven worlds worship and praise — He comes together with one maiden, a cowherd-girl, with the Earth-lady, and with the other, Lakshmi the Lady of Fortune — He of Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai.

232.translit:
thEnamar poompozhil thillaich chiththira kooda mamarndha,
vaanavar thangaL piraanai mangaiyar konmaru vaar,
oonamar vElkali kanRi yoNdami zhonpathO donRum,
thaanivai kaRRuval laarmEl saaraa theevinai thaanE.
232.meaning: Upon the Lord of the celestials who abides in Tiru-Chitrakootam in Thillai with its honey-laden flowering groves, Kalikanri — chief of Mangai, bearer of the flesh-piercing spear — composed these ten fine Tamil verses. Evil deeds shall not draw near those who learn and are able in these.

233.translit:
orukuRaLaa yirunilammoo vadimaN vENdi
 ulaganaiththu meeradiyaa lodukki, onRum
tharukavenaa maavaliyaich chiRaiyil vaiththa
 thaadaaLan thaaLaNaiveer,
thakka keerththi arumaRaiyin thiraLnaankum vELvi yainthum
 angangaL avaiyaaRu misaiga LEzhum,
theruvilmali vizhaavaLamum siRakkum
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
233.meaning: As a single dwarf He begged for three feet of the wide earth, then with two strides compressed all the worlds, and saying 'give one more' He bound Mahabali into bondage — O you who would attain the feet of that masterful Lord! Where fitting fame, the four assembled rare Vedas, the five sacrifices, the six auxiliary sciences, the seven musical notes, and the abundance of festivals thronging the streets all flourish — go and reach Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

234.translit:
naanmuganaaL migaiththarukkai yirukku vaaymai
 nalamigusee rurOmasanaal naviRRi,
nakkan oonmugamaar thalaiyOttooN nozhiththa venthai
 oLimalarchkE vadiyaNaiveer, uzhusE yOdach
soonmugamaar vaLaiyaLaivaa yuguththa muththaith
 tholkurugu sinaiyennach choozhnthi yanga,
thEnmugamaar kamalavayal sElpaaykaazhich
 cheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
234.meaning: Through the deeds of the virtuous, glorious sage Romasa, by the truth of the Rig Veda, He humbled the excessive pride of the four-faced Brahma; my Lord who ended the fleshly skull-begging curse of the naked one (Siva) — O you who would attain His radiant lotus-like blessed feet! Where the ploughing oxen run, where pearls cast up from the mouths of pregnant conches are circled by old herons mistaking them for their young, and where carp leap in the honey-filled lotus fields — go and reach Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

235.translit:
vaiyaNaintha nuthikkOttu varaaga monRaay
 maNNellaa midantheduththu mathangaL seydhu,
neyyaNaintha thigiriyinaal vaaNan thiNdOL
 nErnthavanthaa LaNaikiRpeer, neytha lodu
maiyaNaintha kuvaLaigaLthang kaNka lenRum
 malarkkumudham vaayenRum kadaisi maarkaL,
seyyaNainthu kaLaigaLaiyaa thERum
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
235.meaning: As the single boar with a sharp, pointed tusk He raised up all the earth and performed mighty feats; with His ghee-smeared discus He cut down Banasura's firm shoulders — O you who would attain those very feet that confronted him! Where the women weeders, calling the blue-lily and water-lily their eyes and the blossoming red lotus their mouths, wade into the fields without weeding them — go and reach Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

236.translit:
panjiyalmel ladippinnai thiRaththu
 munnaaL paayvidaika LEzhadarththum ponnanpaimpooN
nenjidanthu kurudhiyuga vugirvE laaNda
 ninmalanthaa LaNaikiRpeer, neela maalaith
thanjudaiya viruLthazhaippath tharaLa maangE
 thaNmadhiyin nilaakkaattap pavaLan thannaal,
senjudara veyilvirikku mazhagaar
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
236.meaning: For the sake of Pinnai of soft cotton-like feet, in days of old He subdued the seven charging bulls; the spotless one who, wielding His nail-spear, tore open the breast of Hiranya of golden ornaments so the blood flowed — O you who would attain His feet! Where, when the dark of night's evening spreads its sheltering gloom, pearls there display the cool moon's moonlight, and corals spread a red-rayed sunlight — go and reach beautiful Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

237.translit:
thevvaaya maRamannar kurudhi koNdu
 thirukkulaththi liRanthOrkkuth thiruththi seydhu,
vevvaaya maakeeNdu vEzha matta
 viNNavarkkOn thaaLaNaiveer, vikirtha maadhar
avvaaya vaaLnedungaN kuvaLai kaatta
 aravindham mugangaatta arugE yaampal,
sevvaayin thiraLkaattum vayalsoozh
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
237.meaning: With the blood of the hostile warrior kings He performed the rites for the departed of His holy lineage (as Parasurama); He who tore open the fierce-mouthed elephant and slew it — the King of the celestials — O you who would attain His feet! Where, as if peerless women show their bright long eyes as blue-lilies, their faces as lotuses, and nearby the white water-lily shows the cluster of their red mouths, the fields surround — go and reach Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

238.translit:
painga NviRal semmugaththu vaali maaLap
 padarvanaththuk kavanthanodum padaiyaarththiNkai,
vengaNviRal viraadhanuga viRku niththa
 viNNavarkkOn thaaLaNaiveer, veRuppOlum
thungamuga maaLigaimE laayang kooRum
 thudiyidaiyaar mugakkamalch sOdhi thannaal,
thingaLmugam panipadaikku mazhagaar
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
238.meaning: So that the green-eyed, valiant, red-faced Vali perished, and bending His firm-handed bow with its arrows against Kabandha and the fierce-eyed mighty Viradha in the vast forest — the King of the celestials — O you who would attain His feet! Where, upon mansions tall as mountains, the radiance of the lotus-faces of the slender-waisted women who play games makes the moon's face acquire dew — go and reach beautiful Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

239.translit:
poruvilvalam puriyarakkan mudigaL paththum
 puRRumaRin thanapOlap puvimEl sintha,
seruvilvalam purisilaikkai malaiththOL
 vEnthan thiruvadisErn thuykiRpeer, thiraineerth
teLki maruvivalam purigaidhaik kazhiyoo daadi
 vayalnaNNi mazhaitharuneer thavazhkaal manni
theruvilvalam puridharaLa meenum
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagarE sErmi neerE.
239.meaning: So that the ten crowns of the matchless, mighty demon (Ravana) scattered upon the earth as if anthills were broken apart, the King with mountain-like shoulders and the victorious bow in His battle-hand — O you who would be saved by attaining His holy feet! Where the clear waters wind and right-coiling screw-pine flowers in the backwaters sway, where the rain-borne waters creeping in reach the fields and abide, and where right-spiraling pearls and fish abound in the streets — go and reach Kaazhi Seeraama Vinnagar.

240.translit:
paTTaravee ragalalkul pavaLac chevvaay
 paNainedunthooL piNainedungkaN paalaaminchol,
maTTavizhungkuzhalikkaa vaanoor kaavil
 marangkoNarnthaa nadiyaNaiveer, aNiLkaLthaava
neTTilaiya karungkamukin chengkaay veezha
 neeLpalavin thaazhchinaiyil nerungku, peenath
theTTapazham chithainthumathuch choriyum
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagaree seermineeree.
240.meaning: O you who wish to reach the feet of Him who, for the sake of a maiden with fragrant flower-decked tresses, brought a tree from the garden of the celestials — she of the broad hood-like loins, the coral-red mouth, the rounded long arms, the deer-like long eyes, the milk-sweet speech: go and reach Kaazhi Sri Rama Vinnagaram, where, as squirrels leap, the red unripe nuts of the tall-leaved dark areca palms fall, and from the drooping branches of the great jackfruit trees the clustered ripe fruits burst and pour forth honey.

241.translit:
piRaithangku sadaiyaanai valatthee vaitthup
 piramanaiththan nunthiyilee thootRu vitthu,
kaRaithangku veelthadangkaN thiruvai maarpil
 kalanthavanthaa LaNaikiRpeer, kazhuneer koodith
thuRaithangku kamalatthuth thuyinRu kaithaith
 thoodaarum pothichootRuch chuNNamnaNNi,
chiRaivaNdu kaLipaadum vayalchoozh
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagaree seermineeree.
241.meaning: O you who wish to reach the feet of Him who placed on His right the matted-haired one bearing the crescent moon, and brought forth Brahma from His own navel, and who united Lakshmi of the wide spear-like (kohl-)stained eyes upon His chest: go and reach Kaazhi Sri Rama Vinnagaram, ringed by fields where, mingling with the red lily, the lotus rests at the ghats, and the winged bees, drawing near the pollen-powder of the sheath-clustered screwpine blossoms, sing in delight.

242.translit:
chengkamalath thayananaiya maRaiyoor
 kaazhichcheeraama viNNagaren chengkaN maalai
angkamalath thadavayalchoo zhaali naadan
 aruLmaari yaraTTamukki adaiyaar seeyam
kongkumalark kuzhaliyarveeL mangkaiveenthan
 kotRaveel parakaalan kaliyan chonna
sangkamukath thamizhmaalai patthum vallaar
 thadangkadalchoo zhulakukkuth thalaivar thaamee.
242.meaning: Of my red-eyed Tirumal at Kaazhi Sri Rama Vinnagaram, the abode of Vedic brahmins resembling Brahma seated on the red lotus, sang Kaliyan — lord of the Aali land ringed by wide fields of fair lotuses, a rain-cloud of grace, the lion to his foes, beloved of the women with honey-fragrant flower tresses, king of Mangai, wielder of the victorious spear, Parakaalan. Those who can master all ten of this Tamil garland of assembled words shall themselves become lords of the world girt by the vast ocean.

243.translit:
vanthuna thadiyeen manampukunthaay pukunthataRpin vaNangkum,en
chinthanaik kiniyaay. thiruvee en naaruyiree,
anthaLi raNiyaa rasooki LiLanthaLirkaL kalanthu, avai yengkum
senthazhal puraiyum thiruvaali yammaanee.
243.meaning: You came and entered the mind of me, Your servant; and after entering, You became sweet to the thought of me who bow to You, O my Treasure, O my dear life-breath! O Lord of beautiful Tirivaali, where the tender shoots of the lovely asoka trees mingle together and everywhere resemble flaming red fire.

244.translit:
neelath thadavarai maamaNi nikazhak kidanthathupool,
aravaNai veelaith thalaikkidanthaa yadiyeen manatthirunthaay,
soolaith thalaikkaNa maamayil nadamaada mazhaimukil poonRezhunthu,
engkum aalaip pukaikamazhum aNiyaali yammaanee.
244.meaning: As though a great gem shone upon a vast blue mountain, You lay upon the serpent-couch upon the surface of the sea, and yet You dwelt in the mind of me, Your servant. O Lord of beautiful Aali, where the flocks of great peacocks dance in the groves, and rising like rain-clouds the smoke of the sugar-mills wafts its fragrance everywhere.

245.translit:
nennalpooy varumenRenR eNNi yiraamaiyen manatthee pukunthathu,
immaik kenRirunthee neRineer vaLamcheRuvil,
chennaR koozhai varampoeeei arivaar mukatthezhu vaaLaipooy,
karumpu annaR kaadaNaiyum aNiyaali yammaanee.
245.meaning: Not thinking “yesterday it went and will come again,” but coming to stay for this very life, You entered into my mind. O Lord of beautiful Aali, where in the water-channel-fed fertile fields, as the reapers clear the ridges of the red paddy, the scabbard-fish that leap up at the reapers' faces go and reach the fine sugarcane grove.

246.translit:
minnil mannu nudangkidai madavaartham chinthai maRanthuvanthu,
ninmannu seevadikkee maRavaamai vaitthaayaal,
punnai mannu serunthi vaNpozhil vaayakan paNaikaL kalanthengkum,
annam mannum vayalaNi aali yammaanee.
246.meaning: Forgetting the thought of the maidens whose lightning-like waists ever sway, I came; and You set me in unforgetting devotion at Your own enduring red feet. O Lord of Aali adorned with fields, where everywhere swans abide amid the wide expanses mingling with the bounteous groves rich in punnai and serunthi trees.

247.translit:
needu panmalar maalaiyiTTu ninniNaiyadi thozhutheetthum,
enmanam vaada neeninaiyeel marameytha maamunivaa,
paada linnoli sangki nooosai paranthu palpaNai yaalmalinthu,
engkum aada loosaiyaRaa aNiyaali yammaanee.
247.meaning: Do not so think that my mind, which adorns You with garlands of many long-strung flowers and worships and praises Your twin feet, should wither, O great sage who once shot the trees! O Lord of beautiful Aali, where the sweet sound of song and the music of conches spread abroad, abounding with many fields, and the sound of dancing never ceases anywhere.

248.translit:
kantha maamala reTTumiTTu ninkaamar seevadi kaithozhuthezhum,
punthiyeen manatthee pukunthaayaip pookaloTTeen,
chanthi veeLvi sadangku naanmaRai oothi yoothuvith thaathi yaayvarum,
antha NaaLa raRaavaNiyaali yammaanee.
248.meaning: Into the mind of me, who rises folding my hands in worship of Your lovely red feet with the offering of the eight kinds of fragrant great flowers, You have entered — and I will not let You go! O Lord of beautiful Aali, where the brahmins never cease — those who perform the twilight rites and sacrifices, chant the four Vedas, teach others to chant, and come down as foremost from of old.

249.translit:
ulavuthiraik kadaRpaLLi koNdu vanthu un adiyeen manampukuntha,
appulava! puNNiyanee! pukunthaayaip pookaloTTeen,
nilavu malarppunnai naazhal neezhal thaNdaamarai malarin misai,
maliyalavan kaNpadukkum aNiyaali yammaanee.
249.meaning: O sage who, resting upon the sea of rolling waves, came and entered the mind of me Your servant — O righteous One! — I will not let You go who have entered! O Lord of beautiful Aali, where in the shade of the moon-bright blossoming punnai and naazhal trees, upon the cool lotus flower, the abundant crabs lie down to sleep.

250.translit:
sangku thangku thadangkadal kadanmallai yuLkidanthaay
 aruLpurinthu ingkennuL pukunthaa yinippooyi naalaRaiyoo,
kongku seNpaka mallikai malarppulki innuLa vaNdu pooy,
ilam thengkin thaathaLaiyum thiruvaali yammaanee.
250.meaning: O You who lie within the vast conch-bearing sea at Kadalmallai, granting Your grace You have now entered into me here — is it right that You should now go away? O Lord of Tirivaali, where the sweet young bees, having embraced the fragrant champak and jasmine blossoms, then go and stir the pollen of the young coconut palms.

251.translit:
oothi yaayira naamamum paNintheetthi ninnadain theeRku,
oru poruL veethiyar! araiyaa uraiyaay orumaatRamenthaay!
neethi yaakiya veethamaa muniyaaLar thootRa muraitthu,
matRavark kaathiyaay irunthaay! aNiyaali yammaanee.
251.meaning: To me who, chanting and bowing in praise of Your thousand names, have taken refuge in You — say one word, O essence of meaning, O Lord of the Vedic ones, O King, O my Father! You who declared the origin of the great sages who are the Vedas that are justice itself, and who remained the primal cause of them all! O Lord of beautiful Aali!

252.translit:
pulli vaNdaRaiyum pozhil pudaichoozh thennaali yoruntha maayanai,
kallin mannu thiNdooL kaliya nolicheytha,
nalla innisai maalai naalumoo rainthumon RumnavinRu, thaamudan
valla raayuraip paarkkida maakum vaanulakee.
252.meaning: Of the wondrous Maayan who abides at southern Aali ringed about by groves where embracing bees hum, Kaliyan of the firm shoulders strong as rock composed this sweet-toned garland of melody. For those who can master and recite all ten — the four and the five and the one — the world of heaven shall be their abode.

253.translit:
thooviriya maluruzhakkith thuNaiyoodum piriyaathee,
pooviriya mathunukarum poRivariya siruvaNdee,
theeviriya maRaivaLarkkum pukazhaaLar thiruvaali,
eevariven chilaiyaanuk kennilaimai yuraiyaayee.
253.meaning: O little bee of speckled and striped wings, who, never parting from your mate, crush the petals of the spreading flowers and sip the honey of the opening blooms: go to the Lord of the fierce arrow-shooting bow at Tirivaali — the place of the renowned ones who nurture the Vedic fire — and tell Him of my plight.

254.translit:
piNiyavizhu naruneela malarkkizhiyap pedaiyoodum,
aNimalarmeel mathunukarum aRukaala siruvaNdee,
maNikezhuneer marungkalarum vayalaali maNavaaLan,
paNiyaRiyeen neechenRen payalainoo yuraiyaayee.
254.meaning: O little six-legged bee who, together with your mate, tear open the fragrant blue blossom whose bud has unfurled and sip the honey upon the lovely flower: I do not know His command — go you and tell the Bridegroom of Vayalaali, where on every side blossom the gem-bright waters of the fields, of my pallor-sickness of love.

255.translit:
neervaanam maNNerikaa laayninRa nedumaal,than
thaaraaya narunthuLavam perunthakaiyeR karuLaanee,
seeraarum vaLarppozhilchoozh thiruvaali vayalvaazhum,
koorvaaya sirukurukee kuRippaRinthu kooraayee.
255.meaning: The great Tirumal who stands as water, sky, earth, fire and wind — the exalted One — will not grant me the fragrant tulasi of His garland. O sharp-beaked little heron that lives in the fields of Tirivaali ringed by splendid flourishing groves: learn His intent and tell it to me.

256.translit:
thaanaaka ninaiyaaneel thanninainthu naiveeRku,oor
meenaaya kodineduveeL valiseyya meliveenoo?
theenvaaya varivaNdee. thiruvaali nakaraaLum,
aanaayaR kennuRunoo yariyacchen Ruraiyaayee.
256.meaning: If He Himself will not think of me, must I, who languish thinking of Him, grow weak when the tall god of love with the fish-banner does his violence? O striped bee of honeyed mouth: go and make known my afflicting sickness to the cowherd who rules the city of Tirivaali.

257.translit:
vaaLaaya kaNpanippa menmulaikaL ponnarumpa
naaNaaLum ninninainthu naiveeRku,oomaNNaLantha
thaaLaaLaa thaNkudanthai nakaraaLaa varaiyedutha
thooLaaLaa, enRanakkoor thuNaiyaaLa naakaayee.
257.meaning: For me who languish thinking of You every day, my sword-like eyes brimming with tears and my soft breasts turning pale-golden — O master of the feet that measured the earth, O ruler of the city of cool Kudanthai, O master of the shoulders that lifted up the mountain: become for me one companion-protector!

258.translit:
thaaraaya thaNduLava vaNduzhutha varaimaarpan,
pooraanaik kompositha puTpaaka nennammaan,
theeraarum neduveethith thiruvaali nakaraaLum,
kaaraayan ennudaiya kanavaLaiyum kavarvaanoo.
258.meaning: He of the mountain-like chest where bees burrow into the cool tulasi of His garland, the rider of Garuda who snapped the tusk of the battle-elephant, my Lord — the dark cowherd who rules the city of Tirivaali with its long streets full of chariots — will He carry off even my heavy bangles?

259.translit:
koNdaravath thiraiyulavu kuraikadalmeel kulavaraipool,
paNdaravi naNaikkidanthu paaraLantha paNpaaLaa!
vaNdamarum vaLarppozhilchoozh vayalaali mainthaa! en
kaNduyilnee koNdaayken kanavaLaiyum kadaveenoo!
259.meaning: O gracious One who of old lay upon the serpent-couch like a great mountain upon the roaring sea where the tumultuous waves roll, and who measured the earth! O youth of Vayalaali ringed by flourishing groves where bees dwell! You have taken away my sleep — must I now lose even my heavy bangles too?

260.translit:
kuyilaalum vaLarppozhilsoozh thaNkudandhaik kudamaadi!
thuyilaadha kaNNiNaiyEn ninninaindhu thuyarvEnO!
muyalaalu miLamadhikkE vaLaiyizhandhERku, idhunaduvE
vayalaali maNavaaLaa. koLvaayO maNiniRamE.
260.meaning: O Lord who danced upon the pot, dwelling in cool Kudandhai surrounded by lush groves where koels sing! Must I, whose paired eyes know no sleep, waste away in grief thinking of You? For me who has lost my bangles over the young moon with its hare-marking, in this midst, O Bridegroom of Vayalaali, will You seize away my jewel-like complexion?

261.translit:
nilaiyaaLaa ninvaNanga vENdaayE yaakinum,en
mulaiyaaLa voruNaaLun nakalaththaal aaLaayE,
silaiyaaLaa marameydha thiRalaaLaa thirumeyya
malaiyaaLaa, neeyaaLa vaLaiyaaLa maattOmE.
261.meaning: O eternal Lord, even if You will not have me bow before You, yet for one day rule my breasts by clasping them to Your chest. O wielder of the bow, O mighty one who pierced the trees, O Lord of the hill of Thirumeyyam—if You will not take me as Yours, I cannot keep my bangles.

262.translit:
maiyilangu karunguvaLai marungalarum vayalaali,
neyyilangu sudaraazhip padaiyaanai nedumaalai,
kaiyilangu vElkaliyan kaNduraiththa thamizhmaalai,
aiyiraNdu mivaivallaarkk aruvinaika LadaiyaavE.
262.meaning: On the long-sighted Tirumal, who dwells in Vayalaali where dark blue kuvalai blossoms bloom on every side, the Lord bearing the radiant flame-edged discus shining with oil—Kaliyan with the gleaming spear in his hand beheld Him and uttered this Tamil garland. For those able to master these ten verses, cruel karmas shall not draw near.

263.translit:
kaLvankol yaanaRiyEn kariyaanoru kaaLaivandhu,
vaLLimarung kulendran madamaaninaip pOdhavendru,
veLLivaLaik kaippaRRap peRRadhaayarai vittakandru,
aLLalam poongazhani yaNiyaali puguvarkkolO.
263.meaning: Is He a thief? I do not know—a dark-hued youth came, called my deer-like, vine-waisted tender daughter to come away, and grasped her silver-bangled hand. Leaving behind the mother who bore her, will she enter beautiful Aali with its lovely flowering fields of soft mire?

264.translit:
paNdiva naayannangaay. padiRanpugundhu, enmagaLthan
thoNdaiyanj sengaNivaay nugarndhaanai yugandhu,avanpin
keNdaiyoN kaNmiLirak kiLipOlmizhar RinadandhE,
vaNdamar kaanalmalkum vayalaali puguvarkkolO.
264.meaning: O lady, of old this fellow was a cowherd. The deceiver entered and tasted the red kovai-fruit lips of my daughter, and she, delighting in him, walking behind him with her bright carp-like eyes flashing, prattling like a parrot—will she enter Vayalaali abounding in seaside groves where bees swarm?

265.translit:
anjuvan venjolnangaay arakkarkkulap paavaidhannai,
venjina mookkarindha viRalOndhirang kEtkil,meyyE
panjiyal melladiyem paNaiththOLi parakkazhindhu,
vanjiyan thaNpaNaisoozh vayalaali puguvarkkolO.
265.meaning: O lady, I dread her harsh words. If she hears of the deeds of the valiant one who, in fierce wrath, cut off the nose of the demon clan's maiden—truly my daughter with cotton-soft feet and bamboo-like shoulders, having lost all restraint, will she enter Vayalaali girt by cool fields lovely as the vanji creeper?

266.translit:
Edhuavan tholpiRappu iLaiya vanvaLai yoodhi,
mannar thoodhuva naayavanoor soluveergaL! soleeraRiyEn,
maadhavan thandhuNaiyaa nadandhaaLthadanj soozhpuRavil,
pOdhuvaN daadusemmal punalaali puguvarkkolO.
266.meaning: What is His ancient birth? The youthful one who blew the conch and became envoy of the kings—O you who can tell of His town, tell me, for I do not know! Taking Madhava as her companion, she has walked away; in the groves surrounded by broad ponds, will the proud maiden enter Punalaali where bees sport upon the blossoms?

267.translit:
thaayenai yendrirangaaL thadandhOLi thanakkamaindha,
maayanai maadhavanai madhiththennai yagandraivaL,
vEyana thOLvisiRip pedaiyanna menanadandhu,
pOyina poongodiyaaL punalaali puguvarkkolO.
267.meaning: She does not relent thinking 'this is my mother'—my broad-shouldered daughter, esteeming the wondrous Mayan, the Madhava who is meant for her, has left me. Swaying her bamboo-like arms, walking like a soft female swan, the flower-creeper-like maiden has gone—will she enter Punalaali?

268.translit:
endhuNai yendreduththER kiRaiyEnu mirangiRRilaL,
thandhuNai yaayavendran thanimaikku mirangiRRilaL,
vandhuNai vaanavarkkaay varanjeRRarang kaththuRaiyum,
indhuNai vannodumpO yezhilaali puguvarkkolO.
268.meaning: To me who cried 'O my support!' she showed not the least pity; nor did she pity my loneliness, she who had been my own companion. Going off with that companion-Lord who, becoming the strong refuge of the celestials, destroyed the boon-blessed foe and dwells at Arangam—will she enter beautiful Aali?

269.translit:
annaiyu maththanumen RadiyOmuk kirangiRRilaL,
pinnaidhan kaadhalandran perundhOLnalam pENinaLaal,
minnaiyum vanjiyaiyum vendrilangu midaiyaaLnadandhu,
punnaiyum annamumsoozh punalaali puguvarkkolO.
269.meaning: She showed no pity to us, her servants—mother and father. She has set her heart on the goodness of the broad shoulders of Nappinnai's beloved. With a waist that shines surpassing the lightning and the vanji creeper, she has walked off—will she enter Punalaali surrounded by punnai trees and swans?

270.translit:
muRRilum paingiLiyum pandhumoosalum pEsugindra,
siRRilmen poovaiyumvit takandrasezhung kOdhaidhannai,
peRRilEn muRRizhaiyaip piRappilipin nEnadandhu,
maRRellaam kaidhozhappOy vayalaali puguvarkkolO.
270.meaning: My lush-garlanded daughter has left behind her little plaything, her green parrot, her ball, her swing, her little play-house and the soft myna bird that she used to talk with. I have lost my fully-adorned girl; walking behind the birthless One, while all others worship with folded hands, will she enter Vayalaali?

271.translit:
kaaviyang kaNNiyeNNil kadimaamalarp paavaiyoppaaL,
paaviyEn peRRamaiyaal paNaiththOLi parakkazhindhu,
thoovisE rannamanna nadaiyaaLnedu maalodumpOy,
vaaviyan thaNpaNaisoozh vayalaali puguvarkkolO.
271.meaning: My daughter with eyes like the blue lotus, if reckoned truly, resembles the goddess seated on the great fragrant flower. Because I, a sinner, bore her, my bamboo-shouldered girl, having lost all restraint, with a gait like the feather-graced swan, has gone off with Tirumal—will she enter Vayalaali girt by cool fields and ponds?

272.translit:
thaaymanam nindrirangath thaniyEnedu maalthuNaiyaa,
pOyina poongodiyaaL punalaali puguvarendru,
kaaysina vElkaliya noliseydhamizh maalaipaththum,
mEviya nenjudaiyaar thanjamaavadhu viNNulagE.
272.meaning: While her mother's heart stood pining, the flower-creeper-like maiden went off all alone with Tirumal as her companion, saying she would enter Punalaali. For those whose hearts cleave to all ten of these melodious Tamil verses composed by Kaliyan of the wrath-blazing spear, the world of heaven shall be their refuge.

273.translit:
nandhaa viLakkE aLaththaR kariyaay naranaa raNanE! karumaa mugilpOl endhaay,
emakkE yaruLaay, enanindru imaiyOr paravu midam,eth thisaiyum
kandhaa ramandhE nisaipaadamaadE kaLivaN dumizhaR Ranizhal thudhaindhu,
mandhaa ranindru maNamal kunaangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
273.meaning: O unfading lamp, O One immeasurable, O Nara-Narayana! O my Father like a great dark cloud—the place where the celestials stand and praise, saying 'Grace us alone!'—in every direction honey-music sounds in the kandharam mode, while beside it joyous bees hum amid dense shade, and mandara flowers stand spreading fragrance. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple of Naangoor.

274.translit:
mudhalaith thanimaa muraNtheera vandru mudhuneerth thadaththuch sengaNvEzha muyya,
vidhalaith thalaichchendr adhaRkE yudhavi vinaidheerththa vammaanidam viNNaNavum
padhalaik kapOdhath thoLimaada neRRip pavaLak kozhungaal paingaal puRavam,
madhalaith thalaimen pedaikoodu naangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
274.meaning: The place of the Lord who, of old, to break the unique might of the crocodile and to save the red-eyed elephant in the deep-watered pool, hastened in haste to its aid and put an end to its affliction—the Naangoor where, upon the brows of the radiant mansions with their lofty cornices, doves with coral-rich legs and green feet join their soft mates upon their fledglings. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple there.

275.translit:
kolaippuN thalaikkundra mondruyya vandru kodumaa mudhalaik kidarseydhu,
kongaar ilaippuNda reekath thavaLinba manpOdaNaindhitta vammaanidam,
aaLariyaal alaippuNda yaanai maruppu magilum aNimuththum veNsaa maraiyOdu,
ponni malaippaNda maNdath thiraiyundhu naangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
275.meaning: The place of the Lord who, of old, to save the elephant struck with mortal wounds, brought distress upon the cruel great crocodile, and lovingly drew near in joy the goddess of the fragrant-petalled lotus—the Naangoor where the Ponni river's waves wash ashore in abundance the tusks of elephants attacked by lions, eaglewood, fine pearls, and white chowries from the hills. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple there.

276.translit:
siRaiyaar uvaNappuL Lond[n]REri yandru thisainaangum naangu miriya,
seruvil kaRaiyaar neduvE larakkar madiyak kadalsoo zhilangai kadandhaa nidandhaan,
muRaiyaal vaLarkkindra muththeeyar naalvEdhar aivELvi yaarangar Ezhi nisaiyOr,
maRaiyOr vaNangap pugazheydhu naangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
276.meaning: The place of Him who, mounting the winged Garuda of full pinions, of old made the four directions and the four quarters scatter, and crossing the sea-girt Lanka destroyed in battle the demons with their long blood-stained spears—the Naangoor that gains renown as the masters of the three sacred fires, the four Vedas, the five sacrifices, the six Vedic limbs, the seven musical notes, and the Vedic seers bow before it. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple there.

277.translit:
izhaiyaadu kongaith thalainanja muNdittu iLangandru koNdu viLangaa yeRindhu,
thazhaivaada vandhaaL kurundha mosiththuth thadandhaama raippoygai pukkaani dandhaan,
kuzhaiyaada valli[k] kulamaada maadE kuyilkoova needu kodimaada malgu,
mazhaiyaadu sOlai mayilaalu naangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
277.meaning: The place of Him who drank the poison from the breasts adorned with jewels, who took the young calf and hurled it to knock down the wood-apple fruits, who snapped the kurundha tree, and entered the broad lotus-pond—the Naangoor where, beside mansions over which creepers and tendrils sway, koels call, tall flag-bannered mansions abound, and peacocks dance in groves where the rain-clouds play. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple there.

278.translit:
paNNEr mozhiyaay siyaranja vanjap pakuvaayk kazhudhuk kirangaadhu,
avaLthan uNNaa mulaimaR RavaLaavi yOdum udanE suvaiththaa nidam,
Ongu paindhaaL kaNNaar karumbin kazhaidhindru vaigik kazhuneeril moozhgich sezhuneerth thadaththu,
maNNEndh iLamEdhi kaLvaigu naangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
278.meaning: The place of Him who, showing no mercy to the deceitful gaping-mouthed ghoul Pootana so that the cowherd-women trembled, sucked away together with her life the very milk of her never-to-be-suckling breast—the Naangoor where young buffaloes, having eaten the canes of tall green-stalked broad sugarcane, linger, bathe in the red-lily ponds, plunge into the rich waters, and rest bearing mud. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple there.

279.translit:
thaLaikkat tavizhdhaa maraivaigu poygaith thadambukku adangaa vidangaa laravam,
iLaikkath thiLaiththit tadhanuchchi thanmEl adivaiththa ammaa nidam,
maamadhiyam thiLaikkum kodimaaLigaisoozh theruvil sezhumuththu veNNeR kenachchendru,
moondril vaLaikkai nuLaippaavai yarmaaRu naangoor maNimaadak kOyil vaNangen mananE.
279.meaning: The place of the Lord who entered the broad pond where lotuses bloom unfettered, and wearying the unsubduable venom-spewing serpent, set His feet upon its crest—the Naangoor where, in the streets girt by flag-bannered mansions tall enough that the great moon sports among them, fisher-maidens with bangled hands go offering bright pearls in exchange for white paddy at three-to-one. O my heart, worship the Manimaadak Koyil temple there.

300.translit:
kanRadhanaal viLaverindhu kaniyudhirththa kaaLai
 kaamarusiir mugilvaNNan kaaligaLmun kaappaan,
kunRadhanaal mazhaidhaduththuk kudamaadu kooththan
 kulavumidam kodimadhiLgaL maaLigaikoo purangaL,
dhunRumaNi maNdapangaL saalaigaLthoo maRaiyoor
 thokkiiNdith thozhudhiyodu migappayilum soolai,
anRalarvaay madhuvuNdang kaLimuralu naangoor
 arimeya viNNagaram vaNangumada nenjee.
300.meaning: He, the youthful bull who flung a calf to knock down wood-apples and made the ripe fruit fall, the cloud-hued one of lovely glory who once protected the cattle, the dancer who held up a mountain to ward off the rain and danced the pot-dance — the place he delights to dwell is rich with banner-topped ramparts, mansions, towers, jewel-studded halls, and avenues where the pure brahmins, learned in the Vedas, gather in throngs to worship. There in the groves of Naangoor, where bees that have drunk the honey of newly-opened blossoms hum, lies Arimeya Vinnagaram — bow to it, O my tender heart.

301.translit:
vanjanaiyaal vandhavaLtha nuyiruNdu vaayththa
 thayiruNdu veNNeyamu dhuNdu, valimikka
kanjanuyi radhuvuNdiv vulaguNda kaaLai
 karudhumidam kaavirisan dhagilkanaga mundhi,
manjulavu pozhiloodum vayaloodum vandhu
 vaLangoduppa maamaRaiyoor maamalargaL thoovi,
anjaliththang karisaraNen RiRainjumaNi naangoor
 arimeya viNNagaram vaNangumada nenjee.
301.meaning: He took the life of her who came by deceit, ate the curd that was set out, ate the butter and the milk-sweets, took the life of the mighty Kamsa, and swallowed this whole world — the youthful bull. The place he chooses to dwell is where the Kaveri carries sandalwood, eaglewood and gold, flowing through cloud-wreathed groves and fields to bestow abundance, where the great brahmins scatter great blossoms, fold their hands and bow saying 'Hari is our refuge.' That jewel-like Arimeya Vinnagaram of Naangoor — bow to it, O my tender heart.

302.translit:
senRusina vidaiyezhum padavadarththup pinnai
 sevviththoaL puNarndhugandha thirumaalthan koayil,
anRayanu maranseyu manaiyavargaL naangoor
 arimeya viNNagara mamarndhasezhung kunRai,
kanRinedu velvalavan mangaiyartham koamaan
 kaliganRi yolimaalai yaindhinodu moonRum,
onRinodu monRumivai kaRRuvallaar ulagath
 thuththamarkat kuththamaraa yumparumaa vargaLe.
302.meaning: This is the temple of Tirumal, who once charged at and subdued the seven fierce bulls and, embracing the fair shoulders of Nappinnai, rejoiced — the fertile hill who abides at Arimeya Vinnagaram of Naangoor, the dwelling of Ayan (Brahma), Aran (Siva), his son, and the like. Those who can master these resounding garland-verses — five and three and one and one more — of Kalikanri, the lord of the Mangai people, skilled with the wrathful long spear, will become the best among the best of this world and will dwell with the immortals above.

303.translit:
poadhalarndha pozhilsoalaip puRamengum porudhiraigaL
thaadhudhira vandhalaikkum thadamaNNith thenkaraimeel
maadhavanRaa nuRaiyumidam vayalnaangai varivaNdu
theedhenaven Risaipaadumthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
303.meaning: On the southern bank of the broad Mannai river, whose battling waves come dashing all around the flower-filled groves and orchards so that pollen scatters — there is the place where Madhava himself dwells, the field-rich Naangai, where the striped bees sing their melody 'thee-thena': Tiruttevanaartogai.

304.translit:
yaavarumaa yaavaiyumaa yezhilveedhap poruLgaLumaay
moovarumaay mudhalaaya moorththiyamarn dhuRaiyumidam,
maavarumthiN padaimannai venRikoLvaar mannunaangai
theevarumsen RiRainjupozhilthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
304.meaning: He who is all beings and all things, who is the meaning of the beautiful Vedas, who is the three (Brahma, Vishnu, Siva) and yet the primal one — the place where that Lord abides is the enduring Naangai of those who win victory with strong armies and horses, where even the gods come and worship amid the groves: Tiruttevanaartogai.

305.translit:
vaanaadum maNNaadum maRRuLLa palluyirum
thaanaaya vempemaan thalaivanamarn dhuRaiyumidam,
aanaadha perunjelvath tharumaRaiyoor naangaidhannuL
theenaaru malarpozhilsoozhthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
305.meaning: He, my Lord, who is himself the world of heaven, the world of earth, and all the many living beings — the place where that chief abides is, within Naangai of the rare Vedic brahmins of unfailing great wealth, encircled by honey-laden flowering groves: Tiruttevanaartogai.

306.translit:
indhiranu mimaiyavarum munivargaLum ezhilamaindha
sandhamalarch sadhumuganum kadhiravanum sandhiranum,
endhaiyemak karuL, enanin RaruLumidam ezhilnaangai
sundharanal pozhilpudaisoozhthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
306.meaning: The place where he graciously stands and grants his grace as Indra, the immortals, the sages, the four-faced Brahma seated on the beautiful fragrant lotus, the sun and the moon all pray, 'Our Father, grace us!' — is the lovely Naangai, encircled all around by fair, fine groves: Tiruttevanaartogai.

307.translit:
aNdamumiv valaikadalu mavanigaLum kulavaraiyum
uNdapiraa nuRaiyumidam oaLimaNisan dhagilkanagam,
theNdiraigaL varaththirattum thigazhmaNNith thenkaraimeel,
thiNthiRalaar payilnaangaiththiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
307.meaning: The place where the Lord who swallowed the cosmic sphere, this surging sea, the worlds and the great mountains dwells is on the southern bank of the shining Mannai, whose clear waves bring and heap up bright gems, sandalwood, eaglewood and gold — the Naangai thronged by men of firm valour: Tiruttevanaartogai.

308.translit:
njaalamellaa mamudhuseydhu naanmaRaiyum thodaraadha
paalaganaa yaalilaiyil paLLikoLLum paramanidam,
saalivaLam perugivarum thadamaNNith thenkaraimeel
seelugaLum vayalnaangaiththiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
308.meaning: He who consumed all the world as nectar and, as an infant beyond the reach even of the four Vedas, lies in sleep upon a banyan leaf — the abode of that Supreme One is on the southern bank of the broad Mannai where the wealth of paddy grows abundant, the Naangai whose fields leap with sael-fish: Tiruttevanaartogai.

309.translit:
oadaadha vaaLariyi nuruvaagi yiraNiyanai
vaadaadha vaLLugiraal piLandhaLaindha maaladhidam,
eedeaRu perunjelvath thezhilmaRaiyoor naangaidhannuL,
seedeaRu pozhilthazhuvuthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
309.meaning: He took the form of the unretreating fierce man-lion and tore open Hiranya with his sharp, never-withering claws and grappled him — the place of that Tirumal is within Naangai of the beautiful Vedic brahmins of flourishing great wealth, embraced by lofty groves: Tiruttevanaartogai.

310.translit:
vaaraaru miLangongai maidhiliyai maNampuNarvaan,
kaaraarthiN silaiyiRuththa thanikkaaLai karudhumidam
eraarum perunjelvath thezhilmaRaiyoor naangaidhannuL,
seeraarum malarpozhilsoozhthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
310.meaning: To wed Mythili (Sita) of the young, bodice-bound breasts, the matchless bull broke the dark, strong bow — the place he chooses is within Naangai of the beautiful Vedic brahmins of splendid great wealth, encircled by lovely flowering groves: Tiruttevanaartogai.

311.translit:
kumbamigu madhayaanai paaganodum kulaindhuviizha
kombadhanaip paRiththeRindha kooththanamarn dhuRaiyumidam,
vambavizhum seNpagaththin maNangamazhum naangaidhannuL,
semponmadhiL pozhilpudaisoozhthiruththeavanaarthogaiye.
311.meaning: The dancer who, making the rutting elephant with its great frontal-lobe collapse and fall together with its mahout, plucked out its tusk and flung it — the place where he abides is within Naangai fragrant with the scent of the budding shenbaga blossom, encircled by groves and red-gold ramparts: Tiruttevanaartogai.

312.translit:
kaaraarndha thirumeenik kaNNanamarn dhuRaiyumidam,
seeraarndha pozhilnaangaiththiruththeavanaarthogaimeel
kooraarndha veaRkaliyan kooRudhamizh paththumvallaar
eraarndha vaigundhath thimaiyavaroa diruppaare.
312.meaning: Kannan of the dark, beautiful sacred form abides and dwells here. Those who can master the ten Tamil verses spoken by Kaliyan of the keen-edged spear upon Tiruttevanaartogai of glorious, grove-rich Naangai will dwell with the immortals in splendid Vaikuntha.

313.translit:
kamba maagada ladaiththilang kaikkuman kadhirmudi yavaipaththum
ambi naalaRuththu, arasavan thambikku aLiththava nuRaikoayil
sempa laanirai seNpagam maadhavi soodhagam vaazhaigaLsoozh
vambu laamkamu koangiya naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
313.meaning: He bridged the trembling great ocean, severed with his arrows the ten radiant crowns of Lanka's king, and granted the kingdom to that king's brother — the temple where he dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, surrounded by rows of red lilies, shenbaga, maadhavi creepers, mango trees and plantains, where fragrance-spreading areca palms rise tall.

314.translit:
palla vandhigazh poongadam beaRiyak kaaLiyan paNavarangil,
ollai vandhuRap paayndharu nadanjeydha umbarkkoa nuRaikoayil,
nalla vendhazhal moonRunaal veedhamai veaLviyoa daarangam,
valla vandhaNar malgiya naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
314.meaning: Climbing upon the flowering kadamba tree bright with shoots, he came swiftly down and leapt onto the hood-stage of Kaliya and danced his rare dance — the temple where that lord of the heavens dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, thronged with brahmins skilled in the three sacred fires, the four Vedas, the five sacrifices and the six limbs (of the Vedas).

315.translit:
aNda raanavar vaanavar koanukken RamaiththasoaR radhuvellaam
uNdu koanirai meyththavai kaaththavan ugandhini dhuRaikoayil,
konda laarmuzha vilkuLir vaarpozhil kulamayil nadamaada,
vaNdu thaanisai paadidu naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
315.meaning: He ate up all the food that the cowherds had prepared for Indra, king of the celestials, then grazed the herds of cattle and protected them — the temple where he gladly and sweetly dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, where, as clouds drum like festive drums in the cool spreading groves, flocks of fine peacocks dance and the bees themselves sing their melody.

316.translit:
parungai yaanaiyin kombinaip paRiththadhan paaganaich saadippukku,
oRunga mallaraik konRupin kanjanai udhaiththava nuRaikoayil,
karumbinooduyar saaligaL viLaidharu kazhaniyil malivaavi
marunge laampozhi loangiya naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
316.meaning: He plucked the tusk of the thick-trunked elephant, struck down its mahout, rushed in and slew the wrestlers, and then kicked Kamsa to death — the temple where he dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, with fields where paddy ripens tall among the sugarcane, ponds brimming on every side, and groves rising high all around.

317.translit:
saadu poayvizhath thaaLnimirth theesanthan padaiyodung kiLaiyoadum
oada vaaNanai yaayiran thoaLgaLum thuNiththava nuRaikoayil,
aadu vaankodi yagalvisum baNavippoayp pagalava noLimaRaikkum
maada maaLigai soozhdharu naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
317.meaning: He stretched out his foot so that the cart (Sakatasura) went tumbling down, and, as Siva fled with his army and kin, he severed the thousand arms of Banasura — the temple where he dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, encircled by storeyed mansions whose waving banners reach into the wide sky and hide the brightness of the sun.

318.translit:
angai yaaladi moonRunii reaRRayan alarkodu thozhudheaththa,
kangai poadharak kaalnimirth tharuLiya kaNNanvan dhuRaikoayil,
kongai koangavai kaattavaay kumudhangaL kaattamaa padhumangaL,
mangai maarmugam kaattidu naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
318.meaning: With his cupped palm he received the water (of the gift), measured the three worlds with his strides while Brahma worshipped and praised him with blossoms, and graciously stretched out his foot so that the Ganga came forth — the temple where Kannan comes and dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, where kongu buds show forth like women's breasts, water-lilies show their mouths, and great lotuses show the faces of the maidens.

319.translit:
uLaiya voNdiRal ponpeya roanthana dhurampiLan dhudhiraththai
aLaiyum, venjinath tharipari kiiRiya appanvan dhuRaikoayil,
iLaiya mangaiya riNaiyadich silambinoa dezhilkoLpan dhadippoarkai
vaLaiyil ninRoli malgiya naangoor vaNburu doaththamame.
319.meaning: He, the lion of fierce wrath, tore open the chest of the mighty one named Hiranya ('gold') so that he writhed, and smeared himself with his blood — the temple where that Father comes and dwells is Naangoor's bountiful Purushottamam, filled with the ringing sound of the paired anklets on the feet of young maidens and the bangles on the hands of those who play with lovely balls.

320.translit:
vaaLai yaar thadang kaNNumai panganvan saabamat tadhuneenga
mooLai yaar sirath thaiya mun aLiththa em mugil vaNNan uraikoyil
paaLai vaan kamu kooduyar thenginvan pazhamvizha veruvippoy
vaaLai paaydhadam soozhtharu naangoor vaNpuru doththamamE.
320.meaning: To free Shiva, who bears Uma of wide carp-like eyes on one half, from his fierce curse, our cloud-hued Lord once offered the skull-bearing head he carried. The temple where He dwells is Nangur Vanpurudottamam, surrounded by ponds where carp leap in fright as the hard fruits drop from tall coconut palms with their towering spathes.

321.translit:
indhu vaar sadai eesanaip payandha naanmuganaith than ezhilaarum
undhi maamalar meemisaip padaiththavan ugandhinidh uraikoyil,
kundhi vaazhaiyin kozhungkani karndhu than kuruLaiyaith thazhuvippoy,
mandhi maampaNai mel vaigu naangoor vaNpuru doththamamE.
321.meaning: He created Brahma of four faces upon the great beautiful lotus rising from His own splendid navel — the Brahma who in turn brought forth Shiva with the moon in his long matted locks. The temple where He gladly dwells is Nangur Vanpurudottamam, where female monkeys crouch to bite the rich ripe plantain fruit, then embrace their young and go to rest upon the great mango branches.

322.translit:
maNNuL aar pugazh vEdhiyar naangoor vaNpuru doththaththuL,
aNNal sEvadik keezh adaindhu uyndhavan aaliman aruLmaari,
paNNuL aar tharap paadiya paadalip paththum vallaar, ulagil
eNNilaadha pErinbamut imaiyavar Odum kooduvarE.
322.meaning: Tirumangai, the lord of Ali and a rain of grace, who took refuge beneath the holy red feet of the Lord at Nangur Vanpurudottamam — the town of Vedic Brahmins praised throughout the earth — was saved thereby. Those who can master all ten of these melodious verses he sang will, in this world, attain countless great bliss and join the company of the deathless celestials.

323.translit:
pEr aNindhu ulagaththavar thozhudhu Eththum pEraruLaaLan em piraanai,
vaar aNi mulaiyaaL malarmagaLodu maNmagaLum udan nitpa,
seer aNi maada naangainan naduvuL sembon sey koyilin uLLE,
kaar aNi mEgam nindradhop paanaik kaNdukoNdu uyndhozhindhEnE.
323.meaning: The Lord of supreme grace, our Master, whom the people of the world adore and praise by reciting His names — with the lotus-born Lakshmi of breasts bound in a bodice and the Earth-goddess standing beside Him — within the temple wrought of pure gold in the fair midst of Nangur of splendid mansions, I beheld Him standing like a dark rain-laden cloud, and was saved.

324.translit:
pirappodu mooppu ondru illavan thannaip pEdhiyaa inba veLLaththai,
irapp edhir kaalak kazhivumaa naanai Ezhisai in suvai thannai,
sirappudai maraiyor naangainan naduvuL sembon sey koyilin uLLE,
maraip perum poruLai vaanavar konaik kaNdunaan vaazhndhozhindhEnE.
324.meaning: Him who has neither birth nor aging, the unfailing flood of bliss, who is past, future and present time itself, the very sweetness of the seven-noted music — within the temple wrought of pure gold in the fair midst of Nangur of glorious Vedic Brahmins, the great inner meaning of the scriptures, the King of the celestials — I beheld Him and lived, redeemed.

325.translit:
thida visumb eri neer thingaLum sudarum sezhunilath thuyirgaLum matrum,
padar poruLgaLumaay nindravan thannai, pangayath thayan avan anaiya, thidamozhi
maraiyor naangainan naduvuL sembon sey koyilin uLLE,
kadal nira vaNNan thannai naan adiyEn kaNdukoNdu uyndhozhindhEnE.
325.meaning: Him who stands as the firm sky, fire, water, the moon and the sun, the living beings of this rich earth, and all the wide-spreading things besides — within the temple wrought of pure gold in the fair midst of Nangur of the lotus-born Brahma-like Brahmins of firm, scriptural speech, the Lord hued like the ocean — I, His servant, beheld Him and was saved.

326.translit:
vasai aru kuRaLaay maavali vELvi maN aLavitt avan thannai,
