  dhEvanaith thihazhum pavaLath thoLi
oppanai, ulahu Ezhinai Uzhiyai
  Azhi Endhiya kaiyanai andhaNar
kaRpinai, kazhu neer malarum vayal
  kaNNa mangaiyuL kaNDu koNDEnE.
698.meaning: The strong one, the resplendent one who raged and slew the horse (Keshi), the casket filled with bright heavenly ornament, the bridegroom of Tirumangai (Lakshmi), the God, the one resembling the gleaming coral's luster, the seven worlds, the eons, the one bearing the discus in His hand, the learning of the brahmins — Him I went and found and beheld within Kannamangai, whose fields blossom with red water-lilies.

699.translit:
thiruththanaith thisai nAnmuhan thandhaiyaith
  dhEva dhEvanai mUvaril munniya
viruththanai, viLangum sudarch chOdhiyai
  viNNai maNNinaik kaNNudhal kUDiya
aruththanai, ariyaip parik keeRiya
  appanai appil Ar azhal Ay ninRa
karuththanai, kaLi vaNDu aRaiyum pozhil
  kaNNa mangaiyuL kaNDu koNDEnE.
699.meaning: The pure one, the father of four-faced Brahma of the directions, the God of gods, the ancient one foremost among the three, the shining radiant flame, the sky and the earth, the one joined with the forehead-eyed Shiva as a single meaning, Hari, the Father who tore apart the horse, the will who stands as the unquenchable fire in the waters — Him I beheld within Kannamangai, whose groves resound with the humming of joyous bees.

700.translit:
venchi nakkaLitT raiviLang kaayvizhak
kanRu veesiya eesanai, peymakaL
tunycha nanychusu vaiththuNda thoonRalaith
thoonRal vaaLarak kankedath thoonRiya
nanychi nai, amu thaththinai naathanai
nachchu vaaruchchi meelniRkum nambiyai,
kanycha naiththunycha vanychiththa vanychanaik
kaNNa mangaiyuL kaNdukoN deenee.
700.meaning: Him who, with the wood-apple as His tool, hurled the calf to slay the fierce raging elephant (demons); the Lord who as a child sucked and swallowed the poison so that the demoness Putana died; the poison that arose to destroy the renowned mighty demon (Ravana) yet who is sweet ambrosia to His devotees; the Master who stands upon the heads of those who long for Him; the deceiver who through guile destroyed Kamsa — Him I beheld and grasped within Kannamangai.

701.translit:
paNNi naippaNNil ninRathoor paanmaiyaip
paaluL neyyinai maaluru vaayninRa
viNNi nai, viLang kumsudarch choothiyai
veeLvi yaiviLak kinoLi thannai,
maNNi naimalai yaiyalai neerinai
maalai maamathi yaimaRai yoorthangaL
kaNNi nai, kaNka LaaraLa vumninRu
kaNNa mangaiyuL kaNdukoN deenee.
701.meaning: Him who is the melody, and the nature abiding within that melody; the ghee within the milk; the heaven that stands as the great form (of Tirumal); the radiant blazing effulgence; the sacrifice and the light of the lamp's glow; the earth, the mountain, the surging waters, and the great cool moon; the very eye of those who hold the Vedas — Him I stood and beheld to my eyes' full content, and grasped within Kannamangai.

702.translit:
kaNNa mangaiyuL kaNdukoN deenenRu
kaatha laalkali kanRiyu raiseytha,
vaNNa voNdami zhonpathoo donRivai
valla raayuraip paarmathi yamthavazh
viNNil viNNava raaymakizh veythuvar
meymmai sollilveN sangamon Reenthiya
kaNNa, ninRanak kumkuRip paakil
kaRka laamkavi yinporuL thaanee.
702.meaning: These nine and one (ten) beautiful bright Tamil verses, which Kalikanri (Tirumangai Alwar) composed out of love saying 'I beheld and grasped Him within Kannamangai' — those who can recite them well shall become celestials in the heaven where the moon glides, and rejoice. To tell the truth, O Kanna who bears the white conch, even for You, if You so intend, the very meaning of poetry could be learned (from these).

703.translit:
silaiyilangu ponnaazhi thiNpadaithaNdoNsangam enkin RaaLaal
malaiyilangu thooLnaankee maRRavanukkeRReekaaN enkin RaaLaal
mulaiyilangu poompayalai munpooda anpoodi yirukkinRaaLaal,
kalaiyilangu mozhiyaaLar kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
703.meaning: She speaks of the bow, the gleaming golden discus, the strong weapons, the mace, and the noble conch; she says 'For Him those four mountain-like shoulders — how fitting they are, behold!'; with the lovely pallor blooming first upon her breasts, she sits absorbed in love. Has this maiden of learned, melodious speech beheld the Lord of Kannapuram?

704.translit:
seruvaraimun naasaRuththa silaiyanRoo kaiththalaththa thenkinRaaLaal
poruvaraimun poortholaiththa ponnaazhi maRRorukai enkinRaaLaal
oruvaraiyum ninnoppaa roppilar ennappaa enkinRaaLaal
karuvaraipool ninRaanaik kaNNapuraththammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
704.meaning: She says 'Is it not the bow upon His palm, which once cut down the warring (demon-)hill?'; she says 'And in His other hand is the golden discus that once destroyed the foe in battle'; she says 'None is equal to You, none compares, O my Father!'. Has she beheld Him who stands like a dark mountain, the Lord of Kannapuram?

705.translit:
thunnumaa maNimudimeel thuzhaayalangal thoonRumaal enkinRaaLaal
minnumaa maNimakara kuNdalangaL vilveesum enkinRaaLaal
ponninmaa maNiyaaram aNiyaakath thilangumaal enkin RaaLaal
kannimaa mathiLpudaisoozh kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
705.meaning: She says 'Upon His close-set great gem-crown the tulasi garland appears!'; she says 'His flashing great jewelled makara ear-rings shoot forth light like a bow!'; she says 'The great gem-necklace of gold gleams as an ornament upon His chest!'. Has she beheld the Lord of Kannapuram, girt round by mighty new-built ramparts?

706.translit:
thaaraaya thaNduLapa vaNduzhutha varaimaarpan enkinRaaLaal,
pooraanaik kombosiththa putpaakan ennammaan enkin RaaLaal,
aaraanum kaaNminkaL ambavaLam vaayavanuk kenkinRaaLaal
kaarvaanam ninRathiruk kaNNapuraththammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
706.meaning: She says 'He has a mountain-like chest, where bees burrow in the cool tulasi garland!'; she says 'My Lord is the bird-rider (on Garuda) who snapped the tusk of the battling elephant!'; she says 'Whoever you are, look — His mouth is like beautiful coral!'. Has she beheld the Lord of holy Kannapuram, where the dark rain-clouds abide?

707.translit:
adiththalamum thaamaraiyee angaiyum pangayamee enkinRaaLaal
mudiththalamum poRpooNu mennenychath thuLLakalaa enkinRaaLaal
vadiththadangaN malaravaloo varaiyaakath thuLLiruppaaL enkinRaaLaal
kadikkamalam kaLLukukkum kaNNapuraththammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
707.meaning: She says 'The soles of His feet are lotuses, and His open palms too are lotuses!'; she says 'His crowned head and golden ornaments will not depart from within my heart!'; she says 'She of the wide, sharp flower-eyes (Lakshmi) dwells upon His mountain-like chest!'. Has she beheld the Lord of Kannapuram, where the fragrant lotus drips its honey?

708.translit:
peeraa yiramudaiya peeraaLan peeraaLan enkinRaaLaal
eeraar kanamakara kuNdalaththan eNthooLan enkin RaaLaal
neeraar mazhaimukilee neeLvaraiyee okkumaal enkin RaaLaal
kaaraar vayalamarum kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
708.meaning: She says 'The Great One of a thousand names, the Great One!'; she says 'He who wears beautiful heavy makara ear-rings, He of eight shoulders!'; she says 'He resembles the water-laden rain-cloud, He resembles the towering mountain!'. Has she beheld the Lord of Kannapuram, set amid dark fertile fields?

709.translit:
sevvaraththa vudaiyaadai yathanmeeloor sivaLikaikkach chenkin RaaLaal,
avvaraththa vadiyiNaiyu mangaikaLum pangayamee enkin RaaLaal,
maivaLarkkum maNiyuruvam marakathamoo mazhaimukiloo enkin RaaLaal,
kaivaLarkku mazhalaaLar kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
709.meaning: She speaks of the reddish robe He wears, and over it a fine ornamental cloth-band; she says 'That pair of His ruddy feet and His palms too are lotuses!'; she says 'His dark-hued gem-like form — is it emerald, or is it a rain-cloud?'. Has this tender maiden, nurtured in (her parents') hands, beheld the Lord of Kannapuram?

710.translit:
koRRappuL LonReeRi mannoodee varukinRaan enkinRaaLaal
veRRippoo rinthiraRku minthiranee okkumaal enkinRaaLaal
peRRakkaa lavanaakam peNpiRanthoom uyyoomoo enkinRaaLaal
kaRRanool maRaiyaaLar kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
710.meaning: She says 'Riding upon the one victorious bird (Garuda), He comes through the realm!'; she says 'He is like Indra himself to the victorious battling Indra!'; she says 'If we should attain His embrace, we who are born women — shall we then not be saved?'. Has she beheld the Lord of Kannapuram of the Veda-learned scholars?

711.translit:
vaNdamarum vanamaalai maNimudimeel maNanaaRum enkinRaaLaal
uNdivarpaa lanpenakken Rorukaalum pirikileen enkinRaaLaal
paNdivaraik kaNdaRiva thevvooril yaam enRee payilkinRaaLaal
kaNdavartham manamvazhangum kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaNdaaL kolloo.
711.meaning: She says 'Upon His gem-crown the wild-flower garland where bees gather wafts its fragrance!'; she says 'I have love for Him, and I shall never for a moment part from Him!'; she keeps repeating 'In which town was it that I once knew and beheld Him before?'. Has she beheld the Lord of Kannapuram, who captures the hearts of all who see Him?

712.translit:
maavaLaru mennookki maatharaaL maayavanaik kaNdaaL enRu
kaavaLarum kadipozhilsoozh kaNNapurath thammaanaik kaliyan sonna
paavaLarum thamizhmaalai panniyanool ivaiyainthu mainthum vallaar
poovaLarum kaRpakamseer ponnulakil mannavaraayp pukazhthak kooree.
712.meaning: These ten — five and five — the garland of Tamil verses rich in poetry, the well-wrought work that Kalikanri (Tirumangai Alwar) composed about the Lord of Kannapuram, encircled by fragrant groves and gardens, saying that the doe-eyed, gentle lady beheld the wondrous Lord — those who can master them shall become praiseworthy kings in the golden world where the flowering Kalpaka tree grows.

713.translit:
theLLiyeer. theevarkkum theevar thiruththakkeer
veLLiyeer veyya vizhunithi vaNNar, oo
thuLLuneerk kaNNa puramthozhu thaaLivaL kaLviyoo,
kaivaLai koLvathu thakkathee?
713.meaning: O clear and lucid One! O God of even the gods, worthy of all glory! O One bright (as silver), O One of the hue of fiery precious treasure! Oh — this girl who has worshipped Kannapuram of the leaping waters, is she a thief? Is it right of You to seize away her wrist-bangles?

714.translit:
neeNilaa muRRaththu ninRivaL nookkinaaL,
kaaNumoo kaNNa puramenRu kaattinaaL,
paaNanaar thiNNa mirukka iniyivaL
naaNumoo, nanRunan RunaRai yoorarkkee.
714.meaning: Standing in the moonlit courtyard, this girl gazed out; she pointed, saying 'Can I see Kannapuram?'. With the noble bard (her go-between) firmly on her side, will this girl now feel any shame? Good, very good — (this is the way) for the Lord of Naraiyur!

715.translit:
aruvisoor veengadam neermalai enRuvaay
veruvinaaL meyyam vinavi yirukkinRaaL,
perukuseerk kaNNapuram enRu peesinaaL
urukinaaL, uLmelin thaaLithu venkoloo.
715.meaning: Her mouth trembling, she uttered 'Venkatam where the waterfalls pour, Tirunirmalai'; she sits inquiring after Tirumeyyam; she spoke the name 'Kannapuram of abounding glory' and melted, growing faint within. What can this state of hers be?

716.translit:
uNNumnaa Lillai uRakkamun thaanillai,
peNmaiyum saala niRainthilaL peethaithaan,
kaNNanoor kaNNa puramthozhum kaarkkadal
vaNNarmeel, eNNa mivatkithu venkoloo.
716.meaning: There is no day she eats, and sleep too is gone from her; the simple girl's womanly modesty is no longer wholly intact. She worships Kannan's town, Kannapuram, set upon the One of the dark-ocean hue — what can this longing of hers be for Him?

717.translit:
kaNNanoor kaNNa puramthozhum kaarikai,
peNmaiyum thannudai yuNmai yuraikkinRaaL,
veNNeyuN daappuNda vaNNam viLambinaaL,
vaNNamum ponniRa maava thozhiyumee.
717.meaning: The lovely girl who worships Kannan's town, Kannapuram, speaks aloud her womanhood and her own true state. She told of the way (Krishna) was bound for having eaten the butter; and her own complexion has faded to a (pale) golden hue.

718.translit:
vadavarai ninRumvanthu inRu kaNapuram,
idavakai koLvathu yaam enRu peesinaaL,
madavaral maatharen peethai yivarkkivaL
kadavathen, kaNduyi linRivar koLLavee.
718.meaning: She spoke saying 'Having come from the northern mountain, today He is taking up His abode in Kannapuram'. My gentle, simple maiden — what does she owe to Him, that He should take away her sleep like this?

719.translit:
tharanganeer peesinum thaNmathi kaayinum,
irangumoo eththanai naaLirun theLkinaaL
thurangamvaay keeNdukan thaanathu thonmai oor
arangamee enpa thivaLthanak kaasaiyee.
719.meaning: Though one speaks of the wave-tossed waters, though the cool moon scorches her, will He show any mercy? For how many days has she sat and pined! That which is called Srirangam, the ancient town of Him who delighted in splitting open the horse-(demon's) mouth — that is her one desire.

720.translit:
thoNDellaam ninnaDi yEthozhu thuyyumaa
kaNDu,thaan kaNapuram kaithozhap pOyinaaL
vaNDulaam kOthaiyen pEthai maNiniRam
koNDuthaan, kOyinmai seyvathu thakkathE?
720.meaning: My innocent daughter, whose tresses swarm with bees, having seen that all servitude attains salvation only by worshipping Your feet, set out to worship Kannapuram with folded hands. Is it fitting, O Lord, that You take away the jewel-like fair complexion of my girl and act so cruelly toward her?

721.translit:
muLLeyi REyndhila, koozhai muDikoDaa,
teLLiya Lenpadhōr thEsilaL enseykEn,
kaLLavizh sOlaik kaNapuram kaithozhum
piLLaiyai, piLLaiyen ReNNap peRuvarE?
721.meaning: Her tiny teeth like thorns are not yet worn smooth, her short hair does not yet reach to be tied up, nor has she the radiance that marks one as discerning and grown. What can I do? Can anyone rightly think of this child, who worships Kannapuram with its groves where honey drips, as a mere child?

722.translit:
kaarmali kaNNa puratthem aDikaLai,
paarmali mangaiyar kOnbara kaalansol,
seermali paaDa livaipatthum vallavar,
neermali vaiyatthu neeDuniR paarkaLE.
722.meaning: These are the words of Parakala (Kaliyan), king of the Mangai women, in praise of our Lord of Kannapuram filled with rain-clouds. Those who can master these ten glorious songs rich in excellence will long endure and flourish in this water-girt world.

723.translit:
karaiyeduttha surisangum kanapavaLat thezhukoDiyum,
thiraiyedutthu varupunalsoozh thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
viraiyeduttha thuzhaayalangal viRalvaraitthOL puDaipeyara
varaiyeduttha perumaanuk kizhandhEnen varivaLaiyE.
723.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, encircled by surging waters whose waves cast up spiralled conches and rising coral like banners on the shore. As His mighty mountain-like shoulders, garlanded with fragrant tulasi, swayed when He lifted up the mountain, I have lost my striped bangles to that great Lord.

724.translit:
ariviravu mukiR kaNatthaa nakilpukaiyaal varaiyODum
therivariya maNimaaDat thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
variyaravi naNaitthuyinRu mazhaimadhattha siRudhaRukaN,
kariveruva maruppositthaaR kizhandhEnen kanavaLaiyE.
724.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram with its jewelled mansions hard to discern, where the smoke of incense rises like mountains mingling with the clustered clouds. He who reclines upon the striped serpent, and who broke the tusk of the fierce small-eyed rutting elephant so that the herd fled in terror — to Him I have lost my rich bangles.

725.translit:
thungamaa maNimaaDa neDumukaTTin soolikaipOm
thingaLmaa mukilthuNikkum thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum
paingaNmaal viDaiyaDartthup panimadhikOL viDutthukandha
sengaNmaa lammaanuk kizhandhEnen seRivaLaiyE.
725.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, where the lofty pinnacles of the tall jewelled mansions cut through the great clouds and the cool moon. He, the red-eyed dark Lord who subdued the green-eyed bulls and who delighted to release the cool moon from its eclipse — to Him I have lost my close-set bangles.

726.translit:
kaNamaruvu mayilakavu kaDipozhilsoozh neDumaRukil,
thiNamaruvu kanamadhiLsoozh thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
maNamaruvu thOLaaycchi yaarkkappOy uralODum
puNarmarutha miRanaDandhaaR kizhandhEnen ponvaLaiyE.
726.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, girt by strong massive ramparts, with long streets and fragrant groves where flocks of peacocks dance. He who, bound to a mortar by the fragrant-shouldered cowherd woman, crawled along and snapped the twin marutha trees as He passed — to Him I have lost my golden bangles.

727.translit:
vaayeduttha mandhiratthaa landhaNartham seythozhilkaL
theeyedutthu maRaivaLarkkum thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum
thaayeduttha siRukOluk kuLaindhOdith thayiruNDa,
vaaythuDaittha maindhanuk kizhandhEnen varivaLaiyE.
727.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, where the brahmins, performing their ordained rites with mantras uttered aloud, kindle the sacred fire and foster the Vedas. The young Lord who, trembling and running from the small rod His mother raised, ate the curd and then wiped His mouth — to Him I have lost my striped bangles.

728.translit:
maDaleduttha neDundhaazhai marungellaam vaLarpavaLam,
thiDaledutthuc cuDarimaikkum thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
aDalaDartthan RiraNiyanai muraNazhiya aNiyukiraal,
uDaleduttha perumaanuk kizhandhEnen oLivaLaiyE.
728.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, where all around the tall thaazhai with spreading fronds and the coral rising in mounds gleam and sparkle. The great Lord who, mustering His might, tore open the body of Hiranya with His beautiful claws so that his arrogance was destroyed — to Him I have lost my lustrous bangles.

729.translit:
vaNDamarum malarppunnai varineezha laNimuttham,
theNDiraikaL varatthiraTTum thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
eNDisaiyu mezhusuDaru mirunilanum peruvisumbum,
uNDumizhndha perumaanuk kizhandhEnen oLivaLaiyE.
729.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, where the clear waves bring and heap up fine pearls in the dappled shade of the bee-haunted punnai blossoms. The great Lord who swallowed and brought forth again the eight directions, the seven luminaries, the great earth and the vast sky — to Him I have lost my lustrous bangles.

730.translit:
konkumali karunguvaLai kaNNaaka theNkayangaL
sengamala mukamalartthum thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
vankamali thaDangaDaluL variyaravi naNaitthuyinRaa,
sengamala naabanuk kizhandhEnen seRivaLaiyE.
730.meaning: He dwells in Thirukkannapuram, where the clear ponds open their red-lotus faces with the fragrant dark blue-lilies for their eyes. He, the lotus-naveled Lord who reclines in sleep upon the striped serpent in the broad ocean thronged with ships — to Him I have lost my close-set bangles.

731.translit:
vaaraaLu miLangongai neDumpaNaitthOL maDappaavai,
seeraaLum varaimaarvan thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum,
pEraaLa naayirambE raayiravaa yaravaNaimEl
pEraaLar perumaanuk kizhandhEnen peyvaLaiyE.
731.meaning: On His mountain-like chest dwells Lakshmi, the tender maiden with youthful breasts bound by a band and long bamboo-like arms. He dwells in Thirukkannapuram — the great One of a thousand names, Lord of the noble ones, who reclines upon the thousand-mouthed serpent couch — to Him I have lost the bangles I wear.

732.translit:
thEmaruvu pozhilpuDaisoozh thirukkaNNa puratthuRaiyum
vaamananai, maRikaDalsoozh vayalaali vaLanaaDan,
kaamaruseerk kalikanRi kaNDuraittha thamizhmaalai,
naamaruvi yivaipaaDa vinaiyaaya naNNaavE.
732.meaning: This is the Tamil garland that Kalikanri (Tirumangai Alwar), lord of the fertile land of Vayalali girt by the tossing sea, beheld and uttered about Vamana who dwells in Thirukkannapuram surrounded by honeyed groves. For those who sing these verses with their tongues, the karmas that are their afflictions will not come near.

733.translit:
viNNavar thangaL perumaan thirumaarvan,
maNNava rellaam vaNangum malipukazhsEr,
kaNNa puratthem perumaan kadhirmuDimEl,
vaNNa naRundhuzhaay vandhoothaay kOlthumbee.
733.meaning: He is the Lord of the celestials, on whose chest Lakshmi dwells, of abounding fame whom all the people of the earth worship. O lovely bee, come and hum upon the fragrant beautiful tulasi on the radiant crown of our Lord of Kannapuram.

734.translit:
vEdha mudhalvan viLangu purinoolan,
paadham paravip palarum paNindhEtthi,
kaadhanmai seyyum kaNNapurat themperumaan,
thaadhu naRundhuzhaay thaazhndhoothaay kOlthumbee.
734.meaning: He is the first cause of the Vedas, wearing the shining sacred thread, at whose feet many bow, praise and lovingly offer their devotion. O lovely bee, descend and hum upon the fragrant pollen-laden tulasi of our Lord of Kannapuram.

735.translit:
viNDamala rellaa moothinee yenpeRudhi,
aNDa mudhalva namararka Lellaarum,
kaNDu vaNangum kaNNapurat themperumaan
vaNDu naRundhuzhaay vandhoothaay kOlthumbee.
735.meaning: What do you gain by humming over all the blossoms that have opened? O lovely bee, He is the first cause of the cosmic egg, whom all the immortals behold and worship — our Lord of Kannapuram. Come and hum upon His fragrant tulasi where bees gather.

736.translit:
neermali kinRadhOr meenaayO raamaiyumaay,
seermali kinRadhOr singa vuruvaaki,
kaarmali vaNNan kaNNapurat themperumaan,
thaarmali thaNDuzhaay thaazhndhoothaay kOlthumbee.
736.meaning: He became a fish that swam in the swelling waters, and also a tortoise, and took the glorious form of a man-lion. O lovely bee, descend and hum upon the cool tulasi garland of our dark cloud-hued Lord of Kannapuram.

737.translit:
Eraar malarellaa moothinee yenpeRudhi,
paaraa rulakam paravap perungaDaluL,
kaaraamai yaana kaNNapurat themperumaan,
thaaraar naRundhuzhaay thaazhndhoothaay kOlthumbee.
737.meaning: What do you gain by humming over all the beautiful blossoms? O lovely bee, He became the dark tortoise in the great ocean so that all the worlds of the earth might praise Him — our Lord of Kannapuram. Descend and hum upon His fragrant tulasi garland.

738.translit:
maarvil thiruvan valanEndhu sakkaratthan,
paaraip piLandha paraman paranjOdhi,
kaaril thikazhkaayaa vaNNan kadhirmuDimEl,
thaaril naRundhuzhaay thaazhndhoothaay kOlthumbee.
738.meaning: Lakshmi dwells on His chest, He holds the discus in His right hand, the Supreme One who split open the earth, the supreme effulgence. O lovely bee, descend and hum upon the fragrant tulasi garland on the radiant crown of Him whose hue is the kaaya flower gleaming against the dark clouds.

739.translit:
vaamanan kaRki madhusoodhan maadhavan
thaarmannu thaasa rathiyaaya thaDamaarvan,
kaamanRan thaadhai kaNNapurat themperumaan,
thaama naRundhuzhaay thaazhndhoothaay kOlthumbee.
739.meaning: He is Vamana, Kalki, Madhusudana, Madhava, and the broad-chested Lord who became Dasaratha's son (Rama) wearing the enduring garland, the father of Kama (Manmatha) — our Lord of Kannapuram. O lovely bee, descend and hum upon His fragrant tulasi garland.

740.translit:
neela malarkaL nedhuneer vayalmarungil,
saala malarellaa moodhaadhE, vaaLarakkar
kaalan kaNNapurath emperumaan kadhirmudimEl,
kOla naRundhuzhaay koNdoodhaay kOlthumbee.
740.meaning: O beautiful bee with your stick-like body! In the wide watery fields blue lotuses and so many other flowers bloom abundantly, yet do not boast of being supreme. Go and blow your hum upon the fragrant, beautiful tulasi garland on the radiant crown of our Lord of Kannapuram, who was death itself to the sword-bearing demons.

741.translit:
nandhan madhalai nilamangai nalthuNaivan,
andha mudhalvan amararkaL tamperumaan,
kandham kamazhkaayaa vaNNan kadhirmudimEl,
kondhu naRundhuzhaay koNdoodhaay kOlthumbee.
741.meaning: O beautiful bee with your stick-like body! He is the son of Nanda, the worthy consort of the Earth-maiden, the first cause who is the end of all, the Lord of the celestials, hued like the fragrant kaaya blossom. Go and blow your hum upon the cluster of fragrant tulasi on his radiant crown.

742.translit:
vaNdamarum sOlai vayalaali nannaadan,
kaNdaseer venRik kaliyan olimaalai,
koNdal niRavaNNan kaNNa puratthaanai,
toNdarOm paada ninaindhoodhaay kOlthumbee.
742.meaning: O beautiful bee with your stick-like body! Kaliyan (Tirumangai Alwar), the good lord of fertile Vayalali whose groves teem with bees, of celebrated glory and victory, composed this garland of music. Think of it and blow your hum upon the Lord of Kannapuram, hued like the dark rain-cloud, so that we, his servants, may sing.

743.translit:
thandhai kaalil vilangaRavandhu thOnRiya thOnRalpin, thamiyEnRan
sindhai pOyiRRuth thiruvaruL avanidaip peRumaLa virundhEnai
andhi kaavalan amudhuRu pasunkadhi ravaisuda adhanOdum
mandha maarudham vanamulai thadavandhu valiseyva thozhiyaadhE.
743.meaning: After that radiant one (Krishna) who appeared so that the fetters fell from his father's feet, my lonely heart went chasing behind him. While I stay waiting only to obtain his sacred grace, the guardian of the evening (the moon) scorches me with its cool green rays that should be like nectar, and along with it the gentle breeze keeps caressing my fair bosom and torments me without ceasing.

744.translit:
maari maakkadal vaLaivaNaR kiLaiyavan varaipurai thirumaarbil
taari naasaiyil pOyina nenjamum thaazhndhadhOr thuNaikaaNEn
oorum thunjiRRu ulagamum thuyinRadhu oLiyavan visumbiyangum
thErum pOyiRRuth thisaigaLum maRaindhana seyvadhon RaRiyEnE.
744.meaning: My heart went away yearning for the tulasi garland on the mountain-like sacred chest of the younger brother of the conch-hued Lord dark as the rain-bearing great ocean; I find no companion to ease me. The village has fallen asleep, the world slumbers, the chariot of the radiant one (sun) that moves across the sky has departed, and the directions have vanished into darkness; I know not what to do.

745.translit:
aayan maayamE yanRimaR Renkaiyil vaLaigaLum iRainillaa
pEyin aaruyi ruNdidum piLLainam peNNuyirk kirangumO
thooya maamadhik kadhirchchudath thuNaiyillai iNaimulai vEginRadhaal
aayan vEyinuk kazhiginRadh uLLamum anjElen paarilaiyE.
745.meaning: Apart from the wonders of the cowherd, the bangles on my hands will not stay even a moment. Will that child who sucked away the very life of the demoness (Putana) take pity on the life of us women? With the rays of the pure great moon scorching me, I have no companion; my twin breasts are burning. My heart melts away at the sound of the cowherd's flute, and there is no one to say 'fear not.'

746.translit:
kayangoL puNthalaik kaLiRundhu vendhiRal kazhalmannar perumpOril
mayangaveNsangam vaayvaiththa maindhanum vandhilan, maRikadalneer
thayangu veNthiraith thivalainuN paniyennum thazhalmugandh iLamulaimEl
iyangu maarudham vilangilen aaviyai enakkenap peRalaamE.
746.meaning: The hero who set the white conch to his lips in the great war so that the fierce anklet-wearing kings, driving their wounded-headed elephants, were thrown into confusion, has not come. If only the breeze that moves over my young bosom, carrying what seems like fire in the fine spray and mist from the white waves of the surging sea, would turn aside, I could keep my life for my own.

747.translit:
Ezhu maamaram thuLaipadach silaivaLaith ilangaiyai malanguvitha
aazhi yaan, namak karuLiya aruLodum pagalellai kazhiginRadhaal
thOzhi naamidhaR kenseydhum thuNaiyillai sudarpadu mudhuneeril
aazha aazhginRa aaviyai aduvadhOr andhivan dadaiginRadhE.
747.meaning: The Lord with the discus, who bent his bow to pierce the seven great trees and threw Lanka into ruin, has let the day pass by along with the grace he gave us. O friend, what shall we do for this? We have no companion. The evening has come and closed in to slay my life that is sinking ever deeper into the old waters where the sun has set.

748.translit:
muriyum veNthirai mudhukayam theeppada muzhangazha leriyambin
varigoL venjilai vaLaivitha maindhanum vandhilan enseygEn
eriyum vengadhir thuyinRadhu paaviyEn iNainedung kaNthuyilaa
kariya naazhigai oozhiyil periyana kazhiyumaa RaRiyEnE.
748.meaning: The hero who bent his fierce striped bow with flaming arrows that set the old sea of breaking white waves ablaze with roaring fire, has not come; what shall I do? The burning hot-rayed sun has gone to sleep, yet my long paired eyes find no sleep, sinner that I am. I know not how these dark hours, longer than aeons, will ever pass away.

749.translit:
kalanga maakkadal kadaindhadaith ilangaiyar kOnadhu varaiyaagam
malanga venjamath thadusaram thurandhavem madigaLum vaaraanaal
ilangu vengadhi riLamadhi yadhanodum vidaimaNi yadum, aayan
vilangal vEyina thOsaiyu maayini viLaivadhon RaRiyEnE.
749.meaning: My Lord who churned the troubled great ocean, who dammed it, and who loosed cruel arrows in fierce battle to crush the mountain-like chest of the king of Lanka's people, has not come. Along with the bright hot-rayed sun and the young moon, the bells of the bulls torment me, and so too does the sound of the cowherd's mountain-bamboo flute. I know not what is to become of me now.

750.translit:
muzhudhiv vaiyagam muRaiketa maRaidhalum munivanum muniveydhi
mazhuvi naalmannar aaruyir vavviya maindhanum vaaraanaal
ozhugu nuNpanik kodungiya pEdaiyai adangavanj siRaikOli
thazhuvu naLLiruL thanimaiyiR kadiyadhOr koduvinai yaRiyEnE.
750.meaning: When this whole earth's order failed and the law was lost, the sage (Parasurama) grew wrathful; that hero who seized away the very lives of the kings with his axe has not come. The midnight darkness, enfolding me with its dense black wings like a female bird shrinking in the trickling fine mist, embraces me in my loneliness; I have known no crueler, harsher affliction than this.

751.translit:
kananjey maamadhiL kaNapurath thavanodum kanavini lavanthandha
mananjey inbamvan duLpuga veLgiyen vaLainega irundhEnai
sinanjey maalvidaich siRumaNi Osaiyen sindhaiyaich sindhuvikkum
anandhal anRilin arikural paaviyEn aaviyai aduginRadhE.
751.meaning: When in a dream the joy he had granted me came and entered my heart together with the Lord of Kanapuram of dense great ramparts, I sat abashed, my bangles loosening. The little bell-sound of the angry great bull scatters my thoughts, and the sleepless anril bird's plaintive cry slays the very life of me, sinner that I am.

752.translit:
vaarkoL menmulai madandhaiyar tadangadal vaNNanaith thaaLnayandhu
aarvath thaalavar pulambiya pulambalai aRindhumun uraiseydha,
kaarkoL paimpozhil mangaiyar kaavalan kalikanRi yolivallaar
ErkoL vaikundha maanagar pukku imaiyavarodum kooduvarE.
752.meaning: Kalikanri (Tirumangai Alwar), the protector of Mangai-nadu with its lush green groves heavy with rain-clouds, knew and set into words the lamentations of the maidens of soft girdled bosoms who longed for the feet of the ocean-hued Lord and grieved in their fervor. Those who can master this music will enter the glorious great city of Vaikuntha and join the company of the celestials.

753.translit:
toNdeer. uyyum vagaikaNdEn thuLangaa arakkar thuLanga mun
thiNdOL nimirach silaivaLaiyach siRidhE munindha thirumaarban
vaNdaar koondhal malarmangai vadikkaN madandhai maanOkkam
kaNdaaL kaNdu koNdu kandha kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
753.meaning: O devotees! I have found the way to be saved. He of the sacred chest, who once raised his firm shoulders and bent his bow with but a little wrath to make the unshakable demons tremble, is the one upon whom the flower-maiden Lakshmi of bee-swarmed tresses cast her grace-filled glance from her sharp eyes. Let us worship fragrant Kannapuram where she beheld him.

754.translit:
porundhaa arakkar venjamatthup ponRa anRu puLLoorndhu
perundhOL maali talaipuraLap pErndha arakkar tennilangai
irundhaar tammai yudankoNdang ezhilaar pilatthup pukkoLippa
karundhaaL silaikaik koNdaanoor kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
754.meaning: On that day he rode upon his bird (Garuda) so that the hostile demons perished in fierce battle, and the broad-shouldered Mali's head rolled away; when the demons of southern Lanka who remained took their kin and went and hid themselves in a beautiful cavern, he took up his dark-stemmed bow in his hand. Let us worship Kannapuram, his town.

755.translit:
valli yidaiyaaL poruttaaga madhiLnee rilangai yaarkOvai,
allal seydhu venjamatthuL aaRRal migundha aaRRalaan
vallaaL arakkar kulappaavai vaada munitan vELviyai
kalvich silaiyaal kaaththaanoor kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
755.meaning: For the sake of the creeper-waisted lady (Sita), he afflicted the king of the moat-girt, water-surrounded Lanka and showed surpassing might in fierce battle. He who made the doll-like daughter of the mighty demon clan wither, and who guarded the sage's (Visvamitra's) sacrifice with his well-learned bow — let us worship Kannapuram, his town.

756.translit:
mallai munnee radharpada varivenjilaikaal vaLaivitthu,
kollai vilangu paNiseyyak kodiyOn ilangai pugaluRRu
tollai marangaL pugappeydhu thuvalai nimirndhu vaaNaNava
kallaal kadalai yadaitthaanoor kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
756.meaning: He bent at its foot his fierce striped bow so that a path opened through the mighty deep sea; he made the wild forest creatures do his bidding and set out to enter the Lanka of the evil one (Ravana). Casting in the ancient trees and stones, with the spray rising up to touch the heavens, he dammed the ocean with rocks. Let us worship Kannapuram, his town.

757.translit:
aamai yaagi ariyaagi anna maagi andhaNartam
Oma maagi oozhiyaay ulagu soozhndha nedumpuNari
sEma madhiLsoozh ilangaikkOn siramungaramum thuNitthu mun
kaamaR payandhaan karudhumoor kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
757.meaning: Becoming the tortoise, becoming the lion, becoming the swan, becoming the brahmins' sacred fire-oblation, being the aeon itself, being the vast ocean that encircles the worlds — he who once severed the heads and arms of the king of well-walled, protected Lanka, and who begot Kama (Manmatha) — let us worship Kannapuram, the town he holds dear.

758.translit:
varundhaa thirunee madanenjE nammEl vinaigaL vaaraa mun
thirundhaa arakkar tennilangai sendhee yuNNach sivandhorunaaL,
perundhOL vaaNaR karuLpurindhu pinnai maNaaL anaagi mun
karundhaaL kaLiRon Rositthaanoor kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
758.meaning: Do not grieve, O foolish heart; abide in peace, before our karmic deeds come upon us. He who once made the southern Lanka of the unreformed demons glow red as it was consumed by raging fire, who granted his grace to the broad-shouldered Banasura, who became the bridegroom of Nappinnai, and who long ago broke the dark-legged elephant (Kuvalayapida) — let us worship Kannapuram, his town.

759.translit:
ilaiyaar malarppoom poygaivaay mudhalai tannaal adarppuNdu
kolaiyaar vEzham nadukkuRRuk kulaiya adhanuk karuLpurindhaan
alainee rilangaith thasakkireevaRku iLaiyORk karasai yaruLi,mun
kalaimaach silaiyaal eydhaanoor kaNNa puramnaam thozhudhumE.
759.meaning: When the murderous elephant (Gajendra), seized and oppressed by the crocodile in the flowering lotus-pond rich with leaves and blossoms, trembled and faltered, he granted it his grace. He who bestowed the kingdom upon the younger brother of the ten-headed Ravana of wave-washed Lanka, and who once shot down the great deer with his bow — let us worship Kannapuram, his town.

760.translit:
maalaay manameyaruntuyaril varuntaa tirunee valimikka
kaalaar marutum kaaysinatta kazhutum katamaak kazhutaiyum,
maalaar vidaiyum matakariyum mallar uyirum madivittu,
kaalaal sakatam paayntaanoor kaNNapuram naam tozhutume.
760.meaning: O mind, do not grieve, bewildered, in great sorrow; firmly worship. He destroyed the strong-footed maruda trees, the fierce demon, the raging wild donkey-demon, the great rutting bull, the mighty rutting elephant, and the lives of the wrestlers, and with His foot kicked the cart-demon. That Lord's town is Kannapuram, which we worship.

761.translit:
kunraal maari pazhutaakkik kodiye ridaiyaaL poruttaaka,
vanraaL vidaiye zhanradartta vaanor perumaan maamaayan,
senraan tootu panjavarkkaayt tirikaal sakatam sinamazhittu
kanraal viLangkaa yerintaanoor kaNNapuram naam tozhutume.
761.meaning: For the sake of the slender-waisted cowherd maiden Nappinnai, He raised a hill to thwart the rain and subdued the seven fierce-footed bulls; He is the Lord of the celestials, the great mystic one. He went as a messenger for the five Pandavas, destroyed the fury of the whirling cart-demon, and flung a calf to fell the wood-apple fruits. That Lord's town is Kannapuram, which we worship.

762.translit:
karumaa mukiltoy nedumaadak kaNNapurattem adikaLai
tirumaa makaLaa ruLmaari sezhunee raali vaLanaadan
maruvaar puyalkaik kalikanri mangkai venta noliyvallaar
irumaa nilattuk karasaaki imaiyor irainjca vaazhvaare.
762.meaning: Our Lord at Kannapuram with tall mansions that touch the great dark clouds, who is grace-showering through Tirumamagal (Lakshmi) — of Him sang Kalikanri (Tirumangai Alwar), king of the rich Mangai land watered by the fertile Aali river, whose hand pours like the gathering rain-cloud. Those able in these melodious verses will live as kings over this great wide earth and be revered even by the celestials.

763.translit:
viyamudai vidaiyinam udaitara madamakaL,
kuyamidai tadavarai yakalama tudaiyavar,
nayamudai nadaiyanam iLaiyavar nadaipayil,
kayamidai kaNapuram adikaLtamidame.
763.meaning: To win the tender maiden, He broke the herd of mighty bulls; He bears upon His broad mountain-like chest where she rests. Where young women whose graceful gait rivals the swans practise their walk, amid the tanks of Kanapuram — that is the place of the Lord.

764.translit:
iNaimali marutino derutira ikalseytu
tuNaimali mulaiyavaL maNamiku kalaviyuL,
maNamali vizhavino dadiyavar aLaviya,
kaNamali kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
764.meaning: He fought and felled the paired maruda trees and the bulls, and in the fragrant union with the well-paired-breasted maiden (Nappinnai). Where devotees mingle in throngs amid fragrant festivals, the crowded Kanapuram — that is the place of the Lord.

765.translit:
puyaluru varaimazhai pozhitara maNinirai,
mayalura varaikudai yeduviya nediyavar,
muyalturaL miLaimuyal turaLavaLa viLaivayal,
kayalturaL kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
765.meaning: When the storm-driven rain poured upon the hill, the tall Lord lifted the mountain as an umbrella so that the herds of cattle would not be deluded with distress. Where carp fish leap in the fertile fields by the thickets — that crowded Kanapuram is the place of the Lord.

766.translit:
etalar nakaiseya iLaiyavar aLaiveNNey
potusey tamariya punitarnal viraimalar
kotiya matukaram kulaviya malarmakaL
kaatalsey kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
766.meaning: While His foes laughed, the young one ate the churned butter — the pure Lord who delighted in it. Where bees hum, gathering nectar from the good fragrant blossoms, the place beloved of the lotus-born Lakshmi — that Kanapuram is the place of the Lord.

767.translit:
toNdarum amararum munivarum tozhutezha
aNdamo dakalidam aLantavar amarcceytu
viNdavar padamati LilangkaimuN neriyezha
kaNdavar kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
767.meaning: As devotees, celestials, and sages rose worshipping, He measured the cosmos and the wide earth (as Trivikrama); and waging war, He made fire blaze up against the walled Lanka of His foes who scattered and fell. That Lord's place is Kanapuram.

768.translit:
mazhuviyal padaiyudai yavanidam mazhaimukil,
tazhuviya uruvinar tirumakaL maruviya
kozhuviya sezhumalar muzhusiya paravaipaN
ezhuviya kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
768.meaning: He whose hue embraces that of the rain-cloud, in whom Lakshmi abides — His place, shared too with the one bearing the axe-weapon. Where birds dive into the rich luxuriant blossoms and their song rises up — that Kanapuram is the place of the Lord.

769.translit:
paritiyo daNimati panivarai tisainilam
eritiyo denavina iyalvinar selavinar
surutiyo darumarai muraisolu madiyavar
karutiya kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
769.meaning: He is of the nature of the sun, the lovely moon, the snow-mountains, the directions, the earth, and the fire — moving as all these. Where devotees duly chant the Vedas and the precious sacred scriptures, the cherished Kanapuram — that is the place of the Lord.

770.translit:
padipulku madiyiNai palartozha malarvaiku
kodipulku tadavarai akalama tudaiyavar
mudipulku neduvayal padaisela adimalar
kadipulku kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
770.meaning: His pair of flower-soft feet that touch the earth, worshipped by many; He of the broad mountain-like chest where the creeper (Lakshmi) clings. Where the plough moves through the long fields and the fragrance of His flower-feet pervades — that Kanapuram is the place of the Lord.

771.translit:
pulamanu malarmisai malarmakaL puNariya
nilamaka Lenavina makaLirka Livarodum
valamanu padaiyudai maNivaNar nitikuvai
kalamanu kaNapuram adikaLtam idame.
771.meaning: He who is joined with the flower-dwelling Lakshmi upon the lotus, and with the Earth-goddess and these maidens — the sapphire-hued one bearing the mighty discus-weapon. Where heaps of treasure and vessels abound, that Kanapuram is the place of the Lord.

772.translit:
malipukazh kaNapura mudaiyavem adikaLai
valikezhu matiLayal vayalaNi mangkaiyar
kaliyana tamizhivai vizhumiya isaiyinodu
olisolum adiyavar urutuya rilare.
772.meaning: Of our Lord who possesses Kanapuram of abounding glory — these Tamil verses of Kaliyan (Tirumangai Alwar), lord of Mangai adorned with fields beside its strong walls. The devotees who recite these aloud with excellent melody will have no sorrow that afflicts them.

773.translit:
vaano raLavum mutumunneer vaLarnta kaalam, valiyuruvil
meenaay vantu viyantuyyak koNda taNdaa maraikkaNNan
aanaa vuruvi laanaayan avanai yammaa viLaivayaluL
kaanaar puravil kaNNapurat tadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
773.meaning: At the time the ancient ocean swelled up to the world of the celestials, He came in mighty form as a Fish and wondrously saved (the world) — the cool-lotus-eyed Krishna. The cowherd in the form of cattle, that Lord — I, His servant, beheld and attained Him at Kannapuram with abundant fields and forested groves.

774.translit:
malangku vilangku neduveLLam maruka angkor varainattu
ilangku soti yaaramutam eytu maLavor aamaiyaay
vilangkal tiriyat tadangkaduluL sumantu kidanta vittakanai,
kalangkal munneerk kaNNapurattadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
774.meaning: When the long flood surged and churned, planting a mountain there to obtain the shining nectar-ambrosia, He lay in the vast ocean as a Tortoise, bearing up the whirling mountain — that wondrous one. I, His servant, beheld and attained Him at Kannapuram of the turbid sea.

775.translit:
paaraar aLavum mutumunneer paranta kaalam, vaLaimaruppil
eraar uruvat tenamaay edutta aatra lammaanai,
kooraar aaral iraikarutik kuruku paayak kayaliriyum,
kaaraar puravil kaNNapurat tadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
775.meaning: At the time the ancient ocean spread over the whole earth, the mighty Lord lifted it (the earth) up in the beautiful form of a Boar with curved tusks. Where the crane pounces seeking the sharp eel as prey and the carp flee — at Kannapuram with dark groves, I, His servant, beheld and attained Him.

776.translit:
uLainta ariyum maanidamum udanaayt tonra onruvittu
viLainta seetram viNvetumpa vetron akalam venjcamattu
piLantu vaLainta vukiraanaip peruntaN senner kulaitadintu
kaLanjcey puravil kaNNapurattadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
776.meaning: Appearing as the raging Lion and man combined as one (Narasimha), with His kindled fury making the heavens scorch, in fierce battle He split open the chest of His foe with His curved claws. At Kannapuram with groves where the great cool red paddy clusters are cut and threshing-floors are made, I, His servant, beheld and attained Him.

777.translit:
tozhuneer vadivil kuraLuruvaay vantu tonri maavalipaal
muzhuneer vaiyam munkoNda moovaa vuruvi nammaanai
uzhuneer vayaluL ponkiLaippa orupaal mullai mukaiyodum
kazhuneer malarum kaNNapurattadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
777.meaning: In a worship-worthy form, coming as a Dwarf (Vamana), He took from Mahabali the whole sea-girt earth — the ageless-formed Lord. Where in the ploughed wet fields gold gleams, and on one side the jasmine buds and the red-lily (kazhuneer) blossom — at Kannapuram, I, His servant, beheld and attained Him.

778.translit:
vadivaay mazhuve padaiyaaka vantu tonri moovezhukaal
padiyaar arasu kaLaikatta paazhi yaanai yammaanai
kudiyaa vaNdu koNduNNak kola neelam mattukukkum
kadiyaar puravil kaNNapurat tadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
778.meaning: Coming with the sharp axe as His weapon (Parasurama), He uprooted the kings of the earth thrice seven times — that mighty Lord of great strength. Where bees settle and drink, and the lovely blue lily spills its nectar — at Kannapuram with fragrant groves, I, His servant, beheld and attained Him.

779.translit:
vaiya mellaa mudanvaNangka vaNangkaa manna naayttonri
veyya seetrak kadiyilangkai kudikoN dota venjcamattu,
seyta vempor namparanaic sezhuntaN kaanal maNanaarum
kaitai velik kaNNapurat tadiyen kaNdu koNdene.
779.meaning: Appearing as a king (Rama) whom all the world bows to yet who bows to none, in fierce battle with His blazing wrath He drove out the dwellers of guarded Lanka and waged fierce war — that supreme Lord of ours. At Kannapuram, fenced with fragrant screwpine (kaitai) by the cool lush seashore groves, I, His servant, beheld and attained Him.

780.translit:
ottraik kuzhaiyum naanjilum orupaal thoondrath thaan-thoondri
vettrith thozhilaar veel-veendar viNpaal sella venjamaththu
settra kottrath thozhilaanaich chenthee moondrum illiruppa
kattra maraiyoor kaNNapuraththadiyeen kaNdu koNdeene.
780.meaning: He who appeared on his own, with a single ear-ornament and a plough showing on one side, the Lord whose victorious deeds in fierce battle sent the spear-wielding kings to the realm of the sky in the slaughter of his triumphant act of war. This Lord, who abides where the learned Vedic brahmins keep the three sacred fires, I, his servant, have beheld and possessed at Kannapuram.

781.translit:
thuvarik kanivaai nila-mangai thuyar-theernth uyyap paarathaththuL
ivarith tharasar thadumaara iruL-naaL pirantha ammaanai
uvari yootham muththuntha orupaa lorupaa loN-sennel
kavari veesum kaNNapurath thadiyeen kaNdu koNdeene.
781.meaning: The Lord who was born on a dark night so that the kings would be thrown into confusion in the Bharata war, so that the Earth-maiden of coral-red lips might have her sorrow ended and be saved. Where on one side the ocean's waves cast up pearls, and on the other side the bright fine paddy waves like a fan, that Lord I, his servant, have beheld and possessed at Kannapuram.

782.translit:
meeno daamai keezhal-ari kuRaLaai munnu miraamanaaith thaanaai
pinnu miraamanaaith thaamoo tharanaaik kaRkiyum aanaan Rannai
kaNNapurath thadiyeen kaliya nolisaitha
theenaa rin-sol thamizh-maalai seppap paavam nillaave.
782.meaning: He who became the Fish, the Tortoise, the Boar, the Man-lion, and the Dwarf, who first was Rama (Parashurama), then again was Rama, then Damodara, and also became Kalki. Of this Lord at Kannapuram, when the sweet honeyed Tamil garland of words composed by Kaliyan, his servant, is recited, sin shall not stand.

783.translit:
kaimmaana mathayaanai yidar-theerththa karu-mukilai
maimmaana maNiyai aNi-koL marakathaththai,
emmaanai yempiraanai yeesanai yen-manaththuL
ammaanai, adiyee nadainthuynthu pooneene.
783.meaning: The dark rain-cloud who removed the distress of the proud elephant Gajendra, the dark gem, the lovely emerald, my Master, my Lord, the Supreme One who is the Father within my heart. Reaching him, I his servant have been saved and have gone forth redeemed.

784.translit:
tharumaana mazhai-mukilaip piriyaathu thannadainthaar,
varumaanam thavirkkum maNiyaiyaNi-uruvil,
thirumaalai yammaanai amuthaththaik kadar-kidantha
perumaanai adiyee nadainthuynthu pizhaiththeene.
784.meaning: The generous rain-cloud, the gem who removes the recurring births of those who reach him and never part from him, of beautiful form. Tirumal, the Master, the nectar, the Lord who reclines upon the ocean - reaching him, I his servant have been saved and have lived.

785.translit:
vidaiyeezhan Radarththu veguNdu vilangaluRa
padaiyaalaazhi thatta paraman paranjoothi,
madaiyaar neelam-malkum vayal-soozh kaNNapuramon
Rudaiyaanukku adiyeen oruvark kuriyeenoo?
785.meaning: The Supreme One, the Supreme Light, who in anger subdued the seven bulls, and who with his weapon, the discus, struck (the mountain). To him who possesses the one Kannapuram, surrounded by fields where blue waterlilies abound at the sluices, I am the servant - shall I belong to any other?

786.translit:
mikkaanai maraiyaai virintha viLakkai ennuL
pukkaanaip pukazh-seer poliginra pon-malaiyai
thakkaanaik kadikaith thadangundrin misaiyiruntha
akkaarak kaniyai adainthuynthu pooneene.
786.meaning: The supreme one, the lamp that spread forth as the Vedas, who entered within me, the golden mountain shining with abundant glory, the worthy one. He who abides upon the great hill of Kadikai - that sugar-sweet fruit I reached, and was saved, and went forth redeemed.

787.translit:
vanthaayen manaththe vanthunee pukunthapinnai,
enthaai pooyariyaai ithuve yamaiyaathoo
konthaar paim-pozhil-soozh kudanthaik kidanthukantha
mainthaa unnaiyendrum maravaamaip petreene.
787.meaning: You came; and after you came and entered into my heart, O my Father, you have not known to leave - is this not itself enough? O youthful Lord who delight in reclining at Kudandai surrounded by fresh groves full of clustered blossoms, I have obtained the grace of never forgetting you.

788.translit:
enjaa vennarakath thazhunthi nadungukindreerku,
anjeelen Radiyeenai aatkoLLa vallaanai,
nenje nee-ninaiyaathu iraippozhuthumiruththikaNdaai,
manjaar maaLikai-soozh vayalaali mainthanaiye.
788.meaning: For me who was sinking and trembling in the unending fierce hell, the Lord who is able to take me, his servant, into his service saying 'Fear not.' O my heart, even for a moment do not remain without thinking of him - look! - the youthful Lord of Vayalali, surrounded by cloud-touching mansions.

789.translit:
pettraar pettrozhinthaar pinnumnin Radiyeenukku,
uttraanaai vaLarththu ennuyiraaki nindraanai,
muttraa maamathikooL viduththaanai yemmaanai
eththaal yaanmarakkeen ithusollenan-eezhai-nenje.
789.meaning: Those who bore me bore me and then ceased; yet standing further as a near kinsman to me his servant, he raised me up and became my very life. He who released the unwaning great moon from the grip (of the eclipse), my Master - by what means could I forget him? Tell me this, O my poor heart.

790.translit:
kattraar pattraRukkum piravip perung-kadalee
pattraa vanthadiyeen pirantheen pirantha-pinnai
vattraa neervayal-soozh vayalaali yammaanaip
pettreen pettrathum piravaamai petreene.
790.meaning: O great ocean of birth which the learned cut off their attachment from! Without grasping for it, I his servant was born; and after being born, I obtained the Master of Vayalali surrounded by fields of never-drying water - and what I obtained was the obtaining of no more births.

791.translit:
kaNNaar kaNNapuram kadikai kadi-kamazhum
thaNNaar thaamarai-soozh thalaichchanga meel-thisaiyuL
viNNoor naaN-mathiyai viriginra venjudarai
kaNNaarak kaNdukoNdu kaLikkindrathing kendrukoloo.
791.meaning: The fair Kannapuram, Kadikai, and Talaichchangam in the western direction surrounded by cool fragrant lotuses - the bright moon of the celestials, the spreading fierce radiance. When shall it be that here I behold him to my eyes' content and rejoice?

792.translit:
seruneera veel-valavan kalikandri mangaiyarkoon
karuneer mukil-vaNNan kaNNa puraththaanai
iruneer in-thamizh in-isai maalaikaL koNduthoNdeer,
varuneer vaiyamuyya ivaipaadi yaadumine.
792.meaning: Kaliyan, the king of the Mangai land, skilled with the spear in the waters of battle, sang of the Lord of Kannapuram who is hued like the dark water-bearing cloud. O devotees, taking these garlands of grand, sweet Tamil and sweet music, sing and dance them so that the sea-girt earth may be saved.

793.translit:
vaNdaarpoo maamalar mangai maNa-nookkam
uNdaane unnai yukanthukanth undranakkee
thoNdaaneerku en-seykin Raaisollu naal-veetham
kaNdaane kaNNa puRaththuRai yammaane.
793.meaning: O you who received the loving glance of Lakshmi, born in the great flower swarming with bees! What will you do for me who, delighting and delighting in you, am wholly your devotee - tell me. O you who revealed the four Vedas, O Master who dwells at Kannapuram!

794.translit:
peruneerum viNNum malaiyu mulakeezhum
oruthaaraa ninnu Lodukkiya ninnaiyallaal
varutheevar mattruLaren Renmanath thiraiyum
karutheennaan kaNNa puraththuRai yammaane.
794.meaning: The great waters, the sky, the mountains, and the seven worlds - you who absorb them all together within yourself: apart from you, I do not in my heart consider, even for an instant, that there exists any other god that comes. O Master who dwells at Kannapuram!

795.translit:
mattrumoor theyva muLathen Riruppaaroo
duttrileen uttrathu munnadi yaarkkadimai
mattrellam peesilum nin-thiru vettezhuththum
kattru naan kaNNa puraththuRai yammaane.
795.meaning: I have not associated with those who hold that there exists some other god; what I have attained is servitude to your ancient devotees. Whatever else I may speak, I have learned your sacred eight syllables. O Master who dwells at Kannapuram!

796.translit:
peNNaanaaL periLang kongaiyi naarazhal-pool
uNNaananj suNdukanth aayai yukantheennaan
maNNaaLaa vaaL-nedung kaNNi mathu-malaraaL
kaNNaaLaa kaNNa puraththuRai yammaane.
796.meaning: You who, delighting, drank the poison that consumed inward like fire from the great young breasts of her (Putana) who came as a woman - you I have delighted in. O ruler of the earth, O beloved of her of the long sword-like eyes who dwells on the honeyed lotus (Lakshmi), O Master who dwells at Kannapuram!

797.translit:
pettraarum suttramu mendrivai peeNeennaan
mattraarum pattrilee naathalaal ninnadaintheen
uttraanen RuLLaththu vaiththaruL seykaNdaai
kattraarchcheer kaNNa puraththuRai yammaane.
797.meaning: I do not cherish these - those who bore me and my kinsfolk; and since I have no other refuge, I have reached you. Hold me in your heart as one near to you and grant your grace - look! O Master who dwells at Kannapuram, the abode of the learned!

798.translit:
eeththiyun seevadi yeNNi yiruppaarai,
paarththirunth angu namandramar pattraathu
sooththamnaam anjuthu mendru thodaamai nee
kaaththipool kaNNa puraththuRai yammaane.
798.meaning: Those who abide praising you and meditating on your red feet - the messengers of Yama, watching there, do not seize them, but saying 'We salute you, we are afraid,' do not touch them. You guard them so. O Master who dwells at Kannapuram!

799.translit:
veLLaineer veLLath thaNaintha aravaNaimeel
thuLLuneer meLLath thuyinra perumaane
vaLLale undramark kendrum namandramar
kaLLarpool kaNNa puraththuRai yammaane.
799.meaning: O Lord who gently slept upon the serpent-couch set amid the flood of white waters with leaping waves! O bountiful one - to your devotees, the messengers of Yama are forever like thieves (who dare not approach). O Master who dwells at Kannapuram!

800.translit:
maaNaaki vaiya maLandhadhuvum vaaLavuNan
pooNaakam keeNdadhuvum eeNdu ninaindhirundhEn
pENaadha valvinai yEnidar ettanaiyum
kaaNEnnaan kaNNa purattuRai yammaanE.
800.meaning: Becoming a dwarf (Vamana) and measuring the worlds, and tearing open the jewel-adorned chest of the sword-wielding asura (Hiranya) — these deeds I have stood here meditating upon. Though I am one of fierce, irredeemable karma, however much suffering may come, I will not regard it, O Lord who dwells in Tirukkannapuram.

801.translit:
naattinaa yennai yunakkumun thoNdaaka
maattinE nattanaiyE koNden valvinaiyai
paattinaa lunnaiyen nenjath thirundhamai
kaattinaay kaNNa purattuRai yammaanE.
801.meaning: You established me long ago as a servant unto you; I gave myself over only to that, and you took away my fierce karma. Through song you revealed to me that you dwell within my heart, O Lord who dwells in Tirukkannapuram.

802.translit:
kaNdaseerk kaNNa purattuRai yammaanai
koNdaseerth thoNdan kaliya nolimaalai
paNdamaayp paadu madiyavark kennjaanRum
aNdampO yaatchi yavarkka dharindhOmE.
802.meaning: This garland of resounding verses by Kaliyan, the devoted servant of distinguished glory, sings of the Lord of beheld splendor who dwells in Tirukkannapuram. We know that for the devotees who sing it as their treasured wealth, sovereign rule in the celestial realm forever belongs to them.

803.translit:
vangamaa munneer variniRap periya vaaLara viNaNai mEvi
sangamaa rangaith thadamala rundhich saamamaa mEniyen thalaivan
angamaa raindhu vELvinaal vEdham arungalai payinRu eri moonRum
sengaiyaal vaLarkkum thuLakkamil manaththOr thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
803.meaning: Reclining upon the great striped, bright serpent in the vast ocean where ships sail, with a conch in his beautiful hand and a great lotus on his navel, my dark-hued Lord — he stands in Tirukkannankudi of the steady-minded ones who practice the six Angas, the four Vedas, the rare arts, and tend the three sacred fires with their fair hands.

804.translit:
kavaLamaa kadhattha kariyuyyap poykaik karaamkoLak kalangiyuL ninaindhu
thuvaLamEl vandhu thOnRivan mudhalai thuNipadach chudupadai thurandhOn
kuvaLaineeL muLari kumudhamoN kazhuneer koymmalar neydhaloN kazhani
thivaLummaa Likasoozh sezhumaNip purisaith thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
804.meaning: When the proud, rutting elephant was seized by a crocodile in the pond and, distressed within, cried out to be saved, he came forth and hurled his burning discus so that the strong crocodile was cut to pieces. He stands in Tirukkannankudi, ringed with gleaming gem-set ramparts and shining mansions, with fields of kuvalai, tall lotus, kumudham, bright kazhuneer, freshly blooming neydhal flowers.

805.translit:
vaadhaivan dhadara vaanamum nilanum malaikaLum alaikadal kuLippa
meedhukoN dukaLum meenuru vaaki viripunal variyakat toLiththOn
pOdhalar punnai mallikai mauval pudhuvirai madhumala raNaindhu
seedhavoN thenRal thisaithoRum kamazhum thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
805.meaning: When affliction came pressing and the heavens, the earth, the mountains and the surging seas were submerged, he took the form of a fish leaping above the waters, and hid (the worlds) within his striped belly amid the spreading flood. He stands in Tirukkannankudi, where the cool, gentle breeze, having touched the fresh fragrant honeyed blossoms of punnai, jasmine and mauval, wafts its fragrance in every direction.

806.translit:
venRisEr thiNmai vilangalmaa mEni veLLeyiR RoLLerith thaRukaN
panRiyaay anRu paarmakaL payalai theerththavan panjavar paakan
onRalaa vuruvath thulappilpal kaalaththu uyarkodi yoLivaLar madhiyam,
senRusEr senniis sikaranan maadath thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
806.meaning: With victorious strength and a great mountain-like body, white tusks, blazing fierce eyes, he became the Boar (Varaha) that day and removed the pallor (sorrow) of the Earth-maiden; he is the charioteer of the five Pandavas. He of countless forms, beyond all ages, stands in Tirukkannankudi whose tall banners and the radiant rising moon reach and touch the peaks of fine mansions.

807.translit:
mannavan periya vELviyil kuRaLaay moovadi neerodum koNdu
pinnumE zhulakum eeradi yaakap perundhisai yadangida nimirndhOn
annamen kamalath thaNimalarp peedath thalaipuna lilaikkudai neezhal
senneloN kavari yasaiveeR Rirukkum thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
807.meaning: At the great sacrifice of king (Mahabali), as a dwarf he received three paces of land along with the water of donation, then grew tall so that the seven worlds and all the vast quarters were spanned in two strides. He sits enthroned in Tirukkannankudi upon the beautiful lotus seat soft as a swan, beneath the canopy of leaves and water, with the white chowries of fine paddy swaying.

808.translit:
mazhuvinaal avani arasaimoo vezhukaal maNimudi podipaduththu udhirak
kuzhuvuvaar punaluL kuLiththuveng kOpam thavirndhavan, kulaimali kadhalik
kuzhuvumvaar kamukum kuravumnaR palavum kuLirtharu soodhammaa thaviyum
sezhumaiyaar pozhilkaL thazhuvunan maadath thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
808.meaning: With his axe he ground to dust the jeweled crowns of the kings of the earth thrice seven times, and bathing in the gathered streams of (their) blood, abated his fierce wrath (as Parasurama). He stands in Tirukkannankudi, whose fine mansions are embraced by lush groves of clustered plantains, tall areca palms, kuravu, fine jackfruit, cooling mango and madhavi creepers.

809.translit:
vaanuLaa ravarai valimaiyaal naliyum malikadal ilangaiyaar kOnai
paanusEr saraththaal panangkani pOlap parumudi yudhiravil vaLaiththOn
kaanulaa mayilin kaNangaLnin Raadak kaNamukil murasanin Radhira
thEnulaa varivaN dinnisai muralum thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
809.meaning: Bending his bow, he loosed sun-bright arrows so that the great heads of the Lord of sea-girt Lanka — who tormented the dwellers of heaven by his might — fell like palmyra fruits. He stands in Tirukkannankudi, where flocks of forest peacocks stand and dance, dense clouds resound like drums, and striped bees roaming for honey hum their sweet music.

810.translit:
aravuneeL kodiyOn avaiyuLaa sanaththai anjidaa dhEyida, adhaRkup
periyamaa mEni yaNdamoo duruvap perundhisai yadangida nimirndhOn
varaiyinmaa maNiyum marakathath thiraLum vayiramum vedhirudhir muththum
thiraikoNarn dhundhi vayalthoRum kuvikkum thirukkaNNang kudiyuL nin RaanE.
810.meaning: When the one of the long serpent-banner (Duryodhana) fearlessly offered (Krishna) a seat in his assembly, for that (he scorned it and) grew so that his great vast body pierced through the cosmic egg and spanned all the wide quarters. He stands in Tirukkannankudi, where the waves bring and heap up in every field the great gems of the mountains, masses of emerald, diamonds, and pearls dropped from the bamboo.

811.translit:
panniya paaram paarmakat kozhiyap paaratha maaperum pOril
mannarkaL madiya maNinedun thiNdEr maiththunark kuyththamaa maayan
thunnumaa thaviyum surapunaip pozhilum soozhndhezhu seNpaka malarvaay
thennaven RaLikaL muranRisai paadum thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin RaanE.
811.meaning: So that the spread burden (of the earth) might be lifted from the Earth-maiden, in the great Bharata war he drove the strong, tall jeweled chariot for his brother-in-law (Arjuna) while the kings were slain — that great wondrous one (Maya). He stands in Tirukkannankudi, where in the mouths of the senbaka blossoms, encircled by dense madhavi creepers and fragrant kura groves, the bees hum 'thenna' and sing tunefully.

812.translit:
kalaiyulaaval kul kaarikai thiRattuk kadalperum padaiyodum senRu
silaiyinaal ilangai theeyezhach chetra thirukkaNNang kudiyuLnin Raanai
malaikulaa maada mangaiyar thalaivan maanavEl kaliyanvaa yoligaL
ulavusol maalai yonpadhO donRum vallavark killainal kuravE.
812.meaning: For the sake of the lady of the garment-adorned waist (Sita), he went forth with a great army across the sea and, with his bow, burned Lanka to ashes — the Lord who stands in Tirukkannankudi. For those able in these ten verses, the flowing garland of words resounding from the mouth of Kaliyan, chief of Tirumangai with its mountain-like mansions, wielder of the mighty spear, there shall be no poverty.

813.translit:
ponnivar mEni maraka thaththin pongkiLanj sOdhi yakalaththaaram min
ivar vaayilnal vEdha mOdhum vEdhiyar vaanava raavarthOzhee,
ennaiyum nOkkiyen nalkulum nOkki Endhilang kongkaiyum nOkkukinRaar
annaiyen nOkkumen Ranju kinREn achchO oruvar azhakiyavaa.
813.meaning: His body shines like gold, with a surging tender radiance like emerald, and a garland (of lightning-like brightness) upon his broad chest. From his mouth the noble Vedas are chanted; he must be a celestial, O friend! He looks at me, looks at my waist, looks at my raised young breasts — and I dread what my mother will say. Ah! how beautiful is this one!

814.translit:
thOdavizh neelam maNangko dukkum soozhpunal soozhkudan thaikkidandha
sEdarko lenRu therikka maattEn senjsuda raazhiyum sangumEndhi
paadaka melladi yaarva Nangap panmaNi muththodi langusOdhi
aadakam pooNdoru naangu thOLum achchO oruvar azhakiyavaa.
814.meaning: I cannot tell whether he is the great One who reclines at Kudandai (Tirukkudandai), girded by waters where the petal-loosened blue nelumbo flowers spread fragrance. Holding the red-rayed discus and the conch, while ladies of soft anklet-clad feet worship, with radiant gold ornaments set with many gems and pearls shining upon all four shoulders — Ah! how beautiful is this one!

815.translit:
vEyirunj sOlai vilangal soozhndha meyya maNaaLar iv vaiyamellaam
thaayina naayaka raavar thOzhee thaamaraik kaNkaL irundhavaaRu,
sEyirung kunRam thikazhndha thoppach cheviya vaaki malarndhasOdhi
aayiram thOLo dilangu pooNum achchO oruvar azhakiyavaa.
815.meaning: He is the Bridegroom of Meyyam, encircled by mountains and groves dense with bamboo; he is the Lord who measured all this world, O friend! See how his lotus eyes are — radiant and red, blossomed like a great glowing distant hill. With a thousand shoulders and shining ornaments — Ah! how beautiful is this one!

816.translit:
vambavi zhumthuzhaay maalai thOLmEl kaiyana aazhiyum sangum Endhi,
nambarnam millam pukundhu ninRaar naakari karperi dhumiLaiyar
sembava Lamivar vaayin vaNNam thEva rivara dhuruvamsollil
ampava Laththira LEyu moppar achchO oruvar azhakiyavaa.
816.meaning: Wearing on his shoulders a garland of fragrance-bursting tulasi, holding in his hands the discus and the conch, the dear One entered and stood within our home; he is most refined and very youthful. The color of his mouth is like red coral; if I describe the form of this celestial, he is like a mass of beautiful coral. Ah! how beautiful is this one!

817.translit:
kOzhiyum koodalum kOyil koNda kOvala rEyoppar kunRamanna
paazhiyum thOLumOr naangu daiyar paNdivar thammaiyum kaNdaRiyOm
vaazhiya rOvivar vaNNa meNNil maakadal pOnRuLar kaiyilveyya,
aazhiyon REndhiyOr sangu paRRi achchOvoruvarazhakiyavaa.
817.meaning: He is like the Cowherd-Lord who has taken as his shrines Kozhi (Uraiyur) and Koodal (Madurai); he has mountain-like mighty strength and four shoulders. We have never before seen this one. Long may he live! If one reckons his hue, he is like the great ocean; in his hand bearing one fierce discus and grasping a conch — Ah! how beautiful is this one!

818.translit:
venjsina vEzham maruppo siththa vEndharkol Endhizhai yaarmanaththai
thanjsudai yaaLarkol yaana RiyEn thaamaraik kaNka LirundhavaaRu
kanjsanai yanjsamun kaalvi saiththa kaaLaiyaa ravarkaN daarvaNangum
anjsana maamalai yEyu moppar achchOvoruvarazhakiyavaa.
818.meaning: Is he the King who broke the tusk of the fierce, furious elephant (Kuvalayapida)? Is he the One who holds in safekeeping the hearts of the jewel-adorned ladies? I do not know — see how his lotus eyes are! He is the youthful One who kicked out his foot of old so that Kamsa was struck with fear; he is like a great mountain of dark collyrium that those who behold worship. Ah! how beautiful is this one!

819.translit:
piNiyavizh thaamarai mottalarththum pEraru LaaLarkol? yaanaRiyEn,
paNiyumen nenjami dhenkol thOzhee paNdivar thammaiyum kaNdaRiyOm
aNikezhu thaamarai yanna kaNNum angaiyum pangaya mEnivaanaththu,
aNikezhu maamuki lEyu moppar achchOvoruvarazhakiyavaa.
819.meaning: Is he the One of great grace who makes the bound buds of the lotus blossom open? I do not know! Why does my heart bow down to him, O friend? We have never before seen this one. With eyes and beautiful hands like the lovely lotus, and a body (dark) — he is like the beautiful great cloud adorning the sky. Ah! how beautiful is this one!

820.translit:
mañcuyar maamadhi theeNda neeNda maaliruñ choolaima Naa</br>aLarvandhu en
neñcuLLum kaNNuLLum ninRu neeNgaar neermalai yaarkol? ninaikkamaaTTeen?
mañcuyar ponmalai meele zhundha maamukil poonRuLar vandhukaaNeer
añciRaip puLLumon ReeRi vandhaar acchoovoru varazhakiyavaa.
820.meaning: The bridegroom of vast Maaliruncholai (Azhagar Koyil), so lofty that the high clouds and the great moon touch it, has come and stands within my heart and within my eyes and will not depart. Who is he of Neermalai? I cannot fathom it. He is like a great cloud that has risen over a golden mountain whose peaks pierce the clouds; come and behold him. He came riding upon a bird with beautiful wings -- ah, how exceedingly beautiful is this one!

821.translit:
eNNdisai yumeRi neerkka dalum eezhula kumuda neevizhungi
maNdiyoo raalilaip paLLi koLLum maayarkol? maayam aRiyamaaTTeen
koNdalnan maalvarai yeeyu moppar kongalar thaamaraik kaNNumvaayum
aNdath thamarar paNiya ninRaar acchoovoruvarazhakiyavaa.
821.meaning: Is he the wondrous one who, at the deluge, swallowed up the eight directions, the surging sea, and the seven worlds all at once, and then lay in repose upon a single banyan leaf? I cannot comprehend his magic. He resembles a dark rain-cloud and a great noble mountain; his eyes and mouth are like blossoming honey-laden lotuses. He stood while the celestials of the heavens bowed before him -- ah, how exceedingly beautiful is this one!

822.translit:
annamum keezhalum meenu maaya aadhiyai naakai yazhakiyaarai
kanninan maamadhiL mangai veendhan kaamaru seerkkali kanRi kunRaa
innisai yaalsonna señcol maalai eezhu miraNdumo ronRumvallaar,
mannava raayula kaaNdu meeNdum vaanava raaymakizh veydhuvaree.
822.meaning: This is the garland of true and righteous words, sung in undiminished sweet melody by Kaliyan (Tirumangai Alwar), the king of Mangai with its strong, fair, great ramparts, and of abundant beauty -- about the Primordial Lord who became the swan, the boar, and the fish, the beautiful Lord of Nagai. Those who can master these ten verses -- the seven and the two and the one -- shall rule the earth as kings, and then, becoming celestials, shall attain bliss in heaven.

823.translit:
thannai naivik kileenval vinaiyeen thozhudhumezhu
ponnai naivikkum appooñ cherundhi maNineezhalvaay
ennainai vitthezhal koNdakan Raperumaanidam,
punnaimuth thampozhil sUzhndhazha kaaya pullaaNiyee.
823.meaning: Of great cruel deeds though I am, I will not let myself suffer; let us worship and arise, O heart. In the jeweled shade of that lovely cherundhi tree whose blossoms shame even gold, the Lord made me waste away with longing and then departed -- his abode is Pullaani, lovely and surrounded by groves where the punnai trees bear pearl-like blooms.

824.translit:
uruki neñcee ninaindhing kirundhen thozhudhumezhu
murukuvaN duNmalark kaidhaiyin neezhalil munnorunaaL,
perukukaa dhanmai yennuLLa meydhap pirindhaanidam
porudhumun neerkkaraik keemaNi yundhu pullaaNiyee.
824.meaning: What use is it to remain here melting and brooding, O heart? Let us worship and arise. In the shade of the fragrant screw-pine where bees drink the nectar, on a day long ago, when overflowing love had filled my whole being, he parted from me -- his abode is Pullaani, where the battling waves of the deep sea wash jewels onto the shore.

825.translit:
eedhu seydhaal maRakkeen manamee thozhudhumezhu
thaadhu malku thadañcUzh pozhildhaazhvar thodarndhu pin
peedhai ninaip piriyee niniyen RakanRaanidam poodhu
naaLung kamazhum pozhilsUzhndha pullaaNiyee.
825.meaning: What could he do that would make me forget him, O heart? Let us worship and arise. Following after me through groves girdled with broad pollen-laden ponds, he said, "O innocent girl, I will not part from you henceforth," and yet he departed -- his abode is Pullaani, surrounded by groves whose flowers are fragrant every day.

826.translit:
konguN vaNdee kariyaaka vandhaan kodiyeeRku mun
nanga Leesan namakkee paNitha mozhiseydhilan
mangai nallaay thozhudhu mezhupoo yavan mannumUr
pongu munneerk karaikkee maNiyundhu pullaaNiyee.
826.meaning: Our Lord came before cruel me with only the honey-sipping bee as witness, yet he did not keep the word he had pledged to me alone. O good lady, let us worship and arise and go; the town where he abides is Pullaani, where the surging deep sea washes jewels upon the shore.

827.translit:
uNari luLLam sudumaal vinaiyeen thozhudhumezhu
thuNari naazhal naRumpoodhu namsUzh kuzhalpeydhu pin
thaNari laavi thaLarumena anbu thandhaanidam,
puNari yoodham paNilam maNiyundhu pullaaNiyee.
827.meaning: When I recall him, my heart burns within me, sinful that I am; let us worship and arise. Placing the fragrant cluster of cherundhi blossoms upon my braided hair, he gave me his love, saying my life would wither if he were parted from me -- his abode is Pullaani, where the surging tide casts conch-shells and gems upon the shore.

828.translit:
eLki neñcee ninaindhing kirundhen thozhudhumezhu
vaLLal maayan maNivaNNa nemmaan maruvumidam
kaLLa vizhummalark kaaviyum thUmadaRkaidhaiyum,
puLLu maLLal pazhanang kaLumsUzhndha pullaaNiyee.
828.meaning: What use is it to stay here disheartened and brooding, O heart? Let us worship and arise. The abode where our generous, wondrous, sapphire-hued Lord dwells is Pullaani, surrounded by water-lily blossoms dripping with honey, by pure-petaled screw-pines, by birds, and by muddy paddy-fields.

829.translit:
paravi neñcee thozhudhum ezhupoo yavanpaalamaay
iravum naaLum inikkaN thuyilaa thirundhenpayan?
viravi muttham neduveN maNalmeel koNdu veNthirai
puravi yennap pudhañceydhu vandhundhu pullaaNiyee.
829.meaning: Let us praise him, O heart, and worship and arise and go to him; what is gained by lying awake without sleep, night and day? The town where he abides is Pullaani, where the white waves, mingling pearls, scatter them upon the long white sands and come rushing forward leaping like horses.

830.translit:
alamu maazhip padaiyu mudaiyaar namakkanbaraay,
salama dhaakith thakavon Rilarnaam thozhudhumezhu,
ulavu kaalnal kazhiyoongu thaNpaim pozhilUdu isai
pulavu kaanal kaLivaN dinampaadu pullaaNiyee.
830.meaning: He who bears the plough and the discus, though loving toward us, has become deceitful and shows no grace; nevertheless let us worship and arise. His town is Pullaani, where through the cool green groves that rise by the breeze-swept backwaters the swarms of joyous bees sing their music in the fish-scented seaside groves.

831.translit:
oodhi naamam kuLitthucchi thannaal oLimaamalarp
paadham naaLum paNivoom namakkee nalamaadhalin
aadhu thaaraa nenilum tharum anRiyumanbaraayp
poodhum maadhee thozhudhum avanmannu pullaaNiyee.
831.meaning: Reciting his name and bathing, with our heads we shall daily bow at his radiant lotus-like feet, for this is our true good. Even if he gives us nothing, yet he will give; and besides, with love, let us go, O lady, and worship Pullaani where he abides.

832.translit:
ilangu mutthum pavaLak kozhundhu mezhildhaamarai
pulangaL muRRum pozhilsUzhndh azhakaaya pullaaNimeel
kalanga lillaap pukazhaan kaliya nolimaalai
valangoL thoNdark kidamaa vadhupaadil vaikundhamee.
832.meaning: This is the resonant garland of verses by Kaliyan of untroubled, flawless fame, set upon Pullaani -- beautiful and girt with groves, with gleaming pearls, coral shoots, and lovely lotuses across all its fields. For the devotees who hold it dear, if they sing it, the very abode it grants becomes Vaikuntha (the supreme heaven).

833.translit:
kaavaar madalpeNNai anRil arikuralum
eevaayi nUdiyangum eqkil kodidhaaloo
pUvaar maNamkamazhum pullaaNi kaidhozhudheen
paavaay idhunamakkoor paanmaiyee yaakaadhee.
833.meaning: The piteous cry of the anril bird upon the leafy palmyra in the grove is sharper and crueler than a spear that pierces the mark of its target. With my hands I have worshipped Pullaani, fragrant with the scent of full-blown flowers. O doll-like maiden, is this not become a settled fate for us?

834.translit:
munnam kuRaLuruvaay mUvadimaN koNdaLandha,
mannan saridhaikkee maalaakip ponpayandheen
ponnam kazhikkaanal puLLinangaaL pullaaNi
annamaay nUlpayandhaaR kaangidhanaic cheppuminee.
834.meaning: Enthralled by the deeds of the King who long ago took the form of a dwarf, begged three feet of earth, and measured the worlds, I have grown pale and golden with pining. O flocks of birds in the seaside groves by the golden backwaters, go to Pullaani and tell this of mine to him who, as the swan, brought forth the Vedic scriptures.

835.translit:
vavvith thuzhaayadhanmeel senRa thanineñcam
sevvi yaRiyaadhu niRkungkol nitthilangaL
pavvath thiraiyulavu pullaaNi kaidhozhudheen
theyvac chilaiyaaRken sindhainooy seppuminee.
835.meaning: My lonely heart, seized and gone after his tulasi garland -- will it stand there, not knowing the right moment to return? With my hands I have worshipped Pullaani, where the waves of the sea roll pearls about. O tell to him of the divine bow the sickness of my mind.

836.translit:
pariya iraNiyan thaakam aNiyukiraal
ariyuruvaayk keeNdaan aruLthandha vaanamakku
porudhiraikaL poondhulavu pullaaNi kaidhozhudheen
arimalarkkaN Neertthadhumba andhukilum nillaavee.
836.meaning: He who, taking the form of the man-lion, tore open with his beautiful claws the broad chest of Hiranya -- such is the grace he has shown us! With my hands I have worshipped Pullaani, where the battling waves come surging and rolling. The tears brim over from my lotus eyes, and even my fair garment will not stay upon me.

837.translit:
villaal ilangai malangac charandhurandha
vallaaLan pinpoona neñcam varumaLavum,
ellaaru menRannai yeesilum peesidinum,
pullaaNi yemperumaan poykeeT TirundheenE.
837.meaning: Until my heart, which has gone after the mighty hero who with his bow shot arrows and routed Lanka into confusion, comes back to me -- even though everyone reviles me and speaks ill of me -- I have stayed on, having listened to the false promise of my Lord of Pullaani.

838.translit:
suzhanRilangu vengadhiroon theeroodum pooymaRaindhaan
azhanRu kodidhaaki añcudaril thaanadumaal
sezhundhadampUñ choolaisUzh pullaaNi kaidhozhudheen
izhandhirundhee nenRan ezhilniRamum sangumee.
838.meaning: The whirling, blazing fierce-rayed sun has gone with his chariot and set; now the beautiful evening lamplight, grown fierce and cruel, burns and torments me. With my hands I have worshipped Pullaani, girt with rich, broad, flowering groves. I have been left bereft of my lovely complexion and of my bangles.

839.translit:
kanaiyaar idikuralin kaarmaNiyin naavaadal
thinaiyeenum nillaadhu theeyiR kodidhaaloo punaiyaar
maNimaadap pullaaNi kaidhozhudheen
vinaiyeenmeel veelaiyum vendhazhalee veesumee.
839.meaning: The clapper-tongue of the dark bell, sounding like the booming roll of thunder, will not stay still even for an instant, and is crueler than fire. With my hands I have worshipped Pullaani of jeweled mansions adorned with gems. Yet even the sea casts burning flames upon me, woman of evil fate that I am.

840.translit:
thoombudaik kai vEzham veruva maruppositta
paambin aNaiyaan aruL thandhavaa namakku
poonjcherundhi ponsoriyum pullaaNi kaithozhudhEn
thEmbal iLampiRaiyum endhanakkOr vendhazhalE.
840.meaning: The Lord who reclines upon the serpent-couch, He who once snapped the tusk of the elephant so that its hollow-trunked might tremble in terror — what grace has He shown to us! I worshipped with folded hands at Pullani, where the flowering cherundhi tree scatters its golden blossoms. Yet the slender young crescent moon has become for me a single scorching flame.

841.translit:
vEdhamum vELviyum viNNum irusudarum,
aadhiyum aanaan aruLthandhavaa namakku,
pOdhalarum punnaisoozh pullaaNi kaithozhudhEn,
Odhamum naanum uRangaadhu irundhEnE.
841.meaning: He who became the Vedas, the sacrifices, the heavens, the two luminaries (sun and moon), and the primal cause of all — what grace has He shown to us! I worshipped with folded hands at Pullani, encircled by punnai trees bursting into bloom. Both the surging sea-flood and I remained sleepless through the night.

842.translit:
ponnalarum punnaisoozh pullaaNi ammaanai
minnidaiyaar vEtkainOy koora irundhadhanai
kannavilum thiNdOL kaliyan olivallaar
mannavaraay maNNaaNdu vaanaadu munnuvarE.
