842.meaning: Concerning the Lord of Pullani, surrounded by punnai trees bursting into golden bloom, and how lightning-waisted women suffer as the malady of longing love grows acute in them — those who can master the melodious verses of Kaliyan of strong mountain-like shoulders shall reign as kings, rule the earth, and thereafter attain the celestial world.

843.translit:
thavaLa iLampiRai thuLLumunneerth thaNmalarth thenRalODu anRilonRith
thuvaLa en nenjakam sOravErum soozhpani naaL thuyilaadhiruppEn
ivaLumOr peNkodi enRirangaar en nalam aindhum munkoNdupOna
kuvaLai malarniRa vaNNarmannu kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
843.meaning: The white young crescent, the cool-flowered southerly breeze upon the leaping ocean, joined with the cry of the anril bird — all assail my heart till it withers and faints away, and on these mist-laden nights I lie sleepless. He does not feel any pity, thinking 'she too is but a tender creeper of a maiden,' but seized away all the fivefold beauty of me. O carry me away to Kurunkudi, the abiding place of the Lord whose hue is that of the blue kuvalai blossom!

844.translit:
thaadhavizh mallikai pullivandha thaNmadhiyin iLavaadaiyinnE
oodhai thiridhandhu uzhaRiyuNNa Oriravum uRangEn uRangum
pEdhaiyar pEdhaimaiyaal irundhu pEsilum pEsuka peyvaLaiyaar
kOdhai naRumalar mangaimaarvan kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
844.meaning: The cold northern breeze, which has embraced the pollen-laden jasmine, now under this cool moonlight whirls and wanders about to devour me, and so through the whole night I do not sleep. Let the foolish women adorned with bangles, in their foolishness, speak whatever they will — O carry me away to Kurunkudi, the place of the Lord upon whose chest dwells the Lady of fragrant blossom-garlands!

845.translit:
kaalaiyum maalaiyoththu uNdu kangul naazhikai oozhiyin neeNdulaavum,
pOlvadhOr thanmai pugundhuniRkum pongazhalE okkum vaadaisolli
maalavan maamaNi vaNNanmaayam matRum uLavavai vandhidaamun kOla
mayilpayilum puRavil kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
845.meaning: Morning has become like evening, and the hours of the night stretch out wandering like aeons — such a state has come over me; the chill northern wind feels like a surging fire. Before the spells of the dark Lord of sapphire hue, and whatever other torments there be, come upon me, O carry me away to Kurunkudi in the grove where lovely peacocks throng!

846.translit:
karumaNi pooNdu veNNaakaNaindhu kaarimilEtRu aNar thaazhndhulaavum
orumaNi yOsaiyen uLLandhaLLa Oriravum uRangaadhiruppEn
perumaNi vaanavar uchchivaiththa pEraruLaaLan perumaipEsi
kurumaNi neerkozhikkum puRavil kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
846.meaning: The single bell that sways from the drooping dewlap of the dark-maned bull, decked with black beads and clinging white ones — the sound of that bell jolts my heart, and through the whole night I lie sleepless. Telling of the greatness of the Lord of vast grace, whom the celestials set as a great jewel upon their heads, O carry me away to Kurunkudi in the grove where the waters wash up gleaming bright gems!

847.translit:
thiNdimilEtRin maNiyum aayan theenkuzhal Osaiyum thenRalODu
koNdadhOr maalaiyum andhiyeenRa kOla iLampiRaiyOdu koodi
paNdaiya allavivai namakkup paaviyEn aaviyai vaattanjcheyyum
koNdal maNiniRa vaNNarmannu kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
847.meaning: The bell of the sturdy-humped bull, the sound of the cowherd's sweet flute, the evening that has gathered with the southerly breeze, joined with the lovely young crescent born of twilight — these are not what they were before; they torment the very life of sinful me. O carry me away to Kurunkudi, the abiding place of the Lord whose hue is that of the dark sapphire rain-cloud!

848.translit:
elliyum nanpaga lum irundhE Esilum Esuka Endhizhaiyaar
nallar avarthiRam naamaRiyOm naaNmadam achcham namakkingillai
vallana solli magizhvarEnum maamaNi vaNNarai naam maRavOm
kollai vaLariLa mullaipulgu kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
848.meaning: Through both night and bright day let the well-adorned women sit and revile me as they please — we do not know their ways; modesty, reserve, and fear are no longer ours in this state. Though they delight in speaking whatever sharp things they can, we will never forget the Lord of great sapphire hue. O carry me away to Kurunkudi, where the tender young jasmine of the field clings and grows!

849.translit:
sengaN nediya kariyamEnith thEvar oruvaringE pugundhu en
angam meliya vaLaikazhala aadhukolO enRu sonnapinnai
aingaNai villidhan aaNmaiyennOdu aadum adhanai aRiyamaattEn
kongalar thaNpaNai soozhpuRavil kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
849.meaning: A certain Lord, red-eyed and tall, of dark-hued body, having entered here, said 'What may this be?' as my body grew thin and my bangles slipped loose — and after that, I cannot fathom how the five-arrowed archer (Manmatha) plays his manly assault upon me. O carry me away to Kurunkudi, in the grove encircled by cool fields where fragrant blossoms open!

850.translit:
kEvalam anRu kadalinOsai kENminkaL aayankai aambalvandhu en
aavi yaLavum aNaindhuniRkum anRiyum aindhu kaNaiterindhittu
Evalangkaatti ivanoruvan ippadiyE pugundhu eydhidaamun
kOvalar koothan kuRippaRindhu kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
850.meaning: The roar of the sea is no small thing — listen, all of you; and the cowherd's flute-music comes and presses right up to my very life-breath. Moreover, before this one (Manmatha) selects his five arrows, displays his deadly skill, and entering thus strikes me down, O understand the heart of the cowherd-dancer (Krishna) and carry me away to Kurunkudi!

851.translit:
sOththena ninRu thozhavirangaan thonnalam koNdenakku inRukaaRum
pOrppadhOr poRpadam thandhupOnaan pOyina ooraRiyEn enkongai
mooththidukinRana matravanRan moyyakalam aNaiyaadhu vaaLaa
koothan imaiyavar kOnvirumbum kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
851.meaning: Though I stood crying 'Hail!' and worshipped, He showed no pity; He took away my long-held beauty and, leaving me one fine golden garment to cover myself, departed — and I do not know the town to which He has gone. My breasts grow old in vain, unembraced by His broad and lovely chest. O carry me away to Kurunkudi, which the Dancer, the King of the celestials, holds dear!

852.translit:
setRavan thennilangai malangath thEvarpiraan thirumaamagaLaip petRum
en nenjakam kOyilkoNda pEraruLaaLan perumaipEsak
katravan kaamaru seerkkaliyan kaNNagaththum manaththum agalaak
kotravan mutrula gaaLininRa kuRunkudikkE ennai uyththidumin.
852.meaning: He who destroyed the foe, throwing southern Lanka into ruin, the Lord of the celestials — though He won the great Lady Lakshmi, He yet took my heart as His temple, this Lord of boundless grace. Kaliyan of lovely renown, who is learned in singing His greatness, holds Him who never departs from his eye and his mind. O carry me away to Kurunkudi, where stands the Victor who rules over all the worlds!

853.translit:
akkum puliyin adhaLum udaiyaar avaroruvar,
pakkam niRka ninRa paNbar oorpOlum
thakka maraththin thaazhsinaiyERi, thaayvaayil
kokkin piLLai veLLil uNNum kuRunkudiyE.
853.meaning: He of gracious nature, beside whom there stands one (Shiva) who wears bones and a tiger's skin — this seems to be His town. It is Kurunkudi, where the heron's chick climbs the low branch of a fitting tree and eats the wood-apple from its mother's beak.

854.translit:
thungaaraaravaththiraivandhu ulavath thodukadaluL,
pongaaraaravil thuyilum punithar oorpOlum,
sengaal annam thigazhthaN paNaiyil pedaiyOdum,
kongaar kamalath thalaril sErum kuRunkudiyE.
854.meaning: He, the pure one, who sleeps amid the resounding garland-couch within the deep ocean, where towering, roaring waves come and roll — this seems to be His town. It is Kurunkudi, where the red-legged swan together with its mate nestles in the pollen-rich, opened lotus blossoms of the gleaming cool fields.

855.translit:
vaazhak kaNdOm vandhu kaaNmin thoNdeerkaaL,
kEzhal sengaN maamugil vaNNar maruvumoor,
Ezhaich chengaal indhuNai naaraikku iraithEdi,
koozhaip paarvaik kaarvayal mEyum kuRunkudiyE.
855.meaning: We have seen the way to live — come and behold it, O devotees! This is the town where the Lord, of the form of the boar (Varaha), red-eyed and dark as a great cloud, abides. It is Kurunkudi, where, seeking food for its loving mate, the slender red-legged stork forages with downcast gaze in the dark, fertile fields.

856.translit:
siramunnaindhumaindhum sindhach chenRu, arakkan
uramum karamum thuNiththa uravOn oorpOlum,
iravum pagalum eendhEn mural, manRellaam
kuravin poovE thaanmaNam naaRum kuRunkudiyE.
856.meaning: He, the mighty one, who went and severed the demon's (Ravana's) ten heads — five and five — to fall, along with his chest and his arms — this seems to be His town. It is Kurunkudi, where night and day the bees hum over the sweet honey, and throughout every common-square the very blossoms of the kura tree spread their fragrance.

857.translit:
kavvaik kaLitru mannar maaLak kalimaanthEr
aivarkkaay, anRu amaril uyththaan oorpOlum,
maivaiththilangu kaNNaar thangaL mozhiyoppaan,
kovvaik kanivaayk kiLLai pEsum kuRunkudiyE.
857.meaning: He who, on that day, drove the swift-horsed chariot in battle for the sake of the five (Pandavas), so that the kings with their tumultuous war-elephants perished — this seems to be His town. It is Kurunkudi, where the parrot with its mouth like the ripe kovvai fruit speaks in tones resembling the words of women whose eyes shine darkened with collyrium.

858.translit:
theeneer vaNNa maamalar koNdu viraiyEndhi,
tooneer paravith thozhumin ezhumin thoNdeerkaaL,
maaneer vaNNan maruvi uRaiyum idam vaanil
kooneer madhiyai maadam theeNdum kuRunkudiyE.
858.meaning: Taking great fragrant flowers and holding pure waters, scatter and worship — arise, O devotees! The place where the Lord of the hue of the vast ocean dwells in union is Kurunkudi, whose mansions reach up and touch the bent, watery crescent moon in the sky.

859.translit:
vallich chiRunuN Nidaiyaar idaineer vaikkinRa,
allal sindhai thavira adaiminadiyeerkaaL
sollil thiruvE anaiyaar kanivaay eyiRoppaan,
kollai mullai melarumbu eenum kuRunkudiyE.
859.meaning: To be rid of the anguish of heart that you place upon women of slender, creeper-like fine waists, draw near, O devotees! It is Kurunkudi, where the field-jasmine puts forth its soft buds resembling the teeth set in the ripe-red mouths of women who are like Lakshmi herself in speech.

860.translit:
naaraar iNdai naaNmalar koNdu nam thamarkaaL,
aaraa anpoo dempemaanoor adaiminkaL,
thaaraavaarum vaarpunal meeyndhu vayalvaazhum koorvaay
naarai peedaiyo daadum kuRunkudiyee.
860.meaning: O our devoted ones! Taking fresh blossoms of the day, woven into garlands, go and reach the abode of our Lord with insatiable love. That place is Kurunkudi, where in the long flowing waters the ducks graze and live among the fields, and the sharp-beaked herons sport with their mates.

861.translit:
ninRa vinaiyum thuyarum keda maa malareendhi,
senRu paNimi nezhumin thozhumin thoNdeerkaaL,
enRum miravum pakalum vaNdu isaipaada,
kunRin mullai manRidai naaRum kuRunkudiyee.
861.meaning: O devotees! To destroy the deeds that cling to you and your sorrow, holding up great blossoms, go and worship, rise up and adore. That place is Kurunkudi, where always, by night and by day, the bees sing their music, and the jasmine of the hillside spreads its fragrance through the common ground.

862.translit:
silaiyaal ilankai seRRaan maRRoor sinaveezham,
kolaiyaar kombu koNdaan meeya kuRunkudimeel,
kalaiyaar panuval vallaan kaliya nolimaalai
nilaiyaar paadal paadap paavam nillaavee.
862.meaning: He who with His bow destroyed Lanka, and who seized the killing tusk of a furious elephant, dwells at Kurunkudi. If one sings the enduring songs of this resounding garland of verses by Kaliyan, who is master of learned poetry, sin will not stay.

863.translit:
thandhaithaay makkaLee suRRamen RuRRuvar paRRi ninRa
pandhamaar vaazhkkaiyai nondhunee pazhiyenak karudhi naayeel
andhamaa yaadhiyaay aadhikkum aadhiyaay aaya naaya
maindhanaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee!
863.meaning: Father, mother, children and kin — held fast by these, gripped by a bonded life, if grieving you have come to regard it as a thing of blame, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the youthful Lord who became the cowherd, who is the end, the beginning, and the very source of the source.

864.translit:
minnumaa valliyum vanjiyum venRanuN Nidainudankum,
annamen nadaiyinaar kalaviyai aruvarudh thanji naayeel,
thunnumaa maNimudip panjavark kaakimun thoodhu senRa
mannanaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
864.meaning: If you have grown weary and afraid of the embrace of women whose slender waists sway, defeating the lightning-like creeper and the vanji vine, women of soft swan-like gait — then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the King who, of old, went as an envoy for the five Pandavas of the close-set jeweled crowns.

865.translit:
pooNulaa menmulaip paavaimaar poyyinai meyyi thenRu,
peeNuvaar peesumap peechchainee pizhaiyenak karudhi naayeel
neeNilaa veNkudai vaaNanaar veeLviyil maNNi randha
maaNiyaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
865.meaning: If you have come to regard as error the talk that those cherishing women speak — women of soft breasts adorned with ornaments — taking their falsehood for truth, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the celibate boy (Vamana) who begged for the earth at the sacrifice of Banasura of the long-shadowed white parasol.

866.translit:
paNNulaam menmozhip paavaimaar paNaimulai aNaidhum naam enRu,
eNNuvaar eNNama dozhiththunee pizhaitthuyyak karudhi naayeel,
viNNuLaar viNNinmee dhiyanRa veenkadatthuLaar vaLankoL munneer
vaNNanaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
866.meaning: Setting aside the thoughts of those who think, 'We shall embrace the full breasts of women of soft melodious speech,' if you have resolved to escape and be saved, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the Lord colored like the bountiful ocean, who dwells in Venkatam set high above the heavens, where the celestials abide.

867.translit:
manjuthooy veNkudai mannaraay vaaraNam soozha vaazhndhaar
thunjinaa renbadhoor sollainee thuyarenak karudhi naayeel
nanjuthooy konkaimeel ankaivaay vaiththavaL naaLai yuNda
manjanaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
867.meaning: Those who lived as kings with cloud-touching white parasols, surrounded by elephants — if you regard as sorrow the saying that even they have perished, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the dark Lord who drank away the very life of her (Putana) who placed her poison-smeared breast at His lovely mouth.

868.translit:
uruvinaar piRavisseer oonpodhi narambuthool kurambai yuLpukku
aruvinooy seydhuninRu aivardhaam vaazhvadhaR kanji naayeel
thiruvinaar veedhanaan kaindhuthee veeLviyoo danka maaRum
maruvinaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
868.meaning: If you have grown afraid of the way the five senses live within, entering the hut of the body bound up with flesh, sinew and skin that brings forth birth, causing torment — then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the Lord whom the holy ones embrace with the four Vedas, the five fires of sacrifice, and the six auxiliary sciences.

869.translit:
nooyelaam peydhadhoor aakkaiyai meyyenak koNdu vaaLaa
peeyardhaam peesumap peechchainee pizhaiyenak karudhi naayeel,
theeyalaa venkadhirth thinkaLaay mankulvaa naaki ninRa
maayanaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
869.meaning: If you have come to regard as error the talk that demented folk speak in vain, taking for truth this body that is the dwelling of every disease, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the wondrous Lord who stands as the fiery hot-rayed sun, the moon, and the cloud-filled sky.

870.translit:
manjuseer vaaneri neernilam kaalivai mayanki ninRa
anju seeraak kaiyai araNaman RenRuyyak karudhi naayeel,
sandhuseer menmulaip ponmalarp paavaiyum thaamum naaLum
vandhuseer vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
870.meaning: If, holding that this body composed of the mingled five elements — cloud-bearing sky, fire, water, earth and wind — is no true refuge, you have resolved to be saved, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh, where the Lord and the golden-blossom Lady of soft sandal-fragrant breasts come and abide each day.

871.translit:
veLLiyaar piNdiyaar poodhiyaar enRivar oodhu kinRa
kaLLanool thannaiyum karumaman RenRuyyak karudhi naayeel,
theLLiyaar kaithozhum theevanaar maamunee amudhu thandha,
vaLLalaar vallavaazh sollumaa vallaiyaay maruvu nenjee.
871.meaning: If, holding that the deceitful doctrines which these recite — the Sakyas (white-clad), the Jains of the asoka tree, and the Buddhists of the bodhi tree — are no true deeds, you have resolved to be saved, then, O my heart, draw near and become able to utter the name of Vallavaazh of the generous Lord, the God whom the clear-minded worship with folded hands, who gave the nectar from the great ocean.

872.translit:
maRaivalaar kuRaivilaar uRaiyumoor vallavaazhadikaL thammai,
siRaikulaa vaNdaRai soolaisoozh koolaneeL aali naadan
kaRaiyulaa veelvalla kaliyanvaay oliyivai kaRRu vallaar
iRaivaraay irunilam kaavalpooN dinpanan keydhu vaaree.
872.meaning: Concerning the Lord of Vallavaazh, the abode where masters of the Vedas free of all defect dwell, those who can learn this resounding utterance from the mouth of Kaliyan — lord of the lovely vast land of Aali ringed by groves where winged bees hum, wielder of the blood-stained spear — they shall become rulers, take up the guardianship of this wide earth, and attain abundant good and bliss.

873.translit:
mundhuRa vuraikkeen viraikkuzhal madavaar kalaviyai vidudhadu maaRal
andhara meezhum alaikada leezhum aayavem madikaLtham kooyil,
sandhodu maNiyum aNimayil thazhaiyum thazhuvivan daruvikaL nirandhu,
vandhizhi saaral maalirunj soolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
873.meaning: I shall say it first: do not waver in giving up the embrace of fair women of fragrant tresses. The temple of our Lord, who became the seven skies and the seven surging seas, is at Maalirumsolai — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come! Its slopes are where the cascades flow down in lines, embracing sandalwood, gems, and the lovely plumes of peacocks.

874.translit:
iNdaiyum punalum koNdidai yinRi ezhuminoo thozhudhum enRu imaiyoor
aNdarum parava aravaNaith thuyinRa sudarmudik kadavuLtham kooyil
viNdalar thooLi veeyvaLar puRavil viraimalark kuRinjiyin naRundheen
vaNdamar saaral maalirunj soolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
874.meaning: The temple of the radiant-crowned God who slept upon the serpent couch, while the celestials and the dwellers of the heavens praised Him saying 'Rise, let us worship without pause, bearing garlands and water,' is at Maalirumsolai — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come! Its slopes, where bees gather, are filled with the burst pollen-dust of flowers, the fragrant honey of the kurinji bloom in the bamboo-grown wild.

875.translit:
piNivaLar aakkai neenka nin Reeththap perunilam aruLinmun aruLi
aNivaLar kuRaLaay akalidam muzhudhum aLandhavem madikaLtham kooyil
kaNivaLar veenkai nedunila madhanil kuRavartham kavaNidaith thurandha
maNivaLar saaral maaliruncoolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
875.meaning: The temple of our Lord who, that the disease-ridden body might be cast off, graciously gave the great earth to those who stood and praised Him, and who as the beautiful dwarf measured the whole wide world, is at Maalirumsolai — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come! On its slopes where ripe-fruited venkai trees grow, gems abound, hurled from the slings of the hill-folk across the long stretch of land.

876.translit:
soormayi laaya peeymulai suvaiththuch sudusaram adusilaith thurandhu
neermaiyi laadha thaadakai maaLa ninaindhavar manamkoNda kooyil
kaarmali veenkai koonkalar puRavil kadimalar kuRinjiyin naRundheen
vaarpunal soozhthaN maalirunj soolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
876.meaning: The temple that the Lord set His heart upon — He who sucked the breast of the demoness disguised as a fiend, and who loosed scorching arrows from His slaying bow so that the merciless Tataka perished — is at Maalirumsolai, encircled by long flowing waters and cool. On its slopes the cloud-rich venkai and the kongu blooms fill the wild, and the kurinji bears fragrant flowers and sweet honey — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come!

877.translit:
vaNankalil arakkan serukkalath thaviya maNimudi orupadum puraLa
aNankezhun thavanRan kavandhamninRu aada amarchcheydha adikaLtham kooyil
piNankalil neduveey nudhimukam kizhippap pirasamvan dizhidhara perundheen
maNankamazh saaral maalirunj soolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
877.meaning: The temple of the Lord who waged war so that the unbowing demon (Ravana) perished on the battlefield, his ten jeweled crowns rolling down, while a terror arose and his headless trunk stood dancing, is at Maalirumsolai — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come! On its fragrance-laden slopes the tall bamboo pierces the honeycomb so that the honey trickles down in plenty.

878.translit:
vidankalan thamarndha aravaNaith thuyinRu viLanganik kiLankanRu visiRi,
kudankalan thaadik kuravaimun koththa kooththavem madikaLtham kooyil
thadankadal mukandhu visumbidaip piLiRath thadavaraik kaLiRenRu munindhu
madankalninRu adhirum maalirunj soolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
878.meaning: The temple of our dancing Lord, who slept upon the venom-blended serpent couch, who flung a young calf to bring down the wood-apple fruits, who danced mingling among the pots and first wove the kuravai dance, is at Maalirumsolai — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come! There the lion, taking the cloud that has drawn up the broad sea and roars across the sky to be a great-mountained elephant, rises in wrath and thunders.

879.translit:
theenukan aavi pooyuka ankoor sezhundhiraL panankani yudhira thaanukan
theRindha thadankadal vaNNar eNNimun idankoNda kooyil,
vaanakas soolai marakadhas saayal maamaNik kalladhar niRaindhu,
maanukar saaral maalirunj soolai vaNankudhum vaamada nenjee.
879.meaning: The temple that the Lord, colored like the vast ocean, chose and took for His abode of old after due thought — He who, that the life of Dhenuka might depart, gladly hurled him there so that the rich clusters of palm fruits fell — is at Maalirumsolai. Its sky-high groves have an emerald-like coolness, and its slopes, where deer feed, are filled with paths of great gem-like stones — let us worship it, O gentle heart, come!

880.translit:
puthamigu visumbil puNarisen RaNavap porukadal aravaNait thuyinRu,
pathamigu pariyin migusinam thavirttha panimugil vaNNartham kOyil,
kathamigu sinaththa kadatadak kaLiRRin kavuLvazhik kaLivaNdu paruga,
mathamigu saaral maaliruny chOlai vaNanguthum vaa mada nenjE.
880.meaning: He who reclines in sleep upon the serpent-couch on the surging ocean, where the great expanse of waters joins the elements of the sky; the Lord of cool-cloud hue who once curbed the rising fury of the swift horse — this is His temple, where intoxicated bees drink from the cheek-channels of the rutting, wrathful elephant. O dull heart, come, let us worship Maaliruncholai of the rut-flowing slopes.

881.translit:
punthiyil samaNar puththaren RivargaL
oththana pEsavum uganthittu,
enthaibem maanaar imaiyavar thalaivar
eNNimun idangoNda kOyil,
santhanap pozhilin thaazhsinai neezhal
thaazhvarai magaLirgaL naaLum,
manthirath thiRainjum maaliruny chOlai
vaNanguthum vaa mada nenjE.
881.meaning: The temple which our Father, the great Lord, the chief of the celestials, took as His abode of old after due deliberation — though the Jains and Buddhists in their minds speak alike-sounding worthless words, taking pleasure in them. There the women of the hill-slopes daily worship with sacred chants in the shade of the drooping boughs of sandal-groves. O dull heart, come, let us worship Maaliruncholai.

882.translit:
vaNdamar saaral maaliruny chOlai
maamaNi vaNNarai vaNangum,
thoNdaraip paravum sudaroLi neduvEl
soozhvayal aalinan naadan,
kaNdalnal vEli mangaiyar thalaivan
kaliyanvaa yolisey tha panuval,
koNdivai paadum thavamudai yaargaL
aaLvarik kuraikad lulagE.
882.meaning: Kaliyan, lord of Mangai in the goodly land of Aali, ringed with fertile fields and bright fences of fragrant-screwpine, bearing a long radiant spear — he sang this garland of verses extolling the devotees who worship the great sapphire-hued Lord of Maaliruncholai on the bee-thronged slopes. Those who, taking up these verses and singing them in penance, shall rule over this world girdled by the roaring sea.

883.translit:
moova rilmunmu thalvan muzhang kaarkadal uLkidanthu,
poovula runthithan nuL buvanam padaith thuNdumizhntha,
thEvar kaLnaa yaganaith thirumaa liruncholai ninRa,
kOva largOvin thanaik kodi yEridai koodungkolO.
883.meaning: He who is the foremost first among the three deities, who lies within the roaring dark ocean, who created the worlds from the lotus on His navel, swallowed them and brought them forth again; the Lord of the celestials, the cowherd Govinda who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai — O when shall this slender, creeper-waisted maiden be united with Him?

884.translit:
punaivaLar poompozhi laar ponni soozharang kanagaruL
munaivanai, moovula gumpadaith tha muthal moorththithannai,
sinaivaLar poompozhil soozh thiru maaliruny chOlaininRaan
kanaikazhal kaaNungko lOgayal kaNNiyem kaarigaiyE.
884.meaning: The First Lord who in the city of Arangam (Srirangam), girt by the Kaveri and adorned with blossoming flower-groves, is the foremost one who created all the three worlds; He who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai ringed with flowering groves of spreading boughs — O when shall our lovely, carp-eyed maiden behold His resounding anklet-clad feet?

885.translit:
uNdula gEzhinai yum oru paalagan aalilaimEl,
kaNduyil koNdukan thakaru maaNikka maamalaiyai,
thiNdiRal maagari sErthiru maaliruny chOlaininRa,
aNdartham kOvinai yinRaNu kungkolen aayizhaiyE.
885.meaning: He who swallowed all the seven worlds and, as a lone infant, took His joyous sleep upon a banyan leaf; the great mountain of dark sapphire; the Lord of the celestials who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai, the resort of mighty strong great elephants — O when shall my finely-jewelled maiden draw near to Him this day?

886.translit:
singgama thaayavu Nanthira laagammun keeNdukantha,
pangaya maamalark kaNbara naiyem paranjsudarai,
thingaLnan maamugil sErthiru maaliruny chOlaininRa,
nangaLpi raanaiyin RunaNu kungkolen nannuthalE.
886.meaning: He who, becoming the man-lion, joyfully tore open of old the mighty chest of the strong asura (Hiranya); the Lord with eyes like the great lotus blossom, our supreme effulgence; our Master who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai where the moon and the fair great clouds gather — O when shall my fair-browed maiden draw near to Him this day?

887.translit:
thaanavan vELvi thannil thaniyE kuRaLaay nimirnthu,
vaanamumaN Nagamum aLantha thijeevik kiraman,
thEnamar poompozhil soozh thirumaaliruny chOlaininRa,
vaanavar kOnaiyinRu vaNangiththozha vallaL kolO.
887.meaning: The Trivikrama who, alone becoming a dwarf at the sacrifice of the asura (Bali), grew tall and measured both the heavens and the earth; the King of the celestials who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai ringed with flowering, honey-laden groves — O is she able this day to bow down and worship Him?

888.translit:
nEsami laathavark kumninai yaathavark kummariyaan,
vaasama larppozhil soozhvada maamathu raippiRanthaan,
thEsamel laamvaNang kumthiru maaliruny chOlaininRa,
kEsava nampithan naikkeNdai yoNkaNNi kaaNungkolO.
888.meaning: He who is hard to attain for those without love and for those who do not meditate on Him; He who was born in great northern Mathura ringed with fragrant flower-groves; Kesava, the noble Lord who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai, worshipped by every land — O when shall the bright carp-eyed maiden behold Him?

889.translit:
puLLinai vaaypiLan thuporu maagari komposiththu,
kaLLach chakaduthaith thakaru maaNikka maamalaiyai,
teLLaru vikozhik kumthiru maaliruny chOlaininRa,
vaLLalai vaaNutha laaLvaNang kiththozha vallaLkolO.
889.meaning: He who split open the beak of the bird (the demon Bakasura), broke the tusk of the warring great elephant, and kicked apart the deceitful cart-demon; the great mountain of dark sapphire; the bountiful Lord who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai where clear waterfalls cascade — O is the bright-browed maiden able to bow down and worship Him?

890.translit:
paarththanuk kanRaru LippaaRa thaththoru thErmunninRu,
kaaththavan RannaiviN NOrkaru maaNikka maamalaiyai,
theerththanaip poompozhil soozhthiru maaliruny chOlaininRa,
moorththiyaik kaithozha vummudi yungkolen moykuzhaRkE.
890.meaning: He who, of old showing grace to Arjuna, stood before a chariot in the Bharata war and protected him; the great mountain of dark sapphire of the celestials; the holy pure One; the Lord who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai ringed with flowering groves — O will it become possible for my thick-tressed maiden even to fold her hands and worship Him?

891.translit:
valampuri yaazhi yanaivarai yaarthiraL thOLanRannai,
pulampuri noolava naippozhil vEngada vEthiyanai,
silambiya laaRudai yathiru maaliruny chOlaininRa,
nalanthigazh naaraNa nainaNu kungkolen nannuthalE.
891.meaning: He who bears the right-whirling conch and the discus, He of the mountain-like rounded shoulders, the Vedic One wearing the sacred thread, the Lord of the groves of Venkatam; Narayana radiant with goodness who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai, which has the resounding river of the hills — O when shall my fair-browed maiden draw near to Him?

892.translit:
thEdaR kariyava naiththiru maaliruny chOlai ninRa,
aadal paRavai yanai aNi yaayizhai kaaNumenRu,
maadak kodimathiL soozhmangai yaarkali kanRisonna
paadal panuvalpath thumpayil vaarkkillai paavangaLE.
892.meaning: Saying "Behold Him, O finely-adorned maiden" — Him who is hard to seek, who stands at Tirumaaliruncholai, who has the dancing bird (Garuda) as His mount; Kalikanri of Mangai, ringed with bannered, mansioned ramparts, sang these ten poetic verses. For those who recite them there shall be no sins.

893.translit:
engga LemmiRai yembiraa nimai yOrkku naayagan, Eth thadiyavar
thangaL thammanaththup piriyaa tharuLpurivaan,
pongguthaN Naruvi puthamseyyap ponkaLE sithaRu milangoLi,
sengamala malarum thirukkOtti yooraanE.
893.meaning: Our Master, our Lord, the chief of the celestials, who without parting dwells in the hearts of His praising devotees and bestows His grace — He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, where the swelling cool cascades, doing their work, scatter gold-like brilliance and where bright red lotuses blossom.

894.translit:
evvanOy thavirppaan emakkiRai innagaith thuvarvaay, nilamagaL
sevvi thOya vallaan thirumaa magatkiniyaan,
mauval maalaivaNdaadum mallikai maalaiyodu maNanthumaarutham
theyvam naaRa varumthiruk kOtti yooraanE.
894.meaning: He who removes every grievous affliction, our Master, He of the sweet smile and coral-red lips, who is able to embrace the freshness of the Earth-goddess, the beloved of the great Lakshmi — He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, where the breeze comes mingled with garlands of jasmine over which bees hover, and with mullai blossoms, bearing a divine fragrance.

895.translit:
veLLiyaan kariyaan maNiniRa vaNNan viNNavar thamakkiRai, emakku
oLLiyaa nuyarnthaa nulagEzhu muNdumizhnthaan,
thuLLuneer moNdu koNdu saamaraik kaRRaich santhana munthi vanthasai,
teLLuneerp puRavil thirukkOtti yooraanE.
895.meaning: He who is bright (silver-white), He who is dark, He of sapphire-jewel hue, the Master of the celestials, the resplendent and exalted One for us, who swallowed the seven worlds and brought them forth again — He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur in its tracts of clear waters, where the dancing waters scoop up and bear along tufts of chowrie-grass and sandal, pushing them forward to sway.

896.translit:
ERumERi ilangumoN mazhuppaRRum eesaR kisainthu, udambilOr
kooRuthaan koduththaan kulamaamagat kiniyaan,
naaRu saNbaka mallikai malarpulgi inniLa vaNdu, nannaRunth
thERalvaay madukkum thirukkOtti yooraanE.
896.meaning: He who, in accord with Siva — who rides the bull and grasps the bright shining axe — granted one half of His own body, the beloved of the high-born great Lakshmi — He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, where the sweet young bees, embracing the fragrant champak and jasmine blossoms, draw in the goodly fragrant nectar at their mouths.

897.translit:
vanga maakadal vaNNan maamaNi vaNNan viNNavar kOnma thumalarth
thonggal neeNmudi yaannedi yaanpadi kadanthaan,
mangul thOymaNi maada veNkodi maaga meethuyarn thERi, vaanuyar
thingaL thaaNaNavum thirukkOtti yooraanE.
897.meaning: He of the hue of the great ship-bearing ocean, He of great sapphire hue, the King of the celestials wearing the long crown with honeyed-blossom strands, the lofty One who strode across the worlds — He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, where white banners on the jewelled mansions that touch the clouds rise up high into the sky and reach toward the lofty moon.

898.translit:
kaava nilangaikk iRaikalang kachcharam selavuyththu, maRRavan
Evalam thavirththaan ennai yaaludai yempiraan,
naava lampuvi mannar vanthu vaNanga maaluRai kinRathingena,
thEvar vanthiRainjum thirukkOtti yooraanE.
898.meaning: He who let fly His arrows so that the guardian-king, lord of Lanka, was shaken, and put an end to his sway — my Lord who has taken me as His own. He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, where the celestials come and bow saying "It is here that Tirumal dwells," and where the kings of the jambu-island earth come and worship.

899.translit:
kanRu koNdu viLangani yeRinthu aaniraik kazhivenRu, maamazhai
ninRu kaaththugan thaannila maamagat kiniyaan,
kunRin mullaiyin vaasamum kuLirmallikai maNamum aLainthu, iLanth
tenRal vanthulavum thirukkOtti yooraanE.
899.meaning: He who hurled a calf to knock down the wood-apple fruits, who saved the cattle-herds, and who joyfully held up the great mountain to shield them from the heavy rain, the beloved of the great Earth-goddess — He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, where the young southern breeze comes wafting, mingled with the fragrance of the hill-jasmine and the scent of cool mallikai blossoms.

900.translit:
poongkurundhu osiththaanai kaayndhu arimaa seguththu, adiyEnai aaLugandhu
Ingku en uL pugundhaan imaiyOrgaL tham perumaan,
thoongku thaN palavin kani thogu vaazhaiyin kaniyodu maangkani
thEngku thaN punal soozh thirukkOttiyooraanE.
900.meaning: He who in anger broke the slender kurundham tree and slew the great horse-demon, who graciously took me, His servant, into His service and entered into my heart here — He is the Lord of the celestials. He is the Lord of Tirukkottiyur, encircled by cool waters where ripe jackfruit hangs low, gathered with banana and mango fruits.

901.translit:
kOvaiyin tamizh paaduvaar kudam aaduvaar tada maa malar misai,
mEvu naanmuganil viLangku puri noolar,
mEvu naanmaRai vaaNar aivagai vELvi aaRangkam vallavar tozhum,
dEva dEva piraan thirukkOttiyooraanE.
901.meaning: Those who sing strung-together Tamil verses, who dance the pot-dance, who wearing the sacred thread shine more radiantly than the four-faced Brahma seated on the great lotus — those masters of the four Vedas, skilled in the five sacrifices and six limbs of learning, worship Him. He is the Lord of gods, the benefactor, the Lord of Tirukkottiyur.

902.translit:
aalu maavalavan kalikanRi mangaiyar talaivan aNi pozhil
sElgaL paaykazhani thirukkOttiyooraanai,
neela maamugil vaNNanai nedumaalai in tamizhaal ninaindha, ind
naalum aaRum vallaarkku idamaagum vaanulagE.
902.meaning: Kalikanri (Tirumangai Alwar), the lord of Mangai, master of the dancing horse, sang in sweet Tamil of the Lord of Tirukkottiyur — where carp leap in fields beside beautiful groves — Nedumal of the hue of a great dark cloud. For those who can master these ten verses and the six limbs (of devotion), the heavenly world shall be their abode.

903.translit:
oru naR suRRam enakku uyir oN poruL
varu nal tol gathi aagiya maindhanai
nerunal kaNdadhu neermalai inRu pOy
karu nel soozh kaNNamangaiyuL kaaNdumE.
903.meaning: He who is my one true kinsman, my very life, my precious wealth, the goodly ancient refuge that comes to me — that young Lord I beheld yesterday at Neermalai. Today, going forth, we shall behold Him at Kannamangai, surrounded by dark paddy fields.

904.translit:
ponnai maa maNiyai aNiyaarndhadhOr
minnai vEngkadaththu uchchiyiR kaNdu pOy
ennai aaLudai Isanai em piraan
thannai yaam senRu kaaNdum thaN kaavilE.
904.meaning: Him who is like gold, like a great gem, like a lightning flash adorned with beauty, I beheld upon the summit of Venkatam. Going forth, we shall go and behold our Lord and Master who rules over me, at cool Tirukkavalampadi (Tirukkavi).

905.translit:
vElai aal ilaip paLLi virumbiya
paalai aar amudhaththinai pain thuzhaay maalai
aaliyil kaNdu magizhndhu pOy
naala munniyai kaaNdum naangkoorilE.
905.meaning: He who, upon the ocean, delights to recline on a banyan leaf, who is sweet as milk-nectar, wearing a garland of fresh tulasi — Him I beheld with joy at Tiruvali. Going forth, we shall behold the One who came before the world (the primordial Lord) at Tirunangur.

906.translit:
thuLakkam il sudarai, avuNan udal
piLakkum maindhanai pEril vaNangki pOy
aLappil aar amudhai amararkku aruL
viLakkinai senRu veLLaRai kaaNdumE.
906.meaning: The unwavering radiance, the young Lord who tore apart the body of the demon (Hiranya) — Him I worshipped at Tipper (Tiruppernagar) and went forth. The measureless brimming nectar, the lamp that bestows grace upon the immortals — we shall go and behold Him at Tiruvellarai.

907.translit:
sudalaiyil sudu neeRan amarndhadhu Or
nadalai theerththavanai naRaiyoor kaNdu, en
udalai uL pugundhu uLLam urukki uN
vidalaiyai senRu kaaNdum meyyaththuLE.
907.meaning: Him who removed the great affliction in which He resides one with the ash-smeared one (Siva) of the burning-ground, I beheld at Tirunaraiyur. The youthful hero who entered into my body, melted my heart and consumed it — we shall go and behold Him at Tirumeyyam.

908.translit:
vaanai aar amudham tandha vaLLalai
thEnai neeL vayal sERaiyil kaNdu pOy
aanai vaatti aruLum amarar tham
kOnai, yaam kudandhai senRu kaaNdumE.
908.meaning: The generous Lord who gave the heavens and the abundant nectar, who is sweet as honey, I beheld at Tiruchcherai of the long fields. Going forth, the King of the immortals who graciously subdued the elephant (Kuvalayapida / Gajendra) — we shall go and behold Him at Tirukkudandhai (Kumbakonam).

909.translit:
koondhalaar magizh kOvalan aay veNNey
maandhu azhundhaiyil kaNdu magizhndhu pOy
paandhaL paazhiyil paLLi virumbiya
vEndhanai senRu kaaNdum vehkaavuLE.
909.meaning: As the cowherd who delighted the women of lovely tresses, who ate the butter, I beheld Him with joy at Tiruvazhundhur. Going forth, the King who delights to recline upon the serpent-bed in the vast (ocean) — we shall go and behold Him at Tiruvehka.

910.translit:
paththar aaviyai paal madhiyai, aNith
thoththai maal irun jOlai thozhudhu pOy
muththinai maNiyai maNi maaNikka
viththinai, senRu viNNagar kaaNdumE.
910.meaning: The very life of His devotees, who is like the moon of milk-white radiance, the beautiful cluster of blossoms — Him I worshipped at Tirumal irun Solai and went forth. The pearl, the gem, the seed-source of sapphire and ruby — we shall go and behold Him at Tiruvinnagar.

911.translit:
kampa maa kaLiRu anji kalanga, Or
kombu koNda kurai kazhal kooththanai
kombu ulaam pozhil kOttiyoor kaNdu pOy
nambanai senRu kaNdum naavaayuLE.
911.meaning: The dancer of the resounding anklets who seized a tusk so that the great trembling elephant (Kuvalayapida) feared and was thrown into confusion — Him I beheld at Kottiyur of groves where branches sway, and went forth. The trustworthy Lord — we shall go and behold Him at Tirunavay.

912.translit:
peRRam aaLiyai pEril maNaaLanai
kaRRa nool kalikanRi urai seydha
soR tiRam ivai solliya toNdarkatku
aRRam illai aNdam avarkku aatchiyE.
912.meaning: Of Him who tends the cattle (the Lord of cowherds), the Bridegroom of Tipper (Tiruppernagar), Kalikanri of learned scriptures composed these eloquent words. For the devotees who recite them, there shall be no want, and the celestial realm shall be theirs to rule.

913.translit:
irakkam inRi eng kOn seydha theemai immaiyE emakku eydhiRRu kaaNeer
parakka yaam inRu uraiththu en iravaNan pattananini avarkku uraikkOm
kurakku naayagarkaaL iLam kOvE kOla val vil iraama piraanE
arakkar aadu azhaippaar illai naangkaL anjinOm tadam pongath tham pongO.
913.meaning: See how the cruelty our king (Ravana) committed without mercy has come upon us in this very life. What use is it that we speak at length today — Ravana has perished; to whom now shall we appeal? O chiefs of the monkeys, O youthful prince, O Rama, Lord of the splendid mighty bow! There is none to summon the demons to dance; we are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

914.translit:
paththu neeL mudiyum avaRRu iratti paazhith thOLum padaiththavan selvam,
siththam mangaiyar paal vaiththu kettaan seyvadhu onRu aRiyaa adiyOngaL
oththa thOL iraNdum oru mudiyum oruvar tham tiRaththOm anRi vaazhndhOm
aththa em perumaan emmai kollEl anjinEm tadam pongath tham pongO.
914.meaning: He who possessed ten tall crowns and twice as many mighty shoulders — that wealthy one (Ravana) — set his heart upon women and perished; we his servants knew nothing of what was done. With our matched pair of shoulders and single head, we lived loyal to none but our one master. O Father, our Lord, do not slay us; we are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

915.translit:
thaNda kaaraNiyam pugundhu anRu thaiyalai thagavu ili eng kOmaan
koNdu pOndhu kettaan emakku ingku Or kuRRam illai kollEl kula vEndhE
peNdiraal kedum ikkudi thannai pEsuginRadhu en? thaasarathee, un
aNda vaNar ugappadhE seydhaay anjinOm tadam pongath tham pongO.
915.meaning: That day, our merciless king entered the Dandaka forest, carried off the lady (Sita), and perished; we have no fault here. Do not slay us, O King of our clan! Why speak of this house that is ruined through women? O son of Dasaratha, You have done only what pleases the dwellers of the heavens; we are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

916.translit:
enjal il ilangaikku iRai eng kOn tannai mun paNindhu engaL kaN mugappE
nanju taan arakkar kudikku enRu nangaiyai avan tambiyE sonnaan
vinjai vaanavar vENdiRRE pattOm vEri vaar pozhil maa mayil anna
anji lOdhiyai koNdu nadamin anjinOm tadam pongath tham pongO.
916.meaning: His own brother (Vibhishana) first bowed before our flawless king, the lord of Lanka, and before our very eyes declared that the lady (Sita) was poison to the demon clan. We have suffered what the skilled celestials wished. O You who are like a great peacock of the fragrant flowing groves, take back the lady of beautiful tresses and depart; we are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

917.translit:
sem pon neeL mudi engaL iravaNan seethai enbadhu Or theyvam koNarndhu
vambu ulaam kadi kaavil siRaiyaa vaiththadhE kuRRam aayiRRu kaaNeer
kumbanOdu nigumbanum pattaan kooRRam manidam aay vandhu tOnRi
ambinaal emmai konRidukinRadhu anjinOm tadam pongath tham pongO.
917.meaning: See — was it our Ravana of the tall crown of pure gold who brought a goddess named Sita and held her captive in the fragrant guarded grove that became the fault? Kumbha and Nikumbha too have fallen; Death itself has appeared in human form and slays us with arrows. We are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

918.translit:
Odha maa kadalai kadandhu ERi uyar koL maa kadi kaavai iRuththu
kaadhal makkaLum suRRamum konRu kadi ilangai malanga eriththu
doodhu vandha kurangukkE ungaL tOnRal dEviyai vittu kodaadhE
aadhar ninRu paduginRadhu andhO anjinOm tadam pongath tham pongO.
918.meaning: He crossed the roaring great ocean and came ashore, broke the lofty guarded grove, slew the beloved sons and kinsmen, and burned the well-guarded Lanka into confusion. Your chief (Ravana), not giving up the Devi even to the monkey that came as messenger, stands in folly and is destroyed — alas! We are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

919.translit:
thaazha minRi mun neerai anjnaanRu thagaindhadhE kaNdu vanji nuN marungkul
maazhai maan mada nOkkiyai vittu vaazhgilaa madhi il manaththaanai
Ezhaiyai ilangaikku iRai tannai engaLai ozhiya kolaiyavanai
soozha maa ninai maa maNi vaNNaa sollinOm tadam pongath tham pongO.
919.meaning: O Lord of the hue of a great sapphire, consider it well all around: seeing how that day You curbed the ocean without delay, this senseless one of foolish mind (Ravana), who would not release the lady of slender waist and the tender startled gaze of a young deer — that wretched king of Lanka, that murderer who would destroy us — of him we have spoken. We are afraid — let the flood swell, let our (refuge) swell.

920.translit:
manangkoN deerum maNDoodari mudalaa angka yaRkaNNi naarkaL iruppa
tanangkoL menmulai nookka mozhindu tanja mee sila taapada renRu
punangkoL menmayi laichchiRai vaitta punmai yaaLan nenj chilpuka eyda
ananga nannatiN DooLemmi raamaR kanji noom tadam pongkattam pongkoo.
920.meaning: While Mandodari and the other lovely fish-eyed women of beauty waited with longing hearts, he scorned even to glance at their soft swelling breasts, saying these few were merely ascetics seeking refuge; that mean-spirited one who imprisoned the soft peacock-like maiden (Sita) of the grove — we have struck terror into his heart. We have grown afraid of our Lord Rama of those mighty shoulders like the god of love; let the great flood swell, let it swell!

921.translit:
purangkaL muunRum oor maattiraip poodil pongke rikkirai kaNDavan ambin
sarangka Lee kodi taay adu kinRa saamba vaanudan niRkat tozhudoom
irangku nee yemak kendaipi raanee ilangku vengkadi roonRan siRuvaa
kurangku katkara see emmaik kollee kuuRi noom tadam pongkattam pongkoo.
921.meaning: While Sambavan, who is slain by the cruel arrows of the one who in a single instant made the three cities prey to blazing fire (Shiva), stands by, we worship you. Have mercy on us, O our Father and Lord, O son of the shining fierce-rayed Sun, O king of the monkeys — do not kill us; thus we have pleaded. Let the great flood swell, let it swell!

922.translit:
angkav vaanavark kaakulam teera aNiyi langkai azhittavan Ranai
pongku maavala vankali kanRi pukanRa pongkattang koNDu iv vulagil
engkum paadi ninRaadumin toNDeer immai yee yida rillai iRandaal
tangku muur aNDa mee kaNDu koNmin saaRRi noom tadam pongkattam pongkoo.
922.meaning: O devotees, take up this 'pongkam' song that the mighty, surging Kaliyan (Tirumangai Alwar) composed about the One who destroyed beautiful Lanka to end the affliction of the celestials, and sing and dance with it everywhere in this world. In this very life there shall be no sorrow, and when you depart, behold and gain the heavenly realm as your abiding place — thus we proclaim. Let the great flood swell, let it swell!

923.translit:
eettu kinRoom naatta zhumba iraaman tirunaamam
soottam nambee sukki riivaa ummait tozhukinRoom
vaartthai peeseer emmai ungkaL vaanaram kollaamee
kuuttar poola aadu kinRoom kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
923.meaning: We praise the holy name of Rama until our tongues grow calloused. We salute you, O perfect Lord; O Sugriva, we worship you. Speak a word so that your monkey host does not kill us — and like dancers we dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

924.translit:
embi raanee ennai yaaLvaay enRen RalaRRaadee
ambin vaayppat taaRRa killaa tindira sittazhindaan
nambi anumaa sukki riiva angkada nee naLanee
kumba karNan pattup poonaan kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
924.meaning: No longer crying out 'O my Lord, rule over me!' over and over — Indrajit, unable to bear the force of the arrows, has perished. O noble Hanuman, O Sugriva, O Angada, O Nala! Kumbhakarna too has been struck down and is gone — we dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

925.translit:
njaala maaLu mungkaL koomaan engkaL iravaNaRkuk
kaala naaki vanda vaakaN Danji karumukilpool
neelan vaazhka sudeeNan vaazhka angkadan vaazhka enRu
koola maaka aadu kinRoom kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
925.meaning: Seeing that your king who rules the earth has become as death come upon our Ravana, we fear. Crying 'Long live Nila, dark like the rain-cloud! Long live Sushena! Long live Angada!' we dance in splendid array the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

926.translit:
maNangkaL naaRum vaarkuzha laarkaL maadarka Laadarattai
puNarnda sindaip punmai yaaLan ponRa varisilaiyaal
kaNangka LuNNa vaaLi yaaNDa kaavala nukkiLaiyoon
guNangkaL paadi yaadu kinRoom kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
926.meaning: He whose mean heart clung to the love of long-haired women whose tresses breathe fragrance perished — and we sing the virtues of the younger brother (Lakshmana) of the Protector (Rama) who wielded arrows from the curved bow so that hosts (of demons) were devoured, and we dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

927.translit:
venRi tandoom maanam veeNDoom taanam emakkaaka
inRu tammi nengkaL vaaNaaL emperu maan tamarkaaL
ninRu kaaNeer kaNka Laara neeremaik kollaadee
kunRu poola aadu kinRoom kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
927.meaning: We have granted you victory; we do not seek honor — grant us as a gift this very day our lives, O attendants of our Lord. Stand and watch to your eyes' content; do not kill us, and like a mountain we dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

928.translit:
kallin munneer maaRRi vandu kaaval kadandu ilangkai
allal seydaa nungkaL koomaan emmai amarkkaLattu
vella killaa tanji noongkaaN vengkadi roonsiRuvaa
kolla veeNDaa aadu kinRoom kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
928.meaning: Your king, who turned aside the ocean with stones, came, crossed the guard, and brought affliction upon Lanka. Unable to defeat us on the field of battle, we have grown afraid, behold — O son of the fierce-rayed Sun, do not kill us; we dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

929.translit:
maaRRa maava titta naiyee vammin arakkaruLeer
seeRRam nummeel teera veeNDil seevakam peesaadee
aaRRal saanRa tolpiRappil anumanai vaazhka enRu
kuuRRa mannaar kaaNa aadeer kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
929.meaning: This much is the only answer — come, O demons! If you wish the wrath upon you to be assuaged, speak no boasts of valor, but cry 'Long live Hanuman of mighty strength and ancient birth!' and dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram so that those death-like ones may behold.

930.translit:
kavaLa yaanai paaypura vittee rooda rakkarellaam
tuvaLa venRa venRi yaaLan Ran tamar kollaamee
tavaLa maadam needa yootti kaavalan Ran siRuvan
kuvaLai vaNNan kaaNa aadeer kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
930.meaning: Lest the attendants of the victorious one — who conquered so that all the demons with their tusked elephants, leaping horses and chariots were laid low — should kill you, dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram so that the lily-hued son of the lord of Ayodhya, where white mansions rise tall, may behold.

931.translit:
eedot teendum neeLi laiveel engkaL iravaNanaar
oodip poonaar naangkaL eyttoom uyvadoor kaaraNattaal
suudip poondoom ungkaL kooma naaNai todareenmin
kuudi kuudi yaadu kinRoom kuzhamaNi tuuramee.
931.meaning: Our Ravana, who held aloft the long-bladed spear leaf-like in form, has fled away; we are worn out. For the sake of some means of deliverance we have come bearing (your token) on our heads — by your king's command, do not pursue us; gathering and gathering we dance the kuzhamaNi-tuuram.

932.translit:
venRa tolseert tenni langkai venjamattu
anRarakkar kunRa mannaa raadi uynda kuzhamaNi tuurattai
kanRi neynneer ninRa veeRkaik kaliya nolimaalai
onRum onRum aindum muunRum padinin Raaduminee.
932.meaning: This garland of resonant song by Kaliyan of the spear-bearing hand glistening like oil, about the kuzhamaNi-tuuram by which, on that day of fierce battle in conquered southern Lanka of ancient glory, those mountain-like demons danced and were saved — dance, standing firm in its rhythm, to these ten verses (one and one and five and three).

933.translit:
sanda malarkkuzhal taazhat taanugan doodit taniyee
vandu en mulaittadan tannai vaangki nin vaayil maduttu
nandan peRappeRRa nambee naanugan duNNum amudee
endai perumaanee uNNaay ennammam seemamuN Naayee.
933.meaning: With your fragrant flower-decked locks hanging loose, gladly running alone, you came and drew my full breast and pressed it to your mouth — O perfect one whom Nanda was blessed to obtain, O nectar that I delight to feed! O my Father and Lord, drink; drink my milk safely and to your fill.

934.translit:
vangka maRikadal vaNNaa maamuki lee yokkum nambee
sengka Nediya tiruvee sengkama lampurai vaayaa
kongkai surandida vunnaik kuuviyum kaaNaadi rundeen
engkirun daayarka Loodum en viLai yaadukin Raayee.
934.meaning: O hued like the surging sea where ships toss, O perfect one who resembles the great cloud! O red-eyed, tall Treasure (Lord), O mouth that resembles the red lotus! With my breasts flowing with milk I called you and yet could not see you — where have you gone playing with the cowherd boys?

935.translit:
tiruvil polinda ezhilaar aayartam piLLaika Loodu
teruvil tiLaikkinRa nambee seykinRa tiimaikaL kaNDittu
urukiyen kongkaiyin tiimpaal oottandu paayndidu kinRa
maruvik kudangkaa lirundu vaaymulai yuNNanee vaaraay.
935.meaning: O perfect one who sport in the street with the handsome cowherd boys radiant with beauty (of Lakshmi)! Seeing the mischiefs you do, my heart melts and the sweet milk of my breasts comes rushing and flowing — come, sit nestled in the bend of my lap and drink the milk at my breast.

936.translit:
makkaL peRudavam poolum vaiyattu vaazhum madavaar
makkaL piRarkaNNuk kokkum mudalvaa madakkaLi Rannaay
sekkar iLampiRai tannai vaangki nin kaiyil taruvan
okkalai mee lirun dammam ukandini duNNanee vaaraay.
936.meaning: It is as if the gentle women living in the world bear children only through penance, and other people's children seem (mere images) compared to you, O primal Lord, O you who are like a rutting elephant! I will pluck the young red crescent moon and place it in your hand — come, sit upon my hip and drink the milk gladly and sweetly.

937.translit:
maitta karungkunji maindaa maamaru tuudu nadandaay
vittaka nee viraiyaadee veNNey vizhungkum vikirtaa
ittanai poodanRi yenRan kongkai surandirukka killaa
uttamanee ammam uNNaay ulakaLan daay ammam uNNaay.
937.meaning: O youth with dark, kohl-glossy curls, who walked between the great marudu trees! O wondrous one, O wondrous-natured one who swallows butter without haste! Beyond this hour my breasts cannot stay flowing — O noblest one, drink the milk; O you who measured the worlds, drink the milk.

938.translit:
piLLaikaL seyvana seyyaay peesin peridum valiyai
kaLLam manatti ludaiyai kaaNavee tiimaikaL seydi
uLLa murukiyen kongkai oottandu paayndidu kinRa
paLLik kuRippuchey yaadee paalamu duNNanee vaaraay.
938.meaning: You do the things children do; if I speak, you are exceedingly strong; you hold deceit in your heart; in plain sight you do mischiefs. My heart melts and my breasts come rushing and flowing — do not feign signs of sleep, but come and drink the milk-nectar.

939.translit:
tanmaka naakavan peeychchi taanmulai yuNNak kodukka
vanmaka naayavaL aavi vaangki mulaiyuNDa nambi
nanmakaL aaymaka Loodu naanila mangkai maNaaLaa
enmaka nee ammamuN Naay ennammam seemamuN Naayee.
939.meaning: When the wicked demoness gave her breast for you to suck as her own child, you, being the mighty one, drew out her life and drank at her breast, O perfect one. O bridegroom of the good cowherd maiden (Nappinnai) and of the Lady of the four-fold earth! O my child, drink the milk; drink my milk safely and to your fill.

940.translit:
untam aDigaL munivar unnai-naan en-kaiyiR koolaal
nondiDa moodavum killeen nungaL-tam aa-nirai ellaam
vandu pugudarum poodu vaaniDai deyvangaL kaaNa
andi-am poodu angu nilleel aazhi-am kaiyanee vaaraay
940.meaning: Your fathers will grow angry; I cannot bear to strike you with the staff in my hand so that you suffer pain. When all your herds of cattle come crowding home, while the gods in the heavens look on, do not linger out there at the dusk hour. O lord who holds the lovely discus in your hand, come back!

941.translit:
peRRat talaivan-em koomaan peer-aruLaaLan madalaay
suRRak kuzhaattu iLam koovee tOndriya tol-pugazhaaLaa
kaRRinam-tORum maRittuk kaanam tirinda kaLiRee
eRRukku-en ammam-uNNaadee em-perumaan irundaayee
941.meaning: O child of our lord, master of the herds, the one of great compassion! O young prince of the circle of kinsmen, you who bear ancient renown! O young elephant who wandered through the forest turning back each herd of calves! Why, my lord, do you sit thus without drinking my milk?

942.translit:
immai iDar-keDa veeNDi eendu-ezhil tOL kalikandri
semmaip panuval nuul koNDu sen-kaN neDiyavan-tannai
ammam-uN endu uraikkinda paaDal ivai aindum aindum
meymmai manattu vaittu eetta vinavar aagalum aamee
942.meaning: These ten songs were composed by Kalikanri of beautiful raised shoulders, who, longing to destroy the sufferings of this present life, took up upright verse of true poetry and sang to the tall lord with red eyes, saying "drink the milk." Those who hold them with truth in their minds and praise will become free of all fault.

943.translit:
puun-koodai aaychchi kaDai-veNNai pukku-uNNa
aangu-avaL aarttup puDaikkap puDai-uNDu
eengi irundu siNungi viLaiyaaDum
ong-Oda vaNNanee chappaaNi oLi-maNi vaNNanee chappaaNi
943.meaning: When the cowherd-woman of fragrant flower-tresses, having stolen and eaten the churned butter, scolded and beat him, he, beaten, sat sighing and whimpering and yet went on playing. O you who are the hue of the surging ocean, clap your hands! O you who are the hue of a radiant gem, clap your hands!

944.translit:
taayar manangaL taDippat tayir-ney-uNDee em-piraakkaL
iru-nilattu engaL-tam aayar azhaga aDigaL aravinda
vaaya-vanee koTTaay chappaaNi maal-vaNNanee
koTTaay chappaaNi
944.meaning: O our lord who ate up the curd and ghee until the hearts of the mothers swelled with anguish! O handsome one of our cowherds upon this wide earth, O master with a lotus-like mouth! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O dark-hued one, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

945.translit:
taam-Or uruTTit tayir-ney vizhungiTTu
taamO tavazhvar endu aaychchiyar taambinaal
taamO tirakkaiyaal aarkkat tazhumbu-irunda
taamOdaraa koTTaay chappaaNi taamarai-kaNNanee chappaaNi
945.meaning: Rolling something to climb up, you swallowed the curd and ghee; thinking "he will crawl away," the cowherd-women bound you with a rope around the waist, leaving a scar there. O Damodara! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O lotus-eyed one, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

946.translit:
peRRaar taLai-kazhalap peerndu angu ayal-iDattu
uRRaar oruvarum indri ulaginil
maRRu-arum anjap-pOy vanjap-peN nanju-uNDa
kaRRaayanee koTTaay chappaaNi kaar-vaNNanee koTTaay chappaaNi
946.meaning: When the fetters of those who bore you fell loose, you moved away into a strange place where in this world there was no one of your own, and there, while even great beings trembled, you went and drank the poison of the deceitful woman. O young cowherd! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O cloud-hued one, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

947.translit:
sOttu-ena ninnait tozhuvan varam-tara
peeychchi mulai-uNDa piLLaay periyana
aaychchiyar appam taruvar avarkkaaga
saaRRi Or aayiram chappaaNi taDan-kaigaLaal koTTaay chappaaNi
947.meaning: I worship you, saying "hail"; grant me your boon. O little one who drank the breast of the demoness! The great cowherd-women will give you cakes; for their sake, declaring a thousand times, clap your hands with your large hands, beat the rhythm!

948.translit:
keevalam andru-un vayiRu vayiRRukku
naan aval appam taruvan karu-viLaip
puu-alar neeL-muDi nandan-tan pOr-eeRee
kOvalanee koTTaay chappaaNi kuDam-aaDee koTTaay chappaaNi
948.meaning: Your belly is no common thing; for that belly I shall give you flattened rice and cakes. O fighting bull of Nanda who wears a tall crown blossoming with kaaruviLai flowers! O cowherd! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O dancer of the pot-dance, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

949.translit:
puLLinai vaay-piLandu puun-kurundam saaittu
tuLLi viLaiyaaDit tuung-uRi veNNaiyai
aLLiya kaiyaal aDiyeen mulai-neruDum
piLLaip-piraan koTTaay chappaaNi peey-mulai uNDaanee chappaaNi
949.meaning: You who split open the beak of the bird-demon, who toppled the beautiful kurundam tree, who leapt and played, and who, with the very hand that scooped up the butter from the hanging pot, fondles this servant's breast. O lord who is a child! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O you who drank from the demoness's breast, beat the rhythm!

950.translit:
yaayum piRarum aRiyaada yaamattu
maaya valavaip-peN vandu mulai-tara
peey endu avaLaip piDittu-uyirai uNDa
vaaya-vanee koTTaay chappaaNi maal-vaNNanee koTTaay chappaaNi
950.meaning: In the dead of night, unknown to your mother or to others, when the cunning, deceitful woman came and offered her breast, you, knowing her to be a demoness, seized her and drank away her very life. O lord with such a mouth! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O dark-hued one, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

951.translit:
kaLLak kuzhaviyaayk kaalaal sagaDattai
taLLi udaittiTTut taayaay varuvaaLai
meLLat toDarndu piDittaar-uyir-uNDa
vaLLalee koTTaay chappaaNi maal-vaNNanee koTTaay chappaaNi
951.meaning: As a sly infant you kicked and overturned the cart-demon with your foot, and the one who came in the guise of a mother (or nurse) you softly followed, seized, and drank away her life. O bountiful one! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O dark-hued one, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

952.translit:
kaaraar puyal-kaik kalikandri mangaiyar-kOn
peeraaLan nenjil piriyaadu iDam-koNDa seeraaLaa
sen-taamarai-kaNNaa taN-tuzhaayt
taaraaLaa koTTaay chappaaNi taDa-maarvaa koTTaay chappaaNi
952.meaning: O glorious one who, ungrudgingly taking your dwelling in the heart of the renowned Kalikanri, lord of Mangai, whose hands are like the dark rain-cloud! O you with red-lotus eyes! O you who wear the garland of cool tulasi! Clap your hands, beat the rhythm! O broad-chested one, clap your hands, beat the rhythm!

953.translit:
engaanum eedu-oppadu Or-maayam uNDee nara-naaraNan-aay ulagatt aRa-nuul
singaamai viritta-van em-perumaan adu-andriyum sen-suDarum nilanum
pongaar kaDalum poruppum neruppum nerukkip puga pon-miDaRRu-anai-poodu
angaandu-avan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
953.meaning: Is there anywhere a wonder to equal this? Being Nara-Narayana, our lord spread out the law-book of righteousness for the world unceasingly. And beyond that, when the red sun, the earth, the surging vast seas, the mountains, and the fire all came crowding in to enter, he opened wide his golden throat to take them in for that time. Behold him! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

954.translit:
kundru-ondru mattaa aravam aLavik kurai-maa kaDalaik kaDaindiTTu oru-kaal
nindru-uNDai koNDu-OTTi vangu-uun nimira ninaitta perumaan adu-andriyum mun
nandru-uNDa tol-seer makarak kaDal-eezh malai-eezh ulagu-eezh ozhiyaamai nambi
andru-uNDavan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
954.meaning: Making a mountain his churning-rod and stretching the great serpent for the churning-cord, he churned the roaring vast ocean, and once, standing firm, he hurled the ball and drove off, the lord who resolved that the boats might right themselves. And beyond that, long ago the perfect one swallowed without remainder the seven crocodile-filled seas, the seven mountains, and the seven worlds. Behold him who consumed them then! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

955.translit:
uLaindiTTu-ezhunda madu-kaiDavargaL ulappil valiyaal avar-paal vayiram
viLaindiTTadu endu-eNNi viNNOr parava avar-naaL ozhitta perumaan muna-naaL
vaLaindiTTa villaaLi val-vaaL eyiRRu malaipOl avuNan-uDal vaL-ugiraal
aLaindiTTavan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
955.meaning: When Madhu and Kaitabha rose up writhing, the lord, thinking that with their boundless strength enmity had grown in them, ended their days while the celestials praised him. And in former days, the lord who wielded the bent bow tore with his sharp claws the mountain-like body of the demon with the fierce sword-like fangs. Behold him! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

956.translit:
taLarndiTTu imaiyOr saraN-taa enat-taan saraNaay muraNaay-avanai ugiraal
piLandiTTu amararkku aruL-sey uganda perumaan tirumaal viri-neer-ulagai
vaLarndiTTa tol-seer viRal-maavaliyai maN-koLLa vanjittu oru-maaN-kuRaLaay
aLandiTTavan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
956.meaning: When the celestials, worn out, cried "give us refuge," he himself became their refuge and tore the hostile one apart with his claws, the lord Tirumal who rejoiced to grant his grace to the gods. To take the earth, he beguiled the mighty Mahabali of long-flourishing renown, and as a single splendid dwarf he measured out the world of spreading waters. Behold him! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

957.translit:
neeNDaan kuRaLaay neDu-vaan-aLavum aDiyaar paDum-aazh-tuyaraaya ellaam
teeNDaamai ninaindu imaiyOr-aLavum selavaitta-piraan adu-andriyum mun
veeNDaamai naman tamar en-tamarai vinavap peRuvaar alar endu ulagu-eezh
aaNDaanavan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
957.meaning: As a dwarf he grew tall, stretching to the far heavens, the lord who arranged that all the deep sufferings into which his devotees fall should not touch them, raising them to the level of the celestials. And beyond that, formerly he declared that Yama's servants should have no power to question "are these our people?" Behold him who ruled the seven worlds! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

958.translit:
pazhittiTTa inbap payan-paRRu aRuttup paNindu-eetta vallaar tuyaraaya ellaam
ozhittiTTu avaraith tanakku-aakka valla perumaan tirumaal adu-andriyum mun
tezhittiTTu ezhundee edir-nindru mannan sinattOL avai-aayiramum mazhuvaal
azhittiTTavan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
958.meaning: For those who, cutting off attachment to the fruits of blameworthy pleasures, can bow and praise him, he removes all their sufferings and is able to make them his own, the lord Tirumal. And beyond that, formerly, when the king rose up roaring and stood facing him, he cut off with his axe those thousand wrathful arms. Behold him! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

959.translit:
paDaittiTTa iv-vaiyam uyya muna-naaL paNindu-eetta vallaar tuyaraaya ellaam
tuDaittiTTu avaraith tanakku-aakka venna teLiyaa arakkar tiRal-pOy aviya
miDaittiTTu ezhunda kurangaip paDaiyaa vilangal pugap-paaychchi vimma kaDalai
aDaittiTTavan-kaaNmin indu aaychchiyaraal aLai-veNNai-uNDu aap-puNDu-irundavanee
959.meaning: That this created world might be saved, he wiped away all the sufferings of those who long ago could bow and praise him, making them his own. So that the might of the obdurate demons should perish, he made an army of the crowding, leaping monkeys, drove the mountains to leap in and fill it, and dammed up the swelling sea. Behold him! He is the very one who today was bound for eating the churned butter at the hands of the cowherd-women.

960.translit:
neRiththitta menkoozhai nanneer izhaiyOdu udanaay villenna vallaeyadhanai,
iRuththittu avaL inpam anpOdu aNaindhittu iLangoTRavanaayth thuLangaadha munneer,
seRiththittu ilangai malanga arakkan sezhuneeN mudithOLodu thaaL thuNiya,
aRuththittavan kaaNmin inRu aaychchiyaraal aLaiveNNey uNdu aappuNdirundhavanE.
960.meaning: He who broke the bow with ease, won the love of her with soft, dressed tresses and embraced her, became the young victor; spanning the unmoving ocean he hemmed in Lanka. He cut off the demon Ravana's stout, towering crowned heads together with his shoulders and feet, casting Lanka into ruin -- behold, that very one is He who, today among the cowherd women, ate the churned butter and stood bound for it.

961.translit:
surindhitta sengaezh uLaippong arimaath tholaiyap piriyaadhu senReydhi, eydhaa
dhirindhittu idangoNdu adangaadha than vaay irukooRu seydha perumaan munanaaL,
varindhitta villaal maramaezhum eydhu malaipOl uruvaththu oriraakkadhi mookku,
arindhittavan kaaNmin inRu aaychchiyaraal aLaiveNNey uNdu aappuNdirundhavanE.
961.meaning: Pursuing without leaving until it perished, He met the curly-maned red lion roaring with rage, and ripped apart its unrestrained mouth into two; the Lord who in former days, with His well-strung bow, pierced through the seven trees. He who cut off the nose of the night-roaming demoness of mountain-like form -- behold, that very one is He who, today among the cowherd women, ate the churned butter and stood bound for it.

962.translit:
ninRaar mugappuch chiRidhum ninaiyaan vayiTRai niRaippaan uRippaal thayir ney,
anRu aaychchiyar veNNey vizhungi uralOdu aappuNdirundha perumaan adimEl,
nanRaaya tholseer vayalmangaiyarkOn kaliyan olisey thamizhmaalai vallaar,
enRaanum eydhaar idar inpam eydhi imaiyOrkkum appaal selaveydhuvaarE.
962.meaning: Caring not the least for the faces of those who stood by, only to fill His belly He swallowed the milk, curd and ghee in the cowherd women's hanging pots, and that day He gulped the butter and stood bound to the mortar -- such is the Lord. Those who master this Tamil garland of resounding verse, full of fine ancient glory, composed by Kaliyan, lord of the fielded Mangai, will never at any time meet sorrow; they will attain bliss and journey beyond even the immortals.

963.translit:
maanam udaiththu ungaL aayar kulamadhanaal piRar makkaL thammai
oonam udaiyana seyyap peRaay enRu irappan urappakillaen,
naanum uraiththilaen nandhan paNiththilan nangaikaaL naan en seykaen?
thaanum Or kanniyum keezhai agaththuth thayirkadaikinRaan pOlum.
963.meaning: "Your cowherd clan has honor; so do no shameful things to other people's children" -- thus I beg him, yet I cannot scold him. I have said nothing, and Nanda has not commanded him; O ladies, what am I to do? It seems he and a young girl are churning curd in the house to the east.

964.translit:
kaalai ezhundhu kadaindha im mOrviRkap pOginRaen kaNdaepOnaen,
maalai naRunguñji nandhan makanallaal maTRu vandhaarum illai,
maelai agaththu nangaay vandhu kaaNminkaL veNNeyae anRu, irundha
paalum padhinkudam kaNdilaen paaviyaen en seykaen en seykaenO.
964.meaning: Rising at dawn, I went out to sell this churned buttermilk, and I saw and left; none came but Nanda's son with his fragrant dark locks. O lady of the house to the west, come and see! Not the butter only -- I cannot even find the ten pots of milk that were there. Sinful me, what am I to do, what am I to do?

965.translit:
theLLiya vaay chiRiyaan nangaikaaL uRimaelaith thadaa niRaindha,
veLLi malai irundhaal oththa veNNeyai vaari vizhungittu,
kaLvan uRangukinRaan vandhu kaaNminkaL kaiyellaam ney, vayiRu
piLLai param anRu iv vaezhulagum koLLum paedhaiyaen en seykaenO.
965.meaning: O ladies, this little one with a clever tongue scooped up and swallowed the butter that filled the high pot, heaped like a silver mountain. Come and see -- the thief lies there sleeping! His hands are all ghee, and his belly -- this is no mere child; he is the Supreme who can swallow these seven worlds. Foolish me, what am I to do?

966.translit:
mai nambu vaelkaN nallaaL munnam peTRa vaLaivaNNa nanmaa maeni,
than nambi nambiyum ingu vaLarndhadhu avan ivai sey thaRiyaan,
poy nambi puLLuvan kaLvam podhiyaRai pOginRavaa thavazhndhittu,
innambi nambiyaa aaychchiyarkku uyvillai en seykaen en seykaenO.
966.meaning: This conch-hued one of fine great form, born long ago to the good lady with kohl-bright spear-like eyes, this darling, this perfect one, grew up here -- and he knows nothing of doing such things! Yet he is a deceiver, a trickster, full of guile -- crawling off as if heading to the storeroom to rob it. Against this darling, this fellow, the cowherd women have no escape. What am I to do, what am I to do?

967.translit:
thandhai pugundhilan naan ingu irundhilaen thOzhimaar aarum illai,
sandha malarkkuzhalaaL thaniyae viLaiyaadum idam kuRugi,
pandhu paRiththuth thugil paTRik keeRip padiRan padiRu seyyum,
nandhan madhalaikku ingu en kadavOm? nangaay en seykaen en seykaenO.
967.meaning: My father did not come in, I was not here, and none of my friends were present; approaching the spot where the lass with flower-decked fragrant hair was playing alone, the rogue snatched her ball, seized her garment and tore it, working his mischief. What are we to do here against Nanda's child? O lady, what am I to do, what am I to do?

968.translit:
maNmagaL kaeLvan malarmangai naayagan nandhan peTRa madhalai,
aNNal ilaikkuzhal oodhi nam saerikkae allil thaan vandha pinnai,
kaNmalar sOrndhu mulai vandhu vimmik kamalach chevvaay veLuppa,
en magaL vaNNam irukkinRavaa nangaay en seykaen en seykaenO.
968.meaning: The consort of Earth-maiden, the lord of lotus-dwelling Lakshmi, the child born to Nanda, the noble one -- after he came into our quarter by night playing his leaf-flute, my daughter's flower-eyes grow languid, her breasts swell and heave, her red lotus-mouth turns pale. O lady, see the state my daughter is in! What am I to do, what am I to do?

969.translit:
aayiram kaNNudai indhiranaarukku enRu aayar vizha veduppa,
paasanam nallana paNdigaLaal puga peydha adhanai ellaam,
pOyirundhu angu oru pootha vadivu koNdu un makan inRu nangaay,
maayan adhanaiyellaam muTRa vaari vaLaiththu uNdirundhaan pOlum.
969.meaning: When the cowherds held a festival for thousand-eyed Indra and loaded good vessels onto carts and brought in all the offerings, your son went there today, O lady, and taking the form of a goblin, that trickster -- it seems he heaped it all up entirely, gathered it round and ate it up.

970.translit:
thOyththa thayirum naRuneyyum paalum Or Orkkudan thuTRidum enRu,
aaychchiyar koodi azhaikkavum naan idhaRku eLgi ivanai nangaay,
sOththam piraan ivai seyyap peRaay enRu irappan urappakillaen,
paeychchi mulai uNda pinnai ip piLLaiyaip paesuvadhu añjuvaenE.
970.meaning: Saying that he gobbles up the soaked curd, the fragrant ghee and the milk all in one go, the cowherd women gather and call out; ashamed of this, O lady, I plead with him, "Bless you, Lord, do not do these things" -- yet I cannot scold him. Ever since he drank from the demoness's breast, I am afraid even to speak of this child.

971.translit:
eedum valiyum udaiya in nambi piRandha aezhu thingaLil,
aadalar kaNNiyinaanai vaLarththi yamunai neeraadap pOnaen,
saedan thirumaRu maarvan kidandhu thiruvadiyaal, malaipOla
oodum sakadaththaich chaadiya pinnai urappuvadhu añjuvaenE.
971.meaning: This darling, possessed of might and strength, in the seventh month after his birth -- I had reared the child garlanded with playful flowers and gone to bathe in the Yamuna's waters; he who rests on Adisesha, who bears the Srivatsa mark on his chest, as he lay there, with his sacred foot kicked over the cart that was rolling on like a mountain. After that, I am afraid to scold him.

972.translit:
añjuvan solli azhaiththida nangaikaaL aayira naazhi neyyai,
pañjiyal melladip piLLagaL uNkinRa paagandhaan vaiyaarkaLae,
kañjan kadiyan kaRaveTTu naaLil en kai valaththaadhum illai,
neñjaththu iruppana seydhu vaiththaay nambee en seykaen en seykaenO.
972.meaning: O ladies, I am afraid to call out and speak; do they not set aside any share of the thousand measures of ghee for the children with cotton-soft tender feet to eat? Kamsa is cruel, and on the day the cows yield no milk there is nothing left in my hand. You have done the very things that fester in my heart, O darling! What am I to do, what am I to do?

973.translit:
angnganam theemaigaL seyvargaLO nambee aayar madamakkaLaip,
pangaya neer kudaindhu aadukinRaargaL pinnae senRu oLiththirundhu,
angu avar poondhugil vaarikkoNdittu aravaeridaiyaar irappa,
mangai nalleer vandhu koNmin enRu maram aeRi irundhaay pOlum.
973.meaning: Do they do such mischief, O darling? When the tender daughters of the cowherds were bathing, dipping into the lotus-pool, you went behind them and hid, gathered up their lovely garments, and when the slender-waisted ones pleaded, you sat up in the tree saying "Fair maidens, come and take them" -- so it seems.

974.translit:
achcham thinaiththanai illai ip piLLaikku aaNmaiyum saevagamum,
uchchiyil muththi vaLarththu eduththaenukku uraiththilan thaan ninRu pOy,
pachchilaip poongadam aeRi visaikoNdu paayndhu pukku, aayiravaay
nachchazhal poykaiyil naagaththinOdu piNangi nee vandhaay pOlum.
974.meaning: This child has not a grain of fear -- such valor and prowess! To me who reared and raised him, kissing his crown, he said nothing; he just went off, climbed the flowering kadamba of fresh leaves, and with great force leapt and plunged in, and in the pool of poisonous, fiery, thousand-mouthed venom you wrestled with the serpent and came back -- so it seems.

975.translit:
thambaram allan aaNmaigaLaith thaniyae ninRu thaam seyvaarO?
emperumaan unnaip peTRa vayiRudaiyaen ini yaan en seykaen?
amparam aezhum adhirum idikural angu anaR sengaN udai,
vambavizh kaanaththu maalvidaiyOdu piNangi nee vandhaay pOlum.
975.meaning: These are not feats within their power -- would they stand all alone and do such things? My Lord, I who bore you in my womb, what am I now to do? In the fragrance-laden forest, against the great bulls with fiery red eyes and thunderous bellowing that shook all seven skies, you wrestled and came back -- so it seems.

976.translit:
anna nadaimada aaychchi vayiRadiththu añja aruvarai pOla,
mannu karunkaLiTRu aaruyir vavviya maindhanai maakadal soozhu,
kanni nanmaamadhiL mangaiyar kaavalan kaamaru seerkkalikanRi
innisai maalaigaL eer aezhum vallavarkku aedhum idarillaiyae.
976.meaning: The youth who seized the dear life of the firm-set dark elephant like a great mountain, making the swan-gaited, gentle cowherd woman strike her belly in fear -- of him sang Kalikanri, the guardian of Mangai with its fine great virgin walls girt by the great sea, full of desirable glory. For those who can master these twice-seven sweet-toned garland-verses, there is no sorrow at all.

977.translit:
kaadhil kadippidukku alingam uduththu,
thaadhu nalladhu aNNandh thuzhaykodu aNindhu,
pOdhum aRuththup puRamae vandhu ninReer,
aedhukku idhu enn idhu enn idhu ennO.
977.meaning: With ornaments tucked in your ears, clad in fine silken cloth, adorned with the pollen-rich, lovely fresh tulasi, you have come and stood outside, taking your leisure. For what is this? What is this, what is this, what indeed?

978.translit:
thuvar aadai uduththu oru seNdu siluppi,
kavar aagam mudiththuk kalikkachchuk katti,
suvaraar kadhavin puRamae vandhu ninReer,
ivar aar idhu enn idhu enn idhu ennO.
