14.translit:
saayaal kariyaanai yuLLaRiyaa raaynenjee,
peeyaar mulaikoduttaar peeyaraay,-neeyaarpOyth
theempooN suvaitthoo naRindhaRindhum, theevinaiyaam
paambaarvaayk kaineettal paartthu.
14.meaning: O heart, those who did not inwardly know the one dark in hue (Putana) gave him their breast and themselves became demons. Who are you to go forth, even while knowing full well the taste of the honeyed sweetness you savor, and to stretch out your hand into the mouth of the serpent that is evil karma?

15.translit:
paartthOr edhiridhaa nenjee, paduthuyaram
peertthOdhap peedazhivaam peechchillai,-aartthOdham
thammeeni thaaLthadavath thaangidandhu, thammudaiya
semmeenik kaNvaLarvaar seer.
15.meaning: O heart, look and consider: there is no use in speech that, by recounting the suffering we endure, only brings loss of dignity. Rather praise the glory of him who lies resting, his red form's eyes in yogic sleep, while the roaring ocean's waves stroke and caress his feet.

16.translit:
seeraal piRandhu siRappaal vaLaraadhu,
peervaama naakaakkaal peeraaLaa,-maarbaarap
pulkinee yuNdumizhndha poominee reeRparidhee?
sollunee yaamaRichchoozhndhu.
16.meaning: If you had not been born in glory and grown in splendor, and not become the renowned Vamana, O great Lord -- would it have been hard for you to receive the water poured as a gift for this earth which you embraced to your chest, swallowed and spat forth? Tell us, that we may know, after considering.

17.translit:
soozhndhadiyaar veeNdinakkaal thOnRaadhu vittaalum
vaazhndhiduvar pinnumtham vaaithiRavaar,-soozhndhengum
vaaLvaraikaL pOlarakkan vandhalaikaL thaamidiya,
thaaLvaraivil leendhinaar thaam.
17.meaning: Even if, when his devotees earnestly pray with deep thought, he does not appear and lets it pass, they go on living and do not again open their mouths to complain -- those who praise him who bent and held the mountain-like bow upon his foot, when the demon (Ravana), like gleaming mountains, came surrounding on every side, so that his waves of attack were crushed.

18.translit:
thaambaalaap puNdaalum aththazhumbu thaaniLaka,
paambaalaap puNdupaa dutRRaalum,-sOmbaadhip
palluruvai yellaam padarvittha vitthaa, un
tholluruvai yaaraRivaar sollu.
18.meaning: Even bound by the rope and bearing that welt's mark until it grew soft (as Krishna bound by Yashoda), even when bound and suffering distress upon the serpent (over Kaliya, or as the one who rests on Adishesha) -- O Seed who, untiring, spread forth all these many forms! Tell us, who can know your ancient, original form?

19.translit:
sollil kuRaiyillais soodhaRiyaa nenjamee,
elli pakalennaa theppOdhum,-thollaik kaN
maatthaanaik kellaamOr aivaraiyee maaRaaka,
kaatthaanaik kaaNdumnee kaaN.
19.meaning: O heart that knows no guile, there is no lack in words: at all times, by night or day, without distinction, we shall behold -- so behold -- him who in ancient time guarded the great host, with the five (the Pandavas) as the sole counterforce to all.

20.translit:
kaaNap pukilaRivu kaikkoNDa nanneñjam,
naaNap paDumanRee naampeesil?-maaNi
uruvaakik koNDulakam neereeRRa seeraan,
tiruvaakam teeNDiRRuc cenRu.
20.meaning: If we speak of it, will not the good heart that has gained understanding feel shame to look upon it? For the Glorious One, who took the form of a dwarf brahmin (Vamana) and had the world poured out to him in water, went and touched his sacred body.

21.translit:
cenRangku vennarakil seeraamal kaappataRku,
inRingken neñjaal iDukkuNDa,-anRangkup
paaruruvum paarvaLaitta neeruruvum kaNputaiya,
kaaruruvan taannimirtta kaal.
21.meaning: To protect us from going and falling into the fierce hell yonder, today the foot which the cloud-hued Lord stretched out and raised long ago - when the form of the earth and the form of the water surrounding the earth were both hidden from sight - is fixed firmly in my heart this day.

22.translit:
kaalee potattirintu kattuva raaminanaaL,
maalaar kuDipukuntaa renmanattee,-meelaal
tarukkumiDam paaTTinooDum valvinaiyaar taam,
veeRRirukkumiDam kaaNaa tiLaittu.
22.meaning: On the day the messengers of death wander about with their feet and shout 'Seize him!', the great Lord Tirumal has entered to dwell within my heart. Henceforth my powerful evil deeds, growing weary, find no place to stand and reign, no place to settle and abide.

23.translit:
iLaippaa yiLaiyaappaay neñjamee! conneen,
iLaikka namantamarkaL paRRi - iLaippeyta,
naaytantu mootaamal nalkuvaan nalkaappaan,
taaytantai evvuyirkkum taan.
23.meaning: O my heart, do not grow weak; I have told you - do not weaken though the servants of Yama seize you. He who gives is the one who, without setting dogs upon us to harass and exhaust us, bestows his grace; he is mother and father to every living being.

24.translit:
taanee tanittoonRal tannaLappon Rillaataan
taanee piRarkaTkum taRRoonRal,- taanee
iLaikkiRpaar keezhmeelaam meeNDa maippaanaanaal,
aLakkiRpaar paarinmeel aar.
24.meaning: He alone is the unique primal source, he who has no measure of himself; he alone is the very self that arises for others too. If he himself is the one who fashions and restores the high and low states of those who grow weary, then who upon this earth is able to measure him?

25.translit:
aaraanum aataanum ceyya, akaliDattai
aaraayn tatutiruttal aavatee?,-seeraar
manattalaivan tunpattai maaRRineen,vaanoor
inattalaivan kaNNanaal yaan.
25.meaning: Can the wide world be set right by examining and amending whatever anyone has done? I, through Kannan who is the lord of the host of celestials, have removed the sorrow of the heart's glorious master.

26.translit:
yaanumen neñjum icaintozhintoom, valvinaiyaik
kaanum malaiyum pukakkaDivaan,- taanoor
iruLanna maameeni emmiRaiyaar tanta,
aruLennum taNDaal aDittu.
26.meaning: I and my heart have agreed together to drive away our powerful evil deeds, making them flee into forest and mountain, beating them with the staff called grace which our Lord - he of the great body dark as darkness itself - has given.

27.translit:
aDiyaal paDikaDanta muttoo,atanReel
muDiyaal vicumpaLanta muttoo,-neDiyaay.
ceRikazhalkaL taaLnimirttuc cenRulaka mellaam,
aRikilaamaal neeyaLanta anRu.
27.meaning: Is it the pearl that crossed the earth with your foot, or rather the pearl that measured the sky with your crown? O tall one, on that day when you stretched out your feet adorned with close-fitting anklets and went and measured all the worlds, no one could comprehend it.

28.translit:
anReenang kaNkaaNum aazhiyaan kaaruruvam,
inReenaam kaaNaa tiruppatu vum,-enReenum
kaTkaNNaal kaaNaata avvuruvai, neñjennum
uTkaNNaal kaaNu muNarntu.
28.meaning: Was it then that our eyes saw the cloud-dark form of the discus-bearing Lord? Or is it now that we remain unable to see him? That form which the outer eyes can never behold at any time, one may see through realization with the inner eye called the heart.

29.translit:
uNara oruvark keLiyanee? cevvee,
iNarum tuzhaayalangkal entai,-uNarat
tanakkeLiya revvaLavar avvaLava naanaal,
enakkeLiyan emmperumaan ingku.
29.meaning: Is he easily known by anyone, my Father adorned with a garland of clustering tulasi? Truly, if he becomes as accessible as those to whom he is easy of access when they strive to realize him, then my Lord is easy of access to me here too.

30.translit:
ingkillai paNDupool veeRRiruttal, ennuDaiya
cengkaNmaal seerkkum ciRituLLam,-angkee
maDiyaDakki niRpatanil valvi naiyaar taam,meeN
DaDiyeDuppa tanRoo azhaku?
30.meaning: Henceforth my little heart will not sit enthroned aloof as before, away from the glory of my red-eyed Lord Tirumal. Rather than standing there idly with hands folded, is it not fitting that the powerful evil deeds should turn back and lift their feet to depart?

31.translit:
azhaku maRivoomaay valvinaiyum teerppaan,
nizhalum aDitooRum aanoom,-suzhalak
kuDangkaLtalai meeteDuttuk koNDaaDi, anRat
taDangkaDalai meeyaar tamakku.
31.meaning: Knowing what is fitting, and to one who removes our powerful evil deeds, we have become the shade at each of his feet - to him who long ago lifted pots upon his head and danced (in the kudakkuttu dance), and who rests upon the vast ocean.

32.translit:
tamakkaDimai veeNDuvoor taamoo taranaar,
tamakkaDimai ceyyenRaal ceyyaatu,-emakkenRu
taamceyyum teevinaikkee taazhvuRuvar neñjinaar,
yaañceyva tivviDatting kiyaatu?
32.meaning: Damodara is the one who desires service to himself; yet when he bids them serve him, the hearts that do not do so sink low through the very evil deeds they perform for their own sake. What am I to do here in this matter?

33.translit:
yaataanum onRaRiyil tannukakkil enkoloo,
yaataanum neerntaNukaa vaaRutaan?,- yaataanum
teeRumaa ceyyaa asurarkaLai, neemiyaal
paaRupaa Raakkinaan paal.
33.meaning: If one comes to know anything at all, and if he delights in him, what reason is there for not in any way drawing near to him - to him who with his discus tore into pieces the demons who would not act so as to attain clarity of mind?

34.translit:
paalaazhi neekiDakkum paNpai,yaam keeTTeeyum
kaalaazhum neñjazhiyum kaNsuzhalum,-neelaazhic
cootiyaay! aatiyaay! tolvinaiyem paalkaDiyum,
neetiyaay! niRcaarntu ninRu.
34.meaning: Even merely hearing of the manner in which you recline upon the ocean of milk, our feet give way, our heart melts, our eyes swim. O radiance shining upon the blue ocean! O primal one! O righteous one who, when we draw near and stand by you, drives away our ancient karma from us!

35.translit:
ninRum iruntum kiDantum tiritantum,
onRumoo aaRRaanen neñjakalaan,-anRangkai
vanpuDaiyaal ponpeyaroon vaaytakarttu maarviDantaan,
anpuDaiya nanRee yavan?
35.meaning: Whether standing, sitting, lying, or roaming about, by no single means is he able to leave my heart, and he does not depart from it. Is he not indeed full of love - he who long ago with the strength of his hand smashed the mouth of Hiranya, named for gold, and tore open his chest?

36.translit:
avanaam ivanaam uvanaam, maRRumpa
ravanaam avanen Diraatee,- avanaam
avanee enatteLintu kaNNanukkee teerntaal,
avanee evaneelum aam.
36.meaning: Do not remain saying 'it is that one, this one, that yonder one, or some other one' - but clearly realizing that he is that very He, if one becomes resolved for Kannan alone, then he becomes one's own, whoever he may be.

37.translit:
aamaa RaRivuDaiyaar aava taritanRee?
naamee atuvuDaiyoom nanneñjee,-puumeey
matukaramee taNDuzhaay maalaarai, vaazhttaam
atukaramee anpaal amai.
37.meaning: Is it not rare to become those who have the wisdom of the right way? We ourselves possess it, O good heart. O honey-bee that hovers over flowers, the proper deed is to praise Tirumal of the cool tulasi - settle yourself to that with love.

38.translit:
amaikkum pozhutuNDee yaaraayil neñjee,
imaikkum pozhutum iDaicci - kumaittiRangkaL,
eesiyee yaayinum eentuzhaay maayanaiyee,
peesiyee pookkaay pizhai.
38.meaning: Is there, if you consider it, O heart, even the time of a blink to compose oneself? Even if it be by chiding the wondrous Lord of the sweet tulasi - as the cowherdess railed at him over the ways he was tormented (churned) - by speaking of him alone, drive away your faults.

39.translit:
pizhaikka muyanRoomoo neñjamee! peesaay,
tazhaikkum tuzhaaymaarvan Rannai,-azhaittorukaal
pooyupakaa rampoliyak koLLaatu, avan pukazhee
vaayupakaa ramkoNDa vaayppu.
39.meaning: Have we striven to escape him, O heart? Speak! Calling upon him whose chest bears the flourishing tulasi, and not going to receive his help abundantly even once - the good fortune that has come to us is to have taken his glory itself as the help upon our lips.

80.translit:
piRappiRappu mooppup piNithuRanthu, pinnum
iRakkavum inpudaiththaa meelum,-maRappellaam
eethamee yenRallaal eNNuvanee, maNNaLanthaan
paadhamee yeethaap pagal.
80.meaning: Renouncing birth and death, old age and disease, and beyond that finding even dying to be sweet, I would deem all forgetfulness to be nothing but harm. The day that does not praise the feet of Him who measured the earth-such a day I count as utterly worthless.

81.translit:
pagaliraa enpadhuvum paaviyaadhu, emmai
igalsey thirupozhudhum aaLvar,--thagavaath
thozhumpar ivar, seerkkum thuNaiyilar enRooraar,
sezhumparavai meeyaar therindhu.
81.meaning: Without considering whether it is day or night, He rules over us at both times, even striving against us. He, who reclines upon the rich ocean, does not discern that these servants are unworthy and have no helper to support them.

82.translit:
therinthuNarvon RinmaiyaaL theevinaiyeen,
vaaLaa irundhozhintheen keezhnaaLkaL ellaam,-karandhuruvil
ammanai annaanRu pindhodarndha aazhiyangai
ammaanai yeethaa dhayarndhu.
82.meaning: Because I had no discerning knowledge, I, the man of evil deeds, passed all my former days idly in vain. I grew weary and failed to praise the Lord of the beautiful discus-bearing hand, who in that day pursued the demoness Putana hidden in disguise.

83.translit:
ayarppaay ayaraappaaya nenjamee. sonneen
uyappoom neRiyidhuvee kaNdaay, - seyaRpaala
allavee seygiRudhi nenjamee! anjineen
mallarnaaL vavvinanai vaazhththu.
83.meaning: O heart, you who forget yet are made not to forget-I have told you: behold, this is the very path of salvation. Yet, O heart, you do what ought not to be done! I am afraid. Praise Him who seized away the day of the wrestlers (slaying them).

84.translit:
vaazhththi avanadiyaiyp pooppu nainthu, nindhalaiyaith
thaazhththirukai kooppenRaal kooppaadhu-paazhththavidhi,
enguRRaay enRavanai eethaadhen nenjamee,
thangaththaa naameelum thangu.
84.meaning: Praising Him, adorning His feet with flowers, bowing your head low and folding your two hands-when told to do so, you do it not, O my ruined and wretched heart. Though it be that I do not even cry out 'where are You?' and praise Him, yet stay-remain steadfast, O heart, even though it be hard.

85.translit:
thangaa muyaRRiyavaayth thaazhvisumpin meedhupaayndhu,
engeepuk keththavamsey thittanakol,-pongoodhath
thaNNampaal veelaivaayk kaNvaLarum, ennudaiya
kaNNanpaal nanniRangoL kaar.
85.meaning: With unceasing effort the rain-clouds leap up over the lowering sky-where did they go and what penance did they perform? For they have taken on the fair hue of my Kannan, who sleeps upon the cool, lovely ocean of milk with its surging waves.

86.translit:
kaarkkalandha meeniyaan kaikalandha aazhiyaan,
paarkkalandha valvayiRRaan paampaNaiyaan,-seerkalandha
solninaindhu pookkaareel soozhvinaiyin aazhthuyarai,
enninaindhu pookkuvarip poodhu.
86.meaning: He whose body is blended with the dark cloud's hue, who holds the discus joined to His hand, in whose mighty belly the whole earth was contained, who reclines on the serpent couch-if men do not drive away their sorrow by meditating on words filled with His glory, by what other means will they dispel the deep grief of encircling karma in this present time?

87.translit:
ippoodhum innum inichchiRudhu ninRaalum
eppoodhu meedheesol ennenjee--eppoodhum
kaikazhalaa neemiyaan nammeel vinaikadivaan
meykazhalee eeththa muyal.
87.meaning: Whether now, or yet a little while later if you linger-at every time, O my heart, say this same thing: strive always to praise the true feet of Him who bears the discus that never leaves His hand, so that He may drive away the karma that besets us.

40.translit:
vaaippoo ithuvoppa maRRillai vaanenjee,
poaippooy vennarakil pooviyeel,-theeppaala
peeyttaai uyirkkaLaaip paaluNdu, avaLuyirai
maaittaanai vaazhtee vali.
40.meaning: O my soaring heart, there is no opportunity equal to this; do not go and fall into the burning hell. Praise instead Him who, made of the very fire, drank the milk of the demoness who came as a deceitful mother, and so destroyed her life. Take that as your strength.

41.translit:
valiyam enaninainthu vantethirntha mallar
valiya mudiyidiya vaangi,-valiyanin
ponnaazhik kaiyaal pudaittiduthi keeLaathee,
pannaaLum niRkumip paar.
41.meaning: You seized and dashed down the mighty crowns of the wrestlers who came confronting You, thinking themselves strong. May You strike with Your strong hand that bears the golden discus, so that this earth, undestroyed, may endure for endless days.

42.translit:
paaruNdaan paarumizhnthaan paaridanthaan paaraLanthaan
paaridam munpadaittaa nenparaal,- paaridam
aavaanum thaanaanaal aaridamee?, maRRoruvarkku
aavaan pookaavaal avai.
42.meaning: They say He swallowed the earth, He gave the earth back, He lifted the earth, He measured the earth, and that He first created the earth. If He Himself becomes the whole earth, who is left? For whom else could these worlds become a refuge or support?

43.translit:
avaya menaninainthu vanthasurar paalee,
navaiyai naLirvippaan Rannai,-kavaiyil
manattuyara vaittirunthu vaazhttaathaark kuNdoo,
manattuyarai maaikkum vakai.
43.meaning: For those who do not keep Him steadfastly in an undivided, exalted mind and praise Him - He who brings ruin upon the asuras that came thinking themselves invincible - is there any way at all to destroy the sorrow of the mind?

44.translit:
vakaiseerntha nannenjum naavudaiya vaayum,
mikavaainthu veezhaa enilum,-mikavaainthu
maalaittaam vaazhttaa thiruppar ithuvanRee,
meelaittaam seyyum vinai.
44.meaning: Though they possess a fitting good heart and a tongued mouth richly endowed, and do not fall away, yet they keenly refrain from praising Tirumal. Is this not the very fruit of the karma they accumulated in former times?

45.translit:
vinaiyaar tharamuyalum vemmaiyee yanji,
thinaiyaam siRithaLavum sella - ninaiyaathu
vaasakathaal leettineen vaanoor thozhuthiRainjum,
naayakattaan ponnadikaL naan.
45.meaning: Fearing the fierce heat of karma that strives to torment, and so that it may not advance even by the smallest measure of a millet grain, I, without other thought, praised with my words the golden feet of the Lord whom the celestials worship and bow before.

46.translit:
naankooRum kooRRava thittanaiyee, naaNaaLum
theengoatha neeruruvan sengaNmaal,-neengaatha
maakathiyaam vennarakil seeraamal kaappathaRku,
neekathiyaa nenjee! ninai.
46.meaning: What I declare is only this much: the red-eyed Tirumal whose form is like the sweet, surging ocean of water. O heart, to be saved from falling into the burning hell that is an unending path, take Him as your refuge and meditate on Him.

47.translit:
ninittiRainjisi maanidavar onRirappa renRee,
ninaittidavum veeNdaanee neeree,-ninaittiRanja
evvaLava revvidattoor maalee, athuthaanum
evvaLavu muNdoo emakku.
47.meaning: You need not even think that mortals, having thought and worshipped, will beg of You for one thing; do not regard it directly so. O Tirumal, how great and from what places are those who think on You and bow! And even that - is it of any measure for us?

48.translit:
emakkiyaam viNNaaTTuk kuchchamathaam veeTTai,
amaittirunthoom aHthanRee yaamaaRu,-amaippolintha
menthoaLi kaaraNamaa vengoaTTee Reezhudanee,
konRaanai yeemanattuk koNdu.
48.meaning: Holding firmly in our minds Him who, for the sake of the soft-shouldered maiden, slew together the seven fierce-horned bulls, we ourselves have made ready the abode of Liberation that is even higher than the realm of heaven. Is that not indeed our way of being?

49.translit:
koNdalthaan maalvaraithaan maakadalthaan kooriruLthaan
vaNdaRaap poovathaan maRRuttaan,-kaNdanaaL
kaaruruvam kaaNthoaRum nenjoodum, kaNNanaar
peeruruven Remmaip pirinthu.
49.meaning: The rain-cloud, the great dark mountain, the vast ocean, the dense darkness, the blossom never free of bees, and all else - whenever, since the day we beheld Him, we see a dark form, our heart rushes after it, parting from us, saying 'this is the great form of Kannan.'

50.translit:
pirinthonRu noakkaathu thammudaiya pinnee,
thirinthuzhulum sinthanaiyaar thammai,-purinthorukaal
aavaa! enavirangaar anthoo! valitheekol,
maavai piLanthaar manam.
50.meaning: For those of devoted mind who, looking at nothing else apart from Him, wander ceaselessly behind Him - He does not even once turn with affection and say 'Alas, alas!' to comfort them. O how hard, alas, is the heart of Him who split apart the horse-demon!

51.translit:
manavaaLum ooraivar vankuRumpar thammai,
sinamaaLvit thooridattee seerttu-punameeya
thaNduzhaa yaanadiyee thaankaaNum aHthanRee,
vaNduzhaam seeraakku maaNpu.
51.meaning: Subduing in their place, through anger, the five powerful unruly ones who rule the mind, one beholds the feet of Him who wears the cool tulasi that thrives in the field. Is that not the glory that makes one worthy of the fragrance over which bees swarm?

52.translit:
maaNpaavit tanjnjaanRu maNNiranthaan, maayavaLnanj
chooNpaavit tuNdaana thooruruvam,-kaaNpaannang
kaNNavaa maRRonRu kaaNuRaa, seerparavaa
tuNNavaai thaanuRumoo onRu.
52.meaning: He who, on that day taking the form of a dwarf, begged for the earth - that same form which drank up the poison-laden breast of the deceitful she-demon. Our eyes long to behold it and care to see nothing else; and without praising His glory, will the mouth attain anything to feed upon?

53.translit:
onRuNdu sengaNmaal. yaanuraippathu,
unnadiyaark kenjeyva nenRee yiriCCini,-ninpukazhil
vaikumtham sinthaiyilum maRRinithoo, neeyavarkku
vaikuntha menRaruLum vaan.
53.meaning: O red-eyed Tirumal, there is one thing I would say. You sit pondering, 'What shall I do for my devotees?' But is even the heaven You graciously grant them, calling it Vaikunta, sweeter to them than their own minds that dwell in praising Your glory?

54.translit:
vaanoo? maRikadaloo? maaruthamoo? theeyakamoo?
kaanoo? orung kiRRum kaNdilamaal,- aaneenRa
kanRuyaratt taameRinthu kaayuthirttaar thaaLpaNinthoom,
vanthuyarai yaavaa! marungu.
54.meaning: Is it the sky? the surging sea? the wind? the inner fire? the forest? We have not been able to see Him fully in any one of them. We have bowed at the feet of Him who, raising the calf high, hurled it and shook down the wood-apple fruits; alas, our great sorrow stands near.

55.translit:
marungoatha moathum maNinaa kaNaiyaar,
marungee varavariya reelum,-orung kee
emakkavaraik kaaNalaa meppoathu muLLaal,
manakkavalai theerppaar varavu.
55.meaning: He who reclines on the jeweled serpent, around which the ocean dashes its waters, may be hard to come near. Yet for us He can always be seen close by; the coming of Him who dispels the worry of the mind is ever within.

56.translit:
varavaaRon Rillaiyaal vaazhvinithaal, ellee!
oruvaaRoruvan pukavaaRu,-urumaaRum
aayavarthaam seeyavarthaam anRulakam thaayavarthaam,
maayavarthaam kaaTTum vazhi.
56.meaning: O wonder! There is no fixed way of coming, and yet life is sweet; there is some single way by which one may enter. He who took various forms, He who is the cowherd, He who is the distant one, He who once measured the worlds, the wondrous one - it is the path that He Himself shows.

57.translit:
vazhittangu valvinaiyai maaRRaanoo nenjee,
thazheeikkoNdu poaravuNan Rannai,-suzhittengum
thaazhvidangaL paRRip pulaalveLLam thaanukaLa,
vaazhvadanga maarvidantha maal.
57.meaning: O heart, will not Tirumal drive away the cruel karma that clings along the way - He who, embracing the warrior asura, tore open his chest so that, swirling everywhere and seizing the low places, a flood of flesh and blood ran while the asura's life was wholly extinguished?

58.translit:
maalee. padiCCoathi maaRReel, iniyunathu
paaleepoal seeril puzhuttozhintheen,-meelaal
piRappinmai peRRadikkeezhk kuRReeva lanRu,
maRappinmai yaanveeNdum maadu.
58.meaning: O Tirumal, radiant in form, do not change. From now I have come to dwell, like a worm, within Your glory that is sweet as milk. Not freedom from rebirth, nor menial service at Your feet hereafter - the wealth I ask of You is only never to forget You.

59.translit:
maadee varappeRuva raamenRee, valvinaiyaar
kaadaanum aathaanum kaikoLLaar,- oodeepoaip
poaroatham sinthuthiraik kaNvaLarum, peeraaLan
peeroatha chinthikkap peernthu.
59.meaning: Those of cruel karma will not take to heart even the forest or any path, thinking they may draw near Him. Passing within, one must turn away and meditate on the great name of the mighty Lord who slumbers upon the warring ocean that scatters its waves.

60.translit:
peerndhonRu nookkaadhu pinniRpaay nillaappaay
eenthuzhaay maayanaiyee enneNjee,- peerndhengum
tollaimaa vennaragil seeraamal kaappadhaRku
illaikaaN maRROr iRai.
60.meaning: O my heart, do not look elsewhere turning away, do not stand back, but stand firm upon the wondrous Lord who wears the fresh tulasi garland. For there is no other God, see, who can guard you from ever falling into the ancient, terrible, fierce hell anywhere.

61.translit:
iRaimuRaiyaan seevadimeel maNNaLandha annaaL,
maRaimuRaiyaal vaanaadar koodi,-muRaimuRaiyin
thaadhilagu poottheLiththaal ovvaadhee, thaazhvisumbin
meedhilagith thaangidakkum meen.
61.meaning: On that day when He measured the earth and set His sacred feet upon the head of the ruling king (Bali), the celestials of the heavenly world gathered in due order according to the Vedas. The stars that shine high in the lowered sky cannot compare to the pollen-rich flowers they scattered and showered upon Him.

62.translit:
meenennum kambil veRiyennum veLLiveey
vaanennum keedilaa vaankudaikku,-thaanOr
maNikkaambu poolnimirndhu maNNaLandhaan,nangaL
piNikkaam perumarundhu pin.
62.meaning: For the great imperishable canopy of the sky, with stars as its supporting frame and fragrance as its silver ribs, He who measured the earth stood tall like a single jewelled staff (handle). Thereafter He is the great medicine for our afflictions.

63.translit:
pinthurakkum kaaRRizhandha soolkoNdal peerndhum pooy,
vanthiraikkaN vandhaNaindha vaaymaiththee, anRu
thiruchcheyya neemiyaan theeyarakki mookkum,
paruchcheviyu meerndha paran.
63.meaning: The Supreme One who bears the auspicious red discus, who on that day severed the nose and the heavy ears of the wicked demoness (Surpanakha), is like a pregnant rain-cloud that, losing the wind that drives it onward, moves away and comes to rest upon the strong-waved ocean.

64.translit:
paranaam avanaadhal paavippa raagil,
uranaa lorumoonRu poodhum,-marameezhan
Reydhaanaip puLLinvaay keeNdaanaiyee,amarar
kaidhaan thozhaavee kalandhu.
64.meaning: If they would meditate steadfastly, with strength of mind, three times each day upon Him as the Supreme One, upon Him who pierced the seven trees with a single arrow and who tore open the beak of the bird-demon (Bakasura), would not even the immortals join together and worship with folded hands?

65.translit:
kalandhu naliyum kadunthuyarai neNjee
malanga adiththu madippaan,-vilangalpool
thonmaalaik keesavanai naaraNanai maadhavanai,
sonmaalai yeppozhudhum soottu.
65.meaning: O heart, to strike down and crush the fierce sorrows that mingle with us and torment us, weave and ever offer your garland of words to Kesava, to Narayana, to Madhava, the ancient Tirumal who stands tall like a mountain.

66.translit:
soottaaya neemiyaan tollarakkan innuyirai,
maattee thuyarizhaiththa maayavanai,-eetta
veRikoNda thaNduzhaay veedhiyanai, neNjee!
aRikaNdaay sonneen adhu.
66.meaning: O heart! Know Him, the Lord who bears the discus as His adornment, the wondrous one who wrought destruction upon the sweet life of the ancient demon (Ravana), the Vedic Lord who wears the cool fragrant tulasi gathered in clusters. This I have told you, so realize it.

67.translit:
anuvOnan RenRang kamarulagO veeNdil
adhuvOr poruLillai yanRee? - adhuvozhindhu
maNNinRaaL venenilung koodum madaneNjee!
kaNNanthaaL vaazhththuvadhee kal.
67.meaning: O foolish heart! If you desire that immortal world as the highest good, is that not a thing easily attainable? And even if, leaving that aside, you should wish to rule upon this earth, that too can be had. Learn therefore only to praise the feet of Kannan.

68.translit:
kallum kanaikadalum vaikundha vaanaadum,
pullen Razhin dhanakol eepaavam,-vella
nediyaan niRangariyaan uLpugundhu neengaan,
adiyeena thuLLath thagam.
68.meaning: Alas, what a pity! Have the hills, the roaring ocean, and the heavenly realm of Vaikuntha all turned faded and forsaken? For the tall Lord of dark hue, having entered the heart-place within me His servant, abides there and does not leave.

69.translit:
agamsivandha kaNNinaraay valviniya raavaar,
mugamsidhaiva raamanRee mukki,-migundhirumaal
seerkkadalai yuLpodhindha sindhanaiyeen Rannai,
aarkkadalaam sevvee yadarththu?
69.meaning: Those of great evil deeds become red-eyed with wrath and their faces grow distorted, gasping in distress. But I, whose thoughts have enclosed within them the ocean of glory that is the great Tirumal, who is there that could ever truly press upon and overcome me?

70.translit:
adarppon mudiyaanai yaayirambee raanai,
sudarkoL sudaraazhi yaanai,-idarkadiyum
maadhaa pidhuvaaga vaiththeen enadhuLLee
yaadhaagil yaadhee ini.
70.meaning: Him of the close-set golden crown, Him of a thousand names, Him who bears the radiant blazing discus, who drives away all distress, I have established within my heart as my mother and my father. Whatever may come now, let it come, what does it matter to me henceforth?

71.translit:
inininRu ninperumai yaanuraippa dhennee,
thanininRa saarvilaa moorththi,- panineer
agaththulavu seNjadaiyaan aagaththaan, naangu
mugaththaannin undhi mudhal.
71.meaning: O Lord who stands alone, without any support, how can I now stand and tell of Your greatness? The one of red matted locks (Shiva) in whose locks the cool waters (Ganga) roam dwells in Your body, and the four-faced one (Brahma) has Your navel as his origin.

72.translit:
mudhalaam thiruvuruvam moonRenbar, onRee
mudhalaagum moonRukkum menbar - mudhalvaa,
nigarilagu kaaruruvaa! ninnagaththa thanRee,
pugarilagu thaamaraiyin poo.
72.meaning: They say the primal sacred forms are three; yet they also say that One alone is the origin of all three. O Primal One, O peerless shining one of dark cloud-like form! Is not the lotus flower of radiant lustre (Brahma's seat) within Your very body?

73.translit:
poovaiyum kaayaavum neelamum pookkinRa,
kaavi malarenRum kaaNthoRum, paaviyeen
mellaavi meymigavee poorikkum, avvavai
ellaam piraanuruvee enRu.
73.meaning: Whenever I behold the poovai blossom, the kaayaa flower, the blue lily, and the blooming water-lily (kaavi), the tender soul of sinful me swells and thrills in body, knowing that all these (dark blue forms) are the very form of my Lord.

74.translit:
enRum orunaaL ozhiyaamai yaanirandhaal,
onRum irangaar urukkaattaar,-kunRu
kudaiyaaga aagaaththa koovalanaar, neNjee!
pudaidhaan peridhee puvi.
74.meaning: Though I beseech Him always, every single day without fail, the cowherd Lord (Govinda) who lifted the hill (Govardhana) as an umbrella shows no pity at all, nor reveals His form. O heart! The earth, after all, is vast on every side (and He is hard to reach).

75.translit:
puviyum iruvisumbum ninagaththa, neeyen
seviyin vazhipugundhen nuLLaay,- avivinRi
yaanperiyan neeperiyai enbadhanai yaaraRivaar,
oonparugu neemiyaay! uLLu.
75.meaning: The earth and the vast heavens are within You, yet You have entered through the path of my ear and dwell within me. O Lord whose discus drinks the flesh (of enemies), reflect upon this: without distinction, who can ever know which of us is the greater, I or You?

76.translit:
uLLilum uLLan thadikkum vinaippadalam,
viLLa vizhiththunnai meyyuRRaal,-uLLa
ulagaLavu yaanum uLanaavan enkol?
ulagaLandha moorththi! urai.
76.meaning: O Lord who measured the worlds! Tell me this: even to think of it makes the mass of my deeds (karma) swell within me; yet if that mass were scattered and I, gazing, were to truly attain You, would I not then exist for as long as the worlds endure?

77.translit:
uraikkilOr suRRaththaar uRRaaren Raaree,
iraikkung kadaRkidandha endhaay,-uraippellaam,
ninnanRi maRRileen kaNdaay, enadhuyirkkOr
solnanRi yaagum thuNai.
77.meaning: O my Father who reclines upon the roaring ocean, if I were to speak of it, are any of my kinsmen or relations truly mine? In all my speaking I have none other than You, see; You alone are the helper, the one good word, for my soul.

78.translit:
thuNainaaL perungiLaiyum tholkulamum,
suRRath thiNainaaLu minbudaiththaa meelum, - kaNainaaNil
ovaath thozhilsaarngan tholseerai nanneNjee,
ovaadha vooNaaga uN.
78.meaning: O good heart! Even though companions for one's days, great kinsfolk, ancient lineage, and a host of relations may all bring pleasure, take instead as your unceasing food the ancient glory of Him who bears the Sharnga bow, ever at work with arrow upon string.

79.translit:
uL naatduth theesanRee! oozhvinaiyai yaNjumee,
viNNaattai yonRaaga mechchumee,-maNNaattil
aaraagi evvizhiviR Raanaalum, aazhiyangaip
peeraayaR kaaLaam piRappu.
79.meaning: Is he not radiant in his inner being? He fears not the karma decreed by fate, nor does he prize the heavenly realm as anything great. In this earthly world, whoever he may be and in whatever lowly birth he may be born, that birth becomes one of servitude to the great cowherd (Kannan) who holds the beautiful discus in His hand.

110.translit:
seppiLa men-mulai-t Devaki nangaikku
soppada-t toondri-t torup-paadi yoom vaitta
tuppamum paalum tayirum vizhungiya
appan vandu appoochi kaattuginraan ammanee appoochi kaattuginraan.
110.meaning: To the lady Devaki of soft, unsqueezed breasts He was born in fitting fashion. He then swallowed up the butter, milk and curd that we, the cowherd folk, had set aside. That Lord has come and is showing the bogeyman; O mother, He is showing the bogeyman!

111.translit:
tattu-k koNDaaL kolo taanee per raaL kolo
sitta manaiyaaL Asodai yiLam-singam
kottaar karum-kuzhal Gopala kooLari
attan vandu appoochi kaattuginraan ammanee appoochi kaattuginraan.
111.meaning: Did Yasoda the housewife of steadfast mind take Him as an adopted child, or did she bear Him herself? He is the young lion of Yasoda, the cowherd-lion Gopala with clustered dark locks. That father has come and is showing the bogeyman; O mother, He is showing the bogeyman!

112.translit:
kongai-van koonni-sor koNDu kuvalayat
tunga-k kariyum pariyum iraachchiyamum
engum Bharatar karuLi van kaanaDai
ang-kaNNan appoochi kaattuginraan ammanee appoochi kaattuginraan.
112.meaning: Heeding the words of the hard-breasted hunchback Manthara, He graciously bestowed upon Bharata the lofty elephants, horses and the entire kingdom of the earth, and went off into the harsh forest. That beautiful-eyed Kannan has come and is showing the bogeyman; O mother, He is showing the bogeyman!

113.translit:
padaga mudalai-vaay-p patta kaLiru
kadari-k kai-kooppi en kaNNaa kaN Naa enna
udava-p puL Loorndu angu uru-tuyar tiirtta
adagan vandu appoochi kaattuginraan ammanee appoochi kaattuginraan.
113.meaning: When the elephant caught in the mouth of the treacherous crocodile trumpeted, joined its trunk in worship and cried 'My Kanna, O Kanna!', He came riding the bird (Garuda) to help and ended the great suffering there. That mighty one has come and is showing the bogeyman; O mother, He is showing the bogeyman!

114.translit:
vallaaL ilangai malanga-c saram-turanda
villaaLanai ViTTuchittan viritta
sollaarnda appoochi-p paaDal ivai pattum vallaar poy
vaikundam manni yirupparee.
114.meaning: These ten well-worded 'bogeyman' songs were composed by Vishnuchittan (Periyalwar) about the archer who shot His arrows to crush the mighty Lanka. Those who can master them will go and dwell firmly in Vaikunta forever.

115.translit:
aravaNaiyaay aayar-eeree amma-m uNNa-t tuyil-ezhaayee
iravum uNNaadu urangi nii poy indru muchchi koNDadaalo
varavum kaaNeen vayiru-asaindaay vana-mulaigaL soorndu-paaya
tiruvuDaiya vaay-maDuttu-t tiLaittu-utaittu-p parugidaayee.
115.meaning: O You who rest on the serpent bed, O bull among the cowherds, awaken from sleep to drink the milk! Having slept through the night without eating, today You have let it reach noon. I do not even see You coming; Your belly has grown lean. As my full breasts flow, place Your blessed mouth to them and, kicking and frolicking, drink Your fill.

116.translit:
vaitta neyyum kaaynda paalum vaDi-tayirum naru-veNNeyum
ittanaiyum perr-ariyeen empiraan nii pirnda pinnai
ettanaiyum seyya-perraay eedum seyyeen kadam-paDaadee
muttu-anaiya muruval seydu mookkuruñji mulai-uNaayee.
116.meaning: The stored ghee, the boiled milk, the strained curd and the fragrant white butter, my Lord, ever since You were born I have not been able to keep any of these. You have managed to take all of them, yet I will do nothing; do not grow angry. Smiling a smile like a pearl, sniffling at the nose, come and suckle at my breast.

117.translit:
tan-tam-makkaL azhudu sendraal taaymaar-aavaar tarikka-killaar
vandu nan-meel poosal-seyya vaazha-valla Vaasudevaa
undaiyaar un tirattar-allar unnai naan ondru urappa-maaTTeen
Nandagopan aNi-siruvaa naan suranda mulai-uNaayee.
117.meaning: When their own children go off crying, mothers cannot bear it and come to quarrel with me; O Vasudeva, mighty to live and thrive! Even your own father is not on your side, yet I will not scold You so much as a word. O lovely little son of Nandagopa, come and suckle at the breast that flows for You.

118.translit:
Kañjan-tannaal puNarkka-p-patta kaLLa-c-cakaDu kalakku-azhiya
pañji-anna mel-aDiyaal paaynda-poodu nondiDum endru
añjineen kaaN amarar-koovee aayar-kooTTatt-aLav-andraalo
Kañjanai un vañjanaiyaal valai-paDuttaay mulai-uNaayee.
118.meaning: When You leaped upon and shattered the deceitful cart that had been deployed by Kamsa, I was afraid, O King of the immortals, that Your soft feet, tender as cotton, would be hurt; You are beyond the measure of the cowherd company. You ensnared Kamsa by Your own cunning. Come and suckle at my breast.

119.translit:
tiiya-pundi-k Kañjan un-meel sinam-uDaiyan soorvu-paarttu
maayan-tannaal valai-paDuk kil vaazha-killeen Vaasudevaa
taayar-vaay-c-col tarumam-kaNDaay saarri-c-conneen pooga-veNDaa
aayar-paaDikku aNi-viLakkee amarndu vandu en mulai-uNaayee.
119.meaning: Kamsa of evil mind bears anger toward You and watches for Your weakness; if he should snare You by some trick, I could not live, O Vasudeva. The word from a mother's mouth is righteousness, see; I tell You plainly, do not go off. O graceful lamp of the cowherd village, settle down, come, and suckle at my breast.

120.translit:
min-anaiya nuN-iDaiyaar viri-kuzhal-meel nuzhainda-vaNDu
in-isaikkum Villiputtoor inidu-amarndaay unnai-kaNDaar
enna-noonbu noorraaL kolo ivanai-perra vayiru-uDaiyaaL
ennum vaarttai yeyduvitta IruDeekeesaa mulai-uNaayee.
120.meaning: O You who dwell sweetly in Villiputtur, where the bees that have entered the spreading tresses of the women with waists slender as lightning make sweet music! You have made those who behold You say, 'What penance did she perform, the one whose womb bore this child?' O Hrishikesha, come and suckle at my breast.

121.translit:
peNDir-vaazhvaar nann-oppaarai-p perudum ennum aasaiyaalee
kaNDavargaL pookk-ozhindaar kaN-iNaiyaal kalakka-nokki
vaNDulaam-poon-kuzhalinaar un vaay-amudam uNNa-veNDi
koNDu-poovaan vandu-nindraar Govindaa nii mulai-uNaayee.
121.meaning: With the longing that 'we women would thrive if we could obtain a fine child like Him,' those who saw You gave up departing, gazing with their pairs of eyes until their minds were stirred. The women with flower-decked tresses where bees hover have come and stand, wishing to taste the nectar of Your mouth and to carry You off. O Govinda, come and suckle at my breast.

122.translit:
iru-malai-pol etirnda-mallar iruvar-angam eri-seydaay un
tiru-malindu tigazh-maarvu teekka vandu en nalgul-eeri
oru-mulaiyai vaay-maDuttu oru-mulaiyai neruDi-koNDu
iru-mulaiyum murai-muraiyaa eengi-eengi irundu-uNaayee.
122.meaning: You destroyed the bodies of the two wrestlers who confronted You like a pair of mountains. Come, climb upon my lap so that I may feast my eyes on Your radiant chest where Lakshmi abounds. Putting one breast to Your mouth and fondling the other, taking both breasts each in its turn, settle down and, cooing and cooing, drink Your fill.

123.translit:
an-kamala-p poodagattil aNi-koL muttam sindinaar-pool
sen-kamala mugam-viyarppa-t tiimai-seydu im murrattooDee
angam-ellaam puzhudi-yaaga aLaiya-veNDaa amma-vimma
ang-amararkk-amudaLitta amarar-koovee mulai-uNaayee.
123.meaning: Like lovely pearls scattering from a beautiful lotus blossom, Your red-lotus face breaks into sweat as You make mischief about this courtyard. Do not roll about until Your whole body is covered in dust, my dear, with heaving breath. O King of the immortals who gave nectar to the gods there, come and suckle at my breast.

124.translit:
ooDa-vooDa-k kinginigaL olikkum-oosai-p paaNiyaalee
paaDi-p-paaDi varuginraayai-p Padma-naaban endru-irundeen
aaDi-yaaDi yasaindu-asaindiTTu adanukk-eerra koottai-yaaDi
ooDi-yooDi-p poy-viDaadee uttamaa nii mulai-uNaayee.
124.meaning: As You run and run, the little ankle-bells make their ringing sound in rhythm, and as You come singing and singing, I knew You to be Padmanabha. Dancing and dancing, swaying and swaying, performing the dance that befits the moment, do not run and run away. O noblest one, come and suckle at my breast.

125.translit:
vaar-aninda kongai-aaychchi Maadavaa-uN Nenra-maarram
niir-aninda kuvaLai-vaasam nigazha-naarum Villiputtoor
paar-aninda tol-pugazhaan PaTTar-piraan paaDal-vallaar
siir-aninda sen-kaN-maal-meel senra-sindai peruvaar-taamee.
125.meaning: The cowherd matron with bodice-clad breasts said, 'O Madhava, drink!' — these words sung by Pattarpiran (Periyalwar) of Villiputtur, fragrant as water-lilies set in water, whose ancient fame adorns the whole earth. Those who can master his song will themselves attain a mind that goes forth toward the glorious red-eyed Tirumal.

126.translit:
poy-p-paaDu-uDaiya nin tandaiyum taazhttaan
 porudiral Kañjan kaDiyan
kaappaarum illai kaDal-vaNNaa
 unnai-t taniyee poy engum tiridi
peey-paal mulai-uNDa pittanee
 Kesava-nambii unnai-k kaadu-kutta
aay-p-paalar peNDugaL ellaarum vandaar
 aDaikkaay tirutti naan vaitteen.
126.meaning: Your father, who has work to do, has been delayed, and the fierce battling Kamsa is cruel; there is none to protect You, O sea-hued one, yet You wander alone everywhere. O mad one who drank the milk from the breast of the demoness, O perfect Kesava, all the cowherd women have come to pierce Your ears; I have prepared and set out the betel-nut offering.

127.translit:
vaNNa-p pavaLam marunginil saatti
 malar-p-paada-k kinginni yaarppa
naNNi-t tozhumavar sindai piriyaada
 Naaraya Naa ingee vaaraay
eNNarkk-ariya piraanee
 tiriyai eriyaamee kaadukk-iDuvan
kaNNukku-nandrum azhagum uDaiya
 kanaga-k kaDippum ivaiyaa.
127.meaning: With colourful coral fastened at Your waist and the ankle-bells on Your flower-soft feet jingling — O Narayana who never departs from the minds of those who draw near and worship — come here! O Lord beyond all reckoning, I will place the wick in Your ear without letting it scorch You. And here are the golden ear-studs, exceedingly good and beautiful to the eye.

128.translit:
vaiyam-ellaam perum vaar-kaDal vaazhum
 makara-k kuzhai-koNDu vaitteen
veyyavee kaadil tiriyai yiDuvan
 nii veeNDiyad-ellaam taruvan
uyya iv-aayar kulattinil toondriya
 oN-suDar-aayar-kozhundee
maiyanmai seydu-iLa vaaychchiyar-uLLattu
 Maadava nee ingee vaaraay.
128.meaning: I have set ready the makara-shaped ear-ornament, prized above the whole earth, that dwells in the wide ocean; I will gently place the wick in Your ear, and I will give You everything You desire. O bright radiance who appeared in this cowherd clan for its salvation, O tender shoot of the cowherds! O Madhava who casts Your spell upon the hearts of the young cowherd-women, come here!

129.translit:
vaNa-nandru-uDaiya vayira-k kaDippiTTu
 vaar-kaadu taazha-p perukki
guNa-nandru-uDaiyar ik-Gopala piLLaigaL
 Govindaa nii sollu-k koLLaay
iNai-nindru azhagiya ik-kaDippu iTTaal
 iniya palaa-p-pazham tandu
suNa-nandru-aNi-mulai-uNNa-t taruvan-naan
 sottam piraan ingee vaaraay.
129.meaning: Having set in the diamond ear-studs of fine form, I will pierce and stretch Your long ear-lobes to hang low. These Gopala boys are of good nature, O Govinda; will You not heed my word? If You let me put in these matched, beautiful studs, I will give You sweet jackfruit, and I will give You my fair adorned breast to suckle. I salute You, my Lord — come here!

130.translit:
chottam piraan endru irandaalum koLLaay
 surikuzhalaarodu nee poy
koottuk kuravai piNaindhu ingu vandhaal
 guNam koNdiduvanoo nambee
peerttum periyana appam taruvan
 piraanee tiri ida vottil
veyttadan tooLaar virumbu karunguzhal
 viduvee nee ingee vaaraay.
130.meaning: Even when I plead, saying 'I bow to you, my Lord,' you will not heed. Will I count it a virtue if you go off with the curly-haired girls, link arms in the kuravai dance, and then come back here, O reverend one? I will give you great big sweet cakes if you let me insert the wick (string) into your ear-hole, my Lord. Leave alone those bamboo-armed maidens with their fragrant dark tresses and come here to me.

131.translit:
viNNellaam keettka azhudhittaay
 un vaayil virumbi adhanai naan nookki
maNNellaam kaNdu en manattuLLee anji
 madhusoodhanee endru arindheen
puNNeedhum illai un kaadhum ariyum
 poruttu iraippoodhu iru nambee
kaNNaa en kaarmugilee kadal vaNNaa
 kaavalanee mulai uNaayee.
131.meaning: You cried so loudly that all the heavens heard. When I looked eagerly into your mouth I saw all the world there, and in my heart I was afraid and knew you to be Madhusudana. There is no wound at all, your ear too knows it; bear with me and stay still a little while, O reverend one. O Kanna, my dark rain-cloud, sea-hued one, my protector, take your mother's breast and feed.

132.translit:
mulai eedhum veeNdeen endru oodi
 nin kaadhil kadippaip parittu erindhittu
malaiyai eduttu magizhndhu kal maari kaattup
 pasunirai meyttaay
silai ondru iruttaay tirivikkiramaa
 tiru aayar paadip piraanee
talai nilaa poodhee un kaadhaip perukkaadhee
 vittitteen kuttramee andree.
132.meaning: Saying 'I want no breast at all,' you ran off, snatched away the stylus (ear-piercing pin) from your ear and flung it down. O you who lifted the mountain and joyfully sheltered the cattle from the hail of stones, who herded the cows, who broke the bow, O Trivikrama, O Lord of the sacred cowherd village! While you are still a small infant I did not enlarge (re-pierce) your ear-lobe — and is that my fault?

133.translit:
en kuttramee endru sollavum veeNdaa kaaN
 ennai naan maNN uNdeen aaga
anbuttru nookki adittum pidittum
 anaivarkkum kaattittilaiyee
vanbuttru aravin pagaikkodi
 vaamana nambee un kaadhugaL tooRum
tunbuttranavellaam teerppaay piraanee
 tiriyittuch sollugeen meyyee.
133.meaning: You need not say it is my fault; recall how, when I had eaten earth, you lovingly looked, and when I struck you and held you, did you not show all of them (the earth in your mouth)? O Vamana with the banner of the enemy of the mighty serpent (Garuda), your ears will grow shut! O Lord who removes all sorrows, let me insert the wick — I tell you truly.

134.translit:
meyyendru solluvaar sollaik karudhit
 toduppuNdaay veNNaiyai endru
kaiyaip pidittuk karai uralodu ennaik
 kaaNavee kattittilaiyee
seydhana sollich siriththu angu irukkil
 siridharaa un kaadhu tooRum
kaiyil tiriyai idu kidaay innindra
 kaarigaiyaar siriyaamee.
134.meaning: Trusting the words of those who say it is true, you were bound for stealing the butter, were you not — when they seized your hand and tied you to the broad-rimmed mortar for all to see? If you sit there laughing and recounting your exploits, your ear will grow shut, you little one; let me put the wick in your ear, so that these ladies standing here do not laugh.

135.translit:
kaarigaiyaarkkum unakkum izhukku uttru en?
 kaadhugaL veengi eriyil
taariyaadhu aagil talai nondhidum endru
 vittitteen kuttramee andree
seeriyil piLLaigaL ellaarum
 kaadhu perukkith tiriyavum kaaNdi
eervidai settru iLam kandru erindhitta
 irudeekeesaa endhan kaNNee.
135.meaning: What harm has come to these ladies or to you? If your ears swell and grow inflamed, and if it cannot be borne and your head should ache — for that reason I left it; is that my fault? You can see all the children in the cowherd-quarter going about with their ears pierced and widened. O Hrishikesha who slew the fierce bull and hurled the young calf (at the wood-apple demon), my own eye!

136.translit:
kaNNaik kuLirak kalandhu engum nookkik
 kadi kamazh poonguzhalaargaL
eNNattuL endrum irundhu
 tittikkum perumaanee engaL amudhee
uNNak kanigaL taruvan
 kadi pondrum noovaamee kaadhukku iduvan
paNNaik kizhiyach sagadam udhaiththitta
 parpanaabaa ingee vaaraay.
136.meaning: O great Lord who, mingling so as to cool the eyes, looking everywhere, ever dwells in the thoughts of the fragrant-tressed women and grows sweet to them, our nectar! I will give you fruits to eat; without the least pain I will fix it in your ear. O Padmanabha who kicked and shattered the cart so that its frame split apart, come here.

137.translit:
vaa endru sollien kaiyaip pidiththu
 valiyavee kaadhil kadippai
noovat tirikkil unakku ing izhukku uttru en?
 kaadhugaL nondhidum killeen
naaval pazham koNdu vaitteen
 ivai kaaNaay nambee
mun vanjamagaLaich saavappaal uNdu sagadu iRappaayndhitta
 daamodaraa ingee vaaraay.
137.meaning: Saying 'come,' I take hold of your hand; if I forcibly twist the pin in your ear and it hurts, what harm is that to you? Your ears will be sore — I cannot bear it. I have set out jamun fruits; look at these, O reverend one. O Damodara, who long ago sucked the deceitful demoness (Putana) to death and who sprang upon and broke the cart — come here.

138.translit:
vaar kaadhu taazhap perukki amaiththu
 magarak kuzhai ida veeNdi
seeraal asoodhai tirumaalaich sonna sol
 sindhaiyuL nindru tigazha
paaraar tol pugazhaan pudhuvai mannan
 pannirunaamattaaR sonna
aaraadha andhaadhi pannireNdum vallaar
 achchudhanukku adiyaaree.
138.meaning: Wishing to pierce and shape the ear so the lobe hangs long, fit for a fish-shaped pendant, glorious Yashoda spoke these words to Tirumal. So that those words shine and abide in the heart, Vishnuchitta, the king of Putuvai (Srivilliputtur) of ancient world-renowned fame, sang them through the twelve names. Those who can master all twelve verses of this insatiable garland-poem (antadi) shall be servants of Achyuta.

139.translit:
veNNai aLaindha kuNungum viLaiyaadu puzhudhiyum koNdu
tiNNena ivviraa unnait teyttuk kidakka naan otteen
eNNaip puLippazham koNdu ingu ettanai poodhum irundheen
naNNal ariya piraanee naaraNaa neeraada vaaraay.
139.meaning: With the smell of the butter you have handled and the dust from your play clinging to you, I will not let you lie down this night so soiled and rub it about. I have stayed here all this long while with the oil and the sour fruit (for your bath) ready. O Lord hard for any to approach, O Narayana, come to bathe.

140.translit:
kandrukaL oodach seviyil kattu erumbu pidiththittaal
tendrik kedum aagil veNNai tiratti vizhungumaa kaaNban
nindra maraamaram saaythaay nee pirandhadhu tiru ooNam
indru nee neeraada veeNdum embiraan oodaadhee vaaraay.
140.meaning: If, while the calves run off, a biting ant catches hold in their ear, they scatter and are lost — meanwhile you, I see, gather up the butter and gulp it down! O you who felled the standing maramara trees, your sacred birth-star is Sravana (Tiru-Onam). Today you must bathe, my Lord; do not run away, come.

141.translit:
peycchi mulai uNNak kaNdu pinnaiyum nallaadhu en nenjam
aaycchiyar ellaam koodi azhaikkavum naan mulai tandheen
kaaycchina neerodu nelli kadaaraththil poorittu vaitteen
vaaytta pugazh maNi vaNNaa manjanam aada nee vaaraay.
141.meaning: Having seen you suck the breast of the demoness (Putana), even after that my heart is not at ease; yet though all the cowherd-women gathered and called out, I gave you my breast. I have filled the cauldron with heated water and gooseberry. O sapphire-hued one of well-earned fame, come, that you may bathe.

142.translit:
kanjan puNarppinil vandha kadiya sagadam udhaiththu
vanjagap peymagaL tunja vaay mulai vaiththa piraanee
manjaLum sengazhuneerin vaasigaiyum naaRu saandhum
anjanamum koNdu vaitteen azhaganee neeraada vaaraay.
142.meaning: O Lord who kicked away the cruel cart that came by Kamsa's contrivance, and who set your mouth to the breast so that the deceitful demoness perished! I have made ready turmeric, a garland of red water-lilies, fragrant sandal-paste, and collyrium. O handsome one, come to bathe.

143.translit:
appam kalandha sittruNdi akkaaram paalil kalandhu
soppada naan suttu vaitteen tinnal urudhiyeel nambee
seppiLa men mulaiyaargaL siruburam peesich sirippar
soppada neeraada veeNdum chottam piraan ingee vaaraay.
143.meaning: I have neatly fried and set out little cakes mixed with sugar-candy and milk; if you are sure you will eat them, O reverend one — for the young women with soft round breasts will whisper behind your back and laugh. You must bathe properly; I bow to you, my Lord, come here.

144.translit:
eNNaik kudaththai uruttti iLam piLLai kiLLi ezhuppi
kaNNaip purattti vizhiththuk kazhakaNdu seyyum piraanee
uNNak kanigaL taruvan oli kadal oodhanee poolee
vaNNam azhagiya nambee manjanam aada nee vaaraay.
144.meaning: O Lord who rolls the oil-pot, pinches the little children to wake them, rolls and stares with your eyes and plays your roguish tricks! I will give you fruits to eat. O reverend one whose hue is lovely like the water of the roaring waves of the sea, come, that you may bathe.

145.translit:
karandha nal paalum tayirum kadaindhu uri meel vaiththa veNNai
pirandhadhuvee mudhalaagap pettru ariyeen embiraanee
sirandha nal taay alar tootrum enbadhanaal pirar munnee
marandhum uraiyaada maatteen manjanam aada nee vaaraay.
145.meaning: The good milk that was milked, and the curds, and the butter churned and set up on the hanging pot — from the very day you were born I have never been able to keep any of it, my Lord. Because people say 'his own good mother spreads scandal,' I will not, even by forgetting, speak of it before others. Come, that you may bathe.

146.translit:
kandrinai vaal olai kattik kanigaL udhira erindhu
pin todarndhu oodi oor paambaip pidiththuk koNdu aattinaay poolum
nan tiratteen allan nambee nee pirandhadhu tiru nal naaL
nindru nee neeraada veeNdum naaraNaa oodaadhee vaaraay.
146.meaning: Tying a palm-leaf to the calf's tail, you flung it to make the fruits fall, and then ran chasing after, seized a snake and made it dance, so it seems. I am not skilled in fine ways (to manage you), O reverend one; your birth is on a sacred good day. You must stand and bathe, O Narayana; do not run away, come.

147.translit:
pooNith tozhuvinil pukkup puzhudhi aLaindha pon meeni
kaaNap peridhum ugappan aagilum kaNdaar pazhippar
naaN ettanaiyum ilaadhaay nappinnai kaaNil sirikkum
maaNikkamee en maNiyee manjanam aada nee vaaraay.
147.meaning: Though I greatly delight to see your golden body smeared with dust, having entered into the cattle-shed, yet those who see it will reproach you. O you who have not the least shame — if Nappinnai sees it she will laugh. O my ruby, my gem, come, that you may bathe.

148.translit:
kaarmali meeni nirattuk kaNNa piraanai ugandhu
vaarmali kongai asoodhai manjanam aattiya aattrai
paarmali tol pudhuvaikkoon pattar piraan sonna paadal
seermala sendhamizh vallaar teevinai yaadhum ilaree.
148.meaning: This song that Pattarpiran (Periyalwar), king of ancient world-famed Putuvai, sang — telling how the full-breasted Yashoda, rejoicing in Krishna the Lord whose body is dark as a raincloud, bathed him: those who can master this flourishing pure Tamil shall have no evil karma whatsoever.

149.translit:
pinnai maNaaLanaip peeril kidandhaanai
munnai amarar mudhal tani vittinai
ennaiyum engaL kudi muzhudhu aatkoNda
mannanai vandhu kuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 maadhavan tan kuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
149.meaning: He who is the bridegroom of Nappinnai, who reclines at Tiruppeer, the primal and unique seed of the ancient celestials, the King who has taken into his service me and all our clan entire — O crow, come and comb his locks! O crow, comb the tresses of Madhava!

150.translit:
peeyin mulaiyuNDa piLLai ivanmunnam
maayach chakaDum marudum iruththavan
kaayaa malarvaNNan kaNNan karunguzhal
tooydaaga vandhuguzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 thamaNi vaNNankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
150.meaning: This child is the one who, of old, sucked the breast of the demoness (Putana); he is the one who shattered the deceitful cart and broke the marudu trees. He is dark like the kaaya flower, Kannan with the dark tresses. O crow, come and comb his hair until it is clean; O crow, come and comb the hair of the gem-hued one.

151.translit:
thiNNak kalaththil thiraiyuri meelvaiththa
veNNey vizhungi viraiya urangiDum
aNNal amarar perumaanai aayardham
kaNNanai vandhuguzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 kaarmugil vaNNankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
151.meaning: He swallowed the butter that was set in a firm pot on the hanging sling, and then quickly fell asleep. O crow, come and comb the hair of that noble Lord, the Lord of the celestials, Kannan of the cowherds; O crow, come and comb the hair of the one hued like a dark rain-cloud.

152.translit:
paLLaththil meeyum paravai yurukkoNDu
kaLLa vasuran varuvaanaith thaankaNDu
puLLidhu venru podhukkOvaay keeNDiTTa
piLLaiyai vandhuguzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 peeymulai yuNDaankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
152.meaning: When the treacherous demon came in the form of a bird that feeds in the marshes, he saw it, knew it for a (false) bird, and tore open its beak. O crow, come and comb the hair of that child; O crow, come and comb the hair of the one who sucked the demoness's breast.

153.translit:
karrinam meeyththuk kanikkoru kanrinai
parri yerindha paraman thirumuDa
urranapeesinee ODith thiriyaadhe
arraikkum vandhuguzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 aazhiyaan thankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
153.meaning: He grazed the herd of calves, and to bring down the fruit he seized a calf and hurled it; he is the Supreme One. Do not run about chattering whatever comes to mind; O crow, come every day and comb his hair; O crow, come and comb the hair of the one who bears the discus.

154.translit:
kizhakkil kuDimannar keeDilaa thaarai
azhippaan nanaindhiTTu av vaazhi yadhanaal
vizhikku maLavile veeraruth thaanai
kuzharkaNi yaagakkuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 kOvindhan thankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
154.meaning: To destroy the unrelenting kings who dwelt in the east, with that discus, in the mere blink of an eye, he uprooted them utterly. O crow, come and comb his hair so that the tresses become an ornament; O crow, come and comb the hair of Govinda.

155.translit:
piNDath thiraLaiyum peeykku iTTa neerchchOrum
uNDarku veeNDinee ODith thiriyaadhe
aNDaththu amarar perumaan azhagamar
vaNDoth thiruNDaguzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 maayavandhan kuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
155.meaning: Do not go running about wanting to eat the lump of rice-balls and the watery rice offered to spirits. O crow, come and comb the beautiful, dark hair that is like a (black) beetle, the hair of the Lord of the celestials of the cosmos; O crow, come and comb the hair of the wondrous Lord (Mayavan).

156.translit:
undhi yezhundha uruvama lardhannil
sandhach chadhumugan thannaip paDaiththavan
kondhakkuzhalaik kurandhu puliyaTTi
thandhaththin seeppaal kuzhalvaaraay ak kaakkaay
 thaamO dharandhankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
156.meaning: In the lovely flower (lotus) that rose from his navel he created the rhythmic four-faced Brahma. O crow, smooth the clustered tresses, rub on tamarind (paste), and comb the hair with an ivory comb; O crow, come and comb the hair of Damodara.

157.translit:
mannandhan deevimaar kaNDu magizhveydha
mun iv vulaginai murrum aLandhavan
ponninmuDiyinaip poovaNai meelvaiththu
pinneyirun dhuguzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay
 peeraa yiraththaankuzhal vaaraay ak kaakkaay.
157.meaning: He is the one who, of old, measured this whole world, so that the queens of the (great) king beheld it and rejoiced. O crow, lay his golden crown upon the flowery bed, sit behind him, and comb his hair; O crow, come and comb the hair of the one with a thousand names.

158.translit:
kaNDaar pazhiyaame akkaakkaay kaarvaNNan
vaNDaar kuzhalvaara vaavenra aaychchi sol
viNdhOy madhiLvilli puththoorkkOn paTTansol
koNDaaDip paaDak kurugaa vinaidhaame.
158.meaning: "O crow, that those who see may not blame him, come and comb the beetle-swarming tresses of the dark-hued one" — these are the words of the cowherd-mother (Yashoda). For those who delight in and sing these words of Pattan (Periyalwar), the lord of Villiputtur with its sky-touching ramparts, no evil deeds (karma) will draw near.

159.translit:
veelikkOl veTTi viLaiyaaDu villerri
thaalik kozhundhaith thaDangazhuth thirpooNDu
peelith thazhaiyaip piNaiththup piraghiTTu
kaalippin pOvaarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 kaDalnara vaNNarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
159.meaning: He cuts a stick from the fence and plays, bending it into a bow; he wears around his broad neck the sprout-shaped pendant (tali); he ties a bunch of peacock feathers and lets it hang behind. For him who goes after the cattle, bring a (herding) staff; for the one hued like the (flower-)honey of the sea, bring a staff.

160.translit:
kongung kuDandhaiyum kOTTiyoo rumperum
engum thirindhu viLaiyaaDum enmagan
sangam piDikkum thaDakkaikkuth thakkanil
anga muDaiyadhOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 arakku vazhiththadhOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
160.meaning: My son who wanders everywhere — through Kongu and Kudandai and Kottiyur and Per — and plays. For his great hand that grasps the conch, fitting it well, bring a fine, well-formed staff; bring a staff that is polished smooth with lac.

161.translit:
karuththiTTu edhirninra kanjanaik konraan
poruththiTTu edhirvandha puLLinvaay keeNDaan
neriththa kuzhalgaLai neengamun nODi
sirukkanru meeyppaarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 deeva piraanukkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
161.meaning: He slew Kamsa who stood before him with rage; enduring it, he tore open the beak of the bird (Bakasura) that came against him. Running ahead so that his curled tresses are pushed aside, for him who grazes the little calves, bring a staff; for the Lord of the gods, bring a staff.

162.translit:
onre yuraippaan orusolle solluvan
thunru muDiyaan thuriyO dhananpakkal
senruangup paaradham kaiyerin dhaanukku
kanrugaL meeyppadhOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 kaDalnira vaNNarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
162.meaning: He speaks but one thing, he utters only one word (keeps his word). He went there to Duryodhana of the thick crown and (when refused) cast aside his hand from the Bharata (cause / waged the Bharata war). For him who grazes the calves, bring a staff; for the one hued like the sea, bring a staff.

163.translit:
seeronru thoodhaayth thuriyO dhananpakkal
oooronru veeNDip peraadha urODaththaal
paaronrip paaradham kaiseydhu paarththarkkuth
theeronrai yoorndhaarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 deeva piraanukkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
163.meaning: Going as a worthy envoy to Duryodhana and, asking for but one town and not receiving it, in wrath he set the world to the Bharata war and drove a chariot for Arjuna (Partha). For him who drove the chariot, bring a staff; for the Lord of the gods, bring a staff.

164.translit:
aalath thilaiyaan aravi naNaimeelaan
neelak kaDaluL neDungaalam kaNvaLarndhaan
paalap piraayaththe paarththarkku aruLseydha
kOlap piraanukkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 kuDandhaik kiDandhaarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
164.meaning: He who lay on the banyan leaf, he who reclines upon the serpent-couch, who slept for ages in the dark sea; who even in his childhood years showed grace to the Pandavas. For that beautiful Lord, bring a staff; for the one who reclines at Kudandai (Kumbakonam), bring a staff.

165.translit:
ponthigazh chiththirakooDap poruppinil
urra vaDivil orukaNNum koNDaak
karraik kuzhalan kaDiyan viraindhuunnai
marraikkaN koLLaame kOlkoNDuvaa
 maNivaNNa nampikkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
165.meaning: On the gold-gleaming hill of Chitrakuta, the one with thick tresses, fierce and swift — lest, having taken (aimed at) one of your eyes, he take the other eye too — hurry and bring a staff; for the gem-hued perfect Lord (Nambi), bring a staff.

166.translit:
minniDaich seethai poruTTaa ilangaiyar
mannan maNimuDi paththum uDanveezha
thannigaron rillaachchilaikaal vaLaiththiTTa
minnu muDiyarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 veelai yaDaiththaarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
166.meaning: For the sake of Sita of the lightning-slender waist, he bent his peerless bow so that all ten jeweled crowns of the king of the Lanka-people (Ravana) fell together. For the one with the shining crown, bring a staff; for the one who dammed the sea, bring a staff.

167.translit:
thennilangai mannansiramthOL thuNi seydhu
minnilang upooN vibheeDaNa nampikku
ennilangu naamaththa Lavum arasenra
minnilang kaararkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa
 veengaDa vaaNarkuOr kOlkoNDuvaa.
167.meaning: He severed the heads and shoulders of the king of southern Lanka (Ravana), and to the noble Vibhishana of glittering ornaments he granted: "Be king as long as my name endures." For that lightning-bright, radiant Lord, bring a staff; for the dweller of Venkatam, bring a staff.

168.translit:
akkaakkaay nimbikkuk kOlkoNDu vaavenru
mikkaa Luraiththasol villiputh thoorppaTTan
okka vuraiththa thamizhpaththum vallavar
makkaLaip perru magizhvarivvaiyaththe.
168.meaning: "O crow, bring a staff for Nambi" — saying thus, with these words the eminent Pattan of Villiputtur (Periyalwar) composed in matching measure these ten Tamil verses. Those who can master them will obtain (good) children and rejoice here in this world.

169.translit:
aanirai meeykkanee pOdhi arumarun dhaava dhariyaay
kaanaka mellaam thirindhuun kariya thirumeeni vaaDa
paanaiyil paalaip parugip parraadhaa rellaam sirippa
theeni liniya piraane seNbagap poochchooTTa vaaraay.
169.meaning: You go off to graze the herds of cows, not realizing what a rare treasure (you are); wandering through all the forest, your dark sacred form grows worn. You drink the milk from the pot, so that all those who have no devotion (or: who are not your kin) laugh. O Lord sweeter than honey, come, that I may adorn you with the shenbaga flower.

170.translit:
karuvuDai meeghangaL kaNDaal unnaik kaNDaalokkum kaNgaL
uruvuDai yaay ulageezhum uNDaaga vandu pirandaay
tiruvuDaiyaaL maNavaaLaa tiruvaranga ttee kiDandaay
maruvi maNamkamazh kinRa mallikaip poochchooTTa vaaraay.
170.meaning: When I behold the rain-laden clouds, they resemble your eyes when I gaze upon them. O you of beautiful form, you came and were born so that all the seven worlds might exist. O bridegroom of Lakshmi who possesses divine wealth, you who recline at holy Srirangam—come, that I may adorn you with fragrant, sweet-scented jasmine flowers.

171.translit:
machchoDu maaLigai yeeRi maadargaL tammiDam pukku
kachchoDu paTTaik kizhittuk kaambu tugilavai keeRi
nichchalum teemaigaL seyvaay neeLtiru veengkaTattu endaay
pachchait tamanagat tooDu paadirip poochchooTTa vaaraay.
171.meaning: Climbing up the lofts and mansions, entering the place of the women, tearing apart their bodices and silken garments, ripping their robes—you who do mischief every single day, O my Lord of the lofty holy Venkatam—come, that I may adorn you with green marjoram together with trumpet-flower blossoms.

172.translit:
teruvinkaN ninRu iLa vaaychchi maargaLait teemaiseyyaadee
maruvum tamanaga mumseer maalai maNam kamazhkinRa
puruvam karungkuzhal neRRi polinda mughiRkanRu poolee
uruva mazhagiya nimbee ugandivai sooTTanee vaaraay.
172.meaning: Standing in the street, do no mischief to the young cowherd-girls. You who, like a young dark cloud, are radiant with eyebrows, dark curls, and forehead, of beautiful comely form, O child—come gladly that I may adorn you with these: clinging marjoram and the glorious fragrant garland that breathes sweet scent.

173.translit:
puLLinai vaaypiLan diTTaay porukari yinkom posittaay
kaLLa varakkiyai mookkoDu kaaval naittalai koNDaay
aLLinee veNNey vizhunga anjaadu aDiyeen aDitteen
teLLiya neerile zhunda sengkazhu neersooTTa vaaraay.
173.meaning: You who split open the beak of the bird-demon, who broke off the tusk of the fighting elephant, who cut off the nose of the deceitful demoness and took the head of her guardian. When you scooped up and swallowed the butter, I, your servant, struck you without fear. Come now, that I may adorn you with the red water-lily that rises from clear waters.

174.translit:
eruduga LooDu porudi eedum uloobaaykaan nambi
karudiya teemaigaL seydu kanjanaik kaalkoDu paayndaay
teruvinkaN teemaigaL seydu sikkena mallarga LooDu
porudu varuginRa ponnee punnaip poochchooTTa vaaraay.
174.meaning: You wrestled with the bulls, O Lord who are never miserly; doing the mischiefs you intended, you leapt upon Kamsa with your foot. Doing mischiefs in the street, O golden one who comes after wrestling firmly with the strongmen—come, that I may adorn you with the punnai flowers.

175.translit:
kuDanga LeDuttERa viTTuk koottaaDa vallaem koovee
maDangkoL madimugat taarai maalseyya vallaen maindaa
iDandiTTu iraNiyan nenjai irupiLa vaagamun keeNDaay
kuDandaik kiDandaemkoovee kurukkattip poochchooTTa vaaraay.
175.meaning: O my King who can dance, tossing up the pots and catching them; O my son who can enchant the women of moon-like beautiful faces; you who long ago split open Hiranya's chest, tearing it into two halves. O my King who reclines at Kudandai (Kumbakonam)—come, that I may adorn you with kurukkatti blossoms.

176.translit:
seemaa liganava nooDu toozhamai koLLavum vallaay
saamaaRu avanainee yeNNich chakkarat taaltalai koNDaay
aamaa RaRiyum piraanee aNiyaranga ttee kiDandaay
eemaaRRam ennait tavirttaay iruvaaTchip poochchooTTa vaaraay.
176.meaning: You who were able even to take up friendship with that Samalikan, then, having resolved that he should die, took off his head with your discus. O Lord who knows the right way of all things, you who recline at beautiful Srirangam, you who removed my distress—come, that I may adorn you with iruvaatchi flowers.

177.translit:
aNDat tamarargaL soozha attaaNi yuLLangki rundaay
toNDargaL nenji luRaivaay toomala raaLmaNa vaaLaa
uNDiTTu ulaginai yeezhum ooraa lilaiyil tuyilkoNDaay
kaNDunaan unnai yugakkak karumugaip poochchooTTa vaaraay.
177.meaning: Surrounded by the celestials of the universe, you sat within the audience-hall; you dwell in the hearts of your devotees, O bridegroom of her who dwells on the pure flower (Lakshmi). Having swallowed all the seven worlds, you slept upon a single banyan leaf. Come, that, beholding and rejoicing in you, I may adorn you with dark jasmine buds.

178.translit:
seNbaga mallikai yooDu sengkazhu neer iru vaaTchi
eNbagar poovum koNarndeen inDru ivai sooTTavaavenDru
maNbagar koNDaanai aaychchi magizhndurai seydaim maalai
paNbagar villiput toorkkoon paTTar piraansonna pattee.
178.meaning: "With champak and jasmine, red water-lily and iruvaatchi, I have brought flowers of many kinds; come today that I may adorn you with these"—thus the cowherd-woman joyfully spoke to him who measured and took the earth. These are the ten verses spoken by Pattarpiran, the chief of melodious Villiputtur.

179.translit:
indira nooDu piraman eesan imaiyava rellaam
mandira maamalar koNDu maRaindu uva raayvandu ninDraar
sandiran maaLigai seerum sadirargaL veLLaRai ninDraay
andiyam poodu idu vaagum azhaganee kaappiDa vaaraay.
179.meaning: Indra together with Brahma, Shiva, and all the celestials, bearing sacred glorious flowers, have come unseen and stand here in delight. O you who stand at Vellarai of the clever ones, whose mansions reach to the moon—this is now the evening hour; O beautiful one, come, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

180.translit:
kanRugaL illam pugundu kadaRukin Drapasu vellaam
ninDrozhin deenunnaik koovi neesamee lonDru milaadaay
manDrilnal leelandip poodu madiLtiru veLLaRai ninDraay
ninDrukaN Daayendan sollu naanunnaik kaappiDa vaaraay.
180.meaning: The calves have entered the houses, and all the cows are bellowing; I stand here calling you, O you who have not the least affection. O you who stand at walled holy Vellarai—it is the good evening hour in the common ground. Heed my word and stand still; come, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

181.translit:
seppoodu menmulai yaargaL siRusooRum illum sidaittiTTu
appoodu naanurap pappooy aDisilu muNDilai aaLvaay
muppoodum vaanava reettum munivargaL veLLaRai ninDraay
ippoodu naanonDrum seyyeen embiraan kaappiDa vaaraay.
181.meaning: Having spoiled the little play-rice and the play-houses of the women of soft breasts like copper-pots, you went off when I scolded you then, and have not even eaten your meal, O Master. O you who stand at Vellarai praised by the celestials and sages at all three times of day—now I shall do nothing; O my Lord, come, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

182.translit:
kaNNil maNalkoDu toovik kaalinaal paayndanai yenDrenDru
eNNarum piLLaigaL vandiTTu ivaraal muRaippaDu kinDraar
kaNNanee veLLaRai ninDraay kaNDaaroo deeteemai seyvaay
vaNNamee veelaiya toppaay vaLLalee kaappiDa vaaraay.
182.meaning: "You threw sand into our eyes and kicked us with your feet"—saying this again and again, countless children have come and make complaint against you. O Kannan who stands at Vellarai, you who do mischief to all whom you meet, O you whose hue resembles the ocean, O bountiful one—come, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

183.translit:
pallaa yiravariv vooril piLLaigaL teemaigaL seyvaar
ellaamun meelanDrip poogaadu embiraan neeingee vaaraay
nallaargaL veLLaRai ninDraay njaanach chuDaree unmeeni
sollaara vaazhtti ninDreettich choppaDak kaappiDa vaaraay.
183.meaning: Many thousands of children in this town do mischiefs, yet all of it does not pass off except upon you (you are blamed for all), O my Lord; come here. O you who stand at Vellarai of the good people, O radiance of wisdom—come, that I, fully praising and extolling your form with my words, may rightly set the protective charm upon you.

184.translit:
kanjang kaRukkoNDu ninmeel karuniRach chemmayirp peeyai
vanjip patharku viDuttaa nenbaduoor vaarttaiyum
uNDu manju tavazhmaNi maaDa madiLtiru veLLaRai ninDraay
anjuvan neeangkuniRka azhaganee kaappiDa vaaraay.
184.meaning: There is also a rumor that Kamsa, holding malice against you, sent the demoness of dark hue and red hair to deceive and destroy you. O you who stand at walled holy Vellarai of jeweled mansions over which the clouds creep—I am afraid while you stand there; O beautiful one, come, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

185.translit:
kaLLach chakaDum marudum kalakkazhi yaudai seyda
piLLai yaraseenee peeyaip piDittu mulaiyuNDa pinnai
uLLavaaRu onDrum aRiyeen oLiyuDai veLLaRai ninDraay
paLLikoL poodu iduvaagum paramanee kaappiDa vaaraay.
185.meaning: O prince of a child, who kicked the deceitful cart and the marudu trees so that they were broken and destroyed, and who, after seizing the demoness and drinking at her breast—I know nothing at all of how things truly are with you. O you who stand at radiant Vellarai—this is now the hour for sleeping; O Supreme one, come, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

186.translit:
inba madanai uyarttaay imaiyavarkku enDrum ariyaay
kumbak kaLiraTTa koovee koDungkanjan nenjiniR kooDree
sempon madiLveL LaRaiyaay selvatti naalvaLar piLLaay
kambak kabaalikaaN angkuk kaDiTooDik kaappiDa vaaraay.
186.meaning: You who exalted bliss, you who are ever hard to attain even for the celestials, O King who slew the rutting elephant, O Death to the heart of cruel Kamsa. O you of Vellarai with its red-gold walls, O child reared in wealth—the terrifying Kapali (a frightful spirit) is there; come running swiftly, that I may set the protective charm upon you.

187.translit:
irukkoDu neersangkil koNDiTTu ezhilmaRai yoorvandu ninDraar
tarukkeelnambi sandi ninDru taaysolluk koLLaay silanaaL
tirukkaappu naanunnaich chaattat teesuDai veLLaRai ninDraay
urukkaaTTum andi viLakkuinDru oLikoLLa eeRRugeen vaaraay.
187.meaning: Taking water in the conch with the Rig chants, the beautiful Vedic brahmins have come and stand here. Do not be willful, O Lord; standing at the twilight juncture, heed your mother's word for these few days. O you who stand at lustrous Vellarai, that I may fasten the sacred protective charm upon you—come, I shall light the evening lamp that reveals forms, that it may give its radiance.

188.translit:
poodamar selvak kozhundu puNartiru veLLaRai yaanai
maadark kuyarnda asoodai magantannaik kaappiTTa maaRRam
veedap payankoLLa valla viTTucit tansonna maalai
paadap payankoLLa valla pattaruL Laarvinai poomee.
188.meaning: This garland, spoken by Vishnuchittan (Periyalvar) who can grasp the fruit of the Vedas, telling how Yasoda—she exalted among women—set the protective charm upon her son, the Lord of holy Vellarai, the wealthy tender shoot dwelling amid flowers: the karma of those devotees who are able to gain the fruit of singing it shall depart.

189.translit:
veNNey vizhungki veRungkalattai
 veRpiDai yiTTu adha noosai keeTkum
kaNNa piraankaRRa kalvi tannaik
 kaakkakil loomun maganaik kaavaay
puNNil puLippeydaa lokkum teemai
 puraipurai yaalivai seyya valla
aNNaRkaN NaanormaganaippeRRa
 asoodai nangkaayun maganaik koovaay.
189.meaning: Having swallowed the butter and dashed the empty pot upon the rock to hear its sound—we cannot guard against the cunning that Kannan, the Lord, has learned; guard your son yourself! His mischiefs are like pouring tamarind-juice (sour) into a wound; he is able to do these things stealthily, place by place. O good lady Yasoda, who bore as your son this Lord with eyes—call your son away!

190.translit:
varuka varuka varuka ingee
 vaamana nambee varuka ingee
kariya kuzhal sheyya vaay-mukhattuk
 kaakutta nambee varuka ingee
ariyan ivan enakku indru nangaay
 anjana vaNNaa asalakattaar
paribavam peesat tarikkagillEn
 paaviyEnukku ingee pootaraayE.
190.meaning: Come, come, come here! Come here, O perfect Vamana! With dark curling hair, red lips and a radiant face, O perfect descendant of Kakutstha (Rama), come here! O dear lady (Yashoda), this child of mine has today become hard to control for me; O one dark as collyrium, the neighbours speak abusively of him and I cannot bear it. Come here to wretched me!

191.translit:
tiruvudaip piLLai taan teeya vaaru
 teekkam ondrum ilan teesudaiyan
uruka vaitta kudattodu veNNey
 urinji udaittittup pondu nindraan
arukirundaar tammai aniyaayam sheyvadu taan
 vazhakkO asOdaay
varukavendru un makan tannaik koovaay
 vaazhavottaan madusoo dananE.
191.meaning: Your fortunate child behaves so wickedly, yet feels no shame at all and is full of splendour! He sucked away the butter from the pot set out to melt, broke the pot, and came and stood here. Is it just, O Yashoda, to wrong those who live near you? Call your son, saying 'come'; that Madhusudana (slayer of Madhu) will not let us live in peace.

192.translit:
koNdal vaNNaa ingee pootaraayE
 kOyil piLLaay ingee pootaraayE
teN-tirai soozh tiruppErk kidanda
 tirunaaraNaa ingee pootaraayE
uNdu vandEn amman endru solli
 Odi akam puka aaychi taanum
kaNd ethirE shendr eduttuk koLLak
 kaNNa-piraan katra kalvi taanE.
192.meaning: O one hued like a rain-cloud, come here! O child of the temple, come here! O sacred Narayana reclining at Tiruppernagar girt by clear waves, come here! Saying 'Mother, I have eaten and come,' he ran to enter the house; and as the cowherd-mother (Yashoda) saw him, went toward him and caught him up in her arms — such is the cunning craft that Lord Kannan has learned!

193.translit:
paalaik karandu adup pEra vaittup
 pal-vaLaiyaaL en makaL iruppa
mElai akattE neruppu vENdich shendr
 iraip pozhudu angE peesi nindrEn
shaaLak kiraamam udaiya nambi
 shaayttup parukittup pondu nindraan
aalaik karumbin mozhi anaiya
 asOdai nangaay un makanaik koovaay.
