193.meaning: Having milked the cow and set the milk on the stove, while my daughter of many bangles sat by, I went to the next house to fetch fire and stood talking there a moment. The perfect Lord who owns Salagrama tilted the pot, drank it up, and came and stood here. O Yashoda, dear lady whose words are sweet as the juice of sugarcane from the press, call your son!

194.translit:
pootar kaNdaay ingee pootar kaNdaay
 pootarEn ennaadE pootar kaNdaay
EdEnum solli asalakattaar
 EdEnum peesa naan kEtka mattEn
kOdukalam udaik kuttanEyaa
 kundr eduttaay kuda-maadu koottaa
vEdap poruLE en vEngadavaa
 vittakanE ingee pootaraayE.
194.meaning: Do come here, do come here; do not say 'I will not come,' do come here! When the neighbours say one thing or another, I cannot bear to hear such talk. O playful little child, O you who lifted the mountain (Govardhana), O dancer of the pot-dance, O very essence of the Vedas, O my Lord of Venkatam, O clever one, come here!

195.translit:
shennel arishi shiru paruppuch
 sheyda akkaaram naru-ney paalaal
pannireNd tiru-vONam attEn
 paNdum ip-piLLai parishu arivan
innam ukappan naan endru solli
 ellaam vizhungittup pondu nindraan
un makan tannai asOdai nangaay
 koovik koLLaay ivaiyum shilavE.
195.meaning: With fine red-paddy rice, small lentils, prepared sugar-candy, fragrant ghee and milk, I cooked the offering for twelve Tiruvonam days; I have known this child's ways from of old. Saying 'I will enjoy still more,' he swallowed it all and came and stood here. O Yashoda, dear lady, call your son to you — and these are but a few of his deeds!

196.translit:
kEsavanE ingee pootaraayE
 killEn ennaadu ingee pootaraayE
nEcham ilaadaar akattirundu
 nee viLaiyaadaadE pootaraayE
toosanam sollum tozhuttai maarum
 toNdarum nindra vidattil nindru
taay solluk koLvadu tanmam kaNdaay
 daamOdaraa ingee pootaraayE.
196.meaning: O Kesava, come here! Do not say 'I will not,' come here! Do not go playing in the houses of those who have no affection for you; come here! Where the maidservants who speak ill of you and the devotees stand, to heed a mother's word is righteousness, you see; O Damodara, come here!

197.translit:
kannal ilattuvattOdu sheedai
 kaar-eLLin uNdai kalattil ittu
en akam endru naan vaittup pondEn
 ivan pukku avatraip peruttip pondaan
pinnum akam pukku uriyai nOkkip
 pirangoLi veNNeyum sOdikkindraan
un makan tannai asOdai nangaay
 koovik koLLaay ivaiyum shilavE.
197.meaning: Sugar laddus along with seedai and balls of dark sesame I placed in vessels and, calling it my own store, set them aside and came away; he entered and made off with them and came back. Again he goes into the house, eyes the hanging pot-rack and searches for the bright glistening butter too. O Yashoda, dear lady, call your son to you — and these are but a few of his deeds!

198.translit:
sollil arasip padudi nangaay
 suzhal udaiyan un piLLai taanE
illam pukundu en makaLaik koovik
 kaiyil vaLaiyaik kazhatrik koNdu
kollaiyil nindrum koNarndu vitra
 angoruttikku av-vaLai koduttu
nallana naaval pazhangaL koNdu
 naan allEn endru shirikkindraanE.
198.meaning: If I tell it, O lady, you will be put to shame — your child indeed is a deceitful trickster! He entered my house, called my daughter, slipped the bangles off her wrist, gave those bangles to a certain woman who had come from the field selling them, took fine jambu (naval) fruits in exchange, and then laughs saying 'It wasn't me!'

199.translit:
vaNdu kaLittu iraikkum pozhil soozh
 varu-punal kaaviri ten-arangan
paNd avan sheyda kireedai ellaam
 pattar piraan vittu-sittan paadal
koNd ivai paadik kunikka vallaar
 gOvindan tan adiyaarkaL aaki
eN-tishaikkum viLakk aaki nirpaar
 iNaiyadi en talai mElanavE.
199.meaning: Of the southern Lord of Srirangam (Ranganatha), girt by groves where bees hum in delight, on the banks of the flowing Kaveri — all the childhood sports that he of old performed are sung in the song of Vishnuchittan (Periyalvar), beloved of devotees. Those who can sing these and dance to them become the servants of Govinda and stand as lamps to all eight directions; their twin feet are upon my head.

200.translit:
aatril irundu viLaiyaaduvOngaLai
 shEtraal erindu vaLai-tukil kaikkoNdu
kaatrin kadiyanaay Odi akam pukku maatramum
 taaraanaal indru mutrum
 vaLaittiram peesaanaal indru mutrum.
200.meaning: As we were sitting by the river at play, he flung mud at us, seized our bangles and clothes, and ran swifter than the wind into the house — and now he gives us no reply at all, and now he will not say a word about the bangles. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

201.translit:
kuNdalam taazhak kuzhal taazha naaN taazha
 eN-tishaiyOrum iraindjit tozhutEtta
vaNdamar poong-kuzhalaar tukil kaikkoNdu
 viN-tOy marattaanaal indru mutrum
 vENdavum taaraanaal indru mutrum.
201.meaning: With his ear-rings hanging low, his locks hanging down and his sacred thread hanging, while the dwellers of all eight directions bow and worship and praise him — he seized the garments of the women whose flowery tresses bees frequent, and climbed up into the sky-touching tree. And now, though we beg, he gives them not. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

202.translit:
tadam padu taamaraip poykai kalakki
 vidam padu naagattai vaal patri eerttu
padam padu pain-talai mEl ezhap paayndittu
 udambai asaittaanaal indru mutrum
 uchchiyil nindraanaal indru mutrum.
202.meaning: Churning the broad lotus-pond, seizing by the tail the venom-bearing serpent (Kaliya) and dragging it out, leaping up to spring upon its raised hooded heads, he set his body swaying in dance — and now he stood upon its crest. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

203.translit:
tEnukan aavi shekuttu
 panang-kani taan erindittu tadam-perun tOLinaal
vaanavar kOn vida vanda mazhai taduttu
 aan-irai kaattaanaal indru mutrum
 avai uyyak koNdaanaal indru mutrum.
203.meaning: He destroyed the life of Dhenuka, and with his broad mighty arms flung down the palmyra fruits; he held back the rain that the king of the gods (Indra) sent, and guarded the herds of cattle — and now he saved them all. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

204.translit:
aaychiyar shEri aLai-tayir paal uNdu
 pErttavar kaNdu pidikkap pidiyuNdu
vEyttadan tOLinaar veNNey koL maattaadu angu
 aappuNdu irundaanaal indru mutrum
 adiyuNd azhudaanaal indru mutrum.
204.meaning: In the cowherd-women's quarter he ate the churned curds and milk, and as they turned and saw him he was caught and seized; unable to take the butter, there by the women of full bamboo-like arms he stood bound, and now he wept when he was struck. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

205.translit:
taLLit taLir-nadai ittu iLam piLLaiyaay
 uLLattin uLLE avaLai ura nOkki
kaLLattinaal vanda pEychchi mulaiyuyir
 tuLLach shuvaittaanaal indru mutrum
 tuvakkara uNdaanaal indru mutrum.
205.meaning: Toddling with tender unsteady steps as a little child, gazing intently within his heart upon her, he sucked at the breast of the demoness (Putana) who came by deceit, so that her life leapt out — and now he drained it utterly. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

206.translit:
maavali vELviyil maaN-uru aaych shendru
 moovadi taa endru iranda im maNNinai
Oradi ittu iraNdaam adi tannilE
 taavadi ittaanaal indru mutrum
 taraNi aLandaanaal indru mutrum.
206.meaning: Going to Mahabali's sacrifice in the form of a dwarf-brahmin and begging 'Give me three paces,' upon this earth he set one pace, and with the second pace he strode wide — and now he measured the whole world. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

207.translit:
taazhai taN-naambal tadam-perum poykaivaay
 vaazhum mudalai valaippattu vaadippuN
vEzham tuyar keda viNNOr perumaanaay
 aazhi paNi koNdaanaal indru mutrum
 adarku aruL sheydaanaal indru mutrum.
207.meaning: In the broad great pond of fragrant screw-pine and cool water-lilies, the elephant (Gajendra), caught in the grip of the crocodile that dwelt there and afflicted with pain — to end its suffering, as the Lord of the celestials he wielded his discus, and now he showed it his grace. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

208.translit:
vaanat tezhunda mazhai-mukil pOl
 engum kaanattu mEyndu kaLittu viLaiyaadi
Enatt uru aay idanda im maNNinai
 taanattE vaittaanaal indru mutrum
 taraNi idandaanaal indru mutrum.
208.meaning: Like a rain-cloud risen in the sky, grazing everywhere in the forest, rejoicing and sporting, in the form of a boar he tore up this earth and set it back in its place — and now he raised up the world. It is finished for today, it is finished for today!

209.translit:
am-kamalak kaNNan tannai asOdaikku
 mangai nallarkaL taam vandu muraippatta
angu avar sollaip puduvaik kOn pattan sol
 ingivai vallavarkku Edam ondr illaiyE.
209.meaning: This is the song of Pattan, the king of Puduvai (Periyalvar of Srivilliputtur), set down from the words of the fair young women who came and complained to Yashoda about the lotus-eyed Kannan. For those who are able with these verses here, there is not a single misfortune.

210.translit:
tannee raayiram piLLai kaLooDu thaLarnaDaiyiTTu varuvaan
p3onneey neyyoDu paalamu thuNDu oru puLLuvan poyyee thavazhum
minneer nuNNiDai vanja magaLkongai thunjavaay vaitha piraanee
annee unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
210.meaning: You who come toddling along, alone in spirit, with a thousand cowherd children at your side; you who ate the golden ghee, milk and nectar, and yet, like a trickster, crawl about feigning innocence; O Lord who set your mouth upon the breast of the deceitful demoness (Putana) of slender lightning-like waist, so that she perished! O dear one, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

211.translit:
ponpool manjana maaTTi amuduuTTip poonen varumaLavu ippaal
vanpaa rachchaka DamiRach chaaDi vaDakki lagampuk kirundhu
minpool nuNNiDai yaaloru kanniyai veeRRuru vamseydhu vaitha
anpaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
211.meaning: I bathed you bright as gold, fed you ambrosia, and went away; in the meantime, on this side, you kicked and shattered the heavy laden cart (the demon Sakatasura), went into the northern house, and there transformed a maiden of slender lightning-like waist into another form. O loving one, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

212.translit:
kummaa yaththoDu veNNey vizhungik kuDaththayir saaiththup parugi
poymmaaya marudhaana asurarai ponRuviththu inRunee vandhaay
immaa yamvalla piLLainam piiunnai enmaga neeyenbar ninRaar
ammaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
212.meaning: You swallowed the kneaded dish and the butter, toppled the pot of curd and drank it, destroyed the deceitful demons who were the (twin) Marudu trees, and have come back today. O our Lord skilled in such wondrous deeds! Those who stand by say of you, 'He is my child!' O father, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

213.translit:
maiyaar kaNDamaDa vaaichchiyar makkaLai maiyanmai seydhuavar pinpooy
koyyaar puundhugil paRRith thanininRu kuRRam palapala seydhaay
poyyaa unnaip puRampala peesuva puththagath thukkuLa keeTTeen
aiyaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
213.meaning: You bewitched the children of the gentle cowherd women whose eyes are dark with collyrium, and following after them, seized their freshly-woven flowered garments, and standing alone you did many, many mischiefs. O truthful one, I have heard from within the books the many things others tell about you. O sire, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

214.translit:
muppoo dhumkaDain thiiNDiya veNNeyi nooDu thayirum vizhungi
kappaa laayargaL kaaviR koNarndha kalaththoDu saaiththup parugi
meyppaa luNDazhu piLLaigaL poolna vimmivim miyazhu kinRa
appaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
214.meaning: You swallowed the butter churned and gathered at all three times of day along with the curd; the pots the cowherds on the far side carried on their yokes you toppled and drank from; and then, like children who have drunk real milk and weep, you sob and sob as if crying. O father, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

215.translit:
karumbaar niiLvayal kaaykadhirch chennelaik kaRRaa niRaimaNDith thinna
virumbaak kanRonRu koNDu viLanganai viizha eRindha piraanee
surumbaar menkuzhal kanni yoruththikkuch chuuzhvalai vaiththuth thiriyum
arambaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
215.meaning: In the long fields rich with sugarcane, you let the herd of calves crowd in and graze upon the ripe red paddy of ear-bearing grain; O Lord who took a reluctant calf and hurled it so that the wood-apples fell! O rogue who roams about, setting a snare all around for a certain maiden with soft tresses thick with bees! I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

216.translit:
maruTTaar menkuzhal koNDu pozhilpukku vaayvaiththuav vaayartham paaDi
suruTTaar menkuzhal kanniyar vanduunnaich chuRRum thozhaninRa soodhi
poruTTaa yamileen emperumaan unnaip peRRakuR Ramallaal maRRingu
araTTaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
216.meaning: Taking up the soft curling tresses (in the flute's tune), you entered the grove and set your mouth (to the flute); O radiance, before whom the maidens of the cowherd village with soft curling tresses came and stood worshipping all around you! O my Lord, I have no quarrel with you for any matter — save the fault of having borne you. O wild one, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

217.translit:
vaaLaa vaagilum kaaNagil laarpiRar makkaLai maiyanmai seydhu
thoozhaa liTTuava rooDu thiLaiththunii sollap paDaadhana seydhaay
keeLaar aayar kulaththavar ippazhi keTTeen vaazhvillai nandhan
kaaLaay unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
217.meaning: Even when others could not so much as look at them in vain, you bewitched the children of others, and casting your arms about them, sported with them and did things that should not be told. The people of the cowherd clan will not bear this disgrace — wretched me, there is no living here! O young bull of Nanda's house, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

218.translit:
thaaymaar moorviRkap poovar thagappanmaar kaRRaa niRaippinbu poovar
niiaayp paaDiiLang kanni maargaLai neerpaTa veekoNDu poodhi
kaaivaarkku enRum ugappana veeseydhu kaNDaar kazhaRath thiriyum
aayaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
218.meaning: The mothers go off to sell buttermilk, the fathers go behind the herds of cattle; and you, in the cowherd village, lead away the young maidens, drawing them straight to you. You always do the very things that delight those who are hostile, and roam about so that all who see you reproach you. O cowherd, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

219.translit:
thoththaar puunguzhal kanni yoruththiyaich choolaith thaDamkoNDu pukku
muththaar kongai puNarndhuiraa naazhigai muuvezhu senRapin vandhaay
oththaarkku oththana peesuvar unnai urappavee naanonRum maaTTeen
aththaa unnai aRindhukoN DeenunakkU anjuvan ammam tharavee.
219.meaning: Taking a certain maiden with flower-laden tresses into the grove by the great tank, you embraced her pearl-like breasts, and came back only after thrice seven night-watches had passed. People who are alike speak among themselves things befitting one another about you, yet I cannot say a single harsh word to rebuke you. O master, I have come to know you now — I am afraid to give you milk.

220.translit:
karaar meeni niRaththembi raanaik kaDikamazh puunguzha laaichchi
aaraa innamu dhuNNath tharuvannaan ammamthaa reenenRa maaRRam
paaraar tholpuga zhaanpudhu vaimannan paTTarpi raansonna paaDal
eeraa rinnisai maalai vallaar iruDiikee sanaDi yaaree.
220.meaning: To the Lord of dark cloud-like hue and form, the cowherd-woman (Yashoda), her fragrant flowered tresses spreading their scent, said, 'I will give you sweet insatiable ambrosia to eat, but I will not give you milk!' — these words of refusal. This garland of sweet music, sung by Pattarpiran, king of Puduvai, of ancient renown throughout the earth — those who master its lovely sweet melody are the devotees of Hrishikesa (Krishna).

221.translit:
anjana vaNNanai aayar kulak kozhundhinai
manjana maaTTi manaigaL thooRum thiriyaamee
kanjanaik kaayndha kazhalaDi noovak kanRinpin
enseyap piLLaiyaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
221.meaning: Having bathed the dark-collyrium-hued one, the tender shoot of the cowherd clan, so that he might not roam from house to house — alas, so that his anklet-adorned feet, which destroyed Kamsa, should ache, why did I send my child off behind the calves? Oh, what a sin is mine!

222.translit:
paRRu manjaLpuusip paavai maaroDu paaDiyil
chiRRil sidhaiththuengum thiimai seydhu thiriyaamee
kaRRuth thuuLi yuDaiveeDar kaaniDaik kanRinpin
eRRukkuen piLLaiyaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
222.meaning: Smearing on clinging turmeric, so that with the doll-like girls in the cowherd village he might not break their little play-houses and roam about doing mischief everywhere — into the forest, raised in dust, of the hunters, behind the calves, why on earth did I send my child? Oh, what a sin is mine!

223.translit:
nanmaNi meegalai nangai maaroDu naaLthoRum
ponmaNi meeni puzhudhi yaaDith thiriyaamee
kanmaNi ninRadhir kaa nadhariDaik kanRinpin
enmaNi vaNNanaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
223.meaning: So that day after day, with the maidens wearing fine jewelled girdles, his gem-golden body might not roll in the dust as he roamed — into the forest-path where rocks and boulders stand and resound, behind the calves, why did I send my gem-hued one? Oh, what a sin is mine!

224.translit:
vaNNak karunguzhal maadhar vandualar thuuRRiDa
paNNip palaseydhuip paaDi yengum thiriyaamee
kaNNuk kiniyaanaikkaa nadhariDaik kanRinpin
eNNaR kariyaanaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
224.meaning: So that the women with beautiful dark tresses might not come and spread slander, and so that, doing many a contrived deed, he might not roam everywhere in this cowherd village — him who is sweet to the eye, him who is hard even to conceive of, into the forest-path behind the calves, I sent away. Oh, what a sin is mine!

225.translit:
avvav viDampukku avvaayar peNDirkku aNukkanaay
kovvaik kanivaay koDuththuk kuuzhaimai seyyaamee
evvam silaiyuDai veeDar kaaniDaik kanRinpin
theyvath thalaivanaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
225.meaning: So that, entering this place and that, growing intimate with the women of those cowherds, he might not give them his coral-fruit-red lips and play the wanton — into the forest of the hunters armed with cruel bows, behind the calves, I sent the Lord of the gods. Oh, what a sin is mine!

226.translit:
miDaRu mezhumezhuth thooDa veNNey vizhungippooy
paDiRu palaseydhuip paaDi yengum thiriyaamee
kaDiRu paladhiri kaa nadhariDaik kanRinpin
iDaRaen piLLaiyaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
226.meaning: So that, swallowing butter till his throat glistened smooth, and going about doing many a knavish deed, he might not roam everywhere in this cowherd village — into the forest-path where many wild elephants wander, behind the calves, so that he should stumble, I sent my child. Oh, what a sin is mine!

227.translit:
vaLLi nuDangiDai maadhar vandualar thuuRRiDa
thuLLi viLaiyaaDith thoozha rooDu thiriyaamee
kaLLi yuNangu vengaa nadhariDaik kanRinpin
puLLin thalaivanaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
227.meaning: So that the women of swaying creeper-slender waists might not come and spread slander, and so that, leaping in play, he might not roam about with his companions — into the hot forest-path where the cactus withers, behind the calves, I sent the Lord of the bird (Garuda). Oh, what a sin is mine!

228.translit:
panniru thingaL vayiRRil koNDaap paanginaal
eniLang kongai amudha muuTTi yeDuththuyaan
ponnaDi noovap pularaiyee kaanil kanRinpin
enniLanj singaththaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
228.meaning: Having carried him in my womb for twelve months in such fond manner, and having raised him by feeding him the nectar of my young breasts — so that his golden feet should ache, at dawn, into the forest, behind the calves, I sent my young lion. Oh, what a sin is mine!

229.translit:
kuDaiyum seruppum koDaadhee thaamoo dharanainaan
uDaiyum kaDiyana uunRu vembaraRkaLuDai
kaDiyaveng kaaniDaik kaalaDi noovak kanRinpin
koDiyeen enpiLLaiyaip pookkineen ellee paavamee.
229.meaning: Without even giving him an umbrella or sandals, I, cruel one, sent Damodara — so that into the harsh, scorching forest full of biting, piercing hot gravel-stones, behind the calves, his tender feet should ache. Cruel-hearted me, I sent my child away. Oh, what a sin is mine!

230.translit:
endrum enakku iniyaanai en maNi vaNNanai
kandrin pin pookkinen endru asoodai kazhaRiya
ponthikazh maadap puduvaiyar koan paTTan sol
inthamizh maalaigaL vallavarkku idar illaiyee.
230.meaning: "He is ever sweet to me, my gem-hued Lord; yet I sent Him behind the calves," thus Yashoda lamented. These words were composed by Pattan (Periyalwar), king of Puduvai of gold-gleaming mansions. For those who can master these garlands of sweet Tamil, there shall be no sorrow.

231.translit:
seelaik kudambai orukaadu orukaadu sennira meel thoondrip poo
koolap paNaikkachchum kooRaiyudaiyum kuLirmuththin koodaalamum
kaalip pinnee varuginRa kadalvaNNan veedaththai vandhu kaaNeer
yaalaththup puthiranaip peRRaar nangaimeer naanoo maRRaarum illai.
231.meaning: In one ear a cloth-stud, in the other a flower glowing rosy-red above; a beautiful broad waistband and lower garment, and a canopy-ornament of cool pearls. Come and behold the ocean-hued one's guise as He comes behind the cattle. O ladies, was it only I in this world who bore such a son, and no one else?

232.translit:
kanninan maamadiL soozhtharu poompozhil kaaviriththennarangam
манniya seermadhusoodanaa keesavaa paaviyeen vaazhvugandhu
unnai iLangandru meykkach chiRukaalee yooTTi oruppaduththeen
ennin manamvaliyaaL oru peN illai en kuTTanee muththam thaa.
232.meaning: O Madhusudana of eternal glory, O Keshava who dwells in southern Srirangam by the Kaveri, ringed by flowering groves and fine great new ramparts! Sinful that I am, delighting in my own living, I fed you early and made you ready to go graze the young calves. There is no woman with a heart as hard as mine. My little one, give me a kiss.

233.translit:
kaadugaL oodupooyk kandrugaL meyththu maRiyoodi kaarkkoodal poos
soodi varuginRa daamodaraa kaRRuth thooLigaaN un udambu
peedai mayiR chaayal pinnai maNaaLaa neeraaTTu amaiththu vaiththeen
aadi amudhusey appanum uNdilan unnoodu udanee yuNbaan.
233.meaning: O Damodara, coming home wearing kantal flowers, having roamed through forests grazing the calves and racing after the kids! Look, your body is covered in dust. O bridegroom of Pinnai, lovely as a peahen, I have made ready your bath. Bathe and eat your meal. Your father too has not eaten, that he may dine together with you.

234.translit:
kadiyaar pozhilaNi veengadavaa karumpoorERee nee yugakkum
kudaiyum seruppum kuzhalum tharuvikkak koLLaadhee poonaay maalee
kadiyaveng kaanidaik kandrin pin poona siRukkuTTach cheng kamala
adiyum vedhumbi un kaNgaL sivandhaay asaindhiTTaay nee embiraan.
234.meaning: O Venkata-dweller adorned with fragrant groves, O dark mighty bull! Though I had your beloved parasol, sandals, and flute fetched, you went off, Tirumal, without taking them. Little one who went behind the calves into the harsh cruel forest, your red-lotus feet are scorched, your eyes have turned red, and you droop with fatigue, O my Lord.

235.translit:
paRRaar nadungamun paanjasanniyaththai vaayvaiththa pooRERee en
siRRaayar singamee seethaimaNaaLaa siRukkuTTach cheng kaN maalee
siRRaadaiyum siRup paththiramum ivai kaTTilin meelvaiththup pooy
kaRRaayaroodu nee kandrugaL meyththuk kalandhudan vandhaay poolum.
235.meaning: O bull who set the Panchajanya conch to your lips so that foes trembled, O lion of the little cowherds, O bridegroom of Sita, little red-eyed Tirumal! Leaving your small garment and little dagger here upon the cot, you went and grazed the calves with the young cowherds, and have come back mingling with them, it seems.

236.translit:
anjudar aazhi un kaiyagaththu eendhum azhagaa nee poygai pukku
nanjumizh naagaththinoodu piNangavum naan uyir vaazhndhirundheen
ensseyya ennai vayiRu maRukkinaay eedhumoor achcham illai
kanjan manaththukku ugappanavee seydhaay kaayaampoo vaNNam koNdaay.
236.meaning: O beautiful one who bears the fiery, dazzling discus in your hand! When you plunged into the pond and grappled with the venom-spewing serpent, how did I go on living? Why did you so churn my insides? You have no fear at all. You did only what gladdened Kamsa's heart, O you who took on the hue of the kaya blossom.

237.translit:
pandriyum aamaiyum meenamum aagiya paaRkadal vaNNaa unmeel
kandrin uruvaagi meypulaththee vandha kaLLa asuran thannai
sendru pidiththuch chiRukkaigaLaalee viLangaay eRindhaay poolum
endrum en piLLaikkuth theemaigaL seyvaargaL anganamaa vaargaLee.
237.meaning: O milk-ocean-hued one who became the boar, the tortoise, and the fish! The deceitful demon who came in the form of a calf into the grazing field, you ran and seized, and with your small hands hurled him to bring down the wood-apple fruits, it seems. Those who would do evil to my child shall ever end up just so.

238.translit:
keeTTaRiyaadhana keeTkindreen keesavaa koovalar indhiraRku
kaaTTiya sooRum kaRiyum thayirum kalandhudan uNdaay poolum
ooTTa mudhalileen unthannaik koNdu oruboodhum enakku aridhu
vaaTTamilaap pugazh vaasudhevaa unnai anjuvan indru thoTTum.
238.meaning: I hear of you what I have never heard before, O Keshava! It seems you mingled and ate up the rice, curries, and curd that the cowherds had set out for Indra. I have no means to feed you; to keep you with me is at no time possible for me. O Vasudeva of unfading fame, from this day on I dread you.

239.translit:
thiNNaar veNsangu udaiyaay thirunaaL thiruvoNam indree zhu naaLmun
paNNeer mozhiyaaraik koovi muLaiyaTTip pallaaNdu kooRuviththeen
kaNNaalam seyyak kaRiyum kalaththu arisiyum aakki vaiththeen
kaNNaa nee naaLaith thoTTuk kandrin pin pookeel koolam sey thingee iru.
239.meaning: O holder of the firm white conch! Seven days hence is the sacred day, your star Tiruvonam. I have already called the sweet-voiced women, set the sprouts, and had the "long-life" benediction recited. For the auspicious rite I have prepared the curries and the rice in vessels. O Kanna, from tomorrow do not go behind the calves; deck yourself in finery and stay here.

240.translit:
puRRaravalgul asoodai nallaaychchi than puththiran goovindhanai
kaRRinam meyththu varakkaNdu ugandhu avaL kaRpiththa maaRRam ellaam
seRRamilaadhavar vaazhtharu thenpuduvai viTTusiththan sol
kaRRivai paadavallaar kadal vaNNan kazhaliNai kaaNbaargaLee.
240.meaning: Of the good cowherd-lady Yashoda, slender of waist as a serpent's hood, who, seeing her son Govinda come home from grazing the calves, rejoiced and instructed him in all these words. These are the verses of Vishnuchitta of southern Puduvai, where the malice-free dwell. Those who can learn and sing them shall behold the twin ankleted feet of the ocean-hued Lord.

241.translit:
thazhaigaLum thongalum thathumbi engum
  thaNNumai ekkam maththaLi thaazh peeli
kuzhalgaLum geethamum aagi engum
  koovindhan varuginRa kooTTam kaNdu
mazhaikoloo varuginRadhu endru solli
  mangaimaar saalaga vaasal paRRi
nuzhaivanar niRpanaraagi engum
  uLLam viTTu ooN maRandhozhindhanaree.
241.meaning: With leafy garments and hanging garlands brimming everywhere, with the small drum, the ekkam, the mridangam, drooping peacock-feathers, with flutes and song everywhere, beholding the throng with which Govinda comes home, the young women cry, "Is it the rain-cloud that comes?" Clutching at the lattice windows, some slipping in, some standing about everywhere, they have lost their hearts and forgotten to eat.

242.translit:
vallinuN idhalanna aadai koNdu
 vasaiyaRath thiruvarai viriththuduththu
pallinuN paRRaaga udaivaaL saaththip
 paNaikkach chundhip palathazhai naduvee
mullai nalnaRu malar veengai malar
 aNindhu pallaayar kuzhaam naduvee
elliyam poodhaagap piLLai varum
 edhir nindru anginavaLai izhaveenminee.
242.meaning: With a garment fine as the thin petal of a creeper, wrapped faultlessly and spread about his sacred waist, with a dagger girded by a fine close grip, with a broad waistband tucked in, amid many leafy adornments, decked with sweet jasmine and vengai blossoms, amid the throng of the many cowherds, at the lovely dusk the boy comes home. Do not stand before him and lose your bangles, O women.

243.translit:
surigaiyum theRivillum seNdu koolum
 meelaadaiyum thoozhanmaar koNdooda
orukaiyaal oruvan than thoLai yoondri
 aanirai inammeeLak kuRiththa sangam
varugaiyil vaadiya piLLai kaNNan
 manjaLum meeniyum vadivum kaNdaaL
arugee nindraaL en peN nookkik kaNdaaL
 adhukaNdu ivvoor ondru puNarkkinRadhee.
243.meaning: His companions running off carrying his sword, his pellet-bow, his ball-stick, his staff and upper garment; resting one hand upon a friend's shoulder; with the conch that sounded for the herds of cattle to turn home, when Kannan, the boy wearied from his coming, my girl beheld him close by, gazing on the turmeric-glow, his form, his beauty. She stood near and looked and looked, and seeing that, this village now weaves a tale about it.

244.translit:
kundreduththu aanirai kaaththa piraan
  koovalanaayk kuzhaloodhi yoodhi
kandrugaL meyththuth than thoozharooda
  kalandhudan varuvaanaith theruvil kaNdu
endrum ivanaiyoppaarai nangaay
  kaNdaRiyeen eedi vandhu kaaNaay
ondrum nillaavaLai kazhandru
  thugal eendhiLa mulaiyum en vasamallavee.
244.meaning: The Lord who lifted the mountain and saved the herds, who as a cowherd plays and plays upon his flute, who grazes the calves and comes home mingling with his companions, seeing him in the street: O dear girl, never have I seen one to equal him; come, friend, and behold him! My bangles, that will not stay, have slipped off; my young breasts, swelling, are no longer in my control.

245.translit:
suRRi nindru aayar thazhaigaL idach
  surulpangi neeththiraththaal aNindhu
paRRi nindru aayar kadaiththalaiyee
  paadavum aadak kaNdeen andrip pin
maRRoruvarkku ennaip peesaloTTeen
  maaliruny soolai em maayaRkallaal
koRRavanukku ivaLaam endreNNik
  koduminkaL kodeeraagil koozhambamee.
245.meaning: With the cowherds standing round offering leafy garments, his curly locks adorned with an eye-shaped ornament, I saw him at the cowherds' gateway as they sang and he danced. Since then I will not let myself be spoken of for any other man, save only our wondrous Lord of Maaliruncholai. Thinking "she belongs to this victorious one," give her to him; if you do not, there will only be confusion.

246.translit:
sindhuram ilangath than thiru neRRimeel
 thiruththiya koorambum thirukkuzhalum
andhara muzhavath thaNthazhaik kaavinkeezh
 varumaayaroodu udan vaLaikool veesa
andhamondrillaadha aayap piLLai
 aRindhaRindhu ivveedhi poodhumaagil
pandhukoNdaan endru vaLaiththu vaiththup
 pavaLavaay muRuvalum kaaNboom thoozhee.
246.meaning: With the vermilion mark gleaming, upon his sacred forehead the adjusted curl and the sacred locks, beneath the cool leafy canopy amid the sky-filling drum-sound, coming with the cowherds, swinging his crook, that endless cowherd boy — if knowingly and again knowingly he should come down this street, saying "He took our ball!" let us surround and detain him, and we shall see his coral-lipped smile, O friend.

247.translit:
saalappal niraippinnee thazhaik kaavinkeezh
  than thirumeeni nindroLi thigazha
neela nalnaRung kunji neeththiraththaal aNindhu
  pallaayar kuzhaam naduvee
koolach chendhaamaraikkaN miLirak
  kuzhaloodhi yisaipaadik kuniththu aayaroodu
aaliththu varuginRa aayap piLLai
  azhagu kaNdu en magal ayarkkinRadhee.
247.meaning: Behind the many great herds, beneath the leafy canopy, his sacred form standing aglow with radiance, his fine fragrant dark forelock adorned with an eye-shaped ornament, amid the throng of the many cowherds, his beautiful red-lotus eyes glittering, playing the flute, singing melody and dancing, the cowherd boy comes rejoicing with the cowherds. Beholding his beauty, my daughter swoons.

248.translit:
sindhurap podikkoNdu senni yappith
  thirunaama miTTangu oril aiyandhannaal
andhara mindrith than neRi pangiyai
  azhagiya neeththiraththaal aNindhu
indhiran poolvarum aayap piLLai
  edhirnindrangu inavaLai izhaveelenna
sandhiyil nindru kaNdeer nangai than
  thugiloodu sari vaLaikazhalginRadhee.
248.meaning: Smearing vermilion powder, anointing his head, setting the sacred name-mark there with a certain leaf, and adorning his curly locks faultlessly with a beautiful eye-shaped ornament, the cowherd boy comes like Indra. When I said, "Do not stand before him and lose your bangles," see — standing at the crossroads my girl gazed, and now along with her garment her bangles are slipping off.

249.translit:
valangaadhin meel thoondrip poo aNindhu
  malligai vanamaalai meLval maalai
silingaaraththaal kuzhal thaazha viTTuth
  theenguzhal vaaymaduththu oodhi yoodhi
alangaaraththaal varumaayap piLLai
  azhagukaNdu en magal aasaippaTTu
vilanginillaadhu edhir nindru kaNdeer
  veLvaLai kazhandru meymmeligalginRadhee.
249.meaning: With a flower blooming above his right ear, wearing jasmine, a forest-garland, and a garland of wild jasmine, his locks let down gracefully, setting the sweet flute to his mouth and playing and playing, the cowherd boy comes in his finery. Beholding his beauty, my daughter, smitten with longing, would not turn aside but stood right before him and gazed — see, her white bangles have slipped off and her body grows faint.

250.translit:
viNNin meedhu amararkaL virumbi thozha miRaiththu aayar paadiyil veedhi yoodE
kaNNan kaalip pinnE ezhundharuLa kaNdu iLavaay kannimaar kaamutta
vaNNam vaNdamar pozhil puduvaiyar koan
vittu chiththan chonna maalai paththum
paNNin pam varap paadum paththar uLLaar
paramaana vaikundham naNNuvarE.
250.meaning: As the immortals in the heavens longed and worshipped, He resplendently went forth through the streets of the cowherd village (Ayarpadi), Kannan walking behind the cattle. Seeing this, the tender young maidens fell in love. These ten verses of the garland, spoken by Vishnuchittan (Periyalwar), the king of Puduvai whose groves are filled with bees -- those devotees who sing them sweetly with melody and joy shall reach the supreme Vaikuntha.

251.translit:
attuk kuvi choattup paruppadhamum
thayir vaaviyum ney aLaRum adanga
potta thuttu maaip pagai puNarththa
poru maa kadal vaNNan poRuththa malai
vatta thadangaN madamaan kanRinai
valai vaay pattik koNdu kuRa magaLir
kottai thalaip paal koduththu vaLarkkum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
251.meaning: The mountain that the ocean-hued Lord lifted up and held -- He who countered the enmity of the rains by gulping down entirely the heaped mounds of cooked rice, the lakes of curd, and the streams of ghee. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where the hill-women catch in their snares the fawn of the gentle round-eyed deer and rear it, feeding it cotton-tipped milk.

252.translit:
vazhu onRum ilaa cheygai vaanavar koan
valip pattu munindhu vidukkap pattu
mazhai vandhu ezhu naaL peydhu maa thadukka
madhusoodhan eduththu maRiththa malai
izhavu thariyaadhadhu oar eettrup pidi
iLan cheeyam thodarndhu mudugudhalum
kuzhavi idaik kaal ittedhirndhu porum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
252.meaning: The mountain that Madhusudana lifted up and held aloft when the king of the gods (Indra), of faultless deeds, grew angry by his might and let loose the clouds, which came and poured rain for seven days as a great flood to obstruct -- it is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where a mother elephant who cannot bear the loss, when a young lion pursues and presses upon her, sets her calf between her legs and fights it head-on.

253.translit:
ammai thadangaN madavaay chiyarum
aanaayarum aa niraiyum alaRi
emmai charaN enRu koLLenRu irappa
ilangaazhi kai endhai eduththa malai
thammai charaN enRa tham paavaiyarai
puna mEyginRa maaninam kaaNmin enRu
kommai puyak kunRar chilai kunikkum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
253.meaning: The mountain that my Father with the radiant discus in His hand lifted up, when the cowherd-women with large lovely eyes, the cowherds, and the herds of cattle all cried out begging 'Be our refuge, accept us!' It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where the hill-men with mountain-like ample shoulders bend their bows, saying 'Behold the herd of deer grazing in the field that the maidens who sought refuge resemble.'

254.translit:
kaduvaay china veng kaN kaLittRinukku
kavaLam eduththuk koduppaan avan poal
adivaay uRak kai ittu ezhap paRiththittu
amarar perumaan koNdu ninRa malai
kadalvaay chenRu mEgam kavizhndhu iRangi
kadhuvaay pada neer mugandhu ERi engum
kudavaay pada ninRu mazhai pozhiyum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
254.meaning: The mountain that the Lord of the immortals plucked up from its very base, thrusting His hand under and lifting it, like one who takes up a ball of food to feed a fierce hot-eyed elephant of cruel mouth, and stood holding it. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where the clouds go to the sea, bend down and descend, scoop up water in their wide mouths, rise, and stand pouring rain everywhere as if from tilted pots.

255.translit:
vaanaththil uLLeer valiyeer uLLeerEl
aRaiyoa vandhu vaangumin enbavan poal
Enaththu uruvaagiya eesan endhai
idavan ezha vaangi eduththa malai
kaanak kaLi aanai than kombu izhandhu
kadhuvaay madham choara than kai eduththu
koonal piRai vENdi aNNaandhu niRkum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
255.meaning: The mountain that my Father, the Lord who once took the form of a boar (Varaha), drew up and lifted with His left hand, as if to say 'O you who dwell in the heavens, if any of you are strong, come challenge and take it away!' It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where a wild forest elephant in rut, having lost its tusk, with rut-fluid trickling from its mouth, raises its trunk and stands gazing upward longing for the crescent moon.

256.translit:
cheppaadu udaiya thirumaal avan than
chenthaamarai kai viral aindhinaiyum
kappaaga maduththu maNi nedunthoaL
kaambaaga koduththu kaviththa malai
eppaadum parandhu izhi theL aruvi
ilangu maNi muththu vadam piRazha
kuppaayam ena ninRu kaatchi tharum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
256.meaning: The mountain that the gracious Tirumal raised aloft as a canopy, propping the five fingers of His red-lotus hand as supports and offering His long jewel-like shoulder as the central pole. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where the clear cascades that fall spreading on every side gleam like a shifting string of bright pearls, standing forth as a sight like a fine garment.

257.translit:
padangaL palavum udaip paamparaiyan
padar boomiyai thaangik kidappavan poal
thadangai viral aindhum malara vaiththu
thaamoadharan thaangu thada varai thaan
adanga chenRu ilangaiyai eedu azhiththa
anuman pugazh paadi tham kuttankaLai
kudangai koNdu mandhigaL kaN vaLarththum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
257.meaning: The broad mountain that Damodara held up, spreading wide the five fingers of His large hand, like the king of serpents with many hoods who lies bearing the vast earth. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where female monkeys cradle their little ones in their cupped palms and lull them to sleep while singing the praises of Hanuman, who went and utterly destroyed the dignity of Lanka.

258.translit:
chala maa mugil pal kaNap poarkkaLaththu
chara maari pozhindhu engum poochalittu
nalivaan uRak kEdagam koappavan poal
naaraayaNan mun mugam kaaththa malai
ilai vEy kurambai thava maa munivar
irundhaar naduvE chenRu aNaar choRiya
kolaivaay china vEngaigaL ninRu uRangum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
258.meaning: The mountain that Narayana held before His face like a shield, when the great dark clouds, like dense ranks in a battlefield, rained down showers of arrows, raising an uproar everywhere to oppress -- He warded them off. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where murderous fierce tigers go and stand among the great ascetic sages dwelling in leaf-thatched huts, and rub their chins against them and doze.

259.translit:
van pEy mulai uNdadhu oar vaay udaiyan
van thooN ena ninRadhu oar van paraththai
than pEr ittuk koNdu tharaNi thannil
thaamoadharan thaangu thada varai thaan
munbE vazhi kaatta musuk kaNangaL
mudhugil peydhu tham udaik kuttankaLai
kombu ERti irundhu kudhi payittRum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
259.meaning: The broad mountain that Damodara -- He of the mouth that drank from the breast of the cruel demoness (Putana), that mighty Being who stood firm as a strong pillar -- held up on this earth, giving it His own name. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where troops of monkeys, with the lead ones showing the way, carry their own little ones on their backs, climb up onto the branches, and train them to leap.

260.translit:
kodiyERu chenthaamarai kai viralgaL
koalamum azhindhila vaadittRila
vadivERu thiru ugir nondhumila
maNivaNNan malaiyum oar champiradham
mudiyERtiya maa mugiR pal kaNangaL
mun nettRi naraiththana poala engum
kudiyERti irundhu mazhai pozhiyum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudaiyE.
260.meaning: The fingers of the splendid red-lotus hand neither lost their beauty nor wilted, nor did the lovely sacred nails ache or suffer -- truly the mountain of the sapphire-hued Lord was but a feat of wonder. It is Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where the dense flocks of great clouds that rise to its summit settle and dwell everywhere, pouring rain, like grey hair whitening upon a forehead.

261.translit:
aravil paLLi koNdu aravam thurandhittu
aravap pagai oordhiyavan udaiya
kuravil kodi mullaigaL ninRu uRangum
ovarthanam ennum kottRak kudai mEl
thiruvil poli maa maRai vaaNar puththoor
thigazh pattar piraan chonna maalai paththum
paravu mana nanku udaip paththar uLLaar
paramaana vaikundham naNNuvarE.
261.meaning: He who reclines upon the serpent (Adisesha), who drove away the serpent (Kaliya), and whose mount is the enemy of serpents (Garuda) -- upon His Govardhana, the victorious umbrella, where the jasmine creepers twining on the bottle-flower trees stand and sleep. These ten verses of the garland, spoken by the revered Bhattar (Periyalwar) of Puttur, the radiant home of great Vedic scholars resplendent with prosperity -- those devotees who possess minds that well cherish and extol them shall reach the supreme Vaikuntha.

262.translit:
naaval am periya theevinil vaazhum
nangaimeergaL idhu oar aRpudham kELeer
thoova lam puri udaiya thirumaal
thooya vaayil kuzhal oasai vazhiyE
koavalar chiRumiyar iLam kongai
kudhukalippa udal uL vizhindhu engum
kaavalum kadandhu kayiRu maalaiyaagi
vandhu kavizhndhu ninRanarE.
262.meaning: O ladies who dwell in the great Jambudvipa, hear of this wonder! At the sound of the flute from the pure mouth of Tirumal who holds the bright auspicious conch, the young cowherd-girls -- their tender breasts thrilling, their bodies melting within -- crossed past all the guards and, becoming like a garland strung on a rope, came and stood drooping over (in surrender).

263.translit:
ida vaNarai idath thoaLodu chaayththu
iru kai koodap puruvam nerindhE
kuda vayiRu pada vaay kadai koodak
koavindhan kuzhal kodu oodhina poadhu
mada mayilgaLoadu maan piNai poalE
mangaimaargaL malark koondhal avizha
udai negizha oar kaiyaal thugil pattRi
olgi oadari kaN oada ninRanarE.
263.meaning: When Govinda played his flute -- bending his waist sideways with his left shoulder leaning, his two hands meeting, his brows knit, his belly drawn in pot-like, the corners of his mouth pursed together -- the young women, like gentle peahens and doe deer, with their flowery tresses loosening and their garments slipping, holding their cloth with one hand, stood swaying with their lovely streaked eyes darting about.

264.translit:
vaan iLa varasu vaikundhak kuttan
vaasudEvan madhurai mannan nandha
koan iLa varasu koavalar kuttan
koavindhan kuzhal kodu oodhina poadhu
vaan iLam padiyar vandhu vandhu eeNdi
manam urugi malark kaNgaL panippa
thEn aLavu cheRi koondhal avizha
chenni vErppa chevi chErththu ninRanarE.
264.meaning: The young prince of heaven, the child of Vaikuntha, Vasudeva, the king of Mathura, the young prince-son of King Nanda, the cowherd lad Govinda -- when he played his flute, the young women of the heavens came crowding again and again, their hearts melting, their flowery eyes brimming with tears, their honey-laden thick tresses loosening, their brows sweating, and stood with their ears intent upon (the music).

265.translit:
thEnugan pilamban kaaLiyan ennum
theep pampoodugaL adanga uzhakki
kaanagam padi ulaavi ulaavik
karum chiRukkan kuzhal oodhina poadhu
mEnagaiyodu thiloaththamai arambai
uruppachi yaravar veLgi mayangi
vaanagam padiyil vaay thiRappinRi
aadal paadalavai maaRinar thaamE.
265.meaning: Having trampled and crushed utterly the venomous serpent-like enemies named Dhenuka, Pralamba, and Kaliya, roaming and roaming about the forest land, when the dark little lad played his flute, the celestial nymphs Menaka, Tilottama, Rambha, Urvashi and the rest grew abashed and bewildered, and in the realm of heaven, unable even to open their mouths, ceased their dancing and singing of their own accord.

266.translit:
mun narachingam adhaagi avuNan
mukkiyaththai mudippaan moo ulagil
mannar anjum madhusoodhanan vaayil
kuzhalin oasai cheviyai pattRi vaanga
nan narambu udaiya thumburuvoadu
naaradhanum tham tham veeNai maRandhu
kinnara midhunangaLum tham tham
kinnaram thodu kiloam enRanarE.
266.meaning: Madhusudana, who long ago became Narasimha to end the demon's (Hiranyakashipu's) supremacy, before whom the kings of the three worlds tremble -- when the sound of the flute from his mouth seized their ears and drew them, Tumburu of the fine-stringed lute and Narada too forgot their own veenas, and the pairs of kinnaras said, 'We will not touch our own kinnara-lutes.'

267.translit:
chem perun thadangaNNan thiraL thoaLan
dhEvagi chiruvan dhEvargaL chingam
namparaman in naaL kuzhal oodhak
kEttavargaL idar uttRana kELeer
ambaram thiriyum kaandhapparellaam
amudha geedha valaiyaal churukkuNdu
namparam anRu enRu naaNi mayangi
naindhu choarndhu kaim maRiththu ninRanarE.
267.meaning: The Lord with red large lovely eyes and rounded shoulders, the son of Devaki, the lion among the gods, our Supreme One -- on this day when he played the flute, hear of the affliction that befell those who heard! All the Gandharvas who roam the sky were ensnared in the net of his ambrosial music, and saying 'This is beyond our power,' grew ashamed and bewildered, withered and fainting, stood wringing their hands.

268.translit:
buviyuL naan kaNdadhu oar aRpudham kELeer
pooNi mEykkum iLam koavalar koottaththu
avaiyuL naagath thaNaiyaan kuzhal oodha
amara loagaththu aLavum chenRu ichaippa
aviyuNaa maRandhu vaanavarellaam
aayar paadi niRaiyap pugundhu eeNdi
chevi uNaavin chuvai koNdu magizhndhu
koavindhanai thodarndhu enRum vidaarE.
268.meaning: Hear the wonder I beheld upon this earth! In the gathering of young cowherds who graze the cattle, when He who rests upon the serpent (Adisesha) played his flute amid them, the sound went forth all the way to the world of the immortals and resounded. Forgetting their oblation-food, all the celestials came and thronged, filling the cowherd village, and savoring the taste through their ears, rejoicing, they followed Govinda and would never leave him.

269.translit:
chiru viralgaL thadavi pari maaRa
chengaN koada cheyya vaay koppaLikka
kuRu veyarppu puruvam koodalippa
koavindhan kuzhal kodu oodhina poadhu
paRavaiyin kaNangaL koodu thuRandhu
vandhu choozhndhu padu kaadu kidappa
kaRavaiyin kaNangaL kaal parappittu
kavizhndhu iRangi chevi yaattagillaavE.
269.meaning: When Govinda played his flute -- his small fingers moving and shifting over it, his red eyes drawn sideways, his lovely mouth swelling out, fine sweat beading and his brows drawing together -- the flocks of birds abandoned their nests and came and gathered round, lying like a felled forest, and the herds of milch cows, spreading out their legs, bent their heads down and stood unable even to flick their ears.

270.translit:
thiraNDe zhuthazhai mazhaimukil vaNNan
 sengkamala malarsoozh vaNDinam poolE
suruNDi ruNDakuzhal thaazhntha mukaththaan
 oothu kinrakuzha loosai vazhiyE
maruNDumaan kaNangkaL meeykai maranthu
 meeyntha pullum kadai vaayvazhi soora
iraNDu paadum thulang kaappudai peyaraa
 ezhuthusith thirangkaL poola ninranavE.
270.meaning: He is dark-hued like the gathered rain-clouds, his face framed by curling, tangled locks that hang down, like a swarm of bees encircling a red lotus blossom. As the sound of the flute he plays drifts forth, the herds of deer, bewildered, forget to graze, the grass they had been eating slipping from the corners of their mouths. On both sides they stood without stirring or moving away, like figures painted in a picture.

271.translit:
karungkaN thookaimayir peeli yaNinthu
 kaTTinan kuduththa peethaka vaadai
arungkala vuruvi naayar perumaan
 avanoru vankuzha loothina poothu
marangkaL ninru mathu thaaraikaL paayum
 malarkaL veezhum vaLar kombukaL thaazhum
irangkum koombum thirumaal ninraninra
 pakkam nookki avaiseyyum kuNamE.
271.meaning: He is adorned with the eye-spotted plumes of the peacock, wearing a fine yellow silk garment well bound about him, the Lord of the cowherds with a form rare as a precious jewel. When that singular one plays his flute, the trees stand and stream forth torrents of honey, their flowers fall, their growing branches bow down. They soften and they fold their leaves, turning toward whatever side Tirumal stands; such are the things they do.

272.translit:
kuzhali ruNDusuruN DEriya kunchik
 koovintha nudaiya koomaLa vaayil
kuzhalmu zhainjukaLi noodu kumizhththuk
 kozhiththi zhintha amutha ppunal thannai
kuzhalmuzha vamviLam pumputhu vaikkoon
 viTTu siththan viriththathamizh vallaar
kuzhalai venrakuLir vaayina raakich
 saathu kooTTiyuL koLLappadu vaarE.
272.meaning: The nectar-stream that wells up and pours down through the holes of the flute held at the tender mouth of Govinda, whose dark, curling hair clusters upward in thick tresses. Those who are skilled in this Tamil composed and expounded by Vishnuchitta, lord of Puthuvai resounding with flute and drum, will become possessors of mouths cool and sweeter than the flute, and will be welcomed into the assembly of the good.

273.translit:
aiya puzhuthi udamba LainthuivaL pEcchu malantha laiyaay
seyya noolin sirraadai seppi nudukkavum valla LallaL
kaiyi nilsiru thoothai yoodu ivaL murril pirinthumilaL
paiya ravaNaip paLLi yaanoodu kaivaiththu ivaLvarumE.
273.meaning: Her little body all smeared with fine dust, her speech still confused and lisping, she is not yet able to dress herself properly in a tiny garment of red thread. She will not part for a moment from the little earthen pot in her hand or from her playthings. And yet this girl comes with her hand laid in the hand of Him who reclines upon the broad-hooded serpent.

274.translit:
vaayil pallum ezhunthila mayirum mudikoo Dirrila
saayvi laatha kurunthalaich silapiL LaikaLoo Dinangki
theeyi Nakkinang kaadivanthuivaL thannanna semmai solli
maayan maamaNi vaNNan mElivaL maaluru kinraaLE.
274.meaning: The teeth in her mouth have not yet come in, her hair has not yet grown enough to be tied, yet she joins with a few small children of unfailing little stature. Mingling and quarreling with them, then making up, she comes and speaks proper words befitting herself, and this girl grows lovesick over the wondrous Lord of the great gem-like hue.

275.translit:
pongku veNmaNal koNDu sirrilum murrath thizhaikkaluril
sangku sakkaram thaNDu vaaLvillu mallathu izhaikka luraaL
kongkai innam kuvinthe zhunthila koovintha noodu ivaLai
sangkai yaakien nuLLam naaLthorum thaTTuLup paakinrathE.
275.meaning: When she sets out to build little play-houses in the courtyard with heaped-up white sand, she will fashion nothing but the conch, the discus, the mace, the sword and the bow. Her breasts have not yet risen and rounded, yet over Govinda and this girl my heart is filled with anxious doubt, and day by day it grows ever more troubled and tossed about.

276.translit:
Ezhai pEthaiOr paalakan vanthuen peNmaka Laiyermki
thoozhi maarpalar koNDu poycheytha soozhcchiyai yaarkkuraikkEn
aazhiyaa nennu maazha moozhaiyil paaychchi akappaduththi
moozhai yuppari yaatha thennum moothuraiyu milaLE.
276.meaning: A frail, foolish young boy came and bewitched my girl-child; with whom shall I share the scheme that her many girl-companions have worked? He has cast her into the deep abyss of one called the bearer of the discus and ensnared her there. She no longer even recalls the old proverb that the ladle knows nothing of the taste of the salt.

277.translit:
naadum oorum ariya vEpooy nalla thuzhaayalangkaL
soodi naaraNan poomida mellaam soothith thuzhitharukinraaL
kEdu vENdu kinraar palaruLar kEsava noodu ivaLai
paadu kaava lidumi nenrenru paarthadu maarinathE.
277.meaning: So that the whole land and town may know it, she goes out wearing fine garlands of tulasi, searching and wandering through every place Narayana goes. There are many who wish her harm; the world has grown bewildered, saying again and again, 'Set guards on every side to keep this girl from Kesava!'

278.translit:
paTTam kaTTip porrOdu peythuivaL paadaka mumsilampum
iTTa maaka vaLarththeduth thEnukku ennoodu irukkaluraaL
poTTap pooypparap paTTuninruivaL poovaippoo vaNNaa vennum
paTTa vaarkuzhal mangkai meerivaL maaluru kinraaLE.
278.meaning: Having bound the forehead-ornament on her, set golden earrings, anklets and ankle-bells upon her, I raised her up with delight, yet she will not stay with me. Slipping abruptly outside and standing there, she cries out, 'O you whose hue is the kaaya flower!' O maidens with broad ornamented tresses, this girl grows lovesick.

279.translit:
pEsa vumtheri yaatha peNmaiyin pEthaiyEn pEthaiivaL
koosa minrinin raarkaL thammethir koolkazhin thaanmoozhaiyaay
kEsavaa venrum kEdilee yenrum kinjuka vaaymozhiyaaL
vaasa vaarkuzhal mangkai meerivaL maaluru kinraaLE.
279.meaning: I am the foolish one, of a girlhood that does not even know how to speak; and this girl of mine is innocent. Without any shame, standing before those who are present, slack as a ladle slipped from the staff, with her coral-red mouth she keeps uttering, 'Kesava!' and 'O imperishable One!' O maidens with fragrant flowing tresses, this girl grows lovesick.

280.translit:
kaarai pooNum kaNNaadi kaaNumthan kaiyil vaLaikulukkum
koorai yudukkum ayarkkumthang kovvaich sevvaay thiruththum
thEriith thEriininru aayirampErth thEvan thirambi tharrum
maaril maamaNi vaNNan mElivaL maaluru kinraaLE.
280.meaning: She puts on her neck-ornament, gazes into the mirror, jingles the bangles on her hand; she dresses in her garment, then forgets herself, and adjusts her red kovvai-fruit lips. Pondering and pondering, she stands and recites the thousand names of the Lord and speaks of his glory. Over the matchless Lord of the great gem-like hue, this girl grows lovesick.

281.translit:
kaiththalath thuLLa maadazhiyak kaNNaa langkaL seythuivaLai
vaiththu vaiththuk koNDuenna vaaNibam nammai vaduppaduththum
seyththa laiyezhu naarrup poolavan seyvana seythukoLLa
maiththa damukil vaNNan pakkal vaLara vidumingkaLE.
281.meaning: Casting his enchanting eye-glances so that the wealth within our hands is ruined, what profit is there for us in keeping and keeping this girl, when it only brings us disgrace? Like the seedlings sprung up at the edge of the field, let him do with her whatever he will do; send her off to grow at the side of Him whose hue is the dark spreading cloud.

282.translit:
peruppe ruththakaN Naalangkalseythu pENinam millath thuLLE
iruththuvaa neNNi naamirukka ivaLumonru eNNu kinraaL
maruththu vappatham neengkinaa Lennum vaarththai paduvathanmun
oruppa duththidu minivaLai ulakaLan thaani daikkE.
282.meaning: While we, casting our great and tender eye-glances upon her, were minded to keep her cherished within our own house, she too is set on a purpose of her own. Before the word goes round that she has passed beyond the reach of any cure, make up your minds and place this girl at the side of Him who measured the worlds.

283.translit:
njaala murrumuNDu aali laiththuyil naaraa yaNanukkuivaL
maala thaaki makizhnthana Lenru thaayurai seythathanai
koola maarpozhil soozhputhuvaiyarkoon viTTusiththansonna
maalai paththum valla varkaTku illai varuthuyarE.
283.meaning: Of how this girl became lovesick and rejoiced over Narayana, who swallowed the whole world and then slept upon a banyan leaf, her mother spoke; and these words, set as ten verses by Vishnuchitta, lord of the men of Puthuvai girdled with beautiful fragrant groves. For those who can master these ten verses, no sorrow shall come.

284.translit:
nallathOr thaamaraip poykai naaNmalar mElpani soora
alliyum thaathum uthirnthiTTu azhakazhin thaaloththa thaalOo
illam veriyooDir raalOo enmaka LaiengkumkaaNEn
mallarai yaTTavan pinpooy mathuraippu rampukkaaL kolOo.
284.meaning: Like a fresh-blooming flower on a good lotus-pond from which the dew has dripped, its petals and pollen fallen away and its beauty undone, so it is, alas. The house has become desolate, alas; nowhere do I see my daughter. Has she gone following after Him who destroyed the wrestlers and entered the outskirts of Mathura?

285.translit:
onru marivonril laatha uruvaraik koopaalar thangkal
kanrukaal maarumaa poolE kanni yirunthaaLaik koNDu
nanrum kiriseythu poonaan naaraaya Nanseytha theemai
enrum emarkaL kudikkuOo Recchukso laayidung kolOo.
285.meaning: Like the cowherds of harsh, formless nature, who know nothing of right and wrong, swapping the legs of one calf for another, he took away my daughter who had been a maiden. Working much trickery, he went off; and this cruel deed that Narayana has done, will it become a lasting reproach upon our family forever?

286.translit:
kumari maNamseythu koNDu koolamseythu illath thiruththi
thamarum pirarum ariyath thaamootha rarkenru saarri
amarar pathiyudaith thEvi arasaaNi yaivazhi paTTu
thumila mezhapparai koTTith thooraNam naaTTidung kolOo.
286.meaning: Having celebrated the maiden's marriage, adorning her and settling her in the house, proclaiming so that kinsmen and strangers alike may know that she is given to Damodara. Will they worship the goddess who rules the city of the immortals, the royal pillar-deity, and beat the drums so the festive tumult rises, and set up the festal arches?

287.translit:
orumakal thannai yudaiyEn ulakam niraintha pukazhaal
thirumakal poola vaLarththEn sengkaNmaal thaankoNDu poonaan
perumaka laaykkudi vaazhnthu perumpiLLai perra asoothai
marumaka laikkaNDu kanthu maNaaTTuppu ramseyyung kolOo.
287.meaning: I possessed one only daughter; with fame that filled the world I raised her like the goddess Lakshmi herself, and the red-eyed Lord took her and went away. Will Yashoda, who lived as the great lady of her household and bore a great son, look upon this daughter-in-law and, delighting in her, perform the wedding festivities?

288.translit:
thammaaman nanthakoo paalan thazheeikkoNDu enmakaL thannai
semmaan thirEyenru sollich sezhungkayar kaNNumsev vaayum
kommai mulaiyum idaiyum kozhumpaNaith thooLkaLum kaNDiTTu
immaka laipperra thaayar iniththari yaarennung kolOo.
288.meaning: His uncle Nandagopala, embracing my daughter, saying 'Be proud and rejoice,' will he behold her lovely carp-like eyes, her red mouth, her full breasts, her slender waist and her plump rounded shoulders? And will they then say, 'The mothers who bore this girl can no longer bear to be parted from her'?

289.translit:
vEdar marakkulam poolE vENDirruch seythuen makaLai
koodiya kooTTamE yaakak koNDu kudivaazhung kolOo
naadum nakarum ariya nallathOr kaNNaalam seythu saadirap
paayntha perumaan thakkavaa kaipparrung kolOo.
289.meaning: Like the warring tribe of hunters who do as they please, will he take my daughter as a mere casual union and live in household with her? Or, so that the whole land and town may know, performing a fine and proper wedding, will the Lord who kicked and felled the demon Sakata take her hand in the rightful, fitting way?

290.translit:
aNDat tamarar perumaan aazhiyaan inRu en magaLai
paNDap pazhippugaL sollip parisaRa aaNDiDum kolO
koNDu kudivaazhkkai vaazhndu kOvalap paTTam kavittu
paNDai maNaaTTimaar munnE paadukaa valvaikkum kolO.
290.meaning: Will the Lord of the celestials of the cosmos, He who holds the discus, today take my daughter—reciting old reproaches against her—and rule over her until she is utterly disgraced? Will He set up a household life with her, place upon her the crown of a cowherd-woman, and then make her stand guarding the sandals before His former consorts?

291.translit:
kudiyil piRandavar seyyum guNamonRum seydilan andO
nadaiyonRum seydilan nangaay nandagOpan magan kaNNan
idai iru paalum vaNanga iLaittiLaittu en magaL Engi
kadai kayiRE paRRi vaangik kai tazhumbu ERidum kolO.
291.meaning: Alas, Kannan, the son of Nandagopa, has not behaved with even one virtue befitting one born of good family; O lady, he has kept no proper conduct. Will my daughter, growing utterly weary as her waist bends to both sides, and pining away, grip the churning rope and pull it until calluses form upon her hands?

292.translit:
veNNiRat tOy tayir tannai veL varaip pin mun ezhundu
kaN uRangaadE irundu kadaiyavum taan vallaL kolO
oNNiRat taamaraic cengaN ulagaLandaan en magaLai
paNNaRai yaap paNi koNDu parisaRa aaNDidum kolO.
292.meaning: Rising before and after the white mountain of churning, staying without letting her eyes close in sleep, will my daughter be able to churn the white set curd? Will He of the bright lotus-red eyes, who measured the worlds, take my daughter and put her to relentless labor, ruling over her until she is wholly disgraced?

293.translit:
maayavan pin vazhi senRu vazhiyidai maaRRangaL kETTu
aayargaL sEriyilum pukku anguttai maaRRamum ellaam
taaya vaL solliya sollait taN puduvaip paTTan sonna
tooya tamizhp pattum vallaar too maNi vaNNaRkaaLarE.
293.meaning: Following the path behind the mysterious Lord, questioning passers-by along the way, entering even the cowherds' settlement and learning all that happened there—these are the words the mother spoke. Those who master these ten pure Tamil verses sung by the lord of cool Puduvai become servants of the Lord hued like a flawless gem.

294.translit:
en naadan dEvikku anRu inbap poo iyaadaaL
tan naadan kaaNavE taN poo marattinai
van naadap puLLaal valiyap paRittiTTa
en naadan vanmaiyaip paadip paRa
   empiraan vanmaiyaip paadip paRa.
294.meaning: When she would not give the blissful flower to the consort of my Lord, He, so that her own lord might see, by means of His mighty-voiced bird forcibly plucked away the cool-flowering tree. Sing of my Lord's might and fly; sing of my Master's might and fly.

295.translit:
en vil valikaNDu pOven enRu edirvandaan
tan villinODum tavattai edirvaangi
mun vil valittu mudu peN uyir uNDaan
tan villin vanmaiyaip paadip paRa
   daasarati tanmaiyaip paadip paRa.
295.meaning: He came forward saying, 'See the strength of my bow and then go,' but the Lord took away both that one's bow and his austerities; bending His own bow of old, He took the life of the aged demoness. Sing of the might of His bow and fly; sing of the nature of the son of Dasaratha and fly.

296.translit:
uruppiNi nangaiyait tEr ERRik koNDu
viruppuRRu ang Ega viraindu edir vandu
serukkuRRaan veeram sidaiya talaiyaic
ciraittiTTaan vanmaiyaip paadip paRa
   dEvaki singattaip paadip paRa.
296.meaning: When, having set the lady Rukmini upon His chariot, He went with delight, one who came rushing forward in arrogance had his pride destroyed and his head shaven. Sing of that Lord's might and fly; sing of the lion of Devaki and fly.

297.translit:
maaRRut taay senRu vanam pOgE enRiDa
eeRRut taay pin todarndu empiraan enRu azha
kooRRut taay sollak kodiya vanam pOna
seeRRam ilaadaanaip paadip paRa
   seetai maNaaLanaip paadip paRa.
297.meaning: When His step-mother went and said, 'Go to the forest,' and His birth-mother followed behind weeping, 'O my Lord,' He went to the cruel forest at the word of that death-like mother—without any anger at all. Sing of Him who bore no wrath and fly; sing of the bridegroom of Sita and fly.

298.translit:
panjavar tootanaayp paaratam kaiseydu
nanju umizh naagam kidanda nal poigai pukku
anjap paNattinmEl paayndiTTu aruLseyda
anjana vaNNanaip paadip paRa
   asOdai tan singattaip paadip paRa.
298.meaning: Becoming the messenger of the five Pandavas and waging the Bharata war, He entered the fair pond where the venom-spewing serpent lay, leapt fearsome upon its hoods and danced, and showed His grace. Sing of the one hued like collyrium and fly; sing of Yashoda's lion and fly.

299.translit:
mudi onRi moovula gangaLum aaNDu un
adiyERku aruLenRu avan pin todarnda
padiyil guNattup parata nambikku anRu
adi nilai eendaanaip paadip paRa
   ayOttiyar kOmaanaip paadip paRa.
299.meaning: To Bharata of matchless virtue, who followed behind Him saying, 'Reign with the crown over all three worlds and grant grace to me your servant,' He that day gave His sandals as His foot-token. Sing of Him who bestowed the sandals and fly; sing of the king of Ayodhya's people and fly.

300.translit:
kaaLiyan poigai kalangap paayndiTTu avan
neeL mudi aindilum ninRu nadamseydu
meeLa avanukku aruLseyda vittagan
tOL vali veeramE paadip paRa
   too maNi vaNNanaip paadip paRa.
300.meaning: Leaping so that the pond of Kaliya was churned into turmoil, standing upon his five tall hoods and dancing, the wondrous one then again showed him grace. Sing of the valor and might of His shoulders and fly; sing of the one hued like a flawless gem and fly.

301.translit:
taarkku iLam tambikku arasu eendu taNdagam
nooRRavaL solkoNDu pOgi nudangidaic
soorppaNakaavaic seviyodu mookku ava
aarkka arindaanaip paadip paRa
   ayOttik karasanaip paadip paRa.
301.meaning: Giving the kingdom to His younger brother adorned with the garland, and going to the Dandaka forest at the word of her of the hundred, He, when slender-waisted Surpanakha clamored, cut off her ears and nose. Sing of Him who cut them away and fly; sing of the king of Ayodhya and fly.

302.translit:
maayac cakaDam udaittu marudiRuttu
aayargaL ODupOy aanirai kaattu aNi
vEyin kuzhal oodi vittaganaay ninRa
aayargaL ERRinaip paadip paRa
   aanirai mEyttaanaip paadip paRa.
302.meaning: Kicking apart the deceitful cart, breaking the marudu trees, going forth with the cowherds and guarding the cattle herds, He stood as the wondrous one playing the lovely bamboo flute. Sing of the bull among the cowherds and fly; sing of Him who grazed the cattle herds and fly.

303.translit:
kaaraar kadalai adaittiTTu ilangai pukku
oraadaan pon mudi onbatODu onRaiyum
nEraa avan tambikkE neeL arasu eenda
aaraa amudanaip paadip paRa
   ayOttiyar vEndanaip paadip paRa.
303.meaning: Damming up the dark, vast sea and entering Lanka, He felled the nine and one golden crowns of the thoughtless one, and gave the great kingdom to that one's brother who had come to His side. Sing of the insatiable nectar and fly; sing of the sovereign of Ayodhya's people and fly.

304.translit:
nandan madalaiyaik kaakuttanai navinRu
undi paRanda oLi izhaiyaargaL sol
sen tamizht ten puduvai viTTucittan sol
aindinODu aindum vallaarkku allal illaiyE.
304.meaning: These are the words sung by the maidens of glowing ornaments who, praising the son of Nanda, the descendant of Kakutstha, hurled themselves up and flew—words of Vishnuchittan of southern Puduvai of pure Tamil. For those who master these ten verses there shall be no sorrow.

305.translit:
neRinda karung kuzhal madavaay ninnadiyEn viNNappam
seRinda maNi muDic canagan silaiyiRuttu ninaik koNarndadu
aRindu arasu kaLaikaTTa arundavattOn idaivilanga
seRinda silai kodu tavattaic cidaittadum Or adaiyaaLam.
305.meaning: O gentle one of dense dark tresses, this is the petition of me your servant: that He broke the bow of Janaka of the close-set jeweled crown and won you—knowing this. And when the one of rare austerity stood in the way as He went to root out the kings, that with His mighty bow He destroyed that one's austerity—this too is a sign of recognition.

306.translit:
alli amboo malark kOdaay adipaNindEn viNNappam
sollugEn kETTaruLaay tuNai malark kaN mada maanE
elli ambOdu iniduruttal irundadu Or idavagaiyil
mallikai maa maalai koNDu angu aarttadum Or adaiyaaLam.
306.meaning: O Kodai garlanded with the lily and the open bloom, I bow at your feet—this is my petition; I shall speak it, graciously hear it, O tender doe of paired flower-like eyes. That in a certain dwelling place we abode sweetly through the gentle night, and that there He bound a great garland of jasmine—this too is a sign of recognition.

307.translit:
kalakkiya maa manattanaLaayk kaikEsi varam vENDa
malakkiya maa manattananaay mannavanum maRaadozhiya
kulak kumaraa kaaDuRaiyap pO enRu vidaikoduppa
ilakkumaNan tannodum angu Egiyadu Or adaiyaaLam.
307.meaning: When Kaikeyi, her great mind agitated, demanded her boon, the king too, his great mind sorely troubled, could not refuse; and when she said, 'O prince of the noble line, go to dwell in the forest,' giving leave, that He departed there together with Lakshmana—this too is a sign of recognition.

308.translit:
vaaraNinda mulai madavaay vaidEvee viNNappam
tEraNinda ayOttiyar kOn perun dEvee kETTaruLaay
kooraNinda vEl valavan kuganODum gangai tannil
seeraNinda tOzhamaiyaik koNDadum Or adaiyaaLam.
308.meaning: O gentle Vaidehi of bosom girded with the breast-band, this is my petition; O great queen of the king of Ayodhya's people who is adorned with the chariot, graciously hear it. That upon the Ganga He took into glorious friendship Guha, the wielder of the keen-tipped spear—this too is a sign of recognition.

309.translit:
maan amaru mel nOkki vaidEvee viNNappam
kaan amarum kal adar pOyk kaaDuRainda kaalattu
tEn amarum pozhiR saaral sittirakooDattu iruppa
paal mozhiyaay parata nambi paNindadum Or adaiyaaLam.
309.meaning: O Vaidehi of the soft doe-like gaze, this is my petition: in the time when, having gone over the stony forest paths, He dwelt in the wilderness, while He abode at Chitrakuta on the slope of honey-laden groves—O you of milk-sweet speech—that Bharata came and bowed to Him there—this too is a sign of recognition.

310.translit:
chittirakoo dattu-iruppa-ch chiRukaakkai mulai-theeNDa
aththirame koNDeRiya anaiththulagum thirinthoodi
viththaganee iraamaavoo ninn-abayam enRu-azhaippa
aththirame adhan-kaNNai aRuththadhum-oo radaiyaaLam.
310.meaning: While he dwelt at Chitrakuta, a little crow pecked at her breast, and he hurled a blade of grass as a weapon; the crow fled circling all the worlds, and crying 'O wise one, O Rama, I take refuge in you!' it returned. That he then cut out only that eye of the crow with the very blade of grass is one token (of recognition).

311.translit:
minn-oththa nuNN-iDaiyaay meyy-adiyeen viNNappam
ponn-oththa maan-onRu pugundhu-inidhu viLaiyaaDa
ninn-anbin vazhi-ninRu silai-piDiththu-em piraan-eega
pinnee-angu ilakkumaNan pirindhadhum-oo radaiyaaLam.
311.meaning: O you of waist slender as lightning, this is the petition of your true servant. When a golden deer came near and played sweetly, and our Lord, following your love's wish, took up his bow and went after it, that Lakshmana then parted from you and followed him there is one token.

312.translit:
maiththagu-maa malar-kuzhalaay vaidheevee viNNappam
oththa-pugazh vaanara-koon udan-irundhu ninaiththeeDa
aththagu-see rayoththiyar-koon adaiyaaLam ivai-mozhindhaan
iththagaiyaal adaiyaaLam eedhu-avan-kai moodhirame.
312.meaning: O Vaidehi with dark, lovely flower-decked hair, this is the petition. While the renowned king of the monkeys sat with me and we recalled and searched, the glorious king of Ayodhya spoke these tokens; by this manner, here is the token his very own ring from his hand.

313.translit:
thikku-niRai pugazhaaLan thee-veeLvi-ch chenRa-naaL
mikka-perun chabai-naDuvee vill-iRuththaan moodhiram-kaNDu
okkumaal adaiyaaLam anumaan-enRu uchchi-meel
vaiththuk-koNDu ugandhanaLaal malar-kuzhalaaL seedhaiyume.
313.meaning: On the day he went to the sacred fire-sacrifice, he who broke the bow amid the great vast assembly his ring, when Sita of flower-decked hair beheld it, she said, 'O Hanuman, the token matches indeed!' and placing it upon her head, she rejoiced.

314.translit:
vaaraarum mulai-maDavaaL vaidheevi-thanai-kaNDu
seeraarum thiRal-anuman therindhuraiththa adaiyaaLam
paaraarum pugazh-puduvai-ch paTTar-piraan paaDal-vallaar
eeraarum vaigundhaththu imaiyavar-oodu iruppaare.
314.meaning: These tokens which the valiant, glorious Hanuman, having beheld the gentle Vaidehi of full breasts, discerned and recited those who can master the song of Pattarpiran of Puduvai, famed throughout the earth, shall abide in beautiful Vaikuntha together with the deathless celestials.

315.translit:
kadhir-aayiram-iravi kalandheri-ththaal-oththa neeL-muDiyan
edhiril perumai iraamanai irukkumidam naaDudhireel
adhirum kazhaR-porudhoaL iraNiyan-aagam piLandhu-ariyaay
udhira maLaindha-kaiyoa Dirundhaanai uLLavaa kaNDaaruLar.
315.meaning: He of the tall crown that shines as if a thousand suns blazed together, Rama of peerless greatness if you seek the place where he abides, there are those who have truly seen him as he is the one who, with resounding warrior-anklets on his fighting shoulders, became the Man-Lion and split open the chest of Hiranya, sitting with his hands smeared in blood.

316.translit:
naandhagam sangu-thaNDu naaN-oli-ch chaarngam thiru-chakkaram
eendhu-perumai iraamanai irukkumidam naaDudhireel
kaandhaL-mugizh-viral seedhai-kkaagi-k kaDun-silai senRiRukka
veendhar-thalaiva-ch chanaka-raasan-than veeLviyil kaNDaaruLar.
316.meaning: Rama of great glory who bears the Nandaka sword, the conch, the mace, the twanging Sharnga bow, and the sacred discus if you seek the place where he abides, there are those who saw him at the sacrifice of King Janaka, lord of kings, when, for the sake of Sita whose fingers were like budding glory-lily flowers, he went and broke the mighty bow.

317.translit:
kolai-yaanai-k kombu-paRiththu-k kooDalar-seenai porudh-azhiya
silaiyaal maraamara-meydha deevanai-ch chikkena-naaDudhireel
thalaiyaal kurakkinam-thaangi-ch chenRu thaDavarai koNDaDaippa
alaiyaar kaDaR-karai veeRRirundhaanai angu-ththai-k kaNDaaruLar.
317.meaning: The Lord who tore out the tusk of the killer elephant and destroyed the army of his foes in battle, who pierced the marvel trees with his bow if you seek him firmly, there are those who saw him there, seated on the shore of the wave-tossed sea, while the host of monkeys bore great mountains on their heads, came and dammed (the ocean).

318.translit:
thoayam parandha naDuvu-soozhalil thollai vaDivu-koNDa
maaya-k kuzhavi-adhanai naaDuRil vammin suvaD-uraikkeen
aayar maDamagaL pinnai-kkaagi aDal-viDai-yeezhinaiyum
veeya-p porudhu viyarththu-ninRaanai meymmaiyee kaNDaaruLar.
318.meaning: Amid the spreading flood in the encircling waters, the wondrous child who took on his ancient form if you seek him, come, and I will tell you the traces: there are those who truly saw him, who for the sake of Nappinnai the gentle daughter of the cowherds, fought and slew the seven fierce bulls and stood there sweating.

319.translit:
neeR-eeRu sen-chaDai neela-kaNDanum naan-muganum muRaiyaal
seer-eeRu vaasagan-cheyya-ninRa thirumaalai naaDudhireel
vaar-eeRu kongai uruppiNiyai valiya-p piDiththu-k koNDu
theer-eeRRi seenai naDuvu-poar seyya-ch chikkena-k kaNDaaruLar.
319.meaning: The blue-throated one (Shiva) with red matted locks bearing the Ganga, and the four-faced Brahma in due order offered him glorious praise if you seek that Tirumal, there are those who saw him firmly, who forcibly seized Rukmini of full, banded breasts, set her on his chariot, and waged war amid the army.

320.translit:
pollaa vaDiv-uDai-p peychchi-thunja-p puNar-mulai vaay-maDukka
vallaanai maa-maNi vaNNanai maruvum-idam naaDudhireel
pal-aayiram perun-deevi-maar-oDu peLvam-eeRi thuvarai
ellaarum-soozha-ch chinghaasanaththee irundhaanai-k kaNDaaruLar.
320.meaning: He who was able to make the foul-formed demoness die when she pressed her poisoned breast into his mouth, the Lord of precious-gem hue if you seek the place where he dwells, there are those who saw him, crossing the sea to Dvaraka with his many thousand great queens, seated upon the throne while all surrounded him.

321.translit:
veLLai viLi-sangu venchuDar-th thiru-chakkaram eendhu-kaiyan
uLLa-vidam-vinavil umakku-iRai vammin suvaD-uraikkeen
veLLai-p puravi-kkurakku vel-koDi-th theer-misai munbu-ninRu
kaLLa-p paDai-thuNai-yaagi-p paaradham kai-seyya-k kaNDaaruLar.
321.meaning: He who bears in his hands the white sounding conch and the fierce-rayed sacred discus if you ask where he is present, O Lord to you, come, and I will tell the traces: there are those who saw him stand in front upon the chariot of white horses with the monkey-victory banner, becoming the stealthy battle-helper, while the Mahabharata war was waged.

322.translit:
naazhigai kooRiTTu-k kaaththu-ninRa arasar-kaL-tham-mugappee
naazhigai poaga-p paDai-porudhavan deevaki-than-siRuvan
aazhi-koNDu anRu-iravi maRaippa-ch chayaththira-than-thalaiyai
paazhi-luruLa-p paDai-porudhavan pakkamee kaNDaaruLar.
322.meaning: Counting out the appointed hours and standing on guard, before the very faces of the kings (who protected Jayadratha), when the hour was about to pass, he waged war the little son of Devaki; with his discus that day he covered the sun, and waged war so that Jayadratha's head rolled into the void. There are those who saw him at his side.

323.translit:
maNNum malaiyum maRi-kaDal-gaLum maRRum yaavum-ellaam
thiNNam vizhungi-umizhndha deevanai-ch chikkena naaDudhireel
eNNaR kariyadhoa reenamaagi iru-nilam pukkiDandhu
vaNNa-k karun-kuzhal maadharoaDu maNandhaanai-k kaNDaaruLar.
323.meaning: The Lord who surely swallowed and spat out the earth, the mountains, the surging seas, and all else besides if you seek him firmly, there are those who saw him, who became the incomparable boar, entered the vast earth and lay within it, and wedded the lady (Bhumi) of beautiful dark tresses.

324.translit:
kariya mugil-purai meeni-maayanai-k kaNDa suvaD-uraiththu
puravi mugam-seydhu sennel-oonghi viLai-kazha nip-puduvai
thiruviR-poli maRai-vaaNan paTTar-piraan sonna maalai-paththum
paravu manam-uDai-p paththar-uLLaar paraman-aDi seervar-gaLee.
324.meaning: Reciting the traces of having seen the wondrous Lord whose form resembles a dark cloud the ten verses of this garland spoken by Pattarpiran, the Vedic master radiant with Lakshmi's grace, of Puduvai where red paddy grows tall in fertile fields the devotees whose minds praise them shall reach the feet of the Supreme One.

325.translit:
alambaa veruTTaa-k konRu thiriyum arakkarai
kulam-paazh paDuththu-k kula-viLak-kaay-ninRa koan-malai
silamb-aarkka vandhu deyva magaLir-kaL aaDum-seer
silamb-aaRu paayum then-thiru maaliruny soalaiye.
325.meaning: The mountain of the Lord who, becoming the lamp of his lineage, ruined the clan of the demons who roamed about troubling and terrifying and killing this is southern Tirumalirunjolai of glory, where the divine maidens come and dance so that their anklets ring, and the Silambaru river flows.

326.translit:
