vall-aaLan thoaLum vaaL-arakkan muDiyum thangai
pollaadha mookkum poakkuviththaan porun thum-malai
ellaa vidaththilum engum parandhu-pal laaNDoli
sellaa niRkum seerth-then thirumaaliruny soalaiye.
326.meaning: The mountain where he abides who struck off the shoulders of the mighty warrior (Vali / Kumbhakarna), the crowned head of the sword-bearing demon (Ravana), and the foul nose of his sister this is the glorious southern Tirumalirunjolai, where in every place and everywhere the sound of 'pallandu' (many long years!) spreads abroad and never ceases.

327.translit:
thakkaar-mik kaar-gaLai-ch chanjalam seyyum salavarai
thekkaa neRiyee poakkuvikkum selvan pon-malai
ek-kaala mum-senRu seeviththu-irukkum aDiyarai
akkaa neRiyai maaRRum thaN-maaliruny soalaiye.
327.meaning: The golden mountain of the Lord who drives down the southward path (of Yama) the wicked who torment the worthy and the eminent this is cool Tirumalirunjolai, which removes that (southward) path for the servants who at all times go and worship and abide there.

328.translit:
aanaayar kooDi amaiththa vizhavai amarar-tham
koanaar-kozhiya-k koavarth thanaththu-ch chey thaan-malai
vaanaaTTil ninRu maa-malar-kaR-pagath thoththizhi
theenaaRu paayum then-thiru maaliruny soalaiye.
328.meaning: The mountain of him who, leaving aside the lord of the celestials (Indra), turned the festival that the cowherds had together arranged into worship at Govardhana this is southern Tirumalirunjolai, where, descending from the heavenly realm, a river of honey flows pouring from the clustered blossoms of the great Kalpaka tree.

329.translit:
oru-vaaraNam paNi-koNDavan poygaiyil kanjan-than
oru-vaaraNam uyir-uNDavan senRuRai yum-malai
karu-vaaraNam than-piDi-thuRan-doaDa kaDal-vaNNan
thiru-vaaNai-kooRa-ch thiriyum thaN-maaliruny soalaiye.
329.meaning: The mountain where he dwells who accepted the worship of the one elephant (Gajendra) at the pond, and who took the life of the one elephant (Kuvalayapida) belonging to Kamsa this is cool Tirumalirunjolai, where a dark wild elephant, deserting his mate so that she flees, roams about while invoking the sacred command of the ocean-hued Lord.

330.translit:
eeviRRuc cheyvaan eenRedirndhu vandha mallarai
saavath thagarttha saandhaNi thoaL sadhuran malai
aavath thanam enRu amararkaLum nan munivarum
seaviththirukkum then thirumaalirunj choalaiyea.
330.meaning: The hill of the skilled Lord, whose fragrant shoulders shattered to death the wrestlers who came forth, sent to do their master's bidding. The celestial gods and good sages worship here, saying it is their refuge and protection — that is southern Tirumalirunjolai.

331.translit:
mannar maruga maiththunan maarkku oru thearin meal
munnangu ninRu moazhai ezhuviththa van malai
konnavil koor veal koan nedu maaRan then koodaR koan
thennan koNdaadum then thirumaalirunj choalaiyea.
331.meaning: The mighty hill of the One who stood upon a single chariot before the eyes of His brothers-in-law (the Pandavas), making the enemy kings tremble and gush forth in terror. It is the hill praised by the Pandiyan king Nedumaaran, lord of the cruel sharp spear, ruler of southern Koodal (Madurai) — that is southern Tirumalirunjolai.

332.translit:
kuRugaadha mannaraik kooduginRu kalakki veng kaanidaic
chiRu kaal neRiyea poakkuvikkum chelvan pon malai
aRu kaal vari vaNdukaL aayira naamam cholli
chiRu kaalaip paadum then thirumaalirunj choalaiyea.
332.meaning: The golden hill of the wealthy Lord, who routed and confounded the hostile kings who would not draw near, and drove them along narrow paths into the fierce forest. There the six-legged striped bees recite His thousand names and sing them in the early dawn — that is southern Tirumalirunjolai.

333.translit:
chindhap pudaiththuc cheng kurudhi koNdu boodhangaL
andhip palikoduththu aavath thanam chey appan malai
indhira koapangaL emperumaan kani vaay oppaan
chindhum puRavil then thirumaalirunj choalaiyea.
333.meaning: The hill of the Father, where the spirits (boothas) struck and made red blood scatter, offered the evening sacrifice, and performed the rite of protection. In its groves the scarlet velvet-mites (indhiragoapam) are scattered, resembling the fruit-red lips of our Lord — that is southern Tirumalirunjolai.

334.translit:
ettuth thisaiyum eNNiRandha perun theavimaar
vittu viLanga veeRRirundha vimalan malai
patti pidigaL pagaduRinjic chenRu maalai vaayth
thettith thiLaikkum then thirumaalirunj choalaiyea.
334.meaning: The hill of the spotless Lord, who sits in majesty radiant in all eight directions surrounded by countless great consorts. There the wandering she-elephants rub against the bull-elephants, and at evening they sport and revel together — that is southern Tirumalirunjolai.

335.translit:
marudhap pozhil aNi maalirunj choalai malai thannai
karudhi uRaiginRa kaark kadal vaNNan ammaan thannai
viradham koNdu eaththum villiputthoor vittuchiththan chol
karudhi uraippavar kaNNan kazhal iNai kaaNbaarkaLea.
335.meaning: Tirumalirunjolai hill, adorned with marudham groves, is the chosen abode where our Lord of the dark-ocean hue dwells. Those who, with devout vow, recite and ponder these words of Vishnuchittan (Periyalvar) of Villiputtur shall behold the twin ankleted feet of Kannan.

336.translit:
uruppiNi nangai thannai meetpaan thodarndhu oadic chenRa
uruppanai oattik koNdittu uRaiththitta uRaippan malai
poruppidaik konRai ninRu muRi aazhiyum kaasum koNdu
viruppodu pon vazhangum viyan maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
336.meaning: The hill of the powerful Lord who, to rescue the lady Rukmini, pursued and drove off Rukma (her brother) who had chased after, and pressed hard upon him. On its slopes the konrai trees stand and shed their splits, while the streams bring discs and coins, and lovingly bestow gold — that wondrous Tirumalirunjolai.

337.translit:
kanjanum kaaLiyanum kaLiRum marudhum erudhum
vanjanai il madiya vaLarndha maNi vaNNan malai
nanju umizh naagam ezhundhu aNavi naLir maamadhiyai
chenj chudar naa vaLaikkum thirumaalirunj choalaiyadhea.
337.meaning: The hill of the sapphire-hued Lord, who grew up and slew without deceit Kamsa, Kaliya, the elephant, the marudham trees, and the bulls. There a venom-spewing serpent rises up, and reaching toward the cool great moon, curls its red-glowing tongue — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

338.translit:
mannu naragan thannaic choazh poagi vaLaiththu eRindhu
kanni magaLir thammaik kavarndha kadal vaNNan malai
punnai cherundhiyodu puna veangaiyum koangum ninRu
ponnari maalaigaL choozh pozhil maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
338.meaning: The hill of the ocean-hued Lord, who went and encircled, struck down the entrenched Naraka, and carried off the young maidens (he had imprisoned). There the punnai, cherundhi, the wild veangai and koangu trees stand wreathed in golden garlands of blossom in the encircling groves — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

339.translit:
maavali thannudaiya magan vaaNan magaL irundha
kaavalaik kattazhiththa thanik kaaLai karudhum malai
koavalar koavindhanaik kuRamaadhargaL paN kuRinjip
paa oli paadi nadam payil maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
339.meaning: The hill cherished by the peerless Bull of a hero, who broke open the guarded fortress where the daughter of Banasura — son of Mahabali — was kept. There the hill-maidens (Kurava women) sing songs of melodious verse in the kurinji mode and dance, praising Govinda the cowherd — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

340.translit:
pala pala naazham chollip pazhiththa sisupaalan thannai
alavalai mai thavirththa azhagan alangaaran malai
kula malai koala malai kuLir maa malai koRRa malai
nila malai neeNda malai thirumaalirunj choalaiyadhea.
340.meaning: The hill of the handsome, splendidly adorned Lord, who put an end to the meddlesome insolence of Shishupala, who reviled Him uttering many many slanders. It is the noble hill, the beautiful hill, the cool great hill, the victorious hill, the hill of the earth, the lofty hill — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

341.translit:
paaNdavar thammudaiya paanjaali maRukkam ellaam
aaNdu angu nooRRuvar tham peNdir meal vaiththa appan malai
paaN dagu vaNdinangaL paNgaL paadi madhup parugath
thoaNdal udaiya malai thollai maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
341.meaning: The hill of the Father who, then and there, turned all the anguish of the Pandavas' Draupadi back upon the wives of the hundred (Kauravas). It is the hill upon which the swarms of bees, fit as minstrels, sing tunes and drink honey, the deep-set ancient hill — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

342.translit:
kanang kuzhaiyaaL poruttaak kaNai paariththu arakkar thangaL
inam kazhu veaRRuviththa ezhil thoaL em piraaman malai
kanam kozhi theLLaruvi vandhu choozhndhu agal njaalam ellaam
inam kuzhu vaadum malai ezhil maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
342.meaning: The hill of our handsome-shouldered Lord, who for the sake of the lady of weighty earrings (Sita) loosed His arrows and impaled the whole clan of the demons upon stakes. It is the hill where clear cascades flow down densely and encircle it, and where, across the wide world, throngs gather and revel — that is beautiful Tirumalirunjolai.

343.translit:
eri chidaRum charaththaal ilangaiyinai thannudaiya
vari silai vaayil peydhu vaaykkoattam thavirththu ugandha
araiyan amarum malai amararodu koanum chenRu
thiri chudar choozhum malai thirumaalirunj choalaiyadhea.
343.meaning: The hill where dwells the King who, with arrows scattering fire from His striped bow, set Lanka aflame and, rejoicing, put an end to its defiant resistance. It is the hill to which the gods go with their lord (Indra), the hill encircled by the three wandering lights (sun, moon and fire) — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

344.translit:
koattu maN koNdu idandhu kudangaiyil maN koNdu aLandhu
meettum adhu uNdu umizhndhu viLaiyaadu vimalan malai
eettiya pal poruLgaL em piraanukku adi uRai enRu
oattarum thaN chilambaaRu udai maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
344.meaning: The hill of the spotless Lord who lifted up the earth on His tusk (as the Boar), measured the earth in His palm (as Trivikrama), and again swallowed and disgorged it in play. It is the hill served by the cool Silambaru river, which comes rushing bearing its many gathered treasures as a tribute laid at our Lord's feet — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

345.translit:
aayiram thoaL parappi mudi aayiram min ilaga
aayiram pain dhalaiya anandha sayanan aaLum malai
aayira maaRugaLum chunaigaL pala vaayiramum
aayiram poom pozhilum udai maalirunj choalaiyadhea.
345.meaning: The hill ruled by the Lord who reclines on Ananta (the serpent), spreading forth a thousand shoulders, a thousand crowns gleaming like lightning, upon a thousand fresh-green hoods. It is the hill possessed of a thousand rivers, many thousand springs, and a thousand flowering groves — that is Tirumalirunjolai.

346.translit:
maalirunj choalai ennum malaiyai udai amalaiyai
naal iru moorththi thannai naal veadhak kadal amudhai
meal irung kaRpagaththai veadhaandha vizhup poruLil
meal irundha viLakkai vittuchiththan viriththanavea.
346.meaning: The spotless Lord who possesses the hill called Tirumalirunjolai, the One of the eightfold form, the nectar of the ocean of the four Vedas, the supreme heavenly Karpaka tree, the lamp that stands highest among the precious truths of the Vedanta — these are the verses that Vishnuchittan (Periyalvar) has unfolded.

347.translit:
naava kaariyam cholli laadhavar naaL thoRum virundhu oambuvaar
theava kaariyam cheydhu veadham payinRu vaazh thiruk koattiyoor
moovar kaariyamum thiruththum mudhalvanaic chindhiyaadha ap
paava kaarigaLaip padaiththavan engnganam padaiththaan koloa.
347.meaning: At Tirukkottiyur dwell those who utter no idle word with their tongues, who daily tend to guests, who perform the rites for the gods, who study the Vedas and flourish. As for those sinful folk who do not meditate upon the Primal One who orders the affairs of the three (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva) — how indeed did the Creator come to create them?

348.translit:
kuRRam inRik kuNam perukkik kurukkaLukku anukoolaraay
cheRRam onRum ilaadha vaN kaiyinaarkaL vaazh thiruk koattiyoor
thuRRi eazh ulagu uNda thoo maNi vaNNan thannaith thozhaadhavar
peRRa thaayar vayiRRinaip peru noay cheyvaan piRandhaarkaLea.
348.meaning: At Tirukkottiyur dwell the generous-handed ones who, free of fault, increase their virtues, who are favorable toward their teachers, and harbor no malice whatsoever. Those who do not worship the pure sapphire-hued Lord who once gathered up and swallowed the seven worlds — they were born only to bring grievous grief to the wombs of the mothers who bore them.

349.translit:
vaNNa nal maNiyum maragadhamum azhuththi nizhal ezhum
thiNNai choozh thiruk koattiyoorth thirumaal avan thiru naamangaL
eNNak kaNda viralgaLaal iRaip pozhudhum eNNagilaadhu poay
uNNak kaNda tham ooththai vaaykkuk kavaLam undhuginRaarkaLea.
349.meaning: At Tirukkottiyur, ringed with raised platforms set with fine colored gems and emeralds that cast forth radiance, dwells Tirumal. Yet those who, with the very fingers fit for counting His holy names, will not count them even for a moment, go and shove morsels of food into their foul mouths only to eat — such are they.

350.translit:
uragam-eL-aNaiyaan kaiyil uRai sangam-pol mada-annangaL
nirai-kaNam parandhu-eRum sengamala vayal Tirukkottiyoor
naraga-naasanai naavil koNd-azhaiyaadha maanida saadhiyar
parugu-neerum udukkum-koorraiyum paavam seydhana-thaangolo.
350.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur, with paddy fields of red lotuses, gentle white swans crowd and spread in flocks like the conch that rests in the hand of Him who reclines on the serpent-couch. As for those of the human race who do not take upon their tongues and call upon the Destroyer of hell (Narayana) -- the very water they drink and the clothes they wear, have these too committed some sin?

351.translit:
aamaiyin mudhugaththidaik kudhi-koNdu thoo-malar saadi-poy
theemai-seydhu iLa-vaaLaigaL viLaiyaadu-neer Tirukkottiyoor
nemi-ser thadang-kaiyinaanai ninaippil-aa-vali nenju-udai
boomi-paarangaL uNNum-sorrinai vaangi-pullai thiNimine.
351.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur of waters where young vaalai fish leap upon the backs of tortoises, strike the pure flowers and frolic mischievously -- O you with strong, hard hearts that have no thought of Him who bears the discus in His broad hand, you burdens of the earth: snatch away the rice you eat and stuff your bellies with grass instead!

352.translit:
bhootham-aindhodu veLvi-aindhu pulangaL-aindhu poRigaLaal
edham-onRum-ilaadha-vaN-kaiyinaargaL vaazh Tirukkottiyoor
naadhanai narasinganai navinR-eththuvaargaL uzhakkiya
paadha-thooLi padudhalaal iv-vulagam paakkiyam-seydhadhe.
352.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur dwell generous-handed men, faultless through the five elements, the five sacrifices, the five senses and their organs. Because the dust of the feet of those who recite and praise the Lord, the Man-Lion (Narasimha), falls upon it, this world has indeed earned great merit.

353.translit:
kurundham-onR-osiththaanodum senRu koodi-aadi vizhaa-seydhu
thirundhu-naan-maRaiyor iraa-pagal eththi-vaazh Tirukkottiyoor
karum-thada-mugil vaNNanaik kadaik-koNdu kai-thozhum paththargaL
irundha-ooril irukkum-maanidar eth-thavangaL-sey thaargolo.
353.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur, where the perfected knowers of the four Vedas go and join, dance, hold festivals and praise Him night and day, dwell the devotees who, taking refuge in Him who is dark like a great rain-cloud (who once broke the kurunda tree), worship with folded hands. What austerities must the people who live in the very town where such devotees abide have performed?

354.translit:
naLirndha-seelan nayaasalan-abhimaana-thunganai naadoRum
theLindha-selvanai sevagam-koNda sengaN-maal Tirukkottiyoor
kuLirndhu-uRaiginRa govindhan guNam paaduvaar-uLLa naattinuL
viLaindha-thaaniyamum iraakkar meedhu-koLLakilaargaLe.
354.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur of the red-eyed Tirumal, who accepted the service of the cool-hearted one of steadfast virtue, the great-souled patron -- the clear and prosperous lord -- in the land where dwell those who sing the qualities of Govinda who abides there cool and serene, even the demons cannot lay their hands upon the grain that ripens there.

355.translit:
kombinaar-pozhil-vaay kuyilinam govindhan-guNam paadu-seer
sem-pon-aar-madhiL soozh sezhum-kazhani-udai Tirukkottiyoor
nambanai narasinganai navinR-eththuvaargaLai kaNdakkaal
em-piraan-thana sinnangaL ivar-ivar-enRu aasaigaL theervane.
355.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur, girt with walls of pure red gold and rich fields, where flocks of cuckoos on the branches in the groves sing the glorious qualities of Govinda -- when I behold those who recite and praise the trustworthy Lord, the Man-Lion (Narasimha), saying 'These, these are the marks of my Lord!', my longings shall be fulfilled.

356.translit:
kaasin-vaay-karam viRkilum karavaadhu maaRRili-soRu-ittu
desa-vaarththai-padaikkum vaN-kaiyinaargaL vaazh Tirukkottiyoor
kesavaa purudoththamaa kiLar-sodhiyaay kuRaLaa-enRu pesuvaar adiyaargaL
endhammai viRkavum peRuvaargaLe.
356.meaning: In Tirukkottiyur dwell generous-handed men who, even if they must sell their hand's labour for a coin, give without stinting food beyond compare and earn praise throughout the land. The devotees who speak saying 'Kesava! Purushottama (Best of Men)! Radiant Light! Vamana (the Dwarf)!' -- such ones may even have the right to sell us (their servants).

357.translit:
seedha-neer-pudai-soozh sezhum-kazhani-udai Tirukkottiyoor
aadhiyaan-adiyaaraiyum adimai-inRi-thirivaaraiyum
kodhil-pattar-piraan kuLir-puduvai-man Vittuchiththan-sol
edham-inRi uraippavar irudeekesanukk-aalare.
357.meaning: These flawless words of Vittuchittan (Periyalvar), the faultless lord of the Bhattas, king of cool Puduvai -- about both the devotees of the Primordial Lord and those who roam without serving Him -- in Tirukkottiyur surrounded by cool waters and rich fields: those who recite them without fault become the servants of Hrishikesa.

358.translit:
aasai-vaay-senRa sindhaiyar-aagi annai aththan-en puththirar boomi
vaasa-vaar-kuzhalaaL-enRu mayangi maaLum-ellaikkaN vaay-thiRavaadhe
kesavaa purudoththamaa enRum kezhal-aagiya kedili-enRum
pesuvaar-avar eydhum perumai pesuvaan-pugil nam param-anRe.
358.meaning: Becoming people whose minds run along the path of desire -- 'mother, father, my sons, lands, my fragrant long-tressed wife' -- bewildered, at the boundary moment of dying without opening the mouth to call on Him: but those who say always 'Kesava! Purushottama! O Faultless One who became the Boar!' -- the greatness they attain, were one to begin to tell it, is beyond our power to describe.

359.translit:
seeyinaal seRindh-eRiya puN-mel seRRal-eRi kuzhamb-irundhu engum
eeyinaal-arippu-uNdu mayangi ellai-vaay-senRu servadhan munnam
vaayinaal namo naaraNaa-venRu maththagaththidaik kaigaLai kooppi
poyinaal pinnai ith-thisaikku-enRum piNai-kodukkilum poga-vottaare.
359.meaning: Before you reach the boundary -- swooning, bitten by flies, with pus festering and turned to clotted mire everywhere upon the wound that has gathered and overflowed -- if you go having said with your mouth 'Namo Narayana!' and folded your hands upon your head, then afterwards, even if a ransom be offered, they (the messengers of death) will never let you be brought back to this side again.

360.translit:
sorvinaal poruL-vaiththadhu-uNdaagil sollu-soll-enRu suRRum-irundhu
aar-vinaavilum vaay-thiRavaadhe andhak-kaalam-adaivadhan munnam
maarvam-enbadhor koyil-amaiththu maadhavan-ennum theyvaththai naatti
aarvam-enbadhor poo-vida vallaarkku arava-thaNdaththil uyyalum-aame.
360.meaning: They sit around saying 'Tell, tell! Is there treasure you hid away in your weakness?' -- but before that hour comes when, however they question you, your mouth opens no more: for those who are able to build a temple called the breast, install therein the deity called Madhava, and offer there a flower called devotion -- it is possible to be saved even from the rod of Yama.

361.translit:
mel-ezhundhadhor vaayuk-kiLarndhu mel-midaRRinai-uL-ezha-vaangi
kaalum-kaiyum vidhir-vidhirth-eRi kaN-uRakkam-adhaavadhan munnam
moolam-aagiya oRRai-ezhuththai moonRu-maaththirai uLL-ezha-vaangi
velai-vaNNanai mevudhir-aagil viNNagaththinil mevalum-aame.
361.meaning: Before the air rises swelling upward and is drawn into the upper throat, before legs and hands shudder and tremble and the eyes sink into death's sleep -- if you draw forth from within the single syllable that is the root (Om) in its three measures, and cling to Him who is hued like the ocean, then you may come to dwell in the realm of heaven.

362.translit:
madi-vazhi-vandhu neer-pulan-sora vaayil-attiya kanjiyum meeNde
kadai-vazhi-vaarak kaNdam-adaippa kaN-uRakkam-adhaavadhan munnam
thodai-vazhi-ummai naaygaL-kavaraa soolaththaal-ummai paayvadhum-seyyaar
idai-vazhiyil-neer kooraiyum izhaveer irudeekesan-enR-eththa-valleere.
362.meaning: Before the senses fail and water flows from the openings of the body, before even the gruel poured into your mouth comes back streaming from the corners and the throat is choked, before the eyes sink into death's sleep -- O you who would otherwise lose even your garment on the road midway, with dogs tearing at your thighs and (Yama's men) piercing you with the trident: be ones able to praise Him, calling 'Hrishikesa!'

363.translit:
angam-vittavai-aindhum-agaRRi aavi mookkinil sodhiththa pinnai
sangam-vittavar-kaiyai maRiththu paiya-ve-thalai saaypadhan munnam
vangam-vittu-ulavum-kadal-paLLi maayanai madhusoodhanai maarbil
thanga-vittu-vaiththu aavadhor karumam saadhippaarkku-enRum saadhikkalaame.
363.meaning: Before the five (senses) have left the limbs and gone, before -- having tested whether the breath is in the nostrils -- the relatives release the hand they held and the head gently droops: those who make Mayan, Madhusudana, who reclines on the sea where ships sail to and fro, abide within their breast, and accomplish that one true work -- for them every good thing may always be accomplished.

364.translit:
thennavan-thamar seppam-ilaadhaar seva-thakkuvaar-pola pugundhu
pinnum-van-kayiRRaal-piNiththu-eRRi pin-mun-naaga izhuppadhan munnam
innavan-inaiyaan-enRu solli eNNi uLLaththu-iruL-aRa nokki
manna-van-madhusoodhanan-enbaar vaanagaththu-manRaadigaL thaame.
364.meaning: Before the messengers of the southern king (Yama), without any mercy, enter like those come to torment, bind you with cruel ropes, fling you down and drag you back and forth -- those who, reciting 'He is such, He is thus,' contemplating and gazing with the darkness gone from their hearts, declare 'Madhusudana is the eternal king!' -- they themselves become the dancers in the courtyard of heaven.

365.translit:
koodi-koodi-uRRaargaL irundhu kuRRam-niRka-naRRangaL paRaindhu
paadi-paadi-or paadaiyil-ittu nari-padaikku-oru paagudam-pole
kodi-moodi-eduppadhan munnam kaiththuvam-udai-govindhanodu
koodi-aadiya-uLLaththar-aanaal kuRippidam-kadandhu-uyyalum-aame.
365.meaning: Before the kinsmen gather and gather, sit, leave the faults aside and recount the virtues, sing and sing, lay you on a bier and lift you covered with cloth like an offering set out for the pack of jackals -- if you become ones whose hearts have joined and danced with Govinda who bears the Kaustubha gem, you may cross beyond that destined place (of death) and be saved.

366.translit:
vaay-oru-pakkam vaangi valippa vaarndha-neer-kuzhi-kaNgaL mizhaRRa
thaay-oru-pakkam thandhai-oru-pakkam thaaramum-oru-pakkam alaRRa
thee-oru-pakkam servadhan munnam sengaN-maalodum sikkena-suRRa
maay-oru-pakkam niRka-vallaarkku arava-thaNdaththil uyyalum-aame.
366.meaning: Before the mouth is pulled to one side and twisted in convulsion, before the sunken eyes brimming with water grow dim, before mother on one side, father on one side, and wife on one side wail aloud, and before the fire (of the pyre) draws near on one side -- for those who are able to stand firm, clinging fast with the red-eyed Tirumal on one side while illusion stands on the other, it is possible to be saved from the rod of Yama.

367.translit:
seththu-povadhor podhu ninaindhu seyyum seygaigaL deva-piraan-mel
paththar-aay-iRandhaar-peRum peRRai paazhi-thoL-Vittuchiththan-puth-thoor-kon
siththam-nanku-orungith-thiru-maalai seydha maalai ivai-paththum vallaar
siththam-nanku-orungith-thiru-maal-mel senRa sindhai-peRuvar-thaame.
367.meaning: Of the reward that those who died as devotees of the Lord of gods obtain -- the deeds done in remembrance of the moment of dying -- this garland of ten verses was made upon Tirumal by the strong-shouldered Vittuchittan (Periyalvar), chief of Srivilliputtur, with his mind well-focused. Those who can master these ten will themselves attain a mind well-gathered and a thought that goes ever toward Tirumal.

368.translit:
kaasum kaRai-udaik kooraikkum angor kaRRaikkum
aasaiyinaal ang-avaththa-per-idum aadhargaaL
kesavan per-ittu neengaL thenithth-iruminoo
naayagan naaraNan tham-annai naragam-pugaaL.
368.meaning: O fools who, out of desire for a coin, for a soiled garment, and for some little hank of thread, give there worthless names (to your children)! Set the name 'Kesava' upon them and dwell sweetly. The mother of him whose lord is Narayana shall not enter hell.

369.translit:
ang-oru kooRai araikku-uduppadhan-aasaiyaal
mangiya maanida-saadhiyin per-idum aadhargaaL
sengaN-nedumaal sireedharaa-enRu-azhaiththakkaal
nangaigaaL naaraNan tham-annai naragam-pugaaL.
369.meaning: O fools who, out of desire merely for a garment to wrap about the waist, give the names of the wretched human race (to your children)! O ladies, when you call out 'Red-eyed Tirumal! Sridhara!' -- the mother of him whose lord is Narayana shall not enter hell.

370.translit:
ucchiyil eNNeyum chuTTiyum vaLaiyum ukandhu
echam polindheerkaaL en cheyvaan pirar periTTeer
picchaippuk kaakilum em piraan thiru naamamE
nacchumin naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
370.meaning: O you who delight in oil on the crown, the forehead-ornament and bangles, who shine adorned with such trifles, why do you bother to give your children the names of others? Even if you must go begging, long for and chant only the divine names of my Lord. Chant Narayana, O mothers, and you shall not enter hell.

371.translit:
maanida saadhiyil thOnDriRRu Or maanida saadhiyai
maanida saadhiyin periT taal maru maikkillai
vaanudai maadhavaa kOvin thaa enru azhaiththakkaal
naanudai naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
371.meaning: Born into the human race, this is but a human creature; if you give a human creature the name of a mere human, there is no benefit for the hereafter. But if you call out 'Madhava, lord of the heavens! Govinda!', then by my Narayana, O mothers, you shall not enter hell.

372.translit:
malamudai yooththaiyil thOnDriRRu Or mala vooththaiyai
malamudai yooththaiyin periT taal maru maikkillai
kulamudaik kOvindhaa kOvindhaa enru azhaiththakkaal
nalamudai naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
372.meaning: Born of a filth-filled, impure vessel of a body, this is but one such impure vessel; if you give such an impure thing the name of another impure thing, there is no benefit for the hereafter. But if you call out 'Govinda of noble lineage, Govinda!', then by gracious Narayana, O mothers, you shall not enter hell.

373.translit:
naadum nakarum ariya maanidap periTTu
koodi yazhungkik kuzhiyil veezhndhu vazhukkaadhE
chaadiRap paayndha thalaivaa thaamO dharaa enru
naadumin naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
373.meaning: Do not, before the whole country and town, give a human name, and gathering together grieve, and slip and fall into the pit. Instead seek the Lord who leaped and kicked Sakatasura to death, crying 'O Damodara!' Seek Narayana, O mothers, and you shall not enter hell.

374.translit:
maNNil piRandhu maNNaakum maanidap periTTu angu
eNNamon Drinri yirukkum Ezhai manisarkaaL
kaNNuk kiniya karumukil vaNNan naamamE
naNNumin naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
374.meaning: O poor foolish people who, born of the earth and returning to earth, give human names and remain there without a single right thought! Draw near to the name of the dark-cloud-hued Lord, sweet to the eyes. Draw near to Narayana, O mothers, and you shall not enter hell.

375.translit:
nambi bimbiyenru naaTTu maanidap periTTaal
nambum bimbumel laam naalu naaLil azhungkippOm
chemperun thaamaraik kaNNan periT tazhaiththakkaal
nambikaaL naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
375.meaning: If you give worldly human names like 'Nambi' and 'Bimbi', all those Nambis and Bimbis will perish and be forgotten within a few days. But if you name your children for the Lord with red lotus eyes and call out to them, then, O trusting ones, by Narayana you shall not enter hell.

376.translit:
ooththaik kuzhiyil amudham paayvadhu pOl ungkaL
mooththirap piLLaiyai en mukil vaNNan periTTu
kOththuk kuzhaiththuk kuNaala maadith thiriminO
naaththaku naaraNan tham annai narakam pukaaL.
376.meaning: As though nectar were poured into a pit of filth, give to your urine-born children the name of my cloud-hued Lord; string it together, melt with love, and roam about dancing in joy. Praise with your tongue Narayana, O mothers, and you shall not enter hell.

377.translit:
cheeraNi maal thiru naama mEyidath thERRiya
veeraNi tholpukazh viTTu chiththan viriththa
OraNi yoNtamizh onpadhO Donrum vallavar
pEraNi vaikundhaththu enrum pENi yirupparE.
377.meaning: Those who are able in these ten beautiful Tamil verses, composed by Vishnuchitta of ancient heroic fame, which urge the bestowing of the glorious holy names of Tirumal alone, shall forever dwell cherished in the splendid array of Vaikuntha.

378.translit:
thangkaiyai mookkum thamaiyanaith thalaiyum thadindha em thaa charadhi pOy
engkum than puka zhaavirundhu arasaaNda em puru dOththaman irukkai
gangkai gangkaiyenra vaachakath thaalE kaduvinai kaLaindhida kiRkum
gangkaiyin karaimEl kaithozha ninra kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
378.meaning: Our Dasarathi (Rama), who cut off the nose of the sister (Surpanakha) and the head of her elder brother (Ravana), and who, his fame spreading everywhere, ruled as king - the abode of our Purushottama: it is the fortified city called Kandam, standing on the bank of the Ganga which, merely by uttering the word 'Ganga, Ganga', can remove cruel sins, where devotees worship with folded hands.

379.translit:
chalampodhi yudambin thazhalumizh pEzhvaaych chandhiran vengkadhir anja
malarndhezhun thaNavi maNivaNNa vuruvin maal puru dOththaman vaazhvu
nalam thikazh chadaiyaan mudikkonRai malarum naaraNan paadhath thuzhaayum
kalandhizhi punalaal pukarpadu gangkaik kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
379.meaning: The dwelling-place of the gem-hued Tirumal Purushottama, before whom the moon and the fierce-rayed sun tremble - he who blossomed forth and rose up: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the Ganga made radiant by waters in which mingle and flow down the konrai blossoms from the matted locks of the good Siva and the tulasi from the feet of Narayana.

380.translit:
adhirmuka mudaiya valampuri kumizhththi azhalumizh aazhi koN derindhu angu
edhirmuka vasurar thalaikaLai yidaRum em puru dOththaman irukkai
chadhumukan kaiyil sadhuppuyan thaaLil sangkaran sadaiyinil thangki
kadhirmuka maNikoN dizhipunal gangkaik kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
380.meaning: The abode of our Purushottama who sounds the deep-roaring conch, hurls the fire-spitting discus, and strikes off the heads of the asuras who face him in battle: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the Ganga whose waters, having rested in the hand of the four-faced Brahma, at the feet of the four-armed Lord, and in the matted hair of Sankara, flow down bearing radiant gems.

381.translit:
imaiyavar irumaan dhirundhara saaLa ERRuvan dhedhirporu chEnai
namapuram naNuka naandhakam visirum nam puru dOththaman nakarthaan
imavandham thodangki irungkadalaLavum irukarai ulakiraith thaada
kamaiyudaip perumaik gangkaiyin karaimEl kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
381.meaning: The city of our Purushottama who, so that the gods may rule in proud splendour, brandishes his Nandaka sword to send the opposing army that comes to fight near to the city of Yama (death): it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the bank of the great forbearing Ganga, beside which the people of the world on its two banks, from the Himalayas to the vast sea, throng and bathe in clamour.

382.translit:
uzhuvadhOr padaiyum ulakkaiyum villum oNchuda raazhiyum sangkum
mazhuvodu vaaLum padaikkala mudaiya maal puru dOththaman vaazhvu
ezhumaiyum koodi eeNdiya paavam iRaippozhu dhaLavinil ellaam
kazhuvidum perumaik gangkaiyin karaimEl kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
382.meaning: The dwelling of the great Tirumal Purushottama who bears the weapons - the plough, the pestle, the bow, the bright radiant discus and the conch, together with the axe and the sword: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the bank of the great Ganga which can wash away in a single instant all the sins amassed through seven births.

383.translit:
thalaippeydhu kumuRich chalampodhi mEkam salasala pozhindhidak kaNdu
malaipperung kudaiyaal maRaiththavan madhurai maal puru dOththaman vaazhvu
alaippudaith thiraivaay arundhava munivar avabira tham kudain thaada
kalappaikaL kozhikkum gangkaiyin karaimEl kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
383.meaning: The dwelling of the great Mathura-born Tirumal Purushottama who, seeing the water-laden clouds gather and pour down in torrents, sheltered the cattle with a great mountain as umbrella: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the bank of the Ganga where great ascetic sages plunge and bathe in the surging-waved waters performing their ablutions, and where the ploughs cast up their gleanings.

384.translit:
viRpidith thiRuththu vEzhaththai murukki mElirun dhavan thalai saadi
maRporu thezhappaayndhu araiyana yudhaiththa maal puru dOththaman vaazhvu
aRpudha mudaiya ayiraavadha madhamum avariLam padiyaroN saandhum
kaRpaka malarum kalandhizhi gangkaik kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
384.meaning: The dwelling of the great Tirumal Purushottama who broke the bow, destroyed the elephant Kuvalayapida, leaped up to strike off the head of its rider, and wrestling sprang up to kick the king (Kamsa): it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the Ganga down which flow, mingled together, the wondrous must of the elephant Airavata, the fine sandal-paste of the young celestial maidens, and the blossoms of the wish-granting kalpaka tree.

385.translit:
thiraiporu kadalchoozh thiNmadhiL thuvarai vEndhu than maiththunan maarkkaay
arasinai yaviya arasinai yaruLum ari puru dOththaman amarvu
nirainirai yaaka nediyana yoopam nirandharam ozhukkuviTTu iraNdu
karaipurai vELvip pukaikamazh gangkai kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
385.meaning: The abode of Hari, the Purushottama, the king of sea-girt Dwaraka with its strong ramparts, who for the sake of his cousins (the Pandavas) destroyed one kingdom (the Kaurava's) and granted them their kingdom: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the Ganga where tall sacrificial posts stand in row upon row in unbroken lines, and the smoke of sacrifices is fragrant along its two banks.

386.translit:
vadadhisai madhurai chaaLakki raamam vaikundham thuvarai ayOdhdhi
idamudai vadhari yidavakai yudaiya em puru dOththaman irukkai
thadavarai yadhirath tharaNiviN didiyath thalaippaRRik karaimaram saadi
kadalinaik kalangak kaduththizhi gangkaik kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
386.meaning: The abode of our Purushottama who has as his places northern Mathura, Salagrama, Vaikuntha, Dwaraka, Ayodhya, and the spacious Badari: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the Ganga which rushes down so that the broad mountains shake, the earth splits and the heavens thunder, seizing at its source and felling the trees on its banks and stirring the sea to turmoil.

387.translit:
moonRezhuth thadhanai moonRezhuth thadhanaal moonRezhuth thaakki moon Rezhuththai
EnDrukoN dirupaarkku irakkamnan kudaiya em puru dOththaman irukkai
moonRadi nimirththu moonRinil thOnRi moonRinil moonRuru vaanaan
kaandadam pozhilchoozh gangkaiyin karaimEl kaNdamen num kadi nakarE.
387.meaning: The abode of our Purushottama, full of good compassion toward those who, by the three syllables (Aum), make the three-syllabled mantra and hold fast to those three syllables - he who stretched out three strides, who appeared among the three (worlds), and who became the three forms in the three: it is the fortified city called Kandam, on the bank of the Ganga ringed by forest groves and wide tracts.

388.translit:
pongkoli gangkaik karaimali kaNdaththu uRaipuru dOththaman adimEl
vengkali naliyaa villiput thoorkkOn viTTuchith than virup puRRu
thangkiya anbaal cheydhamizh maalai thangkiya naavudai yaarkku
gangkaiyil thirumaal kazhaliNaik keezhE kuLiththirun thakaNak kaamE.
388.meaning: On those who keep upon their tongue this Tamil garland, composed with the abiding love of Vishnuchitta, lord of Villiputtur whom the cruel Kali age does not afflict, set at the feet of the Purushottama who dwells in Kandam abounding on the bank of the roaring Ganga - the merit accruing is as though they had bathed beneath the united feet of Tirumal in the Ganga.

389.translit:
maadhavaththOn puththiranpOy maRikadalvaay maaNdaanai
Odhuviththa thakkaNaiyaa uruvuruvE koduththaanoor
thOdhavaththith thooymaRaiyOr thuRaipadiyath thuLumbiengkum
pOdhilvaiththa thEnchoriyum punalarangka menbadhuvE.
389.meaning: The town of him who, as the fee for the teaching he had received, gave back form for form the son of the great ascetic (Sandipani) who had perished in the encircling sea: it is the place called water-girt Arangam (Srirangam), where the pure Vedic ones bathe at the ghats, and where everywhere the waters brim over, pouring forth honey held within the blossoms.

390.translit:
pirappagatthee maaNdozhinda piLLaikaLai naalvaraiyum
iraippozhudil koNarndhukoduthu oruppadhitha uraippanoor
maraipperundhee vaLarthiruppaar varuvirundhai yaLithiruppaar
sirappudaiya maraiyavarvaazh thiruvaranga menbathuvee.
390.meaning: It is the town of the steadfast Lord who in an instant brought back and restored the four children that had died at the very moment of birth. There dwell those who tend the great Vedic fire and those who lovingly receive guests who come. The place where the renowned Vedic brahmins live is called Srirangam.

391.translit:
marumagandhan sandhadhiyai uyirmeettu maitthunanmaar
urumagatthee veezhaamee kurumugamaayk kaatthaanoor
thirumugamaayc cengamalam thiruniramaayk karunguvaLai
porumugamaay ninralarum punalaranga menbathuvee.
391.meaning: It is the town of the Lord who revived the lineage of his son-in-law's son and, by appearing as a teacher, protected his kinsmen from falling into thunderous ruin. There red lotuses bloom like a sacred face and dark blue lilies like his sacred hue, standing and blossoming as if in contest — that watery Srirangam.

392.translit:
koondhozhutthai sidhaguraippak kodiyavaLvaayk kadiyasolkeettu
eenredutha thaayaraiyum iraacchiyamum aangozhiya
kaandhodutha neripoogik kaNdagaraik kaLaindhaanoor
theendhodutha malarcsoolaith thiruvaranga menbathuvee.
392.meaning: It is the town of the Lord who, when the haughty serving-maid spoke maliciously and he heeded the harsh words from that cruel woman's mouth, left behind the mother who bore and raised him and the kingdom, took the forest path, and destroyed the thorny demons. The Srirangam surrounded by honey-laden flowering groves.

393.translit:
peruvaranga Lavaipatrip pizhagudaiya iraavaNanai
uruvarangap porudhazhitthuiv vulaginaikkaN
perutthaanoor kuruvarumbak koongalarak kuyilkoovum kuLirpozhilsoozh
thiruvaranga menbathuvee enthirumaal seervidamee.
393.meaning: It is the town of the Lord who, fighting and destroying the faulty Ravana — who relied on his great boons — until his very form was shattered, restored this world. Where buds open, kongu flowers blossom, and cuckoos call in the cool surrounding groves — that Srirangam is the abode where my Tirumal resides.

394.translit:
keezhulagil asurargaLaik kizhangirundhu kiLaraamee
aazhividutthu avarudaiya karuvazhitha vazhippanoor
thaazhaimada loodurinjith thavaLavaNNap podiyaNindhu
yaazhinisai vaNdinangaL aaLamvaikkum arangamee.
394.meaning: It is the town of the destroying Lord who, hurling his discus so that the asuras of the nether world should not rise up from their very roots, annihilated their offspring. There bees rub against the fronds of the screw-pine, become adorned with its white powdery pollen, and hum tunes sweet as the lute — that Srirangam.

395.translit:
kozhuppudaiya sezhunguruthi kozhitthizhindhu kumizhtheriya
pizhakkudaiya asurargaLaip piNampadutha perumaanoor
thazhuppariya sandhanangaL thadavaraivaay eertthukkoNdu
thezhippudaiya kaavirivandhu adithozhum seerarangamee.
395.meaning: It is the town of the great Lord who made corpses of the offending asuras, so that their thick rich blood gushed down and spurted up in bubbles. There the resounding Kaveri comes dragging along precious sandalwood logs from the broad mountain slopes and worships his feet — that glorious Srirangam.

396.translit:
valleyitruk keezhalumaay vaaLeyitruc ceeyamumaay
ellaiyillaath tharaNiyaiyum avuNanaiyum idandhaanoor
elliyamboodhu irunjiraivaNdu emperumaan guNampaadi
mallikaiveN sangoodhum madhiLaranga menbathuvee.
396.meaning: It is the town of the Lord who became the boar with strong tusks and the lion with sword-like fangs, and tore apart both the boundless earth and the demon. There at dusk the large-winged bees sing the qualities of my Lord and blow upon white jasmine like conches — that walled Srirangam.

397.translit:
kunraadu kozhumugilpool kuvaLaigaLpool
kuraikadalpool ninraadu kaNamayilpool niramudaiya nedumaaloor
kunraadu pozhilnuzhaindhu kodiyidaiyaar mulaiyaNavi
manroodu thenralumaam madhiLaranga menbathuvee.
397.meaning: It is the town of the great Nedumal whose hue is like a rich cloud dancing on a hill, like blue lilies, like the roaring sea, like a flock of peacocks standing and dancing. There the southern breeze enters the hill-side groves, embraces the breasts of slender-waisted women, and wanders through the village square — that walled Srirangam.

398.translit:
paruvaranga Lavaipatrip padaiyaalith tezhundhaanai
seruvarangap porudhazhitha thiruvaaLan thiruppadhimeel
thiruvarangath thamizhmaalai vittusitthan virithanagoNdu
iruvaranga merithaanai eethavallaa radiyoomee.
398.meaning: Upon the sacred city of the gracious Lord who fought and destroyed the one who rose up surging with his armies relying on his great boons — Vishnuchittan (Periyalwar) has composed this Tamil garland of Srirangam. Those who are able to praise with these verses the Lord who burned the two demons become our masters, and we their servants.

399.translit:
maravadiyaith thambikku vaanpaNaiyam vaitthuppooy vaanoor vaazhac
seru udaiya thisaikkarumam thiruthivandhu ulagaaNda thirumaal kooyil
thiruvadithan thiruuruvum thirumangai malarkkaNNum kaatti ninru
uru udaiya malarneelam kaatru aatta olisalikkum oLi arangamee.
399.meaning: It is the temple of Tirumal who entrusted the wooden sandals as great surety to his younger brother and went away, set right the warlike affairs of the directions so that the celestials might flourish, returned, and ruled the world. There, showing his sacred form and the flower-eyes of his consort Lakshmi, the lovely blooming blue lilies, swayed by the wind, ripple and murmur — that radiant Srirangam.

400.translit:
than adiyaar thiratthagatthuth thaamaraiyaaL aagilum sidhagu uraikkumeel
en adiyaar adhu seyyaar seydhaareel nanru seydhaar enbar poolum
man udaiya vibeedaNarkaa madhil ilangaith thisainookki malarkkaN vaitha
ennudaiya thiruvarangarku anriyum matru oruvarkku aaL aavaree?
400.meaning: Even if Lakshmi who dwells on the lotus should speak ill concerning his devotees, he says, as it were, 'My devotees would not do such a thing; and if they did, they did well.' He set his flower-eyes gazing toward walled Lanka for the sake of Vibhishana who would become its king. To my Lord of Srirangam, and to no other, could I ever be a servant — could I belong to anyone else?

401.translit:
karuL udaiya pozhil marudhum kathak kaLirum pilambanaiyum kadiya maavum
uruL udaiya sakadaraiyum mallaraiyum udaiya vittu oosai keettaan
iruL agatrum eri kathiroon maNdalatthoodu eetri vaithu eeNi vaangi
aruL koduttittu adiyavarai aatkoLvaan amarum oor aNi arangamee.
401.meaning: He uprooted the dark marudu trees, slew the furious elephant, Pralamba, the fierce horse, the cart-demon Sakatasura, and the wrestlers — shattering them so the sound was heard. He raised his devotees up into the orb of the radiance-scattering sun, then drew away the ladder, granted his grace and took them into his service. The beautiful Srirangam is the town where that Lord resides.

402.translit:
padhinaaraam aayiravar deevimaar paNiseyyath thuvarai ennum
madhil naayagaraagi veetrirundha maNavaaLar mannu kooyil
puthu naaLmalark kamalam emperumaan pon vayitrir poovee poolvaan
podhu-naayagam paavitthu irumaandhu pon saaykkum punal arangamee.
402.meaning: It is the abiding temple of the bridegroom Lord who reigned as the king of the walled city called Dwaraka, served by sixteen thousand queens. There the fresh day-blooming lotus, resembling the flower upon my Lord's golden belly, imagining itself to hold universal sovereignty, swells with pride and sheds its gold — that watery Srirangam.

403.translit:
aamaiyaayk gangaiyaay aazh kadalaay avaniyaay aru varaigaLaay
naanmuganaay naanmaraiyaay veeLviyaayth thakkaNaiyaayth thaanum aanaan
seemam udai naaradhanaar senru senru thudhitthu irainjak kidandhaan kooyil
poo maruvip puL inangaL puL araiyan pugazh kuzharum punal arangamee.
403.meaning: He became the tortoise, the Ganga, the deep ocean, the earth, the lofty mountains; he became the four-faced Brahma, the four Vedas, the sacrifice, the sacrificial fee — he himself became all these. It is the temple where he reclines, that the protecting Narada might come again and again to extol and worship him. There flocks of birds dwell among the blossoms and babble the praise of Garuda, lord of birds — that watery Srirangam.

404.translit:
maitthunanmaar kaadhaliyai mayir mudippitthu avargaLaiyee mannar aakki
utharaidhan siruvanaiyum uyakkoNda uyiraaLan uraiyum kooyil
patthargaLum bagavargaLum pazhamozhivaay munivargaLum parandha naadum
sitthargaLum thozhudhu irainjath thisai-viLakkaay nirkinra thiruvarangamee.
404.meaning: It is the temple where dwells the Lord of life who had his cousins' beloved's hair bound up (avenging her dishonor), made them into kings, and saved the little son of Uttara (the unborn Parikshit). There devotees, holy ones, sages of proverbial speech, the wide lands, and the siddhas bow and worship — Srirangam that stands as the lamp lighting all the directions.

405.translit:
kuraL biramasaariyaay maavaliyaik kurumbu adhakki arasuvaangi
iraippozhudil paadhaaLam kalavirukkai koduthu uganda emmaan kooyil
erippu udaiya maNivaraimeel iLanjaayiru ezhundhaarpool aravu aNaiyin
sirappu udaiya paNangaLmisaic cezhumaNigaL vittu erikkum thiruvarangamee.
405.meaning: It is the temple of my Lord who, as the dwarf celibate, quelled the insolence of Mahabali, seized his kingdom, and in an instant graciously granted him the nether world as his abode. There, upon the splendid hoods of his serpent couch, the bright gems flash and scatter light like a young sun rising over a glittering jeweled mountain — that Srirangam.

406.translit:
uram patri iraNiyanai ugir-nudhiyaal oLLiya maarvu uraikka oonric
siram patri mudi idiyak kaN pidhunga vaay alarath tezhitthaan kooyil
uram petra malarkkamalam ulagu aLandha seevadi pool uyarndhu kaatta
varambu utra kathirccennel thaaLsaaytthuth thalaivaNakkum thaN arangamee.
406.meaning: It is the temple of the Lord who, seizing Hiranya's body, pressed his sharp claw-tips deep into the gleaming chest, gripped the head so that the crown was crushed, the eyes burst forth and the mouth screamed, and roared aloud. There the strong-grown lotus blossoms rise up tall like his foot that measured the worlds, while the ripened rays of paddy at the field-edge bend their stalks and bow their heads — that cool Srirangam.

407.translit:
deevu udaiya meenamaay aamaiyaay eenamari kuraLum aagi
moouruvil iraamanaayk kaNNanaayk karkiyaay mudippaan kooyil
seevalodu pedai annam sengamala malar eeri oosal aadip
pooaNaimeel thudhaindhu ezhu sempodi aadi viLaiyaadum punal arangamee.
407.meaning: It is the temple of the Lord who became the divine fish, the tortoise, the boar, the man-lion, and the dwarf, who in three forms became Rama, who became Kannan, and who will bring all to an end as Kalki. There the swan with its mate climbs upon the red lotus blossoms and sways as on a swing, then nestling on the flower-bed bathes in the red pollen that rises and plays — that watery Srirangam.

408.translit:
seru aaLum puLLaaLan maNNaaLan serucseyum naandham ennum
oru vaaLan maraiyaaLan oodaadha padaiyaaLan vizhukkai yaaLan
iravu aaLan pagalaaLan enaiyaaLan eezhu ulagap perum peeraaLan
thiruvaaLan inidhaagath thirukkaNgaL vaLarginra thiruvarangamee.
408.meaning: He is the master of the battle-waging bird (Garuda), the lord of the earth, the wielder of the lone war-making sword called Nandaka, the master of the Vedas, the bearer of weapons that never retreat, the lord of plenty, the ruler of night and day, my own ruler, the great great lord of the seven worlds, the lord of Lakshmi. Srirangam is where his sacred eyes rest sweetly in slumber.

409.translit:
kainnaagatthu idar kadindha kanal aazhip padai udaiyaan karudhum kooyil
thennaadum vadanaadum thozhaninra thiruvarangath thiruppadhiyin meel
meynnaavan mey adiyaan vittusitthan viritha thamizh uraikka vallaar
enjnaanrum emperumaan iNaiyadikkeezh iNai piriyaadhu iruppar thaamee.
409.meaning: It is the temple cherished by the Lord who bears the flaming discus, who removed the distress of the trunked elephant (Gajendra) — the sacred city of Srirangam that both the southern and northern lands stand and worship. Those who can recite the Tamil verses composed about it by Vishnuchittan (Periyalwar), the true-tongued, true servant, will forever remain at our Lord's paired feet, never parting from them.

410.translit:
thuppudai yaarai adaivadhu ellaam sorvidaththu thuthuNai yaavar endre
oppilen aagilum ninnadaindhen aanaikku nee aruL seydha maiyaal
eyppu ennai vandhu naliyum podhu angu edhum naan unnai ninaikka maatten
appodhaikku ippodhe solli vaiththen arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
410.meaning: To resort to the mighty is what all do, thinking, 'Who will be our helper in times of collapse?' Though I am beyond compare in unworthiness, I have taken refuge in You, because You showed grace to the elephant (Gajendra). When weariness comes upon me and torments me, at that moment I shall not be able to think of You at all; so for that time I have spoken and arranged it now in advance, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

411.translit:
saamidaththu ennaik kuRikkoL kaNdaay sangodu sakkaram endhinaane
naamadiththu ennai aneka thaNdam seyvadhaa niRpar naman tamarkaL
pomidaththu un thiRaththu eththanaiyum pugaavaNNam niRpadhor maayai vallai
aamidaththe unnaich solli vaiththen arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
411.meaning: At the moment of my death, take heed of me, O You who bear the conch and the discus! The messengers of Yama will stand ready to beat me with their tongues and inflict many tortures. You are mighty in the magic by which You stand barring, so that not even a little of any thought of You can enter at the moment of departing — so I have told You of it while there was still occasion, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

412.translit:
ellaiyil vaasal kuRugach chendraal etri naman tamar patrum podhu
nillumin ennum upaayam illai nemiyum sangamum endhinaane
sollalaam podhe un naamam ellaam sollinen ennaik kuRikkoNdu endrum
allal padaavaNNam kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
412.meaning: When I draw near the gateway at the boundary (of death) and the messengers of Yama strike and seize me, there is no means to say to them 'Stop!', O You who bear the discus and the conch! While there was still time to speak, I have spoken all Your names; so take heed of me and ever guard me from falling into affliction, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

413.translit:
otrai vidaiyanum naanmuganum unnai aRiyaap perumai yone
mutra ulagellaam neeye aagi moondrezhuth aaya mudhalvane yaa
atradhu vaaNaaL ivaRku endru eNNi anja naman tamar patraluRRa
atraikku nee ennaik kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
413.meaning: O You of greatness that even He of the single bull (Shiva) and the four-faced one (Brahma) cannot know! You who became all the worlds in their fullness, the primordial Lord who are the three letters (the sacred syllable)! On that day when the messengers of Yama come to seize me, deciding 'His allotted lifespan is ended for this man,' making me tremble — on that day You must protect me, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

414.translit:
pai aravin aNaip paaRkadaluL paLLi koLgindra parama moorththi
uyya ulagu padaikka veNdi undhiyil thotrinaay naanmuganai
vaiya manisaraip poy endru eNNik kaalanaiyum udane padaiththaay
aiya ini ennaik kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
414.meaning: Supreme Lord who reclines in sleep upon the hooded serpent-couch in the ocean of milk! Wishing to create the worlds for their redemption, You brought forth the four-faced Brahma from Your navel. Deeming the people of the earth to be false, You created Death (Yama) at the same time. O Lord, now You must protect me, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

415.translit:
thaNNena villai naman tamarkaL saalak kodumaigaL seyyaa niRpar
maNNodu neerum eriyum kaalum matrum aagaasamum aagi nindraay
eNNalaam podhe un naamam ellaam eNNinen ennaik kuRikkoNdu
endrum aNNale nee ennaik kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
415.meaning: There is no respite; the messengers of Yama will stand committing many cruelties. O You who stand as the earth and the water and the fire and the wind and also the ether (the five elements)! While there was still occasion to meditate, I have meditated upon all Your names; so take heed of me, O Lord, and ever protect me, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

416.translit:
senjol maRaipporuL aagi nindra devargaL naayagane em maane
enjal il ennudai innamudhe ezhulagum udaiyaay en appaa
vanja uruvin naman tamarkaL valindhu nalindhu ennaip patrum podhu
anjalai endru ennaik kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
416.meaning: O Lord of the gods, who stand as the meaning of the Vedas of pure words, O my Master! O my flawless sweet nectar, O Lord of all the seven worlds, O my Father! When the messengers of Yama of deceitful form forcibly torment and seize me, saying 'Fear not!' You must protect me, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

417.translit:
naan edhum un maayam ondru aRiyen naman tamar patri nalindhittu indha
oone pugu endru modhum podhu angu edhum naan unnai ninaikka maatten
vaaney vaanavar thangaL eesaa madhuraip piRandha maamaayane en
aanaay nee ennaik kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
417.meaning: I know nothing at all of Your wondrous power; when the messengers of Yama seize me and torment me, beating and saying 'Enter into this body of flesh again!', at that moment I shall not be able to think of You at all. O Lord of the celestials of the bright heaven, great wondrous one born in Mathura, You who became my support — You must protect me, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

418.translit:
kundreduththu aanirai kaaththa aayaa konirai meyththavane em maane
andru mudhal indru aRudhi yaaga aadhi am sodhi maRandhaRiyen
nandrum kodiya naman tamarkaL nalindhu valindhu ennaip patrum podhu
andru angu nee ennaik kaakka veNdum arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaane.
418.meaning: O cowherd who lifted up the hill (Govardhana) and guarded the herd of cattle, O You who grazed the cattle herds, O my Master! From that day until this day's end I have never forgotten the primordial beautiful Light. When the exceedingly cruel messengers of Yama torment and forcibly seize me, on that day You must protect me there, O You who recline upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam.

419.translit:
maayavanai madhusoodanan thannai maadhavanai maRaiyorgaL eththum
aayargaL etrinai achchudhanan thannai arangath aravaNaip paLLiyaanai
veyar pugazh villipuththoor man vittusiththan sonna maalai paththum
thooya manaththanar aagi vallaar thoomaNi vaNNanukku aaLar taame.
419.meaning: The wondrous one, Madhusudana, Madhava whom the knowers of the Vedas extol, the bull among the cowherds, the imperishable Achyuta, who reclines upon the serpent-couch at Srirangam — these ten verses of the garland spoken by Vishnuchitta (Periyalvar), lord of Villiputtur praised by the Veyar, those who can master them with pure minds shall themselves become servants of the Lord of pure-gem hue.

420.translit:
vaakkuth thoomai ilaamaiyinaale
 maadhavaa unnai vaaykkoLLa maatten
naakku ninnaiyallaal aRiyaadhu
 naan adhu anjuvan en vasam andru
moorkkup pesugindraan ivan endru
 munivaayelum en naavinukku aatren
kaakkai vaayilum katturai koLvar
 kaaraNaa aruLak kodiyaane.
420.meaning: Because my speech is impure, O Madhava, I am unable to take You upon my lips. Yet my tongue knows nothing but You, and I fear this — it is not within my control. Even if You are angered, saying 'This man speaks foolishly,' I cannot restrain my tongue. People accept good speech even from the mouth of a crow; O cause of all, O You who give grace, do not be cruel to give grace.

421.translit:
sazhakku naakkodu pun kavi sonnen
 sangu sakkaram endhukaiyaane pizhaippar
aagilum tham adiyaar sol
 poRuppadhum periyorgaL adan andre vizhikkum
kaNNilen nin kaN matrallaal
 veRoruvarodu en manam patraadhu
uzhaikku or puLLi migaiyandru kaNdaay
 oozhi ezhulagu uNdumizhndhaane.
421.meaning: With a perverse tongue I have spoken a poor poem, O You whose hands bear the conch and the discus. Even if they err, to forgive the words of one's own devotees is indeed the duty of the great. I have no eye to look upon any but Your eyes; my heart will not cling to any other. For a deer one extra spot is no excess (one more devotee is no burden to You) — see, O You who swallowed the seven worlds in the deluge and brought them forth again.

422.translit:
nanmai theemaigaL ondrum aRiyen
 naaraNaa ennum iththanai allaal
punmaiyaal unnaip puLLuvam pesip
 pugazhvaan andru kaNdaay thirumaale
unnumaaRu unnai ondrum aRiyen
 ovaadhe namo naaraNaa enban
vanmai yaavadhu un koyilil vaazhum
 vaittaNavan ennum vanmai kaNdaaye.
422.meaning: I know nothing at all of good and evil, except only to utter 'Narayana!' I do not, out of baseness, praise You with flattering, deceitful speech, O Tirumal. I know nothing of how to meditate upon You; ceaselessly I say 'Namo Narayana!' My strength is this — see — the strength of being called a Vaishnava who dwells in Your temple.

423.translit:
nedumaiyaal ulagu ezhum aLandhaay
 ninmalaa nediyaay adiyenaik
kudimai koLvadhaRku aiyuRa veNdaa
 koorai soRu ivai veNduvadhu illai
adimai ennum ak koyinmaiyaale
 angange avai podharum kaNdaay
kodumaik kanjanaik kondru nin thaadhai
 koththavan thaLai koLviduththaane.
423.meaning: O You who in Your greatness measured all the seven worlds, O immaculate one, O lofty one! You need have no doubt about taking me, Your servant, into Your service. Garment and food — these I do not seek. By the very service to You, those things will come of themselves here and there, see. O You who slew the cruel Kamsa and released the bonds that bound Your father!

424.translit:
thottam illavaL aaththozhu odai
 thudavaiyum kiNaRum ivai ellaam
vaattam indri un ponnadik keezhe
 vaLaippagam vaguththuk koNdirundhen
naattu maanidaththodu enakku aridhu
 nachchuvaar palar kezhal ondru aagi
kottu maN koNda koLkaiyinaane
 kunjaram veezhak kombosiththaane.
424.meaning: Garden and cattle-pen and pond, vegetable plot and well — all these without any want I have laid out and held wholly beneath Your golden feet (offering all to You). Association with worldly men is hard for me; many would harm me. O You of the resolve who, becoming a single boar, lifted up the earth on Your tusk, who broke the tusk so that the elephant fell!

425.translit:
kaNNaa naanmuganaip padaiththaane
 kaaraNaa kariyaay adiyen naan
uNNaa naaL pasi yaavadhu ondru illai
 ovaadhe namo naaraNaa endru
eNNaa naaLum irukku esuch saama vedha
 naaNmalar koNdu un paadham
naNNaa naaL avai thaththuRum aagil
 andru enakku avai pattini naaLe.
425.meaning: O Krishna, O You who created the four-faced Brahma, O cause of all, O dark-hued one — I, Your servant! There is no day of my fasting in the days I do not eat. But ceaselessly saying 'Namo Narayana,' on the days I do not meditate, and on the days I do not approach Your feet with the fresh blossoms of the Rig, Yajur and Sama Vedas — if such days should befall me, that day is my day of starving.

426.translit:
veLLai veLLaththin mel oru paambai
 meththai yaaga viriththu adhan
mele kaLLa niththirai koLgindra maarkkam
 kaaNalaangol endru aasaiyi
naale uLLam sora ugandhedhir vimmi
 uroma koobangaL aay kaNNa neergaL
thuLLam sorath thuyilaNai koLLen
 sollaay yaan unnaith thaththuRumaaRe.
426.meaning: Upon the white ocean spreading a single serpent as a mattress, and on it feigning sleep — out of longing to behold that posture, my heart melts in joy; I swell with emotion as my hair stands on end like sprouting pores, and tears flow streaming down. I will not take rest upon my bed. Tell me, how am I to attain You!

427.translit:
vaNNa maalvarai ye kudai yaaga
 maari kaaththavane madhusoodaa
kaNNane kari koLviduththaane
 kaaraNaa kaLiRu attapiraane
eNNuvaar idaraik kaLaivaane
 eththarum perung keerththiyinaane
naNNi naan unnai naaLthoRum eththum
 nanmaiye aruL sey embiraane.
427.meaning: O You who held the great beautiful hill as an umbrella and warded off the rain (at Govardhana), O Madhusudana! O Krishna, O You who freed the elephant (Gajendra) from its seizing, O cause of all, O Lord who slew the elephant (Kuvalayapida)! O You who remove the sorrows of those who meditate, O You of great glory hard even to praise! Grant me only the good of drawing near and extolling You day after day, O my Lord.

428.translit:
namban e navindreththa vallaargaL
 naadhane narasingam adhaanaay
umbar kon ulagu ezhum aLandhaay
 oozhi yaayinaay aazhi mun nendhi
kamba maa kari koLviduththaane
 kaaraNaa kadalaik kadaindhaane
embiraan ennai yaaLudaith thene
 ezhaiyeni daraik kaLaiyaaye.
428.meaning: O trustworthy one, Lord of those able to extol You repeatedly, O Narasimha who You became! O King of the celestials, who measured all seven worlds, who became the aeon itself, bearing the discus before You! O You who freed the great rutting elephant from its seizing, O cause of all, O You who churned the ocean! My Lord who hold me as Your own, remove the sorrows of me, this poor one.

429.translit:
kaamar thaadhai karudhalar singam
 kaaNa iniya karunguzhal kuttan
vaamanan en maragatha vaNNan
 maadhavan madhusoodanan thannai
sema nankamarum puduvaiyar kon
 vittusiththan viyandhamizh paththum
naama mendru navindruraippaargaL
 naNNuvaar ollai naaraNan ulage.
429.meaning: The father of Kama (Manmatha), a lion to His foes, the dark-tressed darling sweet to behold, Vamana, my emerald-hued one, Madhava, Madhusudana — these ten wondrous Tamil verses of Vishnuchitta (Periyalvar), king of the people of Puduvai (Srivilliputtur) where well-being abides — those who recite them as a litany, holding them as His sacred name, shall swiftly attain the world of Narayana.

430.translit:
naikkudat taipparri eerum erumbugaL pool niranthu engum
kaikkoNdu nirkindra nooygaaL kaalam peruvuyyap poomin
meykkoNdu vanthu pugunthu veedap piraanaar kidanthaar
paikkoNda paambaNai yoodum paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
430.meaning: O diseases that stand spread everywhere, clinging and taking hold of me like swarms of ants that grasp and climb a pot of ghee! Take the chance while there is yet time and depart to be saved. The Lord of the Vedas has truly come, entered, and reclined here within my body upon his hooded serpent-couch; this town is no longer guarded as it once was.

431.translit:
chittira kuttha nezhuththaal thenpulak koonpori yorri
vaiththa ilachchinai maarrith thoothuvar oodi yoLiththaar
muththuth thiraikkadar serppanmoothari vaaLar muthalvan
paththark kamuthan adiyeen paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
431.meaning: The messengers of Yama, lord of the southern realm, fled and hid, for the seal stamped by Chitragupta's writing and Yama's mark has been wiped away. He is the Lord of the sea whose shores break with pearly waves, the foremost one known to the wise of old, ambrosia to his devotees, and I am his servant; this town is no longer guarded as it once was.

432.translit:
vayirril thozhuvaip piriththuvanpulach sevai yathakki
kayirrumak kaaNi kazhiththuk kaalidaip paasam kazharri
eyirridai maNkoNda enthai iraappagal oothuviththu ennaip
payirrip paNiseyyak koNdaan paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
432.meaning: My Father, who once lifted the earth on his tusk, broke open the prison within the womb, subdued the wild bullock of the unruly senses, loosed the rope and its bond, and undid the noose around my feet. Making me recite his praise night and day, he trained me and took me into his service; this town is no longer guarded as it once was.

433.translit:
mangiya valvinai nooygaaL umakkumoor valvinai kaNdeer
inguppukeenmin pukeenmin eLidanru kaNdeer pukeenmin
singap piraanavan emmaan serum thirukkoyil kaNdeer
pangap padaathuuyyap poominpaNdanru pattinam kaappee.
433.meaning: O cruel karmic diseases now waning! Behold, even you have your own grievous fate. Enter not here, enter not, behold it is not easy, enter not! Behold, this is the sacred temple where my Lord, the Man-Lion (Narasimha), abides. Depart and be saved without coming to ruin; this town is no longer guarded as it once was.

434.translit:
maaNik kuraluru vaaya maayanai enmanath thuLLe
peeNik koNarnthu pugudhavaiththuk koN deenpiri thindri
maaNikkap paNdaaram kaNdeer valivan kurumbarka LuLLeer
paaNikka veeNdaa nadamin paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
434.meaning: I have lovingly brought and installed within my heart the wondrous Lord who took the form of the dwarf Vamana, and nothing else. Behold, this is a treasury of rubies! O you mighty and strong robbers within, do not delay, be off! This town is no longer guarded as it once was.

435.translit:
urra vurupiNi nooygaaL umakkonru sollugeen keeNmin
perranggaL meykkum piraanaar peeNum thirukkoyil kaNdeer
arra muraikkindreen innam aazhvinai gaaLumakku ingor
parrillai kaNdeer nadamin paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
435.meaning: O afflicting diseases that have fastened upon me, I shall tell you one thing, listen! Behold, this is the sacred temple cherished by the Lord who grazes the cattle. I declare to you plainly, O deep-rooted karmas: behold, you have no hold here at all, so be off! This town is no longer guarded as it once was.

436.translit:
kongaich siruvarai yennum pothumbinil veezhnthu vazhukki
angor muzhaiyinil pukkittu azhunthik kidanthuzhal veenai
vangak kadalvaN NanammaaN valvinai yaayina maarri
pangap padaavaNNam seythaan paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
436.meaning: I had fallen and slipped into the thicket called the little hillocks of a woman's breasts, and slid into a deep cave there, sunk down and wandered in misery. The Lord, my Master, hued like the ocean that bears ships, removed the grievous karmas that bound me and made it so that I would not come to ruin; this town is no longer guarded as it once was.

437.translit:
eedhangka Laayina vellaam iranga liduviththuen nuLLe
peethaka vaadaip piraanaar pirama guruvaagi vanthu
poothil kamalavan nenjam pugunthumen sennith thidaril
paatha vilachchinai vaiththaar paNdanru pattinam kaappee.
437.meaning: Causing all the evils that had befallen me to subside, the Lord clad in golden garment came as a noble guru (brahma-guru), entered my heart which is like a blossoming lotus, and set the mark of his feet upon the crown of my head; this town is no longer guarded as it once was.

438.translit:
uraka luraka lurakal oNsuda raazhiye sangke
aravveri naanthaka vaaLe azhagiya saarngame thaNde
iravu padaama liruntha eNmar ulogabaa leerggaaL
paravai yaraiyaa urakal paLLi yaraikkurik koNmin.
438.meaning: Sleep not, sleep not, sleep not, O bright radiant Discus, O Conch! O sword Nandaka that destroys foes by its righteous edge, O beautiful bow Sarnga, O mace! And you eight Guardians of the directions who abide undying, and you, King of birds (Garuda) - sleep not, but keep watch over the bedchamber!

439.translit:
aravath thamaLiyi noodum azhagiya paarkada loodum
aravinthap paavaiyum thaanum agampadi vanthu pugunthu
paravaith thiraipala moothap paLLikoL kindra piraanai
paravugin draanvittu siththan pattinam kaavarpo ruttee.
439.meaning: Vishuchittan (Periyalwar) praises and extols the Lord who, with his serpent-couch and the lovely Milk-Ocean, came together with his lotus-seated Lady (Lakshmi), entered within, and reclines in sleep while the many waves of the sea beat about him - and he does so for the sake of guarding the town.

440.translit:
thukkach suzhalaiyaich suzhnthukidantha valaiyai arappariththu
pukkinil pukkunnaik kaNdukoN deeninip poogavidu vathuNde
makka Laruvaraik kallidai mootha izhanthavaL thanvayirril
sikkena vanthu piranthunin raaythiru maaliruchchoolai yenthaay.
440.meaning: Tearing away utterly the net of sorrow that lay coiled and entangling me, I entered into your sanctum and beheld you within; now, would I ever let you go? O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, you who came and were firmly born in the womb of her (Devaki) who had lost her six sons dashed upon a stone - you, my Father!

441.translit:
vaLaiththuvaith theeninip poogalotteen unthaninthira gnaalanggaLaal
oLiththidil ninthiru vaaNaikaN daayniioruvarkkum meyyanallai
aLiththenggum naadum nagaramum thammudaith theevinai theerkkalurru
teLiththu valanjeyyum theerthamudaith thiru maalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
441.meaning: I have hemmed you in and will not let you go now; if you slip away by your magic illusions, I swear by your sacred self - you would then be no one's true Lord! O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, you whose holy waters every land and town comes to, bathing in them to be cleansed and circumambulating to rid themselves of their own evil karma - you, my Father!

442.translit:
unakkup paNisey thirukkum thavamudai yeeninippoyoruvan
thanakkup paNinthu kadaiththalai nirkainin saayai yazhivu kaNdaay
punaththinai kiLLip puthuvavi kaatiun ponnadi vaazhgavenru
inakkura varputhiya thuNNum ezhilthiru maalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
442.meaning: I possess the penance of remaining ever in your service; if I now go and bow to some other and stand at his doorstep, behold, that would be the ruin of your own honor! O beautiful Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, where the kindred hill-folk pluck the millet from their fields, offer the fresh first-fruits crying 'May your golden feet flourish!', and then eat the new harvest - you, my Father!

443.translit:
kaatham palavum thirinthuzhan reerkkuangor nizhalillai niirumillaiun
paatha nizhalallaal marro ruyirppidam naanengum kaaNgindrileen
thoothusen raaykuru paaNdavark kaayangor poychurram pesichsenru
peethanjeythu engum piNampaduththaay thirumaalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
443.meaning: For me who have wandered roaming many leagues, there is no shade there and no water either; save for the shade of your feet I find nowhere any place of refuge for my life. O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, you who went as a messenger for the Pandavas, your kin, and going there spoke of a false (broken) kinship, sowing division and laying corpses everywhere on the field - you, my Father!

444.translit:
kaalumezhaa kaNNaniirum nillaa udal soornthu nadunggikural
meelu mezhaamayirk koochchumaraa ena thoLgaLum veezhvozhiyaa
maaluga laanirkkum enmana neeunnai vaazhath thalaippeyditteen
seeluga laanirkkum niiLsunai soozhthiru maalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
444.meaning: My feet will not rise, my tears will not cease, my body grows faint and trembles, my voice will not lift, the thrill of my horripilation does not stop, and my shoulders do not cease their sinking down. O my mind that stands deluded with love! I have set myself to live by you. O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, ringed by long ponds where the carp leap and frolic - you, my Father!

445.translit:
eruththuk kodiyudai yaanum piramanum inthira nummarrum
oruththarum ippira viyennum nooykku marunthari vaarumillai
maruththuva naaynindra maamaNi vaNNaa marupira vithavirath
thiruththiung koyir kadaippugap peythiru maalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
445.meaning: Neither the bull-bannered one (Shiva), nor Brahma, nor Indra, nor any other knows the remedy for this disease called birth. O great gem-hued Lord who stands as the physician! End for me the recurrence of birth, set me right, and grant me to enter the threshold of your temple. O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai - you, my Father!

446.translit:
akkarai yennu manaththak kadalu Lazhunthiun peeraruLaal
ikkarai yeeri yiLaiththirun theenaiganj seelenru kaigaviyaay
sakkara mumthadak kaigaLum kaNgaLum peethaka vaadaiyodum
sekkar niraththuch sivappudai yaaythiru maalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
446.meaning: For me, who had sunk into the sea of the mind called craving for the other shore, and who by your great grace climbed onto this shore and rested wearily - lower your hand and say 'Fear not!' O Lord whose redness glows like the crimson evening sky, with your discus and broad arms, your eyes, and your golden garment. O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai - you, my Father!

447.translit:
eththanai kaalamum eththanai yoozhiyum inrodu naaLaiyenree
iththanai kaalamum poaykkirip patteen iniunnaip poogalotteen
maiththunan maargaLai vaazhviththu maarralar nuurruva raikkeduththaay
siththamnin paala tharithiyan reethiru maalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
447.meaning: For age upon age and aeon upon aeon, saying only 'today, tomorrow,' all this long time I have gone on tricked and deceived; now I will not let you go. O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, you who made your kinsmen (the Pandavas) flourish and destroyed the hundred foes (Kauravas) - do you not know that my heart is wholly set on you? You, my Father!

448.translit:
anru vayirril kidanthirunthee adimai seyya lurriruppan
inru vanthu ingu unnaik kaNdu koNdeen inip poaga viduvathuNde
senrangu vaaNanai aayiran thoLum thiruchchakkaramathanaal
thenrith thisaithisai veezhachserraay thirumaalirunj soolaiyenthaay.
448.meaning: Even back then, while I lay within the womb, I was already resolved to do you service; today I have come here and beheld you - now, would I ever let you go? O Lord of Tirumaliruncholai, you who went and with your sacred discus struck down Banasura's thousand shoulders, scattering them to fall in every direction - you, my Father!

449.translit:
senrula gamkudain thaadum sunaiththiru maalirunj soolaithannuL
ninra piraanadi meeladi maiththiram neerpada viNNappanjsey
ponthigazh maadam polinthuthoanrum puthuvaikkon vittusiththan
onrinoa donpathum paadavallaar ula gamaLan thaanthamaree.
449.meaning: Vishuchittan (Periyalwar), the lord of Puduvai where golden mansions shine resplendent, fittingly made this petition concerning the servitude at the feet of the Lord who stands within Tirumaliruncholai, with its ponds where people go to plunge and bathe. Those who can sing these ten verses (these nine together with one) will become the people of the Lord who measured the worlds.

450.translit:
senniyoongu thaN thiruvEngkadamudaiyaay ulagu
thannaivaazha ninRanambee thaamOdharaa sadhiraa ennaiyum
ennudaimaiyaiyum un chakkarapporiyoTRikkoNdu
ninnaruLE purindhirundhEn ini en thirukkuRippE.
450.meaning: O Lord of cool Tiruvengkatam whose peak soars high, O perfect One (Nambi) who stood to give the world life, O Damodara, O skillful One! You have stamped both me and all that is mine with the mark of Your discus, claiming us as Your own. I have remained taking refuge in Your grace alone — what now is Your sacred intention for me?

451.translit:
paRavaiyERu paramburudaa nee ennaik kaikkoNdapin
piRavaiyennum kadalumvaTRip perumpadha maaginRadhaal
iRavuseyyum paavakkaadu theekkoLeei vEginRadhaal
aRivaiyennum amudhavaaRu thalaippaTRi vaaykkoNdadhE.
451.meaning: O Supreme Person who rides upon the bird (Garuda)! Since You took me into Your hand, the ocean called birth has dried up and a great state of bliss arises. The forest of sins that brings death has caught fire and is burning away. The nectarous river called true knowledge has welled up at its source and overflowed into my mouth.

452.translit:
emmanaa en kuladheyvamE ennudaiya naayaganE
ninnuLEnaayp peRRananmai ivvulaginil aarpeRuvaar
namman pOlE veezhththamukkum naattiluLLa paavamellaam
summenaadhE kaividdOdith thooRugaL paaindhanavE.
452.meaning: O my Lord, O my family deity, O my Master! Belonging to You, the good fortune I have gained — who else in this world could gain it? All the sins in the land that would have cast down and destroyed people like us — without a sound, they let go, fled and scattered into the bushes.

453.translit:
kadalkadaindhu amudhamkoNdu kalasaththai niRaiththaaRpOl
udalurugi vaaydhiRandhu maduththuunnai niRaiththukkoNdEn
kodumaiseyyum kooTRamum en kOlaadi kuRugappeRaa
thadavaraiththOL chakkarapaaNee saarngaviR chEvaganE.
453.meaning: As when, having churned the ocean and taken the nectar, one fills the pitcher full — so, my body melting, my mouth opened, I drank deep and filled myself with You. Now cruel Death (Yama) cannot come near me, O Lord of the great mountain-like shoulders, who bear the discus in hand, O warrior wielding the Sarnga bow!

454.translit:
ponnaikkoNdu uraikalmeedhE niRamezha vuraiththaaRpOl
unnaikkoNdu en naavagampaal maaTRinRi uraiththukkoNdEn
unnaikkoNdu ennuLvaiththEn ennaiyum unnilittEn
ennappaa en irudeekEsaa ennuyirk kaavalanE.
454.meaning: As gold is rubbed upon the touchstone so that its true color shows, so have I rubbed and tested You upon my tongue with no alloy of falsehood. I have taken You and set You within me, and I too have placed myself within You. O my Father, O my Hrishikesha, O guardian of my life!

455.translit:
unnudaiya vikkiramam onRozhi yaamalellaam
ennudaiya nenjagampaal suvarvazhi ezhudhikkoNdEn
mannadanga mazhuvalangaik koNda iraamanambee
ennidaivandhu emperumaan iniyengup pOginRadhE.
455.meaning: All of Your heroic deeds, not leaving out a single one, I have drawn and inscribed upon the wall that is my heart. O Parashurama, perfect One, who held the axe in Your strong hand to subdue all kings — having come into my presence, O my Lord, where now will You go?

456.translit:
paruppadhaththuk kayalpoRiththa paaNdiyar kulapadhipOl
thiruppolindha sEvadienj senniyin mElpoRiththaay
maruppOsiththaay malladarththaay enRenRuun vaasagamE
uruppolindha naavinEnai unakkuurith thaakkinaiyE.
456.meaning: Like the lord of the Pandya line who stamped the fish-emblem upon the mountain, You have stamped Your splendid, beautiful sacred feet upon my head. Saying again and again 'You who broke the tusk! You who crushed the wrestlers!' — making my tongue flourish only with Your words, You have made me wholly Your own.

457.translit:
anandhanpaalum karudanpaalum aidhunoydhaaga vaiththuen
manandhanuLLE vandhuvaigi vaazhacheydhaay empiraan
ninaindhuennuL LEninRu nekkukkaNgaL asumbozhuga
ninaindhirundhE siramamtheerndhEn nEmi nediyavanE.
457.meaning: Holding Ananta (the serpent couch) and Garuda as light and slight, You came and abode within my mind and made me live, O my Lord. Meditating, standing within me, my heart melting and my eyes streaming with tears, dwelling in remembrance of You I have been freed of all weariness, O tall One who bears the discus!

458.translit:
