panikkadalil paLLikOLaip pazhagavittu Odivandhuen
manakkadalil vaazhavalla maayamaNaaLa nambee
thanikkadalE thanichchudarE thaniyulagE enRenRu
unakkidamaa yirukkaennai unakkuurith thaakkinaiyE.
458.meaning: Leaving behind Your accustomed repose upon the cool milk-ocean, You came running to dwell and live within the ocean of my mind, O wondrous Bridegroom, perfect One! Saying again and again 'O matchless ocean! O matchless radiance! O matchless world!' — so that I might be a fitting abode for You, You have made me wholly Your own.

459.translit:
thadavaraivaay miLirndhuminnum thavaLanedung kodipOl
sudaroLiyaay nenjinuLLE thOnRumen sOdhinambee
vadadhadamum vaikundhamum madhiLthuva raapadhiyum
idavagaigaL igazhndhittuenpaal idavagai koNdanaiyE.
459.meaning: Like a tall white banner that gleams and flashes upon a great mountain, You appear as radiant light within my heart, O my luminous Lord, perfect One! The northern shore (of the milk-ocean), Vaikuntha, and walled Dvaraka — spurning these as Your abodes, You have taken Your dwelling-place with me.

460.translit:
vEyardhangaL kulaththudhiththa vittusith thanmanaththE
kOyilkoNda kOvalanaik kozhungkuLirmugilvaNNanai
aayarERRai amararkOvai andhaNartha mamudhaththinai
saayaipOlap paadavallaar thaamum aNukkargaLE.
460.meaning: The Cowherd (Krishna) who took up His temple in the heart of Vishnuchitta (Periyazhvar), born in the lineage of the Veyar — the dark, cool rain-cloud-hued One, the bull among cowherds, the king of the celestials, the very nectar of the holy Brahmins — those who can sing of Him following close like His own shadow shall themselves become His intimate ones.

90.translit:
mikka perumpugazh maavali veLviyil
takkadhi thanrenru thaanam vilakkiya
sukkiran kaNNaith thurumbaal kiLaRiya
sakkarakkaiyanee acchoo vacchoo
sanga midatthaanee acchoo vacchoo.
90.meaning: In the sacrifice of the greatly famous Mahabali, when Shukra objected, saying 'this giving is not fitting,' the Lord who poked out Shukra's eye with a blade of grass — O Lord with the discus in your hand, accho vaccho! O Lord who holds the conch in your left hand, accho vaccho!

91.translit:
ennidhu maayam ennappan aRindhilan
munnaiya vaNNamee koNduaLa vaayenna
mannu namusiyai vaanil suzhatriya
minnu mudiyanee acchoo vacchoo
veengada vaaNanee acchoo vacchoo.
91.meaning: 'What magic is this? My lord does not understand it' — when Mahabali said, 'measure out as before,' the Lord who whirled the steadfast Namuchi up into the sky — O Lord with the gleaming crown, accho vaccho! O Lord who dwells at Venkatam, accho vaccho!

92.translit:
kaNda kadalum malaiyum ulageezhum
muNdatthuk kaatraa mugilvaNNaa oovenru
iNdaich chadaimudi eesan irakkoLLa
maNdai niRaitthaanee acchoo vacchoo
maarvil maRuvanee acchoo vacchoo.
92.meaning: O cloud-hued Lord whom the seen oceans and mountains and the seven worlds could not satisfy — when Shiva, the lord with matted, garland-bound locks, came begging crying 'Oh!', the Lord who filled his skull-bowl — accho vaccho! O Lord with the mark upon your chest, accho vaccho!

93.translit:
thunniya peririuL soozhndhuula kaimooda
manniya naanmaRai mutrum maRaindhida
pinniv vulaginil peririuL neenga anru
annama thaanaanee acchoo vacchoo
arumaRai thandhaanee acchoo vacchoo.
93.meaning: When dense great darkness gathered and enveloped the world, and the enduring four Vedas were wholly hidden, that the great darkness might depart from this world, the Lord who then became the swan (Hamsa) — accho vaccho! O Lord who gave back the precious Vedas, accho vaccho!

94.translit:
nacchuvaar munniRkum naaraayaNan thannai
acchoo varugavenru aaycchayu raitthana
macchaNi maadap pudhuvaikoon pattansol
nacchalum paaduvaar neeLvisum paaLvaree.
94.meaning: This is the song of Pattan, the lord of Puduvai with its lofty storeyed mansions, recounting how the cowherd woman called out 'accho, come!' to Narayana, who stands before those who long for him. Those who lovingly sing these verses will rule the vast high heaven.

95.translit:
vattu niduvee vaLarginra maaNikka
mottu nunaiyil muLaikkinra mutheepool
sottuc chottennath thuLikkath thuLikkaen
kuttanvandhu ennaip puRambulguvaan
koovindhan ennaip puRambulguvaan.
95.meaning: Like a pearl sprouting at the tip of a bud growing upon a ruby, dripping drop by drop, my little one comes and embraces me from behind — Govinda embraces me from behind.

96.translit:
kinginikatti kiRikatti kaiyinil
kangaNa mittuk kazhutthil thodarkatti
thankaNath thaalee sadhiraa nadandhuvandhu
enkaNNan ennaip puRambulguvaan
empiraan ennaip puRambulguvaan.
96.meaning: With anklet-bells tied on, a charm fastened, and bangles upon his wrists, a chain tied at his neck, walking gracefully in his own way, my Kannan comes and embraces me from behind — my Lord embraces me from behind.

97.translit:
katthak kadhitthuk kidandha perunjelvam
otthup porundhikkoNdu uNNaadhu maNNaaLvaan
kotthuth thalaivan kudikedath thoonriya
atthanvandhu ennaip puRambulguvaan
aayarka LeRuen puRambulguvaan.
97.meaning: The lord who, ruling the earth without himself enjoying the great wealth that lay heaped and resounding, but appropriating it, appeared so that the lineage of the many-headed chief (Kamsa) would perish — that father comes and embraces me from behind; the bull among the cowherds embraces me from behind.

98.translit:
naandhaka meendhiya nambi saraNenru
thaazhndha thananjayaR kaagi tharaNiyil
veendharka Lutka visayan maNitthiNthee
oorndhavan ennaip puRambulguvaan
umbarkoon ennaip puRambulguvaan.
98.meaning: Wielding the sword Nandaka, for the sake of Arjuna who bowed saying 'O perfect one, you are my refuge,' the Lord who drove Vijaya's sturdy jewelled chariot on the earth so that the kings trembled — he embraces me from behind; the king of the celestials embraces me from behind.

99.translit:
veNkalap patthiram katti viLaiyaadi
kaNpala seydha karundhazhaik kaavinkeezh
paNpala paadippal laaNdisaippa paNdu
maNpala koNdaan puRambulguvaan
vaamanan ennaip puRambulguvaan.
99.meaning: Playing with a bell-metal leaf-ornament tied on, beneath the dark, leafy grove made beautiful in many places, while many tunes were sung and many 'pallandu' (long-life) praises resounded — the Lord who long ago took all the earth embraces me from behind; Vamana embraces me from behind.

100.translit:
satthira meendhith thaniyoru maaNiyaay
utthara veedhiyil ninra oruvanai
katthiriyar kaaNak kaaNimutrumkoNda
patthiraagaaran puRambulguvaan
paaraLan thaanen puRambulguvaan.
100.meaning: As a solitary young brahmachari holding a parasol, the unique one who stood at the northern altar, who — while the kshatriyas looked on — took the whole of the land, that handsome-formed Lord embraces me from behind; he who measured the earth embraces me from behind.

101.translit:
potthavuralaik kavizhtthuadhan meeleeRi
thitthitha paalum thadaavinil veNNeyum
metthath thiruvayi raara vizhungiya
atthanvandhu ennaip puRambulguvaan
aazhiyaan ennaip puRambulguvaan.
101.meaning: Overturning the hollow mortar and climbing upon it, the Lord who swallowed deeply, until his sacred belly was full, the sweet milk and the butter in the pot — that father comes and embraces me from behind; he who holds the discus embraces me from behind.

102.translit:
mootthavai kaaNa mudhumaNaR kunreeRi
kootthuuvan thaadik kuzhalaal isaipaadi
vaaytha maRaiyoor vaNanga imaiyavar
eetthavandhu ennaip puRambulguvaan
empiraan ennaip puRambulguvaan.
102.meaning: While the elders watched, climbing the old sand-hill, dancing joyfully and playing music on the flute, while the worthy Vedic brahmins bowed and the celestials praised — he comes and embraces me from behind; my Lord embraces me from behind.

103.translit:
kaRpagak kaavu karudhiya kaadhalikku
ippozhudhu eevadhenru indhiran kaavinil
niRpana seydhu nilaatthigazh mutratthuL
uytthavan ennaip puRambulguvaan
umbarkoon ennaip puRambulguvaan.
103.meaning: For the beloved (Satyabhama) who desired the Karpaka grove, saying 'I shall give it now,' performing his deeds in Indra's garden and bringing it into the moonlit courtyard — the Lord who carried it off embraces me from behind; the king of the celestials embraces me from behind.

104.translit:
aaycchiyan raazhip piraanpuRam pulgiya
veeytthadan thoaLisol vittusitthan magizhndhu
eetthatha thamizhivai eeraindhum vallavar
vaaythanin makkaLaip petru magizhvaree.
104.meaning: These are the ten sweet Tamil verses that Vishnuchittan joyfully composed, in the words of the cowherd woman with full bamboo-like shoulders who embraced the Lord of the discus from behind. Those who can master them will obtain worthy children and rejoice.

105.translit:
mecchoodhu sangam idatthaan nalveeyoodhi
poycchoodhil thootra poRaiyudai mannarkkaay
patthoor peRaadhuanru paaradham kaiseydha
atthoodhan appoocchi kaattuginraan ammanee appoocchi kaattuginraan.
105.meaning: He who holds the praised, blown conch in his left hand, who played the good flute, who for the sake of the forbearing kings (the Pandavas) defeated by deceitful dice — when not even ten towns were granted — waged the Bharata war as their messenger: that messenger Lord is showing the bogey-man! O mother, he is showing the bogey-man!

106.translit:
malaipuraidhoaL mannavarum maaradharum matrum
palarkulaiya nootruvarum pattazhiya
paartthansilaivaLaiyath thiNtheermeel munninra
sengaN alavalaivandhu appoocchi kaattuginraan ammanee appoocchi kaattuginraan.
106.meaning: So that the kings with mountain-like shoulders and the great charioteers and many others trembled, and the hundred (Kauravas) fell destroyed, the red-eyed mischievous one who stood in front upon the sturdy chariot while Arjuna bent his bow — he comes showing the bogey-man! O mother, he is showing the bogey-man!

107.translit:
kaayumneer pukkuk kadambeeRi
kaaLiyan theeyapaNatthilsilambaarkkap paayndhaadi
veeying kuzhaloodhi vitthaganaayninra
aayanvandhu appoocchi kaattuginraan ammanee appoocchi kaattuginraan.
107.meaning: Entering the seething water and climbing the kadamba tree, leaping and dancing on the evil hoods of Kaliya so that his anklets rang, playing the sweet bamboo flute and standing there as a master — that cowherd comes showing the bogey-man! O mother, he is showing the bogey-man!

108.translit:
iruttil piRandhupooy eezhaival laayar
maruttaith thavirpitthu vanjagan maaLap
purattiann naaLengaL poompattuk koNda
arattanvandhu appoocchi kaattuginraan ammanee appoocchi kaattuginraan.
108.meaning: Born in the darkness and then going to the simple, strong cowherds, removing their confusion, and so that the deceitful one (Kamsa) perished overturning him, the rogue who on that day seized our fine silk garments — he comes showing the bogey-man! O mother, he is showing the bogey-man!

109.translit:
seeppooNda saadu sidhaRi
thirudineykku aappooNdu nandhan manaivi kadaithaambaal
soappooNdu thuLLith thudikka
anru aappooNdaan appoocchi kaattuginraan ammanee appoocchi kaattuginraan.
109.meaning: Shattering the cart that was yoked, and for stealing butter being bound — when Nanda's wife (Yashoda) bound him with the churning rope so that, struck, he jumped and trembled — he who was tied up that day is showing the bogey-man! O mother, he is showing the bogey-man!

0.translit:
vaazhi parakaalan vaazhi kalikanRi,
vaazhi kuRaiyaloor vaazhvEndan, - vaazhiyarO
maayOnai vaaLvaliyaal mandiram koL mangaiyar kOn
tooyOn sudar maana vEl.
0.meaning: Long live Parakalan! Long live Kalikanri! Long live the reigning lord who flourishes at Kuraiyalur! May he live long - the pure-hearted king of the Mangai folk, who by the might of his sword obtained the sacred mantra from the mysterious Lord (Tirumal), he of the radiant, proud spear.

1.translit:
seeraar tiruvezhu kooTRiruk kai ennum sendamizhaal,
aaraa amudan kudandaip piraan Ran adiyiNaik keezh,
Eraar maRaip poruL ellaa meduttu iv vulaguyyavE
sEraamaR sonna aruLmaari paadam tuNai namakkE.
1.meaning: Through the pure Tamil work called the glorious Tiruvezhukutrirukkai, Tirumangai Alwar - the cloud of grace - set forth at the feet of the Lord of Kudandai, the insatiable nectar, all the beautiful inner truths of the Vedas, that this world might be saved, expounding them without confusion. His feet are our sole refuge.

2.translit:
oru pEr undi iru malart tavisil,
oru muRai ayanai eenRanai,
oru muRai iru sudar meedinil iyangaa, mum madiL
ilangai iru kaal vaLaiya, oru silai
onRiya eer eyiR Razhal vaay vaaLiyil aTTanai,
moovadi naanilam vENdi,
muppuri noolodu maan uri ilangum
maarvinil, iru piRappu oru maaN aagi,
oru muRai eer adi, moovula kaLandaanai,
naaR Risai nadunga anjiRaip paRavai
ERi, naal vaay mum madat iru sevi
oru tani vEzhat tarandaiyai, oru naaL
iru neer maduvuL teerttanai mut tee
naan maRai aivagai vELvi, aRu tozhil
andaNar vaNangum tanmaiyai, aim pulan
agattinuL seRuttu, naangudan adakki
muk kuNat tirandavai agaRRi, onRinil
onRi ninRu, aang iru piRappu aRuppOr
aRiyum tanmaiyai, muk kaN naaR ROL
aivaay aravOdu aaRu podi sadaiyOn
aRivarun tanmaip perumaiyuL ninRanai,
Ezhula geyiTRinil koNdanai, kooRiya
aRu suvaip payanum aayinai, sudar vidum
aimpadai angaiyuL amarndanai, sundara
naaR ROL mun neer vaNNa, nin eer adi
onRiya manattaal, oru madi mugattu
mangaiyar iruvarum malarana, angaiyil
mup pozhudum varuda aRi tuyil amarndanai,
neRimuRai naal vagai varuNamum aayinai,
mEdagum aim perum bootamum neeyE,
aRupadam muralum koondal kaaraNam
Ezh vidai adangach cheRRanai, aRuvagaich
samayamum aRivaru nilaiyinai, aimpaal
Odiyai aagat tiruttinai, aRamudal
naanga vaiyaay moorti moonRaay
iruvagaip payanaay onRaay virindu
ninRanai, kunRaa madu malarch chOlai
vaN kodip padappai, varu punal ponni
maa maNi alaikkum, sennel oN kazhanit
tikazhvana muduTТa, kaRpOr purisaik
kanaka maaLigai, nimir kodi visumbil
iLam piRai tuvakkum, selvam malgu ten
tirukkudandai, andaNar mandira mozhiyudan
vaNanga, aadaravamaLiyil aRi tuyil
amarnda parama, nin adiyiNai paNivan
varum idar agala maaT RO vinaiyE.
2.meaning: On the lotus-seat that rose from your single navel, once you begot Brahma. Once, as the three-walled Lanka's two flanks bent down, you destroyed Ravana with an arrow from your single bow, an arrow with two flaming fanged tips, while the two luminaries (sun and moon) above ceased their course. Begging for three paces of the four-quartered earth, twice-born and great upon your chest gleaming the sacred thread and deerskin, in one stride of two feet you measured the three worlds. So that the four directions trembled, you mounted the bird of beautiful wings (Garuda) and on one day, in the deep waters, you delivered the lone elephant in distress, with its drooping mouth, threefold rut and two ears. You are the very nature worshipped by the brahmins of the three fires, the four Vedas, the five sacrifices, the six duties. You are the nature known to those who, restraining the five senses within, subduing the four (inner faculties), driving out the two of the three gunas, abiding fixed in the One, thereby cut off the two births (escape rebirth). You stand in a greatness whose nature is hard to know even for the three-eyed, four-shouldered one (Shiva) bearing a five-mouthed serpent and the six-fold matted locks (Ganga). You held the seven worlds in your tusk; you became the fruit of the six declared tastes; you bear in your fair palm the five flaming weapons. O handsome four-shouldered one, hued like the sea, with a mind fixed on your two feet, the two ladies (Lakshmi and Bhumi) with single-moon faces and lotus-like forms stroking you at the three times of day, you repose in your mystic slumber. You became the four orderly varnas; you yourself are the five great exalted elements. For the sake of her whose hair, where the bee with six legs hums, you subdued and slew the seven bulls. You are the state hard for the six religions to know. You set upon your body the lady of fivefold tresses (Lakshmi). Being the four beginning with dharma, the three forms (murtis), the twofold fruit, you stood as the One who spread out into all. In southern Tirukkudandai - rich with groves of unfading honeyed blossoms, gardens of lush creepers, fields where the inflowing Ponni river washes great gems, bright with paddy fields, walled with stone, golden mansions whose soaring banners brush the young crescent in the sky, abounding in wealth - where the brahmins worship with mantra-words, O Supreme One reposing in mystic slumber upon the spreading serpent-couch, I bow at your two feet. Drive away, then, my coming sorrows and my karma.

0.translit:
muLLich chezhu malaroo taaraan muLai matiyam
koLLikku en uLLam kotiyaamee vaLLal
tiruvaaLan seerk kaliyan kaark kaliyai vetti
maruvaaLan tantaan madal.
0.meaning: Lest my heart should burn as if scorched by the fire of the crescent moon while he does not grant me the garland of the rich blossoms of the thorny mullai, the generous Tirumangai Alwar - lord of fortune, of glorious fame, Kaliyan - cut away the dark age of Kali and gave forth this Madal (the song of love-longing).

1.translit:
kaar aar varaik kongai kaN aar kadal udukkai
seer aar sudarch chutti sengaluzhip peer aaRRup
peer aara maarvil peru maa mazhaik kuuntal
neer aara veeli nilamangai ennum ip
paaroor solappatta muuNRu anRee am muuNRum
1.meaning: She whose breasts are the cloud-capped mountains, whose girdle is the eye-filling wide ocean, whose bright forehead-jewel is the radiant sun, whose great flowing river is the red turbid stream, whose chest-ornament necklace shines, whose tresses are the great dark rain-clouds, and whose hedge-boundary is the water-filled (sea) - she is called the Lady Earth. Are there not three goals proclaimed by the people of this world? Those very three -

2.translit:
aaraayil taanee aRam poruL inpam enRu
aar aar ivaRRinidai atanai eytuvaar
2.meaning: if we examine them, they are righteousness (dharma), wealth (artha), and pleasure (kama). Among these, who indeed are they that attain that (final goal)?

3.translit:
seer aar iru kalaiyum eytuvar sikkena maRRu
aaraanum uNdu enpaar enpatu taan atuvum
ooraamai anRee ulakattaar sollum sol
ooraamai aam aaRu atu uraikkeen keeLaamee
kaar aar puravi eezh puuNda tani aazhi
teer aar niRai katiroon maNdalattaik keeNdu pukku
aaraa amutam angu eyti atil ninRum
3.meaning: They surely attain the two glorious arts (of this world and the next). Yet some say there is still another goal - but what they say is itself born of not knowing; it is the ignorant speech of worldly folk. I shall tell the way that this is ignorance - listen. Piercing through and entering the orb of the radiant sun, whose single-wheeled chariot is yoked with seven dark steeds, attaining there the insatiable nectar (of liberation), and from there -

4.translit:
vaaraatu ozhivatu onRu uNdee? atu niRka
eer aar muyal vittuk kaakkaip pin poovatee
eer aar iLamulaiyeer en tanakku uRRatu taan
kaar aar kuzhal eduttuk katti katir mulaiyai
4.meaning: is there any such soul that never returns again (to the cycle of birth)? Let that be. To leave the beautiful hare and chase after the crow (to forsake the greater for the lesser) - is it fitting? O maidens of lovely young breasts, hear what has befallen me. Binding up my cloud-dark tresses, and over my radiant bosom -

5.translit:
vaar aara veekki maNi meekalai tirutti
aar aar ayil veel kaN anjanattin nooRu aNintu
seer aar chezhum pantu koNdu adiyaa ninReen naan
neer aar kamalam pool sengaN maal enRu oruvan
paaroorkaL ellaam makizha paRai kaRanga
seer aar kudam iraNdu eenti chezhun teruvee
5.meaning: I tied tight the breast-band, set straight the gem-studded girdle, lined my sharp spear-like eyes with collyrium powder, and stood playing with a fine rich ball - when a certain one, the dark Lord with red eyes like the water-borne lotus, while all the people of the world rejoiced and drums resounded, bearing two splendid pots, along the fair street -

6.translit:
aar aar enach cholli aadum atu kaNdu
eer aar iLamulaiyaar annaiyarum ellaarum
vaaraayoo enRaarkkuch chenReen en valvinaiyaal
kaar aar maNi niRamum kai vaLaiyum kaaNeen naan
aaraanum sollirRum koLLeen aRivu azhintu
6.meaning: calling out 'who, who?' and dancing - seeing that, the maidens of lovely young breasts and the mothers and all of them said 'will you not come?', and by my cruel fate I went toward them. (Since then) I cannot see (the brightness of) my sapphire-dark complexion nor my bangles upon my hands; my wits are lost and I heed nothing that anyone says.

7.translit:
tiiraa udampodu peeduRuveen kaNdu irangi
eer aar kiLik kiLavi em anai taan vantu ennaich
seer aar chezhum puzhutik kaappittu seng kuRinjit
7.meaning: Seeing me distraught with a body that finds no cure, and taking pity, my mother, whose speech is sweet like a lovely parrot's, came and applied the protective fine sacred dust upon me, and with the red kurinji -

8.translit:
taar aar naRu maalaich chaattaRkut taan pinnum
neeraatana onRu neerntaaL atanaalum
8.meaning: and, to adorn me further with a fragrant garland of (kurinji) blossoms, she even consented to do something unbefitting; yet even by that -

9.translit:
tiiraatu en sintai nooy tiiraatu en peeduRavu
vaaraatu maamai atu kaNdu maRRu aangee
aaraanum muutu aRiyum ammanaimaar solluvaar
paaroor solappadum kattuppaduttireel
aaraanum meyppaduvan enRaar atu keettu
9.meaning: the sickness of my mind would not cease, my bewilderment would not cease, and my (former golden) complexion would not return. Seeing this, certain elderly knowing women among the matrons said, 'If you perform the divination-rite (kattu) that is known among the people of the world, the one (who has caused this) will surely be revealed.' Hearing that -

10.translit:
kaar aar kuzhal koNdai kattuvichchi katteeRi
seer aar chuLakil sila nel pidittu eRiyaa
veeraa vitirvitiraa mey siliraa kai moovaa
peer aayiram udaiyaan enRaaL peyartteeyum
10.meaning: the soothsayer-woman with cloud-dark coiled tresses, possessed by the spirit, took a few grains of paddy in a fine winnowing-fan and cast them, then sweating, trembling and shivering, her body bristling and smelling her hand, she declared, 'He is the One who bears a thousand names.' And again -

11.translit:
kaar aar tirumeeni kaattinaaL kaiyatuvum
seer aar valampuriyee enRaaL tiruttuzhaayat
11.meaning: she showed (a vision of) his cloud-dark sacred form, and as for what is in his hand, she said, 'It is the glorious right-spiralled conch (Valampuri).' And of the sacred tulasi -

12.translit:
taar aar naRu maalai katturaittaaL katturaiyaa
neer eetum anjeelmin num makaLai nooy seytaan
aaraanum allan aRinteen avanai naan
kuur aar veel kaNNeer umakku aRiyak kuurukenoo?
aaraal iv vaiyam adi aLappuNdatu taan
aaraal ilangai podi podiyaa viizhntatu maRRu
aaraalee kal maari kaattatu taan aazhi neer
12.meaning: she spoke of the fragrant garland (he wears); and having pronounced the oracle she said: 'Fear nothing at all; he who has caused sickness to your daughter is no ordinary one - I have known him. O women with sharp spear-like eyes, shall I tell you so that you may know? By whom was this world measured with his foot? By whom did Lanka fall, crushed to powder? And by whom was the (people sheltered from the) hail-storm of rain protected? Of the ocean-waters -'

13.translit:
aaraal kadaintidappattatu avan kaaNmin
uur aa nirai meeyttu ulaku ellaam uNdu umizhntum
aaraata tanmaiyanaay aangu orunaaL aayppaadi
seer aar kalai alkul seer adich chentuvar vaay
vaar aar vanamulaiyaaL mattu aarap paRRikkoNdu
eer aar idai noova ettanaiyoor pootum aay
seer aar tayir kadaintu veNNey tiraNdatanai
veer aar nutal madavaaL veeRu oor kalattu ittu
naar aar uRi eeRRi nanku amaiya vaittatanaip
poor aar veel kaN madavaaL poontanaiyum poy uRakkam
ooraatavan pool uRangi aRivu uRRu
taar aar tadam tooLkaL uL aLavum kain niitti
aaraata veNNey vizhungi aruku irunta
13.meaning: By whom was it churned? See - it is he! He who grazed the village cattle herds, who swallowed all the worlds and spat them out again, yet remained unsatisfied (in his sport). There, one day in the cowherd village (Ayarpadi), a woman with a girdle of fine cloth on her hips, beautiful feet, red coral lips, breasts bound with a band, took firm hold of the churning-staff and, her lovely waist aching, for a long while churned the rich curd; and the fair-browed woman, sweat upon her forehead, put the gathered butter into a separate vessel and set it well-arranged upon the rope-sling (uri). Until that woman with spear-sharp eyes had gone away, he lay feigning sleep like one who knows nothing, and (when she left) regaining alertness, he stretched his garlanded broad arms in up to their full reach, gulped down the insatiable butter, and (returning) lay beside -

14.translit:
moor aar kudam urutti mun kidanta taanattee
ooraatavan pool kidantaanaik kaNdu avaLum
vaaraat taan vaittatu kaaNaaL vayiRu adittu ingu
14.meaning: having rolled away the buttermilk pot, he lay there in the very place he had lain before, like one who knows nothing; and seeing him so, she too, not finding the butter she had set away, beat her belly here (in dismay) -

15.translit:
aar aar pukutuvaar aiyar ivar allaal
neer aam itu seytiir enRu oor nedung kayiRRaal
uuraarkaL ellaarum kaaNa uraloodee
tiiraa veguLiyaL aay sikkena aarttu adippa
aaraa vayiRRinoodu aaRRaataan anRiyum
15.meaning: 'Who else would enter (here) except this little lord? You yourself have done this!' she said, and with a long rope, before all the village folk, in unabating anger she bound him tightly to the mortar and beat him; though he, with his never-satisfied belly, could not bear it - and besides -

16.translit:
neer aar nedung kayattaich chenRu alaikka ninRu urappi
oor aayiram paNa vem koo iyal naakattai
vaaraay enakku enRu maRRu atan mattakattu
seer aar tiruvadiyaal paayntaan tan siitaikku
16.meaning: going to the deep pool (of the Yamuna) and stirring its waters, he stood and roared, and to the fierce thousand-hooded royal serpent (Kaliya) he said, 'Come to me!' and leaped upon the top of its head with his glorious sacred feet. For the sake of his Sita -

17.translit:
neer aavan enRu oor nisaasari taan vantaaLai
kuur aarnta vaaLaal kodi muukkum kaatu iraNdum
iiraa viduttu avatku muuttoonai vem narakam
17.meaning: when a demoness (Surpanakha), thinking 'I shall become his match,' came, with a sharp-edged sword he cut off her creeper-like nose and both her ears and sent her away; and her elder brother (Ravana), to fierce hell -

18.translit:
seeraa vakaiyee silai kunittaan sentuvar vaay
vaar aar vanamulaiyaal vaiteevi kaaraNamaa
eer aar tadan tooL iraavaNanai iir aintu
18.meaning: so that he should not reach it (be saved), he bent his bow. For the sake of Vaidehi (Sita) of the red coral lips and band-bound beautiful breasts, Ravana of the lovely broad shoulders - his twice-five (ten heads) -

19.translit:
seer aar siram aRuttu cheRRu ukanta sengaN maal
poor aar nedu veeloon ponpeyaroon aakattai
kuur aarnta vaL ukiraal kiiNdu kudal maalai
19.meaning: his glorious heads he cut off, and rejoiced in destroying (him) - the red-eyed dark Lord. The one (Hiranyakashipu) named after gold (Hiranya), bearer of the long war-spear - his chest the Lord tore open with his keen sharp claws, making a garland of his entrails -

20.translit:
siir aar tiru maarbinmeelE kaTTi seng kuruthi
sooraak kidanthaanaik kungkumath thooL koTTi
aaraa ezhunthaan ari uruvaay anRiyum
20.meaning: He pinned upon his radiant, glorious chest the demon Hiranya, and as the red blood flowed without ceasing, slapping his kumkum-smeared shoulders, the Lord rose up unsatisfied in his man-lion (Narasimha) form. And besides that —

21.translit:
peer vaaman aakiya kaalaththu muuvadi maN
thaaraay enakku enRu veeNdi salaththinaal
niir eeRRu ulagu ellaam ninRu aLanthaan maavaliyai
aaraatha pooril asurargaLum thaanumaay
kaar aar varai naTTu naagam kayiRu aaga
peeraamal thaangik kadainthaan thiruth thuzhaayth
21.meaning: In the time when he became the one named Vamana, begging 'Give me three feet of land,' and by his cunning receiving it with water poured into his hand, he stood and measured all the worlds, taking them from Mahabali. And in the unending war with the demons, planting the dark mountain (Mandara) as a churning rod and the serpent (Vasuki) as the rope, holding it unmoving, he churned the ocean — he of the sacred tulasi.

22.translit:
thaar aarntha maarvan thada maal varai poolum
poor aanai poygaivaayk kooTpaTTu ninRu alaRi
niir aar malark kamalam koNdu oor nedung kaiyaal
naaraayaNaa oo maNivaNNaa naagaNaiyaay
vaaraay en aar idarai niikkaay ena veguNdu
22.meaning: He of the garland-laden chest — when the warrior elephant (Gajendra), like a great dark mountain, was seized at the edge of the pond and stood crying out, taking up a water-filled lotus blossom in its one long trunk and calling 'O Narayana! O sapphire-hued one! O you who rest on the serpent! Come, remove my grievous distress!' — then in wrath,

23.translit:
thiiraatha siiRRaththaal senRu iraNdu kuuRu aaga
iiraa athanai idar kadinthaan em perumaan
peer aayiram udaiyaan peeyp peNdiir num magaLaith
thiiraa nooy seythaan ena uraiththaaL sikkena maRRu
23.meaning: With unappeasable fury he went and split the crocodile in two, tearing it apart, and so banished its (Gajendra's) distress — he, our Lord. He who bears a thousand names — 'O demoness-women, upon your daughter he has wrought an incurable malady,' so spoke (the foster-mother) firmly. And further,

24.translit:
aaraanum allaamai keeTTu engaL ammanaiyum
poor aar veel kaNNiir avan aagil puun thuzhaay
thaaraathu ozhiyumee than adicchi allaLee maRRu
aaraanum allanee enRu ozhinthaaL naan avanaik
24.meaning: Hearing that it was none other than him, our mother too (said): 'O you (women) with eyes like battling spears! If it is he, would he fail to give her his flowering tulasi? Is she not his own handmaid? And is he himself not someone (worthy)?' — so saying, she let the matter rest. As for me, him —

25.translit:
kaar aar thirumeeni kaNdathuvee kaaraNamaa
peeraa pithaRRaa thiritharuvan pinnaiyum
25.meaning: Having once beheld his cloud-dark sacred form — that very seeing being the cause — I wander about, ceaselessly babbling and unable to leave off. And yet further,

26.translit:
iiraap puguthalum iv udalaith thaN vaadai
sooraa maRukkum vagai aRiyeen suuzh kuzhalaar
26.meaning: As the cool north wind enters and pierces through, it torments this body until it droops; I do not know the way (to bear it). O women with flowing tresses —

27.translit:
aaraanum eesuvar ennum athan pazhiyai
vaaraamal kaappathaRku vaaLaa irunthozhintheen
vaaraay mada nenjee vanthu maNivaNNan
27.meaning: To guard against the blame — that some or other would mock me — I have remained silent and idle. Come, O foolish heart! Come, the sapphire-hued one —

28.translit:
siir aar thiruth thuzhaay maalai namakku aruLi
thaaraan tharum enRu iraNdaththil onRu athanai
aaraanum onnaathaar keeLaamee sonnakkaal
aaraayumeelum paNi keeTTu athu anRu enilum
pooraathu ozhiyaathee poonthidu nii enReeRku
kaar aar kadal vaNNan pin poona nenjamum
vaaraathee ennai maRanthathu thaan valvineeyeen
28.meaning: Whether he will graciously bestow on us his glorious sacred tulasi garland or will not — saying so, choose one of those two answers, telling it so that no ill-disposed person overhears; if he deliberates and gives his command, and even if the answer is 'no,' do not fail — go without ceasing! — so I bade my heart. But the heart that went after the ocean-dark-hued one came not back, and forgot me, wretched one of cruel fate that I am.

29.translit: uuraar ugappathee aayineen maRRu enakku ingu
29.meaning: I have become only the object of the townsfolk's mockery (gossip). Moreover, for me here —

30.translit:
aaraayvaar illai azhalvaay mezhugu pool
niiraay urugum en aavi nedung kaNgaL
30.meaning: There is none to inquire after me. Like wax at the edge of a flame, my life melts away into water; my long eyes —

31.translit:
uuraar uRangilum thaan uRangaa uththaman than
peer aayinavee pithaRRuvan pinnaiyum
31.meaning: Even when the townsfolk sleep, he does not sleep (ever vigilant) — that noblest one; his very names I babble. And yet further,

32.translit:
kaar aar kadal poolum kaamaththar aayinaar
aaree pollaamai aRivaar? athu niRka
aaraanum aathaanum allaL avaL kaaNiir
vaar aar vanamulai vaasavathaththai enRu
aaraanum sollappaduvaaL avaLum than
32.meaning: Those who have come to possess a love vast as the cloud-laden ocean — who can know what evil (befalls them)? Let that be. Look — she was no ordinary woman: she was the one spoken of by all as Vasavadatta of the beautiful bodice-bound breasts; she too,

33.translit:
peer aayam ellaam ozhiya perun theruvee
thaar aar thadanthooL thaLaik kaalan pin poonaaL
uuraar igazhnthidappaTTaaLee? maRRu enakku ingu
33.meaning: Forsaking all her great retinue, she went down the broad street after the garland-laden, broad-shouldered man with fetters on his feet (Upagupta). Was she (then) scorned by the townsfolk? Moreover, for me here —

34.translit:
aaraanum kaRpippaar naayagaree? naan avanaik
kaar aar thirumeeni kaaNum aLavum pooy
siir aar thiruveengadamee thirukkoova
luuree mathiL kacchi uuragamee peeragamee
peeraa marutu iRuththaan veLLaRaiyee veqkaavee
peer aali thaNkaal naRaiyuur thiruppuliyuur
aaraamam suuzhntha arangam kaNamangai
34.meaning: Are those who would instruct me (in propriety) my masters (that I should obey them)? I, until I behold his cloud-dark sacred form, will go on — to glorious Tiruvenkatam, to Tirukkovalur, to Uragam within walled Kanchi, to Peragam; to Vellarai (and the Lord) who broke the marudu trees without budging, to Vehka, to great Ali, to Tankal, Naraiyur, Tiruppuliyur, to Srirangam girt with groves, to Kanamangai —

35.translit:
kaar aar maNi niRak kaNNanuur viNNagaram
siir aar kaNapuram seeRai thiruvazhunthuur
kaar aar kudanthai kadigai kadalmallai
eer aar pozhil suuzh idaventhai niirmalai
siir aarum maaliruncoolai thirumooguur
35.meaning: To Kannanur of the cloud-dark sapphire hue, to Vinnagaram; to glorious Kanapuram, Serai, Tiruvazhundur; to cloud-laden Kudandai, Kadigai, Kadalmallai (Mahabalipuram); to Idaventhai girt with lovely groves, to Nirmalai; to glory-filled Malirunchozhai (Azhagar Koyil), to Tirumogur —

36.translit:
paaroor pugazhum vatari vadamathurai
uur aaya ellaam ozhiyaamee naan avanai
oor aanai kombu osiththu oor aanai kooLviduththa
siiraanai sengaN nediyaanai theen thuzhaayth
thaaraanai thaamarai pool kaNNaanai eN arunj siir
36.meaning: To Badari praised by all the earth, to North Mathura — leaving out none of these holy towns, I (will go to) him: him of glory who snapped the tusk of one elephant (Kuvalayapida) and released another elephant (Gajendra) from its peril; the red-eyed, tall Lord; he who wears the honeyed tulasi garland, he of lotus-like eyes, of measureless glory —

37.translit: peer aayiramum pithaRRi perun theruvee
37.meaning: Babbling all his thousand names, down the broad street —

38.translit:
uuraar igazhilum uuraathu ozhiyeen naan
vaar aar puum peNNai madal
38.meaning: Even if the townsfolk scorn me, I will not refrain from riding (the madal) — I (will mount) the madal made of the tall, beautiful palmyra palm.

0.translit:
ponnulakil vaanavarum poomakaLum poRRi seyyum
naannutaleer nambi naRaiyoorar mannulakil
ennilaimai kaNDum irangaarE yaamaakil
mannum maDal oorvan vandhu.
0.meaning: O fair-browed one! The perfect Lord of Naraiyur, whom the celestials in the golden world and the Goddess Earth (Poomakal) praise and adore. If even after seeing my plight he in this enduring world shows no compassion toward me, then I shall come and ride the enduring madal (the palmyra-stalk of public love-protest).

1.translit:
manniya pal poRi sEr aayira vaay vaaL aravin
senni maNik kuDumith theyvac chuDar naDuvuL
manni an naagaththu aNaimEl Or maa malai pOl
minnum maNi makara kuNDalangaL vil veesa
thunniya thaaragaiyin pEr oLi sEr aakaasam
ennum vithaanaththin keezhaal iru suDarai
1.meaning: In the very midst of the divine radiance upon the jewelled crests on the thousand mouths of the bright, many-spotted enduring serpent — reclining there upon that serpent-couch like a great mountain, his shining jewelled makara ear-rings casting forth light like a bow, beneath the canopy that is the sky bearing the great brilliance of the thronging stars — he made the twin luminaries (the sun and moon),

2.translit:
mannum viLakku aaka ERRi maRi kaDalum
pannu thiraik kavari veesa nilamangai
2.meaning: into enduring lamps, kindling them; and while the surging ocean fanned him with its spreading waves as a chowrie, the Goddess Earth (Nilamangai),

3.translit:
thannai muna naaL aLaviTTa thaamarai pOl
manniya sEvaDiyai vaan iyangu thaaragai meen
ennum malarp piNaiyal Eyndha mazhaik koondhal
3.meaning: with her rain-cloud-like dark tresses fitted with a garland of flowers that are the stars that move across the heavens, (worshipped) his enduring lotus-red sacred feet — the feet that long ago measured her, the lotus-feet,

4.translit:
thennan uyar poruppum theyva vaDamalaiyum
ennum ivaiyE mulaiyaa vaDivu amaindha
anna naDaiya aNangE aDi iNaiyaith
4.meaning: that divine maiden whose form was shaped with the lofty southern mountain (Podiyil/Malaya) and the divine northern mountain (Meru) as her two breasts, she of the swan-like gait — (worshipped) the pair of his feet,

5.translit:
thannuDaiya angaikaLaal thaan thaDava thaan kiDandhu Or
unniya yOgaththu uRakkam thalaikkoNDa
pinnai than naabi valayaththup pEr oLi sEr
manniya thaamarai maa malar poottu am malarmEl
munnam thisaimukanaith thaan paDaikka maRRu avanum
5.meaning: caressing them with her own fair palms. After he himself lay there absorbed in the sleep of contemplative yoga, then upon the great enduring lotus of surpassing brilliance that blossomed forth in the circle of his navel, and upon that flower, he first created the four-faced one (Brahma) of the directions; and that one in turn,

6.translit:
munnam paDaiththanan naanmaRaikaL am maRai thaan
mannum aRam poruL inbam veeDu enRu ulakil
nal neRi mEmbaTTana naangu anRE? naanginilum
6.meaning: first created the four Vedas. And those Vedas — do they not set forth in the world the four enduring good paths, namely virtue (aram/dharma), wealth (porul/artha), pleasure (inbam/kama), and liberation (veedu/moksha)? And among these four,

7.translit:
pinnaiyathu pinnaip peyartharum enbathu Or
thol neRiyai vEnDuvaar veezh kaniyum oozh ilaiyum
ennum ivaiyE nukarndhu uDalam thaam varundhi
thunnum ilaik kurambaith thunjiyum vem suDarOn
7.meaning: those who desire that ancient path which is said to be the last (liberation), and which is said to grant release at the last — they subsist on nothing but fallen fruits and decayed leaves, wearing out their bodies, dwelling in huts thatched with crowded leaves, and (others) before the fierce sun,

8.translit:
mannum azhal nukarndhum vaN thaDaththinuL kiDandhum
innathu Or thanmaiyaraay eeng uDalam viTTu ezhundhu
thol neRikkaN senRaar enappaDum sol allaal
innathu Or kaalaththu inaiyaar ithu peRRaar
ennavum kETTu aRivathu illai uLathu ennil
8.meaning: partaking of the enduring fire (in penance), and lying within rich pools of water — being of such austere natures, they leave their bodies here and depart, and beyond the mere words 'they went upon the ancient path' — that at such a time such persons attained this — no such thing have we ever heard or known. And if it be said that such a thing exists,

9.translit:
mannum kaDung kathirOn maNDalaththin nal naDuvuL
annathu Or illiyin ooDu pOy veeDu ennum
9.meaning: (it is said) that passing through a certain aperture in the very midst of the orb of the enduring fierce-rayed sun, they go to what is called liberation —

10.translit:
thol neRikkaN senRaaraic chollumingaL sollaathE
annathE pEsum aRivu il siRu manaththu aangu
annavaraik kaRpippOm yaamE athu niRka
10.meaning: those who went upon that ancient path — let someone tell me of them! But rather than speak (vainly), I myself shall instruct those of small, witless minds who keep talking only of such things. Yet let that be —

11.translit:
munnam naan sonna aRaththin vazhi muyanRa
annavar thaam kaNDeerkaL aayirak kaN vaanavar kOn
pon nakaram pukku amarar pORRi seppa pongu oLi sEr
11.meaning: those who strove along the way of virtue (dharma) which I spoke of first — behold them! Entering the golden city of the lord of the celestials, him of the thousand eyes (Indra), while the immortals utter praises, amid surging radiance,

12.translit:
kol navilum kOl arimaath thaan sumandha kOlam sEr
manniya singaasanaththinmEl vaaL neDungaN
12.meaning: upon the enduring, ornate lion-throne that is borne up by fierce, killing lions (sculpted) — (he sits), and the bright long-eyed,

13.translit:
kanniyaraal iTTa kavarip pothi avizhndhu aangu
in iLam poon thenRal iyanga marungu irundha
13.meaning: as the maidens wave the chowries, their fragrant bundles loosen, and a sweet, gentle, flower-laden southern breeze blows about — and seated nearby,

14.translit:
min anaiya nuN marungul mel iyalaar veN muRuval
munnam mukizhththa mukizh nilaa vandhu arumba
annavar tham maan nOkkam uNDu aang aNi malar sEr
pon iyal kaRpakaththin kaaDu uDuththa maaDu ellaam
manniya mandhaaram poottha madhuth thivalai
in isai vaNDu amarum sOlaivaay maalaisEr manniya maa mayil pOl koondhal mazhaith thaDangaN
14.meaning: the tender-natured women of waists slender as lightning — as their white smiles burst forth like budding moonbeams, he savours those women's doe-like glances. And there, all about, girdled by groves of golden kalpaka (wish-fulfilling) trees set with lovely flowers, where amid the spray of honey from the blossoming enduring mandara flowers sweet-humming bees gather in the bowers — (the women) with rain-cloud-broad eyes and tresses (dark) like the enduring great peacock decked at evening,

15.translit:
min iDaiyaarODum viLaiyaaDi vEnDu iDaththu
mannum maNith thalaththu maaNikka manjariyin
minnin oLi sEr paLingu viLimbu aDuththa
mannum pavaLak kaal sem pon sey maNDapaththuL
anna naDaiya arambaiyar tham kai vaLarththa
in isai yaazhp paaDal kETTu inbuRRu iru visumbil
15.meaning: sporting with those lightning-waisted ones, and wherever they please, within a hall of made of red gold with enduring coral pillars whose edges are set with crystal bright as lightning, upon an enduring floor of gems clustered with rubies — listening to the sweet music of the lute-song nurtured by the hands of the swan-gaited apsaras, delighting in it; and in the high sky,

16.translit:
mannum mazhai thavazhum vaaL nilaa neeL mathi thOy
minnin oLi sEr visumbu oorum maaLikaimEl
mannum maNi viLakkai maaTTi mazhaik kaNNaar
16.meaning: atop the mansions that ride the sky shining with lightning-radiance, where enduring rain-clouds creep and where the bright moonlight of the long crescent moon touches — kindling the enduring jewel-lamps, the rain-cloud-eyed women,

17.translit:
pannu visiththiramaap paappaDuththa paLLimEl
thunniya saalEkam soozh kathavam thaaL thiRappa
annam uzhakka nerindhu ukka vaaL neelac
chinna naRun thaathu sooDi Or mandhaaram
17.meaning: upon a couch spread with elaborately wrought, varied bedding, when the bolt is opened of the door surrounded by close-set lattice windows — (the breeze enters) wearing the bright dark-blue pollen-dust shaken loose and scattered where swans had trampled the flowers, and a mandara,

18.translit:
thunnu naRu malaraal thOL koTTi kaRpakaththin
mannu malarvaay maNi vaNDu pin thoDara
in iLam poon thenRal pukundhu eeng iLa mulaimEl
nal naRum sandhanac chERu ularththa thaangu arum seer
18.meaning: slapping its shoulders, as it were, with crowded fragrant blossoms, while jewel-like bees from the enduring blossom-mouths of the kalpaka tree follow behind — the sweet, gentle, flower-laden southern breeze enters here and dries the fine fragrant sandal-paste upon the young breasts; and (with) glory hard to bear,

19.translit:
min iDaimEl kai vaiththu irundhu Endhu iLa mulaimEl
pon arumbu aaram pulamba akam kuzhaindhu aangu
19.meaning: resting her hand upon her lightning-slender waist she sits, while upon her uplifted young breasts the necklace of golden buds laments (lies disordered), and her heart melts within;

20.translit:
inna uruvin imaiyaat tadang kaNNaar
annavar tam maan nookkam uNDu aangu aNi muRuval
in amudam maandi iruppar idu anRee (20)
20.meaning: In this very form, those women of wide unblinking eyes — partaking of their fawn-like glances and there drinking the sweet nectar of their lovely smiles, men remain content. Is this not so?

21.translit:
anna aRattin payan aavadu oN poruLum
anna thiRattadee aadalaal kaamattin (21)
21.meaning: The fruit of such righteousness (dharma), and likewise the goal of wealth (artha), are of that same nature; and so, of desire (kama) too —

22.translit:
mannum vazhimuRaiyee niRRum naam maan nookkin
anna nadaiyaar alar eesa aadavarmeel
mannum madal ooraar enbadu oor vaasagamum
ten uraiyil keettu aRivadu uNDu adanai yaam theLiyoom (22)
22.meaning: We stand firm in that abiding tradition. There is a saying that men, when women of fawn-like glances and gait raise gossip against them, ride the madal (the palmyra-stalk steed of love). We have heard this said in the southern lore (Tamil tradition), but we do not hold it clearly.

23.translit:
mannum vada neRiyee veeNDinoom veeNDaadaar
tennan podhiyil sezhuny chandhanak kuzhambin
annadu oor thanmai aRiyaadaar aayan veey (23)
23.meaning: We have sought the enduring northern way (the Sanskrit tradition). Those who desire it not — who do not know that nature like the rich sandal-paste of the Pandya's Podhiyil mountain, those who, at the cowherd's (Kannan's) flute —

24.translit:
in isai oosaikku irangaadaar maal vidaiyin
mannum maNi pulamba vaadaadaar peNNaimeel (24)
24.meaning: those who do not melt at the sweet music of its sound; those who do not wilt when the abiding bell of the great bull laments; upon the palmyra tree —

25.translit:
pinnum av anRil pedai vaaych chiRu kuralukku
unni udal urugi naiyaadaar umbarvaayt (25)
25.meaning: and again, brooding over the small cry from the mouth of that anril bird's female mate, those who do not melt in body and pine away; up in the high heavens —

26.translit:
thunnum madhi ugutta thoo nilaa neeL neruppil
tam udalam veevath thaLaraadaar kaamaveeL (26)
26.meaning: those who do not grow faint as their own bodies burn in the long fire of the pure moonlight poured down by the close-set moon; the god of love (Manmatha) —

27.translit:
mannum silaivaay malar vaaLi koottu eyya
pon nedu veedhi pugaadaar tam poo aNaimeel (27)
27.meaning: when, fitting the flower-arrow to the string of his abiding bow, he shoots — those who do not enter (run wild through) the long golden streets; upon their own flower-beds —

28.translit:
sinna malark kuzhalum algulum men mulaiyum
in iLavaadai thadava taam kaN thuyilum
pon anaiyaar pinnum thiru uRuga poor veendhan (28)
28.meaning: those gold-hued women who, while the sweet gentle breeze caresses their flower-bedecked hair, their hips, and their soft breasts, sleep peacefully — may they yet attain prosperity. (Now I tell of) the warrior king —

29.translit:
thannudaiya thaadhai paNiyaal arasu ozhindhu
pon nagaram pinnee pulamba valamkoNDu
mannum vaLanaadu kaividuttu maadhirangaL (29)
29.meaning: By his own father's command (Rama) gave up the kingdom and, with the golden city lamenting behind him, went round in respect (took leave), abandoning the abiding fertile land; through the regions (he wandered) —

30.translit:
min uruvil viN theer thirindhu veLippattu
kal niraindhu theeyndhu kazhai udaindhu kaal suzhanRu
pinnum thirai vayiRRup peeyee thirindhu ulavaa
kol navilum vem kaanattoodu kodum kadhiroon (30)
30.meaning: the sky-chariots of lightning form whirling and appearing; stones laid out and scorched, bamboo splitting, the wind swirling; and again, only ghosts roaming through the wave-like (mirage) hollows — through that murderous, fierce wilderness, the cruel-rayed sun —

31.translit:
thunnu veyil vaRutta vem paralmeel panchu adiyaal
mannan iraaman pin vaidheevi enRu uraikkum
anna nadaiya aNangu nadandhilaLee? (31)
31.meaning: upon the hot gravel parched by the dense sunshine — with her cotton-soft feet, behind king Rama, did not that goddess of fawn-like gait, she who is called Vaidehi (Sita), walk?

32.translit:
pinnum karu nedung kaN sev vaay piNai nookkin
min anaiya nuN marungul veegavadhi enRu uraikkum
kanni than in uyiraam kaadhalanaik kaaNaadhu
thannudaiya mun thoonRal koNDu eega thaan senRu aangu
annavanai nookkaadhu azhittu urappi vaaL amaruL (32)
32.meaning: And again — the maiden of long dark eyes and red mouth, of fawn-like glance and waist slender as lightning, she who is called Vegavati: not seeing her beloved who was her own sweet life, when her own elder brother carried him off, she herself went there and, not even looking at that brother, denounced and rebuked him, and in the sword-battle —

33.translit:
kal navil tooL kaaLaiyaik kaippidittu meeNDum pooy
pon navilum aagam puNarndhilaLee? poongkangai (33)
33.meaning: did she not take by the hand the young hero of mountain-firm shoulders, and going back, embrace his gold-gleaming chest? The beautiful Ganga —

34.translit:
munnam punal parakkum nal naadan min aadum
kol navilum neeL veel kurukkaL kula madhalai
than nigar onRu illaadha venRith thananjayanai
pannaagaraayan madap paavai paavai than (34)
34.meaning: the good land where the waters of the Ganga spread before; the child of the Kuru lineage who bore the long murderous lightning-flashing spear — Arjuna of victory, who had none equal to himself: him the gentle, lovely maiden, the daughter of the Naga king (Ulupi) —

35.translit:
manniya naaN achcham madam enRu ivai agala
thannudaiya kongai mugam neriya thaan avan than (35)
35.meaning: casting aside these — the abiding modesty, fear, and reserve — so that the tips of her own breasts were pressed close, she herself, upon his —

36.translit:
pon varai aagam thazheei koNDu pooyt thanadhu
nal nagaram pukku nayandhu inidhu vaazhndhadhuvum
mun uraiyil keettu aRivadhu illaiyee? soozh kadaluL (36)
36.meaning: embracing his golden-mountain-like chest and bearing him off, did she not enter her own good city and live there happily and sweetly in love? Have you not heard and known this in the lore of old? In the encircling ocean —

37.translit:
pon nagaram seRRa purandharanoodu eer okkum
mannavan vaaNan avuNarkku vaaL veendhan
thannudaiya paavai ulagattuth than okkum
kanniyarai illaadha kaatchiyaaL thannudaiya (37)
37.meaning: the king Banasura, who equalled in glory Indra the destroyer of the golden city (of the asuras), the sword-king of the demons — his daughter (Usha), of such beauty that there was no maiden in the world to equal her — she, through her own —

38.translit:
in uyirth thoozhiyaal em perumaan een thuzhaay
mannu maNi varaith thooL maayavan paaviyeen (38)
38.meaning: dear-as-life companion (Chitralekha) — through her, our Lord, the wondrous Maya, of jewel-mountain-like shoulders abiding with cool tulasi — (brought to her). Wretched as I am —

39.translit:
ennai idhu viLaitta eer iraNDu maal varaith thooL
mannavan than kaadhalanai maayattaal koNDupooy
kanni thanbaal vaikka maRRavanoodu ettanai oor
manniya peer inbam eydhinaaL maRRu ivai thaan (39)
39.meaning: the king of four great-mountain-like shoulders, who has wrought this state upon me — when by magic he carried off her (Usha's) beloved (Aniruddha) and set him beside the maiden, how great an abiding bliss did she attain with him! And as for these (other tales) —

40.translit:
ennaale keettiiree eezhaikaaL en uraikkeen?
mannu malai araiyan pon paavai vaaL nilaa
40.meaning: O simple-hearted maidens, you have heard of my plight only through me; what more can I say? She is the golden image of the eternal mountain-king (Himavan), with a face like bright moonlight,

41.translit:
minnum aNi muRuval sev vaay umai ennum
anna naDaiya aNangu nuDangu iDai seer
pon uDambu vaaDa pulan aindhum nondhu agala
tannuDaiya kuuzhaic caDaapaaram thaan tharitthu aangu
anna arun thavatthin uuDu pooy aayiram tooL
41.meaning: She is Uma of the gleaming bright smile and red lips, with a swan-like gait, a graceful one with a swaying slender waist. So that her golden body wasted away and her five senses suffered and left her, she bound up her loose tresses into a matted crown of penance, and through such rare austerity she went on — and (Lord Shiva, having) a thousand shoulders,

42.translit:
mannu karathalangaL maTTitthu maathirangaL
minni eri viisa meel eDuttha cuuzh kazhal kaal
pon ulagam eezhum kaDandhu umbar meel silumba
mannu kula varaiyum maaruthamum thaaragaiyum
tannin uDanee suzhala suzhanRu aaDum
kol navilum muuvilai veel kuutthan poDi aaDi
annavan tan pon agalam senRu aangu aNaindhilaLee?
panni uraikkungkaal paaradham aam paaviyeeRku
42.meaning: clapping his abiding hands together, his ankleted feet raised aloft so that the directions flashed with lightning-fire, he danced — crossing all seven golden worlds and spreading beyond into the heavens, while the eternal lofty mountains, the winds, and the stars whirled along with him. Did she not go and embrace the golden chest of that dancer, who bears the killing three-bladed spear and is smeared with sacred ash? For me, a sinner, if I were to recount it all in detail, it would become an epic like the Mahabharata.

43.translit:
en uRu nooy yaan uraippak keeLmin irum pozhil cuuzh
mannum maRaiyoor tirunaRaiyuur maa malai pool
pon iyalum maaDak kavaaDam kaDandhu pukku
ennuDaiya kaN kaLippa nookkineen nookkudalum
43.meaning: Hear me as I tell of the affliction that has come upon me: at Tirunaraiyur, surrounded by vast groves and home to abiding Vedic brahmins, I passed through the doors of the golden, mountain-like mansions and entered, and gazed with my eyes rejoicing — and as I gazed,

44.translit:
mannan tiru maarbum vaayum aDi iNaiyum
pannu karathalamum kaNkaLum pangayatthin
pon iyal kaaDu oor maNivaraimeel puutthadhupool
minni oLi paDaippa viizh naaNum tooL vaLaiyum
manniya kuNDalamum aaramum niiL muDiyum
tunnu veyil viritta cuuLaamaNi imaippa
mannu marakathak kunRin marungee oor
44.meaning: the Lord's sacred chest, his mouth, his pair of feet, his praised hands and his eyes — as though a forest of golden lotuses had blossomed upon a gem-mountain — shone forth radiantly; his hanging cord, his shoulder-rings, his abiding earrings, his garland, his tall crown, and his crest-jewel that spread out dense sunlight all glittered; and beside that abiding emerald hill (his dark form), there was one —

45.translit:
in iLa vanjikkoDi onRu ninRadhudhaan
annam aay maan aay aNi mayil aay aangu iDaiyee
min aay iLa veey iraNDu aay iNaic ceppu aay
mun aaya thoNDai aay keNDaik kulam iraNDu aay
anna tiru uruvam ninRadhu aRiyaadhee
ennuDaiya nenjum aRivum ina vaLaiyum
pon iyalum meegalaiyum aangu ozhiyap poondheeRku
mannu maRikaDalum aarkkum madhi ugutta
45.meaning: a sweet, tender vanji-creeper stood there — like a swan, like a doe, like a beautiful peacock, and in between (her form) like lightning, like two young bamboo stems, like a pair of golden vessels, like a forward-curving thondai fruit, like a pair of kendai fishes — such a sacred form stood there. Not realizing this, I came away leaving behind my heart, my wits, my cluster of bangles, and my golden girdle; and the abiding, surging sea roars, and the moon pours down

46.translit:
in nilaavin kadhirum en thanakkee veydhu aagum
tannuDaiya tanmai thavira thaan enkoloo?
tennan pothiyil cezhum sandhin thaadhu aLaindhu
man iv ulagai manam kaLippa vandhu iyangum
in iLam puun thenRalum viisum eri enakkee
munniya peNNaimeel muL muLarik kuuTTagatthu
pinnum av anRil peDai vaayc ciRu kuralum
ennuDaiya nenjukku oor iir vaaLaam en ceygeen?
kal navil tooL kaaman karuppuc cilai vaLaiya
kol navilum puung kaNaigaL kootthu potha aNaindhu
tannuDaiya tooL kazhiya vaangi thamiyeenmeel
46.meaning: the rays of its sweet moonlight too turn scorching for me alone — what is this, that it strays from its own (cooling) nature? The sweet, tender, flower-laden southern breeze, that has mingled with the rich sandal pollen of the Pothiyil hills of the Pandya land and comes wandering to gladden the hearts of all this world, blows like fire upon me alone. And on the tall palmyra nearby, in a nest of thorny twigs, the soft little cry from the mouth of the female anril bird becomes a double-edged sword to my heart — what shall I do? Manmatha (Kama), of mountain-firm shoulders, bending his sugarcane bow and fitting deadly flower-arrows, drew them right back past his shoulder, and upon lonely me

47.translit:
ennuDaiya nenjee ilakkaaga eyginRaan
pin idhanaik kaappiir thaam illaiyee peedhaiyeen
47.meaning: he shoots, making my very heart his target. There is no one to protect me from this — and I am a foolish, helpless one.

48.translit:
kal navilum kaaTTagatthu oor vallik kaDi malarin
nal naRu vaasam maRRu aaraanum eydhaamee
mannum vaRu nilatthu vaaLaangu ugutthadhu pool
ennuDaiya peNmaiyum en nalanum en mulaiyum
mannum malar mangai maindhan kaNapuratthu
48.meaning: Like the good fragrant scent of a creeper's lovely blossom in a stony forest being shed in vain upon the abiding barren ground where no one else can reach it, so too my womanhood, my beauty, and my breasts — if they do not touch the golden chest of the youthful Lord of Tirukkannapuram, the consort of the abiding lotus-Lady (Lakshmi),

49.translit:
ponmalai pool ninRavan tan pon agalam thooyaaveel
en ivaidhaan vaaLaa? enakkee poRai aagi
mun irundhu muukkinRu muuvaamaik kaappadhu oor
mannum marundhu aRiviir illaiyee? maal viDaiyin
49.meaning: who stands like a golden mountain — then of what use are these to me? They become only a burden to me alone, sitting before me and mocking me. Do you know of no abiding medicine that would keep them (and me) from withering away? Of the great bull,

50.translit:
tunnu piDar erutthut tuukkuNDu van toDaraal
kanniyar kaN miLirak kaTTuNDu maalaivaayt
50.meaning: its thick-necked nape held high, bound with a strong rope while young maidens' eyes glanced about — at evening time

51.translit:
tannuDaiya naa ozhiyaadhu aaDum tani maNiyin
in isai oosaiyum vandhu en cevi tanakkee
kol navilum eqkil koDidhu aay neDidhu aagum
en idhanaik kaakkumaa? sollaiir idhu viLaitta
51.meaning: the sweet musical sound of the solitary bell whose tongue never stops swinging comes and reaches my very ears, and it grows crueler and sharper than a killing spear-blade, and stretches long. How can I guard against this? Tell me — the one who has brought this about,

52.translit:
mannan naRum thuzhaay vaazh maarban maa madhikooL
munnam viDutha mugil vaNNan kaayaavin
52.meaning: the King, on whose chest the fragrant tulasi flourishes, the cloud-hued Lord who once released (Rahu's) grip upon the great moon, whose hue is like the kaya blossom —

53.translit:
sinna naRum puun tigazh vaNNan vaNNam pool
anna kaDalai malai iTTu aNai kaTTi
mannan iraavaNanai maa maNDu vem samatthup
pon muDigaL patthum puraLa saram thurandhu
ten ulagam eeRRuvittha seevaganai aayirak kaN
53.meaning: his hue like the radiant color of the small fragrant blossoms — that dark Lord cast a mountain into such a sea and built a causeway, and in the great raging fierce battle he loosed arrows so that the crowned king Ravana's ten golden heads rolled, and sent him on to the southern world (of death) — that valiant warrior; and the thousand-eyed

54.translit:
mannavan vaanamum vaanavar tam pon ulagum
tannuDaiya tooL valiyaal kaikkoNDa thaanavanai
pin oor ari uruvam aagi eri vizhitthu
kol navilum vem samatthuk kollaadhee vallaaLan
54.meaning: king's (Indra's) heaven and the gods' golden world that the demon (Hiranya) had seized by the might of his own shoulders — that demon (the Lord) did not slay merely in a fierce deadly battle; instead, becoming a man-lion form with blazing eyes, the mighty one

55.translit:
mannu maNik kunji paRRi vara iirttu
tannuDaiya thaaLmeel kiDaatthi avanuDaiya
55.meaning: seized him by his abiding jeweled tuft of hair and dragged him forward, laid him across his own thigh, and

56.translit:
pon agalam vaL ugiraal poozhndhu pugazhpaDaitha
min ilangu aazhip paDait taDak kai viiranai
mannu iv agal iDatthai maa mudhu niir thaan vizhunga
pinnum oor eenamaayp pukku vaLai maruppil
kol navilum kuur nudhi veel vaitthu eDutha kuutthanai
mannum vaDa malaiyai matthaaga maasuNatthaal
minnum iru suDarum viNNum piRangu oLiyum
tanninuDanee suzhala malai tiritthu aangu
in amudham vaanavarai uuTTi avaruDaiya
mannum tuyar kaDinda vaLLalai maRRu anRiyum
56.meaning: split open his golden chest with sharp claws and won great renown — that hero whose broad hand bears the lightning-flashing discus weapon. When the great ancient waters swallowed up this abiding wide earth, he entered as a boar and lifted it up upon his curved tusk, sharp-pointed like a killing spear — that dancer (Lord). He churned the abiding northern mountain (Mandara), using it as a churning-rod with the great serpent (as rope), while the two flashing luminaries (sun and moon), the sky, and the spreading radiance whirled along with it, and fed the sweet nectar to the celestials — that munificent one who removed the gods' abiding sorrow; and besides that,

57.translit:
tan uruvam aarum aRiyaamal taan angu oor
mannum kuRaL uruvil maaNiyaay maavali tan
57.meaning: so that no one could recognize his true form, he took there the abiding shape of a dwarf, as a (brahmin) bachelor, and to Mahabali's

58.translit:
pon iyalum veeLvikkaN pukku irundhu poor veendhar
mannai manam koLLa vanjitthu nenju urukki
ennuDaiya paadhatthaal yaan aLappa muuvaDi maN
mannaa tharuga enRu vaay tiRappa maRRu avanum
58.meaning: golden sacrifice he came and sat, beguiling the heart of that king among warring monarchs and melting his heart, and opened his lips to say, 'O King, grant me three feet of earth that I may measure out with my own feet'; and he (Bali),

59.translit:
ennaal tharappaTTadhu enRalumee atthuNaikkaN
min aar maNi muDi pooy viN taDava meel eDutha
59.meaning: the moment he said, 'It is granted by me,' at that very instant (the Lord grew so vast that) his gem-set crown, flashing like lightning, rose up to brush against the heavens — he who lifted high

60.translit:
pon aar kanai kazhal kaal eezh ulagum pooyk kadandhu angu
oNRaa asurar thuLanga sela neetti
man iv agal idaththai maavaliyai vanjiththu
than ulagam aakkuviththa thaaLanai thaamaraimeel (60)
60.meaning: He whose golden anklets ring upon His feet, who strode across and transcended all seven worlds, who stretched out His foot so that the asuras, who could not stand as one, trembled and fled; who deceived Mahabali the king of this wide earth and made it His own world — that One of the sacred feet, who rests upon the lotus.

61.translit:
min idaiyaaL naayaganai viN nagaruL pon malaiyai
ponni maNi kozhikkum poong kudandhaip poor vidaiyai
thennan kuRunguddiyuL sem pavaLak kuNRinai
manniya thaN seeRai vaLLalai maamalarmeel (61)
61.meaning: He who is the Lord of her whose waist is slender as lightning (Lakshmi); the golden mountain in the celestial city (Tirunagari/Vinnagaram); the warrior-bull of beautiful Kudandhai (Kumbakonam) where the Kaveri washes down gems; the red coral hillock in southern Kurungudi; the generous one who abides in cool Seerai — He who is upon the great flower.

62.translit:
annam thuyilum aNi neer vayal aali
ennudaiya in amudhai evvuL peru malaiyai
kanni madhiL soozh kaNamangaik kaRpagaththai
minnai iru sudarai veLLaRaiyuL kal aRaimeel
ponnai maragadhaththai putkuzhi em poor eeRRai
mannum arangaththu em maa maNiyai vallavaazhp (62)
62.meaning: He of Tiruvaali, with lovely watered fields where swans slumber; my own sweet nectar; the great mountain of Evvul (Tiruvallur); the wish-granting Karpaka tree of Kanamangai girded by fresh maiden-walls; the lightning and the twin luminaries (sun and moon), He upon the stone within Velllarai; the gold, the emerald, my battling bull of Putkuzhi; my great gem of everlasting Srirangam; He of Vallavaazh.

63.translit:
pinnai maNaaLanai peeril piRappiliyai
thol neerk kadal kidandha thooLaa maNic chudarai
en manaththu maalai idavendhai eesanai
mannum kadalmallai maayavanai vaanavar tham (63)
63.meaning: He who is the bridegroom of Nappinnai; the birthless One of Peril (Tiruppernagar); the unpierced gem-radiance reclining upon the ancient deep ocean; the Tirumal within my heart; the Lord of Idavendhai (Thiruvidavendai); the wondrous One of everlasting Kadalmallai (Mahabalipuram); He of the celestials.

64.translit:
senni maNic chudarai thaNkaal thiRal valiyai
thannaip piRar aRiyaa thaththuvaththai muththinai
annaththai meenai ariyai aru maRaiyai
mun iv ulagu uNda moorththiyai koovaloor (64)
64.meaning: The crowning gem-radiance; the strong and mighty One of Thankaal; the Truth whom others cannot know; the pearl; the Swan (Hamsa), the Fish (Matsya), the Lion (Narasimha), the rare Vedas; the form that of old swallowed up this world; He of Koovaloor (Tirukkovalur).

65.translit:
mannum idaikazhi em maayavanai peey alaRap
pinnum mulai uNda piLLaiyai aLLalvaay (65)
65.meaning: Our enduring wondrous One of Idaikazhi (the central shrine at Tirukkovalur); the Child who sucked the breast so that the demoness (Putana) shrieked and perished; He of the miry-mouthed marsh.

66.translit:
annam irai theer azhundhoor ezhum sudarai
then thillaic chiththirakoodaththu en selvanai (66)
66.meaning: The radiance that rises at Azhundhur, where swans seek their food; my treasure of Chitrakuta in southern Thillai (Chidambaram).

67.translit:
minni mazhai thavazhum veengadaththu em viththaganai
mannanai maalirunjoolai maNaaLanai
kol navilum aazhip padaiyaanai koottiyoor (67)
67.meaning: Our wondrous adept of Venkatam, where lightning flashes and rain-clouds creep; the King; the bridegroom of Maalirunjolai (Azhagar Koyil); He who bears the killing discus as His weapon; He of Kottiyur.

68.translit:
anna uruvin ariyai thirumeyyaththu
in amudha veLLaththai indhaLoor andhaNanai
mannu madhiL kacchi veeLukkai aaL ariyai
manniya paadagaththu em maindhanai vehkaavil (68)
68.meaning: The Lion (Narasimha) of swan-like form, He of Tirumeyyam; the flood of sweet nectar of Indaloor (Thiruvindaloor); the holy Brahmin-form; the man-Lion of Velukkai in Kacchi (Kanchi) with enduring ramparts; our youthful one of everlasting Padagam; He of Vehka.

69.translit:
unniya yogaththu uRakkaththai o000ragaththuL
annavanai atta puyagaraththu emmaan eeRRai
ennai manam kavarndha eesanai vaanavartham (69)
69.meaning: He who is the slumber of contemplated yoga (Yoga-nidra); that One within Ooragam (Thiru-ooragam in Kanchi); our Lord, the bull, of Ashtabhujakaram; the Lord who stole away my heart; He of the celestials.

70.translit:
munnavanai moozhikkaLaththu viLakkinai
annavanai aadhanoor aaNdu aLakkum aiyanai
nennalai inRinai naaLaiyai neermalaimeel (70)
70.meaning: The First One; the lamp of Moozhikkalam; that One; the Master of Aadhanoor who rules and measures out (all things); He who is yesterday, today, and tomorrow; He upon Neermalai (Tiruneermalai).

71.translit:
mannum maRai naangum aanaanai pullaaNith
thennan thamizhai vadamozhiyai naangooril
mannum maNimaadakkooyil maNaaLanai
nal neer thalaicchanga naaL madhiyai naan vaNangum (71)
71.meaning: He who became the four enduring Vedas; He of Pullaani; the Tamil of the southland and the Sanskrit tongue; the bridegroom of the everlasting Manimaadakkoyil in Naangoor; the new-risen moon of fair-watered Thalaichanga(naanmadiyam) whom I worship.

72.translit:
kaNNanai kaNNapuraththaanai then naRaiyoor
mannum maNimaadakkooyil maNaaLanai
kal navil thooL kaaLaiyaik kaNdu aangu kaithozhudhu
en nilaimai ellaam aRiviththaal em perumaan
than aruLum aagamum thaaraaneel thannai naan (72)
72.meaning: Kannan; He of Kannapuram; the bridegroom of the everlasting Manimaadakkoyil in southern Naraiyoor; the young bull whose shoulders are firm as stone — if I behold Him there and worship with folded hands and make known to Him all my condition, and if my Lord still gives me neither His grace nor His embrace, then Him I shall (continue thus).

73.translit:
min idaiyaar seeriyilum veedhiyargaL vaazhvidaththum
than adiyaar munbum tharaNi muzhudhu aaLum
kol navilum veel veendhar koottaththum naattagaththum
than nilaimai ellaam aRivippan thaan muna naaL (73)
73.meaning: In the streets of women whose waists are slender as lightning, and in the dwelling places of the brahmins, and before His own devotees, and in the assemblies of the spear-bearing kings who rule the whole earth, and throughout the land — I shall make known all His state (toward me), even as in the days of old.

74.translit:
min idai aaycchiyar tham seerik kaLavinkaN
thunnu padal thiRandhu pukku thayir veNNey (74)
74.meaning: In the cowherd-women's quarter — they of lightning-slender waists — by stealth He opened the close-set door and entered, and the curds and butter...

75.translit:
than vayiRu aara vizhunga kozhung kayal kaN
mannu madavoorgaL paRRi oor vaan kayiRRaal
pinnum uralodu kattuNda peRRimaiyum
annadhu oor poodhamaay aayar vizhavinkaN
thunnu sagadaththaal pukka perunj sooRRai (75)
75.meaning: ...He swallowed till His belly was full, and the abiding tender women with eyes like fat carp seized Him and bound Him with a great rope, again tied to the mortar — that very deed; and, becoming a certain spirit-being, the great heap of feast-rice that came in by the laden cart at the cowherds' festival...

76.translit:
mun irundhu muRRa thaan thuRRiya theRRenavum
mannar perunj savaiyuL vaazh veendhar thoodhanaayth
thannai igazhndhu uraippa thaan muna naaL senRadhuvum
mannu paRai kaRanga mangaiyar tham kaN kaLippa (76)
76.meaning: ...sitting before it He devoured it utterly — that too is clear; and how, in the great assembly of kings, He went of old as the envoy of the living monarchs, while they reviled and spoke ill of Him; and while the abiding drums resounded and the eyes of the maidens rejoiced...

77.translit:
kol navilum kooththanaay peerththum kudam aadi
en ivan ennappadukinRa eedaRavum
then ilangaiyaatti arakkar kulap paavai
mannan iraavaNan than nal thangai vaaL eyiRRuth (77)
77.meaning: ...becoming a dancer skilled (in the slaying-dance), and again dancing the pot-dance (kudak-koothu), so that men asked in wonder 'what is this one?' — that incomparable greatness too; and she of southern Lanka, the doll of the demon-race, the fair sister of king Ravana, of sword-like fangs...

78.translit:
thunnu sudu sinaththuc choorppaNagaa soorvu eydhi
pon niRam koNdu pularndhu ezhundha kaamaththaal
thannai nayandhaaLaith thaan munindhu mookku arindhu
manniya thiNNenavum vaayndha malai poolum
than nigar onRu illaadha thaadagaiyai maa munikkaa (78)
78.meaning: ...Surpanakha of dense, burning wrath, who grew faint and, kindling with desire that bleached her golden hue, longed for Him — He grew angry at her and cut off her nose; that firm act too; and Tataka, who was like a mountain and had no equal to herself — for the sake of the great sage (Vishwamitra)...

79.translit: then ulagam eeRRuviththa thiN thiRalum maRRu ivaidhaan (79)
79.meaning: ...His firm valour that dispatched (Tataka) to the nether world — all these deeds too, and the rest of them, even these.

80.translit:
unni ulavaa ulagu aRiya oorvana naan 
munni muLaiththu ezhundhu oongi oLi parandha 
manniya poom peNNai madal (80)
80.meaning: Meditating ceaselessly within, I shall ride out so that this unfading world may know of it. Rising and sprouting forth, growing tall and spreading its radiance, the enduring, blossoming palmyra-palm madal is what I take up.

0.translit:
tiruvazhuti naadenrum tenkurukoor enrum
maruviniya vaNporunal enrum - arumaraigaL
andaadi seydaan adiyiNaiye eppozhudum
sindiyaay nenje! teLindu.
0.meaning: He who composed the precious Vedas as a garland of antaadi verses, hailing the Tiruvazhuti land, southern Kurukur, and the sweet bountiful Porunal river - O heart, clearly and at all times meditate upon his twin holy feet.

1.translit:
manattaalum vaayaalum vaNkurukoor peNum
inattaarai allaadu iraignjen - tanattaalum
edum kuraivilen endai sadagopan
paadangaL yaamudaiya parru.
1.meaning: In mind and in speech I will worship none but the kindred who cherish bountiful Kurukur. In wealth too I lack nothing; the feet of my father Satakopa (Nammalwar) are the hold that is mine.

2.translit:
eyndaperung keerti iraamaa nusamunitan
vaayndamalarp paadam vaNangugindren - aayndaperunj
seeraar sadagopan sendamizhve dam-tarikkum
peraada uLLam pera.
2.meaning: I bow before the well-attained lotus feet of the sage Ramanuja of fitting great fame, that I may gain an unswerving heart which holds firmly to the pure Tamil Veda of the deeply-explored, glory-filled Satakopa.

3.translit:
vaantigazhum solai matiL arangar vaNpugazhmel
aanra tamizhmaraigaL aayiramum - eenra
mudaltaay sadagopan; moymbaal vaLartta
idattaay iraamaa nusan.
3.meaning: The thousand consummate Tamil Veda-hymns upon the bountiful glory of the Lord of Arangam (Srirangam), with His sky-touching groves and ramparts - the mother who bore them is Satakopa (Nammalwar); the loving nurse who raised them with strength is Ramanuja.

4.translit:
uyarva ra uyarnalam udaiyavan yavanavan
mayarva ra madinalam aruLinan yavanavan
ayarvarum amararkaL adipadi yavanavan
tuyararu sudaradi tozhudezhen manane.
4.meaning: He who possesses the highest goodness beyond all height - whoever He is; He who, removing all confusion, graciously granted the clarity of wisdom - whoever He is; He who is the chief lord of the never-failing immortals - whoever He is: O my heart, rise up and worship His radiant feet that cut away all sorrow.

5.translit:
mananaga malamara malarmisai yezhutarum
mananuNar vaLavilan, poriyuNar vavaiyilan
inanuNar, muzhunalam, edirnigazh kazhivinum
inanilan, en-en-uyir, migunarai yilane.
5.meaning: He is beyond the reach of the mind's knowing that arises spotless upon the blossom of inner thought, and beyond the knowledge of the senses. He is the One to be known, the whole of goodness; in future, present, and past He has no equal. He, my very life, has none greater than Him.

6.translit:
ilanadu vudaiyani tenaninai variyavan
nilanidai visumbidai uruvinan aruvinan
pulanodu pulanalan, ozhivilan, paranda an
nalanudai yoruvanai naNuginam naame.
6.meaning: He is hard to conceive as either non-existent or possessed of this or that; He is both with form and without form, present on earth and in the sky. He is within the senses and beyond the senses, never absent, all-pervading. That One who is the goodness so spread abroad - Him have we drawn near.

7.translit:
naamava nivanuvan, avaLivaLuvaLevaL
taamavarivaruvar, aduvidu vuduvedu
veemavai yivaiyuvai, yavainalan teengavai
aamavai yaayavai, yaaynindra avare.
7.meaning: He is that one, this one, the one yonder; she is that, this, yonder, and whichever; they are those, these, those yonder; it is that, this, yonder, and whichever. He is all that perishes - these, those near, those far; He is the good and the bad of them; He becomes them and is them - He alone stands as all that is.

8.translit:
avaravar tamadama daRivaRi vagaivagai
avarava riRaiyava renavadi yadaivargaL
avarava riRaiyavar kuraivila riRaiyavar
avarav vidivazhi yadaiyanin ranare.
8.meaning: Each one, according to his own kind and measure of understanding, takes as his god whomever, and reaches that one's feet. Those gods of theirs are without any want; and He stands so that, by the path of their destiny, they each attain their respective gods.

9.translit:
nindranariruntanar kidantanar tirintanar
nindrilariruntilar kidantilartirintilar
enrumoriyalvinar enaninaivariyavar
enrumoriyalvodu nindraventidare.
9.meaning: He stood, He sat, He lay, He wandered; He stood not, sat not, lay not, wandered not. He is hard to conceive as being ever of one single nature; yet He stands, ever with one nature, as the very dispeller of fierce distress.

10.translit:
tidavisum berivaLi neernila mivaimisai
padarporuL muzhuvadu maayavai yavaitorum
udalmisai yuyirenak karandengum paranduLan
sudarmigu surudiyu Livaiyanda surane.
10.meaning: Becoming the whole of every thing that spreads upon firm sky, fire, wind, water and earth, He is hidden everywhere within each of them as the soul is hidden within the body, and so He pervades all. He, the god of the cosmos who swallowed these worlds, dwells within the radiant Vedas.

11.translit:
surarari varunilai viNmudal muzhuvadum
varanmuda laayavai muzhuduNda paraparan
puramoru moonderit tamararkku mariviyandu
aranayan enavula kazhittamait tuLane.
11.meaning: The Supreme of supremes, the first cause of all from the heavens onward - the state hard even for the celestials to know - who swallowed the whole. Burning the three cities into one, and granting wonder-wisdom even to the immortals, He, as Hara (Siva) and Aja (Brahma), dissolves and fashions the worlds.

12.translit:
uLaneni luLanava nuruvamiv vuruvugaL
uLanala nenilavan aruvamiv varuvugaL
uLanena vilanena vivaiguNa mudaimaiyil
uLanniru tagaimaiyo dozhivilan parande.
12.meaning: If we say He exists, He exists - and these visible forms are His form; if we say He exists not, then He is the formless - and these formless things are His. Since 'He is' and 'He is not' are both qualities of His having, He exists in both natures, never absent, all-pervading.

13.translit:
parandataN paravaiyuLneertorum paranduLan
parandaa Ndamidena nilavisum bozhivara
karandasi lidantorum idantigazh porudorum
karandengum paranduLa nivaiyuNda karane.
13.meaning: He pervades through every drop of water in the cool spreading sea; He pervades, leaving no gap, through earth and sky and all this expanse called the cosmos. Hidden within every small place and within every thing that shines in those places, hidden yet everywhere pervading - He is the agent who swallowed all these worlds.

14.translit:
karavisum berivaLi neernila mivaimisai
varanavil tiralvali yaLiporai yaaynindra
paranadi mel-kuru koorccada gopansol
niraNirai yaayirat tivaipattum veede.
14.meaning: Of the Supreme who stands as the eminence, the might, the strength, the grace, and the forbearance pervading sky, fire, wind, water and earth - upon His feet, these ten verses of the well-ordered thousand spoken by Satakopa of Kurukur are themselves liberation.

15.translit:
veedumin murravum veeduseydu ummuyir
veedudai yaanidai veeduseymmine.
15.meaning: Renounce all things, make a full renunciation, and surrender your soul into the One who holds liberation in His keeping.

16.translit:
minnin nilaiyila--mannuyi raakkaigaL
ennu midattu iRai--unnumin neere.
16.meaning: The bodies of the eternal souls are as unstable as a flash of lightning. Knowing this, you yourselves at once meditate upon the Lord.

17.translit:
neernuma denrivai, vermudal maaytt-iRai
sermin uyirkku adaner-niraiyille.
17.meaning: Root out from the very root these notions of 'you' and 'yours,' and at once join the Lord; for the soul there is nothing equal to that.

18.translit:
illadu muLLadum allada vanuru
ellaiyi landalam pulgupar rarre.
18.meaning: His form is neither the non-existent nor the existent. Embrace that boundless goodness of His, with all other attachment cut away.

19.translit:
arradu parrenil urradu veeduuyir
serradu mannuRil arriRai parre.
19.meaning: If your attachment is cut away, the soul has attained liberation. If you would have what is gained abide forever, then, free of all else, lay hold of the Lord.

100.translit:
kamalakkaNNan en kaNNin uLLaan kaaNbaN avan kaNgaLaale,
amalangaLaaga vizhikkum aimpulanum avan moorthi,
kamalatt-ayan nambi-thannai kaNNudalaanodum thoatri
amalat-deyvattodu ulagam aakki en netriyuLaane.
100.meaning: The lotus-eyed Lord dwells within my eyes, and it is through His eyes that I see. The five senses that perceive without blemish are themselves His form.
